payload.formula_explanation: Shows 30 days of daily spend as a stacked area chart. Google Ads spend is shown in blue, Meta Ads in lighter blue stacked on top. The moving average line smooths out daily fluctuations to reveal spending trends. The budget reference line shows daily target.
how_to_read: Blue area represents Google Ads daily spend
Light blue stacked area represents Meta Ads daily spend
Total height shows combined daily spend
The dashed horizontal line marks your daily budget target
Weekend periods may show lower spend (depending on campaigns)
The moving average line reveals spending trends
Hover for exact daily values by platform
decisions_answered: How consistent is our daily spending pattern?
Are there spending anomalies or sudden changes?
Which platform contributes more to daily spend?
Do weekends show different spending patterns?
Is spend trending up or down over the month?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows daily spend over the last 30 days, broken down by platform.

The blue area is Google Ads spend. Stacked on top in lighter blue is Meta Ads spend. Total height shows combined daily spend.

The dashed line marks your daily budget target. When the area exceeds this line, you overspent that day. When it falls short, you underspent.

Look for patterns: consistent spending helps campaign algorithms learn. Sudden drops might indicate budget exhaustion or paused campaigns. Weekend dips are normal for some businesses.

This answers: How consistent is spending? Are there anomalies? Which platform dominates daily spend?

payload.formula_explanation: This table tracks daily checkout flow performance, showing where users drop off during the multi-step checkout process. High abandonment at specific stages (shipping or payment) indicates friction that can be addressed with targeted optimizations.
how_to_read: Each row shows one day of checkout funnel data
Started Checkout is users who began the process
Completed is users who finished purchasing
Abandonment Rate shows the overall drop-off percentage
Red highlighting indicates days with above-average abandonment
decisions_answered: What is our daily checkout abandonment rate?
Which checkout step causes the most drop-offs?
Are there specific days with unusually high abandonment?
Is abandonment trending up or down over time?
payload.narration_text:
This table provides a daily breakdown of your checkout funnel, tracking every step from start to purchase.

The abandonment rate formula: begin checkout minus purchases, divided by begin checkout, times 100. The same logic applies to each sub-step like shipping and payment.

Each row is one day. Compare Started Checkout to Completed to see your success rate. The abandonment rate column highlights problem days in red.

This answers critical questions: What's our daily abandonment rate? Which step loses most users - shipping or payment? Are there spike days to investigate? And is the trend improving over time?

payload.formula_explanation: Daily impression share shows what percentage of eligible impressions your ads captured. Budget lost share indicates impressions missed due to insufficient budget. Rank lost share shows impressions missed due to ad rank (quality score + bid) being lower than competitors.
how_to_read: Blue line shows your actual impression share - higher is better
Orange dashed line shows share lost due to budget constraints
Red dashed line shows share lost due to competitive rank issues
Sum of all three should theoretically equal 100% of eligible impressions
Spikes in budget lost indicate days where budget ran out
Rising rank lost trends suggest increasing competitive pressure
decisions_answered: How has our competitive position changed over the past 60 days?
Are we losing more impressions to budget or rank issues?
When did significant competitive changes occur?
Is our impression share trending up or down over time?
payload.narration_text:
This multi-line chart tracks your competitive position over the past 60 days across three key metrics.

The blue line shows your impression share - the percentage of eligible impressions where your ads appeared. Higher is better, and above 50% typically indicates strong market presence.

The orange dashed line shows budget lost share - impressions you missed because your budget ran out. If this is consistently high, you need more budget to capture available demand.

The red dashed line shows rank lost share - impressions you missed because competitors outranked you. This is driven by quality score and bid amounts.

Watch for trends: rising budget lost means increasing demand you're not capturing. Rising rank lost means competitors are becoming more aggressive. A healthy account typically shows impression share trending up while both loss metrics trend down.

payload.formula_explanation: Meta uses a default attribution window of 7-day click and 1-day view. This chart shows how conversions are attributed across these windows for each action type in the conversion funnel.
how_to_read: Blue segments show 7-day click attribution - conversions after ad click
Purple segments show 1-day view attribution - conversions after viewing without clicking
Longer bars indicate more total conversions for that action type
Purchase and Add to Cart are the most valuable funnel actions
Heavy view-through attribution suggests strong brand awareness impact
decisions_answered: How do conversions distribute between click and view attribution?
Is view-through attribution a significant conversion driver?
Which funnel stages show the most activity?
Should we adjust our attribution model expectations?
payload.narration_text:
This chart breaks down how Meta attributes conversions using their default 7-day click and 1-day view window.

The formula: 7-day click captures conversions within 7 days after someone clicks your ad. 1-day view captures conversions within 1 day of viewing your ad without clicking.

Reading this chart: Blue segments are click-through conversions - someone clicked and later converted. Purple segments are view-through - they saw the ad, didn't click, but converted anyway.

This matters because view-through can represent significant value, especially for brand awareness campaigns. Heavy view attribution suggests your ads influence purchase decisions even without direct clicks.

Key insights: How do click versus view conversions compare? Is view-through driving meaningful value? Which funnel stages show the most activity? This helps calibrate your attribution expectations.

payload.formula_explanation: Number of unique ads that served at least one impression during the period across Google and Meta. An ad with zero impressions is not counted as active.
how_to_read: The value is the count of distinct ad IDs that received impressions in the date range.
A growing count means more creatives are live; a shrinking count may mean ads are being paused or consolidated.
Filter by platform to see how many active creatives exist on Google vs Meta.
Pair with Total Spend to see average spend per active ad.
decisions_answered: How many creatives are actively running right now?
Has the size of my active creative library changed vs the prior period?
Do I have enough creative variety in market across platforms?

payload.formula_explanation: Count of distinct audiences that recorded activity during the period across Google and Meta. Each unique audience ID with data in the date range is counted once.
how_to_read: The value is the number of distinct audience IDs active in the date range.
A larger count means broader audience coverage; a smaller count means a more concentrated targeting strategy.
Use alongside Audience Spend to gauge average investment per active audience.
Track over time to see whether your audience portfolio is expanding or consolidating.
decisions_answered: How many distinct audiences am I actively targeting?
Is my audience portfolio broadening or narrowing?
Am I spreading budget across too many or too few audiences?

payload.formula_text: COUNT(DISTINCT campaign_id) WHERE spend > 0 (or conversion value/impressions/clicks > 0)
payload.formula_explanation: Total count of distinct campaigns with activity in the period across Google Ads, Meta Ads, and Klaviyo. Only campaigns with spend, revenue, impressions, or clicks are counted.
how_to_read: The value is the number of distinct campaigns active in the selected period.
The description breaks the count down by channel (Google, Meta, Klaviyo).
The change indicator compares the campaign count against the previous period.
A growing count can mean expanded testing; a shrinking count can mean consolidation or pausing.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns are we running this period?
How is activity split across Google, Meta, and Klaviyo?
Is our campaign footprint growing or contracting?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts the total number of alert rules that are currently enabled and monitoring your marketing metrics. Each rule defines thresholds for a specific metric (like ROAS, CPC, or Open Rate) and triggers alerts when values fall outside acceptable ranges.
how_to_read: The large number shows total active alert rules across all data sources
The sparkline shows rule count trend over the last 7 days
A higher count indicates more comprehensive monitoring coverage
Click to filter and view all configured rules
Tooltip shows breakdown by data source (GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo)
decisions_answered: How many metrics are we actively monitoring?
Do we have sufficient alert coverage across all channels?
Have we recently enabled or disabled any monitoring rules?
Which data sources have the most alert rules configured?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows the total number of active alert rules monitoring your marketing performance.

Active rules are those currently enabled and evaluating thresholds in real-time. The formula counts all rules with status equals enabled.

A higher number indicates more comprehensive monitoring. However, too many rules can create alert fatigue. Aim for focused coverage of critical metrics.

The sparkline shows if you've been adding or removing rules recently. Click to see the full breakdown by data source.

This answers: How many metrics are monitored? Is coverage sufficient? Which sources have the most rules?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks month-to-date spend and projects end-of-month total based on current run rate. The projection uses your average daily spend to estimate final monthly spend. Status indicates whether you'll hit, exceed, or fall short of budget.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual MTD spend percentage
The marker shows expected percentage based on day of month
Projected Spend shows estimated month-end total at current rate
Days Remaining shows business days left in the month
Status badge shows ON_TRACK, OVER_PACING, or UNDER_PACING
Color indicates projected variance from monthly budget
decisions_answered: Will we hit our monthly budget target at current spending rate?
How much budget do we have remaining this month?
What daily spend rate do we need to hit the monthly target?
Are we trending to over-spend or under-spend for the month?
payload.narration_text:
This progress bar shows month-to-date budget pacing with end-of-month projection.

The formulas: Actual pacing is MTD spend divided by monthly budget. Expected pacing accounts for which day of the month we're on. Projection multiplies average daily spend by days remaining.

The status badge tells you the key insight: ON TRACK means you'll hit budget within 5%. OVER PACING means you'll exceed budget. UNDER PACING means you'll fall short.

This answers: Will we hit monthly budget? How much remains? What daily rate do we need to finish on target?

payload.formula_explanation: Weighted (blended) return on ad spend: platform-reported revenue divided by total spend. The metric also surfaces an 'actual' ROAS using Magento revenue, and the gap between platform and Magento revenue.
how_to_read: The headline is platform-reported revenue divided by total ad spend (a multiplier).
A value of 3.0x means three dollars of attributed revenue per dollar spent.
The description compares platform revenue to Magento revenue, flagging attribution inflation.
The sparkline tracks daily blended ROAS for pacing and volatility.
decisions_answered: What return are we getting on every ad dollar?
How does platform-attributed ROAS compare to actual (Magento) ROAS?
Is overall efficiency improving or declining?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows historical monthly spend totals from MART_BUDGET_PACING, split by Google Ads and Meta Ads. Month-over-month variance reveals spending patterns, seasonality, and growth trends across the selected period.
how_to_read: Each bar is one calendar month's total ad spend, stacked by platform (Google Ads + Meta Ads).
Taller bars mean higher spend; compare bar heights to spot upward or downward trajectories.
The MoM Variance % shows the percentage change from the prior month — positive means spend grew, negative means it shrank.
Use it to identify seasonal peaks and to sanity-check whether current pacing is in line with history.
decisions_answered: Is our monthly spend trending up, down, or holding steady?
Which months historically see the biggest spend swings?
How does this month's spend compare to recent history?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows seasonal performance patterns by month and channel. Index above 1.0 means above-average performance (peak season). Index below 1.0 means below-average (slow season). Use these factors to adjust thresholds seasonally and avoid false alerts during predictable low periods.
how_to_read: Each row represents a marketing channel
Each column represents a month (Jan-Dec)
Cell color indicates seasonal index: red = below average, white = average, green = above average
Numbers show the exact seasonality multiplier
Index > 1.5 indicates peak season (Feb shows 2.0+ for many channels)
Index < 0.5 indicates slow season (Jun-Sep shows low values)
Use these factors to adjust thresholds by month
decisions_answered: Which months are peak season for each channel?
How much should I adjust thresholds for seasonal variation?
When should I expect below-average performance?
Are there channels that don't follow typical seasonality?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap shows seasonal performance patterns for each marketing channel by month.

The seasonality index compares monthly average to overall average. An index of 2.0 means that month typically performs twice as well as average. An index of 0.5 means half as well.

For this business, January through March shows high indices - peak season. Summer months June through September show low indices - slow season.

Use these factors to adjust thresholds. During February with a 2.5 index, a revenue threshold of $27,000 should become $67,500. This prevents false alerts during predictably high periods and catches real issues during slow periods.

This answers: When is peak season? How much should thresholds adjust? When to expect low performance?

payload.formula_text: SELECT * FROM AlertHistory WHERE triggered_at >= NOW() - 30d
payload.formula_explanation: Complete log of all alerts triggered in the last 30 days, including resolution details. Use this for auditing, identifying patterns, and measuring team response effectiveness. Duration shows how long each alert took to resolve.
how_to_read: Severity shows the alert level when triggered
Rule shows which configured rule generated the alert
Triggered shows when the alert first fired
Resolved shows when the alert was marked resolved (if applicable)
Duration shows total time from trigger to resolution
Acknowledged By shows who took ownership of the alert
Use search to find alerts for specific metrics or time periods
decisions_answered: What alerts have we had in the last 30 days?
How long does it typically take to resolve alerts?
Which team members are responding to alerts?
Are there recurring patterns in our alert history?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows the complete history of alerts over the last 30 days.

Each row is an alert that triggered, with details about when it happened, who acknowledged it, and how long it took to resolve.

Duration is particularly important. Alerts that take days to resolve indicate either false positives that should have been dismissed quickly, or real issues that weren't prioritized appropriately.

Look for patterns: the same rule triggering repeatedly, certain team members handling most alerts, or alerts that repeatedly go unacknowledged.

This answers: What's our alert history? How long do resolutions take? Who responds to alerts?

payload.formula_text: SELECT * FROM AlertRules ORDER BY status DESC
payload.formula_explanation: Lists all configured alert rules with their thresholds, notification channels, and recent trigger activity. Rules define what conditions generate alerts. Sorted by enabled status then by recent trigger frequency to highlight active monitoring.
how_to_read: Status indicator shows enabled (green) or disabled (gray)
Rule Name describes what the rule monitors
Metric shows which metric is being watched
Warning and Critical columns show threshold values
7d Triggers shows how often this rule triggered recently
Channels shows which notification methods are configured
Actions allow edit, enable/disable, or delete
decisions_answered: What alert rules do we have configured?
Which rules are triggering most frequently?
Are the thresholds set appropriately?
Which notification channels receive which alerts?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows all your configured alert rules.

Each rule defines a condition that triggers an alert. For example: trigger warning if ROAS drops below 5.91, trigger critical if below 4.89.

The 7-day trigger count helps identify noisy rules. If a rule triggers constantly, the threshold may be too sensitive. If it never triggers, it may be too loose.

Edit rules to adjust thresholds based on your business needs. Enable or disable rules seasonally. Delete rules that are no longer relevant.

This answers: What rules exist? Which trigger often? Are thresholds appropriate?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the trend of alert frequency over the last 30 days, broken down by severity level. Helps identify patterns like increasing alert frequency (potential systematic issues) or specific days with unusual alert spikes.
how_to_read: X-axis shows the last 30 days
Y-axis shows the count of alerts triggered
Red line represents critical alerts
Amber line represents warning alerts
Blue line represents informational alerts
Spikes indicate days with many threshold breaches
Upward trend over time suggests deteriorating performance
Flat low lines indicate stable, healthy performance
decisions_answered: Are alerts increasing or decreasing over time?
Were there specific days with unusual alert spikes?
Is performance generally stable or volatile?
Do alert patterns correlate with campaigns or seasonality?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart shows alert frequency over the last 30 days by severity level.

Each line represents a severity: red for critical, amber for warning, blue for informational. The height shows how many alerts triggered each day.

Look for patterns: A consistent upward trend suggests performance is deteriorating. Spikes might correlate with campaign changes or external factors. Flat lines near zero indicate healthy, stable performance.

If you see increasing alerts over time, investigate systematic causes rather than treating individual alerts. The trend matters more than any single day.

This answers: Are alerts increasing? Were there spike days? Is performance stable?

payload.formula_explanation: Average Order Value divides total revenue by the count of Magento orders for the period. Magento orders (rather than GA4 purchases) are used for financial accuracy.
how_to_read: The value is total revenue divided by the number of completed Magento orders.
A rising AOV means customers are spending more per transaction (bundling, upsells, premium mix).
The sparkline tracks daily AOV, which can be volatile on low-order days.
Compare against the previous period via the change indicator to spot pricing or mix shifts.
decisions_answered: How much does a typical order bring in?
Is AOV improving, suggesting effective upsell or pricing changes?
Should we focus on raising order value or order volume?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the total number of unique creative assets (images and videos) in your Meta advertising library, along with how many are currently active in running ads.
how_to_read: Images count shows total unique image creatives uploaded
Videos count shows total unique video creatives uploaded
Compare active vs total to understand utilization rate
Regular creative refresh prevents ad fatigue
Click to navigate to the full Creative Library for asset management
decisions_answered: How large is our creative asset library?
What is the ratio of images to videos in our strategy?
Are we regularly refreshing creative or reusing old assets?
Do we have enough creative variety to prevent fatigue?
payload.narration_text:
This summary shows your Meta creative asset library at a glance.

The formula counts distinct image and video IDs in your account. We track both total assets and those actively running in ads.

Reading these cards: The images count shows your static creative inventory. Videos count shows motion creative assets. A healthy library has thousands of assets with regular additions.

Creative variety matters for Meta's algorithm. Ad fatigue sets in when the same users see the same creatives repeatedly. Fresh assets maintain engagement and performance.

Key questions answered: How large is the creative library? Are you image-heavy or video-focused? When were new assets last added? Click through to the full Creative Library for detailed asset management.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares conversions where a channel was the final touchpoint (last-click) versus conversions where it assisted earlier in the journey. High assist ratios indicate channels that initiate journeys rather than close them.
how_to_read: Green bars show last-click conversions (channel closed the deal)
Light green bars show assisted conversions (channel was in the journey)
Channels with more assists than last-clicks are "introducers"
Channels with more last-clicks are "closers"
Paid Social often has high assist ratios
Brand Search typically has high last-click ratios
decisions_answered: Which channels start customer journeys vs close them?
Is Paid Social being undervalued by last-click attribution?
Should we give more credit to upper-funnel channels?
How do channels work together in the conversion path?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares two types of conversions for each channel.

Green bars are last-click conversions - where the channel got the final credit. Light green are assisted conversions - where the channel touched the customer earlier in their journey but someone else closed.

Channels with more assists than last-clicks are introducers - they start journeys. Paid Social often falls here. Channels with more last-clicks are closers - they finish journeys. Brand Search typically dominates here.

This matters for budgeting. Last-click attribution undervalues introducers. If a channel shows high assists, it may deserve more investment than last-click ROAS suggests.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares conversions where a channel was the final touchpoint (last-click) versus conversions where it assisted earlier in the journey. High assist ratios indicate channels that initiate journeys rather than close them.
how_to_read: Green bars show last-click conversions (channel closed the deal)
Light green bars show assisted conversions (channel was in the journey)
Channels with more assists than last-clicks are "introducers"
Channels with more last-clicks are "closers"
Paid Social often has high assist ratios
Brand Search typically has high last-click ratios
decisions_answered: Which channels start customer journeys vs close them?
Is Paid Social being undervalued by last-click attribution?
Should we give more credit to upper-funnel channels?
How do channels work together in the conversion path?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares two types of conversions for each channel.

Green bars are last-click conversions - where the channel got the final credit. Light green are assisted conversions - where the channel touched the customer earlier in their journey but someone else closed.

Channels with more assists than last-clicks are introducers - they start journeys. Paid Social often falls here. Channels with more last-clicks are closers - they finish journeys. Brand Search typically dominates here.

This matters for budgeting. Last-click attribution undervalues introducers. If a channel shows high assists, it may deserve more investment than last-click ROAS suggests.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how the attribution gap percentage changes over time for Google and Meta. Increasing gaps may indicate tracking degradation or changing user behavior.
how_to_read: Blue line shows Google Ads gap percentage over time
Purple line shows Meta Ads gap percentage over time
Higher values indicate larger discrepancy from platforms
Spikes may indicate tracking issues or unusual campaign activity
Trend direction shows whether attribution is improving or degrading
Use crosshair to see exact values for any date
decisions_answered: Is the attribution gap getting better or worse over time?
When did significant changes in attribution occur?
Which platform has more volatile attribution?
Should we investigate recent tracking changes?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks how attribution gaps change over 90 days.

The blue line is Google Ads gap percentage. The purple line is Meta Ads. Higher percentages mean platforms are claiming more than GA4 confirms.

Watch for trends. A rising line means the gap is worsening - maybe tracking degraded or user behavior changed. A falling line means improvement.

Spikes deserve investigation. They could indicate tracking issues, iOS changes, or unusual campaign activity.

This helps you monitor: Is attribution accuracy improving? When did problems start? And which platform needs closer attention?

payload.formula_explanation: Total conversions attributed to audience-targeted campaigns across Google and Meta for the period. Total attributed revenue (SUM of CONVERSION_VALUE) is carried alongside as supporting context.
how_to_read: The value is the sum of conversions credited to targeted audiences in the date range.
Compare to the prior period to see whether audience targeting is driving more outcomes.
Read with Audience Spend to understand cost per audience conversion.
Supporting revenue context shows the conversion value those conversions generated.
decisions_answered: How many conversions are my audience-targeted campaigns producing?
Are audience conversions growing vs the prior period?
Which targeting effort is converting most efficiently relative to spend?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how average customer lifetime value varies by acquisition month. Total CLV combines historic realized value with predicted future value. Comparing these metrics across cohorts reveals acquisition quality trends and how CLV matures over customer tenure.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort acquisition month over the past 3 years
Y-axis shows average CLV in dollars
Blue solid line shows Total CLV (historic + predicted)
Green solid line shows Historic CLV (realized purchases only)
Purple dashed line shows Predicted CLV (expected future value)
Older cohorts naturally show higher historic CLV as they have had more time to purchase
The gap between historic and predicted narrows as cohorts mature
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring higher or lower quality customers over time?
How does CLV mature as cohorts age?
Which acquisition months/seasons produce our highest-value customers?
How much future value is predicted for recent cohorts?
Is there seasonality in customer acquisition quality?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value across monthly acquisition cohorts.

The blue line shows total CLV - the sum of what customers have already spent plus what they're predicted to spend. The green line shows just the historic, realized CLV. The gap between them represents predicted future value.

Older cohorts on the left will naturally show higher CLV - they've had more time to make purchases. But watch for trends: Are recent cohorts tracking above or below where older cohorts were at the same age? This reveals whether acquisition quality is improving or declining.

The spring months often show CLV peaks for gardening businesses - customers acquired during planting season tend to be more engaged and valuable. If you see dips, investigate what marketing channels or campaigns were active during those periods.

The predicted CLV line is particularly valuable for recent cohorts - it forecasts their expected value trajectory based on similar customer behavior patterns.

payload.formula_text: SUM(CLICKS) / SUM(IMPRESSIONS) x 100 for all qualifying ads at day N since launch (impression-weighted)
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks CTR degradation over time to detect creative fatigue. Ads typically experience declining engagement as audiences become saturated. This curve helps identify the optimal refresh timing before performance deteriorates significantly.
how_to_read: Lines show average CTR for ads at each day since their launch
Blue line represents Google Ads, purple line shows Meta Ads
Downward slopes indicate fatigue setting in over time
Steeper drops suggest faster audience saturation
First 30 days are shown as this is the critical fatigue window
decisions_answered: How quickly do our creatives experience fatigue?
What is the optimal creative refresh cadence for our campaigns?
Do Google and Meta ads fatigue at different rates?
At what point should we rotate in fresh creative assets?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks creative fatigue by showing CTR over time since ad launch.

The formula calculates average CTR for all ads at each day since launch using row numbering to track ad age. A 20% CTR decline signals the fatigue threshold.

Blue shows Google Ads performance, purple shows Meta. Downward slopes indicate fatigue. Steeper drops mean faster saturation.

This answers critical questions: How quickly do creatives fatigue? What's the optimal refresh timing? And do platforms fatigue differently?

payload.formula_text: AVG(daily REVENUE) across the quarter's months, both years
payload.formula_explanation: Reveals monthly seasonality per channel for the months in the selected quarter, comparing the selected year vs prior year. The seasonality index is each month's average daily revenue divided by the channel's overall average daily revenue, times 100 (100 = average). Computed in SQL against MART_CHANNEL_REVENUE_DAILY; the chart value is average daily revenue with the index in metadata.
how_to_read: Rows represent marketing channels (CHANNEL_GROUPING)
Columns represent the months within the selected quarter
Seasonality index of 100 = average; above 100 = peak, below 100 = slow
Cell value/intensity reflects average daily revenue; index is in metadata
Channels below $100 overall avg daily revenue are excluded
decisions_answered: When are peak periods for each marketing channel?
Which months should we increase budget for specific channels?
Are there low-performing periods where budget could be reallocated?
How do seasonality patterns differ between channels?

payload.formula_text: SUM(IMPRESSIONS) / SUM(REACH) per Meta ad set
payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot showing the relationship between average frequency (how often users see ads) and CTR (engagement rate) for Meta ad sets. Bubble size indicates spend level, color indicates efficiency zone.
how_to_read: X-axis shows average frequency (times each user sees your ad)
Y-axis shows CTR - higher means better engagement at that frequency
Green bubbles (1-2x frequency) are in the optimal efficiency zone
Yellow bubbles (2-3.5x frequency) are approaching saturation
Red bubbles (3.5x+ frequency) show audience saturation - consider refresh
Larger bubbles indicate higher spend - prioritize optimizing these
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are showing audience saturation (too high frequency)?
What is the optimal frequency before CTR starts declining?
Which high-spend ad sets need creative refresh?
Should I expand reach or accept higher frequency?
payload.narration_text:
This scatter plot reveals the relationship between frequency and engagement for your Meta ad sets.

Frequency is how many times each user sees your ads. CTR measures how often they click. The goal is finding the sweet spot - enough frequency for message retention, but not so much that users tune out.

Green bubbles show ad sets in the optimal 1-2x frequency zone. Yellow indicates warning at 2 to 3.5x. Red shows saturation above 3.5x where performance typically drops.

Bubble size represents spend - bigger bubbles are your biggest investments, so prioritize those.

When you see red bubbles, consider refreshing creative, expanding the audience, or reducing bid caps to lower frequency.

payload.formula_explanation: Four summary cards roll up the campaign matrix by quadrant, showing how many campaigns, how much spend, and the average ROAS sit in each. Quadrant boundaries use the median spend and median ROAS of campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
how_to_read: Each card represents one quadrant: Scale, Scale Opportunity, Optimize/Cut, Test/Kill.
Campaign count shows how concentrated your portfolio is in each performance zone.
Total spend exposes how much budget is committed to each quadrant - watch Optimize/Cut.
Average ROAS summarizes the efficiency of campaigns within that quadrant.
decisions_answered: How is spend distributed across performance quadrants?
How many campaigns are winners versus underperformers?
Which quadrant holds the most budget and at what efficiency?

payload.formula_explanation: For each campaign this stacked bar shows the share of impressions that landed below top position (below organic results), computed as 100 minus the impression-weighted top-position rate. Hover metadata also carries the top-position and absolute-top (#1) rates. Campaigns are ranked by the largest below-top share to surface the weakest placements first.
how_to_read: Taller bars mean a larger share of impressions fell below top position — worse competitive placement.
The metadata splits each campaign into top-position and absolute-top (#1) rates.
Only campaigns with more than 100 impressions are shown; the top 15 by below-top share are ranked first.
Focus on the tallest bars first — those campaigns are losing the most visibility to placement.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are losing the most impressions to weak ad position?
How does placement strength vary across my campaigns?
Where should I improve bids or quality to climb above organic results?

payload.formula_explanation: Percentage of impressions that did NOT land in top position (i.e. below organic results), computed as 100 minus the impression-weighted top-position rate from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. Lower is better — it indicates stronger ad placement. Note: the backend metric id is below_top; the absence of true impression-share data means this is a top-position proxy, not a budget-lost-impression-share figure.
how_to_read: The value is the share of impressions that fell below top position.
Lower is better — a high value means most impressions are losing prominent placement.
Track the change versus the comparison period; a rising value means placement is eroding.
Pair it with top-position rate; together they account for all impressions.
decisions_answered: What share of our impressions are stuck below top position?
Is our weak-placement share growing or shrinking?
How much visibility are we losing to poor ad placement?

payload.formula_explanation: Total planned marketing budget for the planning year. The backend sums annual ad spend from the planning summary mart; when no spend has been recorded yet it falls back to the configured $2.24M default. The change is measured against the prior year's spend.
how_to_read: The headline value is the total ad budget allocated for the planning year.
The percentage compares this budget to last year's total spend.
An up trend means you are investing more than the prior year; down means a leaner plan.
The sparkline shows the recent 7-day spend trajectory from the daily pulse mart.
decisions_answered: How much am I planning to invest in marketing this year?
Is my budget growing or shrinking versus last year?
Do I have a configured plan, or am I defaulting to the baseline budget?

payload.formula_explanation: The dollar amount of spend sitting in the Optimize/Cut quadrant (high spend, low ROAS). This is budget being spent inefficiently that could be reallocated.
how_to_read: The value is total spend on campaigns classified as Optimize/Cut (above-median spend, below-median ROAS).
The description shows how many campaigns are at risk and what share of total spend they represent.
Higher values mean more budget is being deployed inefficiently.
Treat this as the reallocation opportunity: money that could move to Scale or Hidden Gem campaigns.
decisions_answered: How much budget is being spent inefficiently right now?
What share of total spend is at risk in the Optimize/Cut quadrant?
How much could be freed up for reallocation to winners?

payload.formula_explanation: Blended Customer Acquisition Cost divides all paid marketing spend across every channel by the total number of new customers acquired in the period. It shows the true all-in average cost to win one customer, regardless of which channel they came from.
how_to_read: The main value is the average dollars spent to acquire one new customer
It blends spend across all paid channels, not a single platform
Lower is better - it means acquisition is getting more efficient
Compare against first-purchase AOV and LTV to judge if acquisition is profitable
Rising CAC with flat new-customer counts signals diminishing ad returns
decisions_answered: How much are we really paying to acquire each new customer?
Is our acquisition cost rising or falling over time?
Is our spend producing enough new customers to justify the budget?
Can we afford to scale spend without breaking acquisition economics?

payload.formula_explanation: Four summary cards roll up the campaign matrix by quadrant, showing how many campaigns, how much spend, and the average ROAS sit in each. Quadrant boundaries use the median spend and median ROAS of campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
how_to_read: Each card represents one quadrant: Scale, Scale Opportunity, Optimize/Cut, Test/Kill.
Campaign count shows how concentrated your portfolio is in each performance zone.
Total spend exposes how much budget is committed to each quadrant - watch Optimize/Cut.
Average ROAS summarizes the efficiency of campaigns within that quadrant.
decisions_answered: How is spend distributed across performance quadrants?
How many campaigns are winners versus underperformers?
Which quadrant holds the most budget and at what efficiency?

payload.display_name: Google Ads Performance by Campaign Type
payload.formula_explanation: Stacked bar chart breaking down Google Ads spend by campaign type (Search, Shopping, Performance Max, etc.) per year, with revenue, conversions, and ROAS attached as metadata. Sourced from the Google campaign performance mart.
how_to_read: Each bar is a year; segments are Google campaign types stacked by spend.
Larger segments indicate where Google budget is concentrated.
Hover metadata exposes ROAS so you can see which campaign types are efficient, not just large.
Compare across years to see how the campaign-type mix is evolving.
decisions_answered: Where is my Google Ads budget going by campaign type?
Which Google campaign types deliver the best ROAS?
Has my Google campaign-type mix shifted over the last few years?

payload.formula_text: Spend, Revenue, ROAS, Conversions BY campaign
payload.formula_explanation: Counts the number of distinct Shopping campaigns that recorded any spend in the selected date range. The comparison value is the total number of Shopping campaigns in the period, so the card shows how many of all campaigns were actually active.
how_to_read: The headline value is the count of campaigns with spend greater than zero
The comparison shows the total number of Shopping campaigns in the period
A low active-to-total ratio means many campaigns are dormant
Only SHOPPING campaign types are counted
Use alongside spend and ROAS to gauge breadth of activity
decisions_answered: How many Shopping campaigns are actively spending?
What share of total campaigns are dormant?
Is campaign activity expanding or contracting?
Should inactive campaigns be reviewed or retired?

payload.formula_explanation: Each bar shows the percentage of users who successfully moved from one stage to the next. Higher percentages indicate smoother transitions, while lower percentages reveal friction points in the customer journey.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher conversion rates between stages
Compare bar lengths to identify weakest conversion points
Industry benchmarks help contextualize your performance
Session to View rates above 100% are common as users view multiple products
Focus optimization on stages with the lowest conversion rates
decisions_answered: Which stage transition has the lowest conversion rate?
Where should we prioritize conversion rate optimization efforts?
How do our stage conversion rates compare to industry standards?
Are there specific transitions that need immediate attention?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down conversion rates between consecutive funnel stages.

Each bar shows the percentage of users moving from one stage to the next. Session to View is views divided by sessions. View to Cart is adds divided by views. And so on through checkout to purchase.

To read it: longer bars mean better conversion. Shorter bars reveal friction points. Note that Session to View can exceed 100% since users often view multiple products.

Use this chart to identify: Which transition is weakest? Where should you optimize? And how do you compare to industry benchmarks? Focus your CRO efforts on the shortest bars.

payload.formula_explanation: Each bar shows the percentage of users who successfully moved from one stage to the next. Higher percentages indicate smoother transitions, while lower percentages reveal friction points in the customer journey.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher conversion rates between stages
Compare bar lengths to identify weakest conversion points
Industry benchmarks help contextualize your performance
Session to View rates above 100% are common as users view multiple products
Focus optimization on stages with the lowest conversion rates
decisions_answered: Which stage transition has the lowest conversion rate?
Where should we prioritize conversion rate optimization efforts?
How do our stage conversion rates compare to industry standards?
Are there specific transitions that need immediate attention?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down conversion rates between consecutive funnel stages.

Each bar shows the percentage of users moving from one stage to the next. Session to View is views divided by sessions. View to Cart is adds divided by views. And so on through checkout to purchase.

To read it: longer bars mean better conversion. Shorter bars reveal friction points. Note that Session to View can exceed 100% since users often view multiple products.

Use this chart to identify: Which transition is weakest? Where should you optimize? And how do you compare to industry benchmarks? Focus your CRO efforts on the shortest bars.

payload.formula_text: Selected Metric (ROAS, CPA, CTR, or Conversions)
payload.formula_explanation: Heatmap showing audience performance across different ad networks (YouTube, Search, Display, etc.) and devices (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet). Darker colors indicate better performance.
how_to_read: Rows represent different ad networks (YouTube, Search, Display, etc.)
Columns represent device types (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet)
Darker green cells indicate higher ROAS - better performance
Lighter or yellow cells indicate lower performance
Cell size corresponds to relative spend level
Click any cell to filter page by that network/device combination
decisions_answered: Which network/device combination has the best ROAS?
Should I allocate more budget to mobile or desktop?
Is YouTube performing better on certain devices?
Where should I reduce or increase targeting?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap shows how audience targeting performs across different ad networks and devices.

Networks are on the Y-axis: YouTube, Search, Display, Discovery, and others. Devices are on the X-axis: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV.

Darker green cells indicate higher ROAS - these are your best performing combinations. Lighter colors or reds indicate opportunities for optimization.

For example, if YouTube on Mobile shows 4x ROAS but only gets 10% of budget, consider shifting more spend there. If Search on Desktop shows 1.5x ROAS, consider reducing allocation.

Use this to guide both network selection and device bid adjustments in your campaigns.

payload.formula_explanation: Bar chart ranking channels by a Sharpe-style efficiency ratio: average daily ROAS divided by the standard deviation of daily ROAS. A higher value means a channel delivers strong returns consistently, which is the basis for the AI budget recommendation.
how_to_read: Each bar is a channel; taller bars mean better risk-adjusted efficiency.
It rewards channels that are both high-ROAS and low-volatility.
A high-ROAS but erratic channel can score lower than a steadier one.
Metadata exposes the underlying average ROAS and volatility separately.
decisions_answered: Which channel offers the best return per unit of volatility?
Should I weight budget toward consistent performers over erratic high-ROAS ones?
What drives the AI-recommended allocation between Google and Meta?

payload.formula_text: Channel Monthly Spend / Total Monthly Spend × 100
payload.formula_explanation: Shows how budget allocation between channels has shifted over 12 months. In absolute view, see actual dollar amounts. In percentage view, see relative share changes regardless of total spend fluctuations.
how_to_read: Stacked areas show relative spend proportion over 12 months
Toggle between Absolute (dollars) and Percentage (share) views
Growing area indicates increasing investment in that channel
Shrinking area indicates decreasing allocation
Watch for seasonal patterns or strategic shifts
decisions_answered: How has our channel investment strategy shifted over time?
Are we increasing or decreasing any specific channel?
What was our historical budget allocation strategy?
Did recent changes align with performance improvements?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows how your budget allocation has evolved over 12 months.

You can toggle between two views: Absolute shows actual dollar amounts spent. Percentage shows relative shares, which is useful when total budgets fluctuate.

A growing area means you're investing more in that channel. Shrinking means you're pulling back. Look for patterns - are shifts deliberate strategy changes or drift?

Use this to answer: How has investment strategy evolved? Are we scaling the right channels? And did past allocation changes correlate with better results?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the impression-weighted average top-position rate for each Google Ads campaign type (Search, Shopping, Performance Max, Display) from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. Channels are ordered by total impressions to highlight where most of your volume sits and how strong placement is there.
how_to_read: Each bar is a channel type; the value is the share of impressions in top position for that channel.
Taller bars mean that channel wins above-organic placement more often.
Compare channels to spot which formats hold position well versus which lag.
Channels are ordered by total impressions, so the leftmost typically carry the most volume.
decisions_answered: Which Google Ads channel types win top placement most often?
Where is placement weakest across my channel mix?
Should I shift effort toward channels with stronger position?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows top Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by sends, with engagement metrics. CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures how compelling the email content is for those who actually opened it.
how_to_read: Campaigns are sorted by total sends (highest first)
Open % shows what fraction of recipients opened the email
Click % shows clicks as a percentage of total sends
CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures clicks relative to opens — a better indicator of content quality
Use search to find specific campaigns by subject line
decisions_answered: Which email campaigns reached the most recipients?
Which subject lines drove the highest open rates?
Which email content generated the most clicks?
How does engagement vary across different campaign types?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your top Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by sends, with engagement metrics.

Open percentage is opens divided by sends. Click percentage is clicks divided by sends. CTOR, or click-to-open rate, measures clicks relative to opens — a better indicator of content quality since it only counts engaged recipients.

Use this to identify which subject lines drive opens, which content drives clicks, and how engagement varies across campaigns.

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows email campaign performance from Klaviyo, separated from the paid scatter plot since email campaigns have no ad spend. Metrics are sourced from Klaviyo event data (sends, opens, clicks) aggregated at the campaign level.
how_to_read: Sends = total emails delivered for the campaign
Opens = total email opens (including multiple opens per recipient)
Clicks = total link clicks within the email
Open % = percentage of sends that were opened
Click % = percentage of sends that received a click
CTOR = click-to-open rate, clicks as a percentage of opens (engagement quality)
decisions_answered: Which email campaigns drive the most engagement?
What open and click rates are our campaigns achieving?
Which campaigns have high open rates but low click-through (content issue)?
Which campaigns have the best click-to-open ratio (best content)?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your Klaviyo email campaign performance, separated from the paid campaign scatter plot since email has no ad spend.

Each row is one email campaign with its engagement metrics. Sends is total emails delivered. Opens and clicks track engagement.

Open Rate tells you subject line effectiveness. Click Rate shows overall campaign performance. CTOR - click to open rate - is the purest content quality metric, showing what percentage of people who opened actually clicked.

A good benchmark: 40 to 50 percent open rates, 2 to 3 percent click rates, and 5 percent or higher CTOR.

payload.formula_explanation: Klaviyo's predictive analytics uses machine learning to forecast customer lifetime value and churn risk. Historic CLV shows past purchase value, Predicted CLV estimates future value, and Churn Probability indicates likelihood of customer disengagement.
how_to_read: Avg Total CLV ($152) shows the complete customer value estimate
Avg Predicted CLV ($14) indicates expected future revenue per customer
Avg Order Value ($38) benchmarks transaction size
High Churn Probability (81%) signals an at-risk subscriber base
Use these metrics for segmentation and retention targeting
decisions_answered: What is the lifetime value of our average customer?
How much future revenue can we expect per subscriber?
Is our subscriber base at risk of churning?
Where should we focus retention and win-back efforts?
payload.narration_text:
The predictive analytics panel shows Klaviyo's machine learning forecasts for your subscriber base.

The formulas: Total CLV equals historic CLV plus predicted future CLV. Churn probability is an ML model output from 0 to 100%.

Reading these metrics: Your average total CLV is $152, meaning each customer is worth about that much over their lifetime. The $14 predicted CLV shows expected additional purchases. The 81% average churn probability is concerning - this means your typical subscriber is at high risk of disengaging.

Key decisions: What's a customer worth? Where should retention focus? With high churn probability, prioritize re-engagement campaigns and consider sunset flows for your least engaged segments before they damage deliverability.

payload.formula_text: Customers acquired in that cohort month
payload.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows the percentage of customers from each cohort who made a purchase in subsequent months. Cohorts are grouped by acquisition month, and retention is calculated as the percentage of the original cohort still actively purchasing at each time interval.
how_to_read: Y-axis shows cohort months (most recent at top), with cohort size in parentheses
X-axis shows months since signup (M0 = first month, M24 = two years later)
Cell color intensity indicates retention rate - darker green = higher retention
Month 0 (M0) shows initial purchase rate, typically highest
Look for diagonal patterns to understand typical retention decay curves
The M12 column often shows a bump due to annual seasonal purchase cycles
decisions_answered: How does customer retention change over time for each cohort?
Which cohorts have the strongest/weakest retention curves?
At what month do we see the biggest retention drop-off?
Is there an annual purchase cycle (M12 bump) in our customer behavior?
Are recent cohorts retaining better or worse than historical cohorts?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap visualizes how customer cohorts retain over time after their initial acquisition.

Each row represents a monthly cohort - a group of customers who first appeared in that month. The cohort size shows how many customers were acquired. As you move right across the row, you see what percentage of those original customers made a purchase in subsequent months.

Month 0 shows the initial purchase rate. The natural decay as you move right is expected - not all customers continue purchasing. However, pay attention to patterns: Do you see a bump at Month 12? This often indicates seasonal or annual purchase behavior - customers who bought seeds last spring return again this spring.

Compare cohorts vertically to identify quality variations. If Q1 cohorts consistently show better retention than Q3 cohorts, this suggests seasonal acquisition quality differences. Spring gardening season customers may be more engaged.

Use this to identify optimal intervention timing - where does retention drop most sharply? That's when lifecycle emails and retention campaigns should trigger.

payload.formula_text: MTD Spend + ((Avg Daily ± Std Dev) × Days Left)
payload.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month spend from MART_BUDGET_PACING using multiple methods. The simple projection uses the period's overall daily average; the weighted projection accounts for distinct weekday-versus-weekend spending; the confidence range is built from the spend standard deviation to bound expected variance.
how_to_read: The headline value is the forecast total spend for the current month at the present run rate.
The weighted projection is usually more accurate when weekday and weekend spend differ noticeably.
The confidence range (low to high) shows how much the final number could swing given recent volatility.
Compare the projection against the monthly budget to see whether you are tracking to over- or under-spend.
decisions_answered: How much will we spend by month end if nothing changes?
Are we projected to overspend or underspend the monthly budget?
How wide is the uncertainty around the month-end spend forecast?

payload.formula_explanation: The performance table consolidates all campaign metrics for detailed analysis. Each row represents one campaign with its key performance indicators. Green highlighting indicates strong performance, red indicates areas needing attention.
how_to_read: Click column headers to sort by any metric
Use search to find specific campaigns by name
Quadrant badges show Scale (green), Opportunity (gold), Optimize (red), or Test (gray)
ROAS values are color-coded: green >=3x, neutral 1-3x, red <1x
Toggle column visibility to focus on specific metrics
Export data to CSV or Excel for further analysis
decisions_answered: Which specific campaigns are our top performers?
What are the exact metrics for any given campaign?
How do campaigns compare when sorted by specific KPIs?
Which campaigns have high CTR but low conversion rate?
What is the full breakdown of spend, revenue, and efficiency?
payload.narration_text:
The Campaign Performance Table gives you the complete picture for every campaign.

Each row shows one campaign with its channel, type, spend, revenue, ROAS, CPC, CTR, and conversion rate. Quadrant badges indicate whether it's in Scale, Opportunity, Optimize, or Test zone.

Click any column header to sort. Search filters by campaign name. Toggle columns to focus on what matters. Export to CSV or Excel for deeper analysis.

Color coding helps interpretation: green ROAS values are 3x or better, red means under 1x.

This answers: What exactly are the numbers for each campaign? How do they compare when ranked by any metric? And which campaigns have the best or worst specific KPIs?

payload.formula_explanation: The funnel visualizes the customer journey from initial session through to completed purchase. Each stage shows the absolute user count and the percentage who progressed from the previous stage. The overall conversion rate represents the end-to-end efficiency of turning visitors into buyers.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a stage in the e-commerce funnel: Sessions, View Item, Add to Cart, Begin Checkout, Purchase
Bar width indicates the relative volume compared to the initial session count
Percentages between stages show the step-to-step conversion rate (drop-off)
Identify the largest drop-offs to prioritize optimization efforts
Green highlighting on the final Purchase stage indicates successful conversions
decisions_answered: What is our overall site conversion rate?
Where in the funnel are we losing the most potential customers?
Which funnel stage requires the most urgent optimization?
How does our funnel performance compare to e-commerce benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel chart shows the complete customer journey from initial site visit to purchase completion.

The formula calculates each stage's conversion rate by dividing users at that stage by the previous stage, times 100. Overall conversion is purchases divided by sessions.

To read this: each bar is a funnel stage. Larger bars mean more users at that point. The percentages between bars reveal where customers drop off. The biggest drops indicate your highest-priority optimization targets.

This answers: What's our conversion rate? Where do we lose customers? Which stage needs attention? And how do we compare to industry standards?

payload.formula_explanation: The total number of conversions reported by the ad platforms (Google + Meta) for the selected period. These are platform self-reported and feed the Average CPA calculation.
how_to_read: This counts platform-reported conversions, summed across Google and Meta.
Platform-reported conversions can include view-through and modeled conversions, so they may exceed GA4 last-click counts.
The sparkline shows daily conversion volume to reveal pacing within the period.
Pair with Total Marketing Spend to derive cost per acquisition.
decisions_answered: How many conversions did paid media drive this period?
Is conversion volume trending up or down versus the prior period?
Which days produced the most conversions?

payload.formula_explanation: Rolls up the account's audience assets (Standard, Custom, and Combined audiences) from the raw Google Ads audience tables, showing the total and enabled count for each type. These assets power audience targeting and bid adjustments.
how_to_read: Total count shows all audiences of that type in the account
Enabled count shows how many are actively usable for targeting
Standard audiences derive from the SCOPE field; Custom and Combined are separate types
Custom Audiences support intent and interest-based targeting
This is snapshot data - date and campaign filters do not apply
decisions_answered: How many targeting audiences do we have available?
Are our audiences properly set up and enabled?
Do we have enough custom audiences for prospecting?
Is our audience infrastructure sufficient for targeting needs?

payload.formula_text: COUNT(DISTINCT AD_ID) per AD_STRENGTH [EXCELLENT | GOOD | AVERAGE | POOR | UNSPECIFIED]
payload.formula_explanation: Visualizes Google Ads quality distribution by ad strength rating. Ad strength is Google's quality indicator based on headlines, descriptions, and asset variety. This reveals how many ads fall into each quality tier and their associated spend.
how_to_read: The donut chart segments represent spend allocation by ad strength category
Green segments (Excellent/Good) indicate high-quality ads following best practices
Orange (Average) and red (Poor) segments highlight improvement opportunities
The center displays total spend across all ad strength categories
CTR metrics below each category show engagement correlation with quality
decisions_answered: What percentage of our Google ads have strong quality ratings?
How much budget is going to low-quality ads that could be improved?
Is there a correlation between ad strength and click-through rate?
Which ads should be prioritized for headline and description optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart displays your Google Ads quality distribution by ad strength rating.

Ad strength is Google's quality indicator based on asset variety, headline quality, and description relevance. The formula counts distinct ads per strength category and sums their associated spend.

Green segments show Excellent and Good rated ads. Orange represents Average. Red indicates Poor ratings. The center shows your total spend.

This helps answer: What percentage of ads have strong ratings? How much budget goes to low-quality ads? And which ads need optimization attention?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares performance metrics across audience categories (Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, Prospecting) and platforms (Google Ads, Meta Ads). Each bar represents the aggregated performance of all audiences within that category.
how_to_read: Grouped bars show impressions by audience category and platform
Taller bars indicate higher impression volume for that category
Compare Google (blue) vs Meta (purple) performance within each category
Retargeting typically shows highest efficiency, Prospecting typically shows lowest
Use the legend to understand which platform each color represents
decisions_answered: Which audience type delivers the best performance across platforms?
How does Google Ads audience targeting compare to Meta Ads?
Should we invest more in retargeting vs prospecting?
Which platform should receive more budget for each audience type?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares audience performance across four key categories: Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, and Prospecting.

The grouped bars show impressions by category, with separate colors for Google Ads and Meta Ads.

Retargeting audiences typically perform best because they target users who already know your brand. Lookalike audiences extend your reach to similar users. CRM/Email audiences are your existing customers, and Prospecting reaches entirely new users.

Use this to decide: Which audience type should get more budget? Which platform performs better for each type? And is your current allocation aligned with performance?

payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot showing the relationship between average frequency (how often users see ads) and CTR (engagement rate) for Meta ad sets. Bubble size indicates spend level, color indicates efficiency zone.
how_to_read: X-axis shows average frequency (times each user sees your ad)
Y-axis shows CTR - higher means better engagement at that frequency
Green bubbles (1-2x frequency) are in the optimal efficiency zone
Yellow bubbles (2-3.5x frequency) are approaching saturation
Red bubbles (3.5x+ frequency) show audience saturation - consider refresh
Larger bubbles indicate higher spend - prioritize optimizing these
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are showing audience saturation (too high frequency)?
What is the optimal frequency before CTR starts declining?
Which high-spend ad sets need creative refresh?
Should I expand reach or accept higher frequency?
payload.narration_text:
This scatter plot reveals the relationship between frequency and engagement for your Meta ad sets.

Frequency is how many times each user sees your ads. CTR measures how often they click. The goal is finding the sweet spot - enough frequency for message retention, but not so much that users tune out.

Green bubbles show ad sets in the optimal 1-2x frequency zone. Yellow indicates warning at 2 to 3.5x. Red shows saturation above 3.5x where performance typically drops.

Bubble size represents spend - bigger bubbles are your biggest investments, so prioritize those.

When you see red bubbles, consider refreshing creative, expanding the audience, or reducing bid caps to lower frequency.

payload.formula_text: Cost at Frequency Band / Clicks at Frequency Band
payload.formula_explanation: Line chart showing how CTR and CPC change as frequency increases. Demonstrates diminishing returns - at higher frequencies, CTR drops and CPC rises, indicating audience fatigue.
how_to_read: X-axis shows frequency bands from 1.0 to 6.0+
Blue line shows CTR - typically highest at lower frequencies
Red dashed line shows CPC - typically rises with frequency
The optimal zone (usually 1.5-2.5x) balances reach and efficiency
Sharp CTR drops indicate the saturation point for your audiences
Use this to set frequency caps in your campaigns
decisions_answered: At what frequency do diminishing returns begin?
What frequency cap should I set for my campaigns?
How much does efficiency drop at high frequencies?
Is it worth the higher CPC for additional frequency?
payload.narration_text:
Saturation curves reveal how performance changes as users see your ads more frequently.

The blue CTR line typically starts high and drops as frequency increases - users get tired of seeing the same ads. The red CPC line usually rises because you're paying more for less engaged users.

The optimal zone is usually 1.5 to 2.5x frequency. Above this, you see diminishing returns.

This chart answers a critical question: at what point should I cap frequency? Find where CTR drops sharply - that's your saturation threshold.

Set your frequency caps just below this point to maintain efficiency while still reaching users enough times to drive action.

payload.formula_text: COUNT(Alerts WHERE severity = 'critical' AND triggered_at >= NOW() - 24h)
payload.formula_explanation: Counts active critical alerts triggered in the last 24 hours. Critical alerts indicate metrics have breached severe thresholds requiring immediate attention, such as ROAS dropping below the P10 historical baseline or revenue falling significantly below expectations.
how_to_read: The number shows critical alerts currently active (last 24 hours)
Zero (0) is ideal - no critical threshold breaches
Any number above zero requires immediate investigation
Pulse animation appears when count > 0 to draw attention
Red coloring indicates severity requiring urgent action
Click to filter and view only critical alerts
decisions_answered: Are there any metrics in critical condition right now?
Which campaigns or channels need immediate attention?
Has performance degraded significantly overnight?
Do I need to take urgent action on any marketing activities?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows critical severity alerts currently active in the last 24 hours.

Critical alerts mean a metric has breached a severe threshold. For example, ROAS below the 10th percentile of historical performance, or daily revenue dropping more than 30% below expected.

Zero is your goal. Any positive number means something needs immediate attention. The pulse animation helps you notice when there are active critical alerts.

Click the card to see exactly which metrics are in critical status and investigate the root cause.

This answers: Are any metrics critical now? Which need immediate attention? Has performance degraded significantly?

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows email campaign performance from Klaviyo, separated from the paid scatter plot since email campaigns have no ad spend. Metrics are sourced from Klaviyo event data (sends, opens, clicks) aggregated at the campaign level.
how_to_read: Sends = total emails delivered for the campaign
Opens = total email opens (including multiple opens per recipient)
Clicks = total link clicks within the email
Open % = percentage of sends that were opened
Click % = percentage of sends that received a click
CTOR = click-to-open rate, clicks as a percentage of opens (engagement quality)
decisions_answered: Which email campaigns drive the most engagement?
What open and click rates are our campaigns achieving?
Which campaigns have high open rates but low click-through (content issue)?
Which campaigns have the best click-to-open ratio (best content)?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your Klaviyo email campaign performance, separated from the paid campaign scatter plot since email has no ad spend.

Each row is one email campaign with its engagement metrics. Sends is total emails delivered. Opens and clicks track engagement.

Open Rate tells you subject line effectiveness. Click Rate shows overall campaign performance. CTOR - click to open rate - is the purest content quality metric, showing what percentage of people who opened actually clicked.

A good benchmark: 40 to 50 percent open rates, 2 to 3 percent click rates, and 5 percent or higher CTOR.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows performance for Google Ads custom audiences including AUTO (Google-generated), PURCHASE_INTENT, and SEARCH custom audiences. These are keyword-based and interest-based targeting options.
how_to_read: AUTO audiences are Google-generated based on your business signals
PURCHASE_INTENT audiences target users actively researching purchases
SEARCH audiences are based on custom search terms you define
Days active shows how long each audience has been running
Compare performance across audience types to optimize targeting strategy
decisions_answered: Which custom audience type performs best for my business?
Are Google auto-generated audiences effective?
Which search custom audiences should I expand?
Should I create more purchase intent audiences?
payload.narration_text:
Custom audiences are keyword and interest-based targeting options in Google Ads.

There are three main types: AUTO audiences that Google generates automatically, PURCHASE_INTENT audiences targeting users researching purchases, and SEARCH audiences based on custom search terms you define.

This table helps you compare performance across these types. Look at the ROAS column to see which audiences drive profitable returns.

Purchase intent audiences often perform well because they target users with clear buying signals. Search custom audiences let you define exactly what topics to target.

Use this data to decide which custom audience types deserve more investment and which need refinement.

payload.formula_text: Clicks at Frequency Band / Impressions at Frequency Band × 100
payload.formula_explanation: Line chart showing how CTR and CPC change as frequency increases. Demonstrates diminishing returns - at higher frequencies, CTR drops and CPC rises, indicating audience fatigue.
how_to_read: X-axis shows frequency bands from 1.0 to 6.0+
Blue line shows CTR - typically highest at lower frequencies
Red dashed line shows CPC - typically rises with frequency
The optimal zone (usually 1.5-2.5x) balances reach and efficiency
Sharp CTR drops indicate the saturation point for your audiences
Use this to set frequency caps in your campaigns
decisions_answered: At what frequency do diminishing returns begin?
What frequency cap should I set for my campaigns?
How much does efficiency drop at high frequencies?
Is it worth the higher CPC for additional frequency?
payload.narration_text:
Saturation curves reveal how performance changes as users see your ads more frequently.

The blue CTR line typically starts high and drops as frequency increases - users get tired of seeing the same ads. The red CPC line usually rises because you're paying more for less engaged users.

The optimal zone is usually 1.5 to 2.5x frequency. Above this, you see diminishing returns.

This chart answers a critical question: at what point should I cap frequency? Find where CTR drops sharply - that's your saturation threshold.

Set your frequency caps just below this point to maintain efficiency while still reaching users enough times to drive action.

payload.formula_text: SELECT * FROM Alerts WHERE status IN ('active', 'acknowledged')
payload.formula_explanation: Lists all alerts that are currently active or acknowledged but not yet resolved. Sorted by severity (critical first) then by trigger time (most recent first). This is your primary view for managing ongoing alert situations.
how_to_read: Severity column uses color badges: red for critical, amber for warning
Rule column shows which alert rule triggered this alert
Metric shows the specific metric being monitored (ROAS, CPC, etc.)
Current vs Threshold shows actual value against the trigger threshold
Status shows active (unacknowledged) or acknowledged (in progress)
Actions allow you to acknowledge, resolve, or mute each alert
Use filters to focus on specific severities or data sources
decisions_answered: Which alerts need my attention right now?
What are the actual metric values causing alerts?
Have any alerts already been acknowledged by team members?
Which alerts have been active the longest?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows all current alerts requiring attention.

Alerts are sorted by severity first - critical on top - then by when they triggered. Color coding makes priority clear at a glance.

For each alert, you see the rule that triggered it, the specific metric, and the actual value compared to threshold. This tells you how far off the metric is.

Use the action buttons to acknowledge alerts you're investigating, resolve alerts when the issue is fixed, or mute alerts during known exceptional periods.

This answers: What needs attention now? What are the actual values? Who's working on what?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows each monitored metric's current value compared to its configured warning and critical thresholds. The percent of threshold column helps visualize how close each metric is to triggering an alert, even when in normal status.
how_to_read: Each row represents one monitored metric
Current Value shows today's actual metric value
Warning and Critical show the configured threshold levels
Status indicator shows normal (green), warning (amber), or critical (red)
Percent of Threshold shows how close to the warning threshold
Progress bar visually shows position relative to warning threshold
Metrics closer to 100% are approaching warning status
decisions_answered: How is each metric performing relative to its thresholds?
Which metrics are closest to triggering alerts?
Are any metrics significantly outperforming their thresholds?
Do any thresholds need adjustment based on current performance?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows every monitored metric with its current value and threshold comparison.

The status column tells you the key fact: is this metric normal, warning, or critical? But the percent of threshold column adds crucial context.

A metric showing 95% of threshold is technically normal but very close to warning. One showing 50% has plenty of buffer. This helps you anticipate problems before they trigger alerts.

Use this view to identify metrics that might need threshold adjustment. If a metric consistently sits at 90-95%, consider whether the warning threshold is set correctly for your business.

This answers: How are metrics performing? Which are close to alerting? Do thresholds need adjustment?

payload.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month revenue from MART_BUDGET_PACING by adding the period's average daily revenue (CONVERSION_VALUE) for each remaining day to month-to-date revenue. Current and projected ROAS show whether efficiency is holding as spend scales toward month end.
how_to_read: The headline value is the forecast total revenue for the current month at the present run rate.
Current ROAS is revenue divided by spend for the selected period — your efficiency right now.
Projected ROAS applies the same trajectory to month end; compare it to Current ROAS to see if returns are improving or eroding.
A projected ROAS below target signals the extra spend may not be paying off.
decisions_answered: How much revenue will this month likely close at?
Is our return on spend (ROAS) holding as we pace toward month end?
Are we forecast to hit our revenue goal at the current run rate?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares performance metrics across audience categories (Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, Prospecting) and platforms (Google Ads, Meta Ads). Each bar represents the aggregated performance of all audiences within that category.
how_to_read: Grouped bars show impressions by audience category and platform
Taller bars indicate higher impression volume for that category
Compare Google (blue) vs Meta (purple) performance within each category
Retargeting typically shows highest efficiency, Prospecting typically shows lowest
Use the legend to understand which platform each color represents
decisions_answered: Which audience type delivers the best performance across platforms?
How does Google Ads audience targeting compare to Meta Ads?
Should we invest more in retargeting vs prospecting?
Which platform should receive more budget for each audience type?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares audience performance across four key categories: Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, and Prospecting.

The grouped bars show impressions by category, with separate colors for Google Ads and Meta Ads.

Retargeting audiences typically perform best because they target users who already know your brand. Lookalike audiences extend your reach to similar users. CRM/Email audiences are your existing customers, and Prospecting reaches entirely new users.

Use this to decide: Which audience type should get more budget? Which platform performs better for each type? And is your current allocation aligned with performance?

payload.formula_text: AVG(daily total spend) over the selected date range (date_from..date_to), NOT a fixed 14-day window
