payload.formula_explanation: Predicts when your monthly budget will run out based on your current spending velocity. Uses your 14-day average daily spend as the run rate. Compares exhaustion date against days remaining in the month to identify risk.
how_to_read: The large number shows days until budget runs out at current rate
Green means you'll have budget through month end
Yellow means exhaustion 10-20% earlier than month end
Red means you'll run out significantly before month end
Budget Remaining shows dollars left to spend
Daily Run Rate shows your current spending velocity
Exhaustion Date shows the projected day budget hits zero
decisions_answered: Will our budget last through the end of the month?
How many days of budget do we have at current rate?
Do we need to reduce daily spend to stretch the budget?
What's our spending runway if we don't make changes?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI card predicts when your monthly budget will run out based on current spending velocity.

The formula: Budget remaining divided by your 14-day average daily spend gives days until exhaustion.

Green means you're on track - budget will last through month end. Yellow is a warning - you'll run out 10 to 20 percent earlier than expected. Red is critical - take action or budget runs out prematurely.

The exhaustion date shows the specific day budget hits zero. Compare this to days remaining in the month.

This answers: Will budget last the month? How many days of runway do we have? Do we need to slow spending to stretch the budget?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows 30 days of daily spend as a stacked area chart. Google Ads spend is shown in blue, Meta Ads in lighter blue stacked on top. The moving average line smooths out daily fluctuations to reveal spending trends. The budget reference line shows daily target.
how_to_read: Blue area represents Google Ads daily spend
Light blue stacked area represents Meta Ads daily spend
Total height shows combined daily spend
The dashed horizontal line marks your daily budget target
Weekend periods may show lower spend (depending on campaigns)
The moving average line reveals spending trends
Hover for exact daily values by platform
decisions_answered: How consistent is our daily spending pattern?
Are there spending anomalies or sudden changes?
Which platform contributes more to daily spend?
Do weekends show different spending patterns?
Is spend trending up or down over the month?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows daily spend over the last 30 days, broken down by platform.

The blue area is Google Ads spend. Stacked on top in lighter blue is Meta Ads spend. Total height shows combined daily spend.

The dashed line marks your daily budget target. When the area exceeds this line, you overspent that day. When it falls short, you underspent.

Look for patterns: consistent spending helps campaign algorithms learn. Sudden drops might indicate budget exhaustion or paused campaigns. Weekend dips are normal for some businesses.

This answers: How consistent is spending? Are there anomalies? Which platform dominates daily spend?

payload.formula_explanation: Daily impression share shows what percentage of eligible impressions your ads captured. Budget lost share indicates impressions missed due to insufficient budget. Rank lost share shows impressions missed due to ad rank (quality score + bid) being lower than competitors.
how_to_read: Blue line shows your actual impression share - higher is better
Orange dashed line shows share lost due to budget constraints
Red dashed line shows share lost due to competitive rank issues
Sum of all three should theoretically equal 100% of eligible impressions
Spikes in budget lost indicate days where budget ran out
Rising rank lost trends suggest increasing competitive pressure
decisions_answered: How has our competitive position changed over the past 60 days?
Are we losing more impressions to budget or rank issues?
When did significant competitive changes occur?
Is our impression share trending up or down over time?
payload.narration_text:
This multi-line chart tracks your competitive position over the past 60 days across three key metrics.

The blue line shows your impression share - the percentage of eligible impressions where your ads appeared. Higher is better, and above 50% typically indicates strong market presence.

The orange dashed line shows budget lost share - impressions you missed because your budget ran out. If this is consistently high, you need more budget to capture available demand.

The red dashed line shows rank lost share - impressions you missed because competitors outranked you. This is driven by quality score and bid amounts.

Watch for trends: rising budget lost means increasing demand you're not capturing. Rising rank lost means competitors are becoming more aggressive. A healthy account typically shows impression share trending up while both loss metrics trend down.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks CTR degradation over time to detect creative fatigue. Ads typically experience declining engagement as audiences become saturated. This curve helps identify the optimal refresh timing before performance deteriorates significantly.
how_to_read: Lines show average CTR for ads at each day since their launch
Blue line represents Google Ads, purple line shows Meta Ads
Downward slopes indicate fatigue setting in over time
Steeper drops suggest faster audience saturation
First 30 days are shown as this is the critical fatigue window
decisions_answered: How quickly do our creatives experience fatigue?
What is the optimal creative refresh cadence for our campaigns?
Do Google and Meta ads fatigue at different rates?
At what point should we rotate in fresh creative assets?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks creative fatigue by showing CTR over time since ad launch.

The formula calculates average CTR for all ads at each day since launch using row numbering to track ad age. A 20% CTR decline signals the fatigue threshold.

Blue shows Google Ads performance, purple shows Meta. Downward slopes indicate fatigue. Steeper drops mean faster saturation.

This answers critical questions: How quickly do creatives fatigue? What's the optimal refresh timing? And do platforms fatigue differently?

payload.formula_explanation: Ranks individual ads by spend to reveal your highest-investment creatives and their performance. Combines Google Ads and Meta Ads in tabbed views with comprehensive metrics for quick comparison and optimization decisions.
how_to_read: Tabs switch between Google Ads and Meta Ads performance tables
Rows are sorted by spend to show highest-investment ads first
ROAS color coding: green (3x+), yellow (2-3x), red (below 2x)
Use search and column sorting to find specific ads or metrics
Click rows to drill into individual ad-level analytics
decisions_answered: Which individual ads receive the most budget?
Are our highest-spend ads also our highest-ROAS ads?
Which ads should be scaled up or paused based on performance?
How do performance metrics vary between Google and Meta ads?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your top ads by spend across Google and Meta platforms.

Key formulas: CTR is clicks over impressions times 100. ROAS is conversion value divided by spend. Days Active counts unique active dates.

Switch tabs to compare platforms. Rows are sorted by spend. ROAS is color-coded: green for 3x plus, yellow for 2 to 3x, red for below 2x.

This answers: Which ads get most budget? Are high-spend ads also high-ROAS? And which should you scale or pause?

payload.formula_explanation: Predicts when your monthly budget will run out based on your current spending velocity. Uses your 14-day average daily spend as the run rate. Compares exhaustion date against days remaining in the month to identify risk.
how_to_read: The large number shows days until budget runs out at current rate
Green means you'll have budget through month end
Yellow means exhaustion 10-20% earlier than month end
Red means you'll run out significantly before month end
Budget Remaining shows dollars left to spend
Daily Run Rate shows your current spending velocity
Exhaustion Date shows the projected day budget hits zero
decisions_answered: Will our budget last through the end of the month?
How many days of budget do we have at current rate?
Do we need to reduce daily spend to stretch the budget?
What's our spending runway if we don't make changes?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI card predicts when your monthly budget will run out based on current spending velocity.

The formula: Budget remaining divided by your 14-day average daily spend gives days until exhaustion.

Green means you're on track - budget will last through month end. Yellow is a warning - you'll run out 10 to 20 percent earlier than expected. Red is critical - take action or budget runs out prematurely.

The exhaustion date shows the specific day budget hits zero. Compare this to days remaining in the month.

This answers: Will budget last the month? How many days of runway do we have? Do we need to slow spending to stretch the budget?

payload.formula_explanation: Device breakdown reveals how mobile, desktop, and tablet users behave differently through the funnel. Mobile typically has lower conversion but higher traffic, while desktop often shows higher conversion rates and order values.
how_to_read: Purple bars show conversion rate by device type
Orange bars show Average Order Value by device
Compare mobile vs desktop performance gaps
Higher mobile conversion rates indicate good mobile UX
Lower mobile AOV may indicate different product preferences
decisions_answered: How does mobile conversion compare to desktop?
Is our mobile experience optimized for purchases?
Which device type generates the highest order values?
Should we invest more in mobile optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart breaks down funnel performance by device type - mobile, desktop, and tablet.

Purple bars show conversion rate for each device. Orange bars show Average Order Value. These metrics often diverge by device.

Typically, desktop converts higher but mobile drives more traffic. If your mobile conversion is close to desktop, your mobile UX is strong. If there's a big gap, there's optimization opportunity.

This answers: How does mobile compare? Is mobile experience optimized? Which device drives highest order values? And should mobile UX be a priority investment?

payload.formula_explanation: Device breakdown reveals how mobile, desktop, and tablet users behave differently through the funnel. Mobile typically has lower conversion but higher traffic, while desktop often shows higher conversion rates and order values.
how_to_read: Purple bars show conversion rate by device type
Orange bars show Average Order Value by device
Compare mobile vs desktop performance gaps
Higher mobile conversion rates indicate good mobile UX
Lower mobile AOV may indicate different product preferences
decisions_answered: How does mobile conversion compare to desktop?
Is our mobile experience optimized for purchases?
Which device type generates the highest order values?
Should we invest more in mobile optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart breaks down funnel performance by device type - mobile, desktop, and tablet.

Purple bars show conversion rate for each device. Orange bars show Average Order Value. These metrics often diverge by device.

Typically, desktop converts higher but mobile drives more traffic. If your mobile conversion is close to desktop, your mobile UX is strong. If there's a big gap, there's optimization opportunity.

This answers: How does mobile compare? Is mobile experience optimized? Which device drives highest order values? And should mobile UX be a priority investment?

payload.formula_explanation: Percentage of total GA4 revenue attributed to Direct traffic (visitors with no tracked referral source). High Direct share usually signals tracking gaps, untagged campaigns, or dark-social traffic that could not be attributed.
how_to_read: The value is Direct revenue divided by total GA4 revenue, expressed as a percentage.
A high or rising share often means attribution is leaking, not that Direct is genuinely growing.
The sparkline shows daily Direct share to reveal sudden tracking changes.
Pair with the Direct Traffic Black Hole chart to estimate what is hidden inside Direct.
decisions_answered: How much of my revenue is going untracked into the Direct bucket?
Are tracking or tagging issues inflating Direct traffic?
Is Direct share trending up in a way that signals attribution loss?

payload.formula_explanation: This table tracks daily checkout flow performance, showing where users drop off during the multi-step checkout process. High abandonment at specific stages (shipping or payment) indicates friction that can be addressed with targeted optimizations.
how_to_read: Each row shows one day of checkout funnel data
Started Checkout is users who began the process
Completed is users who finished purchasing
Abandonment Rate shows the overall drop-off percentage
Red highlighting indicates days with above-average abandonment
decisions_answered: What is our daily checkout abandonment rate?
Which checkout step causes the most drop-offs?
Are there specific days with unusually high abandonment?
Is abandonment trending up or down over time?
payload.narration_text:
This table provides a daily breakdown of your checkout funnel, tracking every step from start to purchase.

The abandonment rate formula: begin checkout minus purchases, divided by begin checkout, times 100. The same logic applies to each sub-step like shipping and payment.

Each row is one day. Compare Started Checkout to Completed to see your success rate. The abandonment rate column highlights problem days in red.

This answers critical questions: What's our daily abandonment rate? Which step loses most users - shipping or payment? Are there spike days to investigate? And is the trend improving over time?

payload.formula_explanation: This table tracks daily checkout flow performance, showing where users drop off during the multi-step checkout process. High abandonment at specific stages (shipping or payment) indicates friction that can be addressed with targeted optimizations.
how_to_read: Each row shows one day of checkout funnel data
Started Checkout is users who began the process
Completed is users who finished purchasing
Abandonment Rate shows the overall drop-off percentage
Red highlighting indicates days with above-average abandonment
decisions_answered: What is our daily checkout abandonment rate?
Which checkout step causes the most drop-offs?
Are there specific days with unusually high abandonment?
Is abandonment trending up or down over time?
payload.narration_text:
This table provides a daily breakdown of your checkout funnel, tracking every step from start to purchase.

The abandonment rate formula: begin checkout minus purchases, divided by begin checkout, times 100. The same logic applies to each sub-step like shipping and payment.

Each row is one day. Compare Started Checkout to Completed to see your success rate. The abandonment rate column highlights problem days in red.

This answers critical questions: What's our daily abandonment rate? Which step loses most users - shipping or payment? Are there spike days to investigate? And is the trend improving over time?

The abandonment rate formula: begin checkout minus purchases, divided by begin checkout, times 100. The same logic applies to each sub-step like shipping and payment.

Each row is one day. Compare Started Checkout to Completed to see your success rate. The abandonment rate column highlights problem days in red.

This answers critical questions: What's our daily abandonment rate? Which step loses most users - shipping or payment? Are there spike days to investigate? And is the trend improving over time?

payload.formula_text: Daily Revenue / Daily Spend (each point is one day; X = Daily Spend, Y = Daily Revenue)
payload.formula_explanation: The efficiency frontier shows the relationship between spend and revenue, revealing diminishing returns. As you spend more, each additional dollar generates less incremental revenue - this is the point where the curve flattens.
how_to_read: X-axis shows daily spend amount, Y-axis shows daily revenue
Each point represents one day's performance
The curve shows the typical spend-to-revenue relationship
Steep curve = efficient spending; Flat curve = diminishing returns
Points above the curve indicate exceptionally efficient days
decisions_answered: At what spend level do returns start diminishing significantly?
Are we over-spending relative to achievable returns?
What is the optimal daily spend level for efficiency?
Should we scale up or pull back based on marginal returns?
payload.narration_text:
The efficiency frontier reveals diminishing returns in your advertising spend.

Each point is one day. The X axis is how much you spent, Y axis is revenue generated. The curve shows the typical relationship.

Here's what to look for: A steep curve means spending efficiently - each dollar works hard. When the curve flattens, you've hit diminishing returns - extra spend generates less extra revenue.

This answers critical questions: At what spend level do returns diminish? Are you over-spending? What's the optimal daily budget? Use this to decide whether to scale up or pull back.

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies high-ROAS campaigns held back by weak ad position (top position below 70%). It estimates the extra top-of-page impressions available if the campaign reached a 90% top-position rate, then prices the rough additional cost to capture them at a 20% higher CPC. Only campaigns with ROAS above 2x are shown so recommendations stay profitable.
how_to_read: Each row is a profitable campaign that is currently winning fewer top placements than it could.
Potential Top Impressions is the additional above-organic impressions available by lifting position to ~90%.
Est. Additional Cost is the rough spend needed to capture them — weigh it against the campaign's ROAS.
Higher current ROAS plus lower top-position rate means a stronger case to invest more.
decisions_answered: Which profitable campaigns are constrained by weak ad position?
How much extra would it cost to capture the missed top-of-page impressions?
Where should I invest more to grab profitable share?

payload.formula_explanation: Estimates what is hidden inside the "Direct" traffic bucket. True Direct traffic is bookmarks and typed URLs. The rest is likely untracked email, social, or paid traffic that lost attribution.
how_to_read: Pie chart shows estimated breakdown of Direct traffic sources
Gray slice is "True Direct" - actual bookmarks and typed URLs
Purple slice estimates email clicks without UTM parameters
Red slice estimates paid traffic with broken UTM tags
Dark gray is "Dark Social" - private messages and shares
Green insight box shows potential recoverable revenue
decisions_answered: What is really hiding in our Direct traffic?
How much revenue could we attribute with better tracking?
Which untracked source is leaking the most attribution?
Should we prioritize UTM tagging improvements?
payload.narration_text:
The Direct Traffic Black Hole breaks down what is hiding in unattributed traffic.

Not all Direct traffic is true direct - people typing your URL or using bookmarks. Much of it is tracking leakage from other channels.

The pie chart estimates: True Direct in gray, untracked email clicks in purple, broken paid UTMs in red, and dark social - private messages and shares - in dark gray.

The insight box shows how much revenue you could recover with proper UTM tagging.

This answers: What is really in your Direct bucket? How much attribution are you losing? And is UTM improvement worth the investment?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks month-to-date spend and projects end-of-month total based on current run rate. The projection uses your average daily spend to estimate final monthly spend. Status indicates whether you'll hit, exceed, or fall short of budget.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual MTD spend percentage
The marker shows expected percentage based on day of month
Projected Spend shows estimated month-end total at current rate
Days Remaining shows business days left in the month
Status badge shows ON_TRACK, OVER_PACING, or UNDER_PACING
Color indicates projected variance from monthly budget
decisions_answered: Will we hit our monthly budget target at current spending rate?
How much budget do we have remaining this month?
What daily spend rate do we need to hit the monthly target?
Are we trending to over-spend or under-spend for the month?
payload.narration_text:
This progress bar shows month-to-date budget pacing with end-of-month projection.

The formulas: Actual pacing is MTD spend divided by monthly budget. Expected pacing accounts for which day of the month we're on. Projection multiplies average daily spend by days remaining.

The status badge tells you the key insight: ON TRACK means you'll hit budget within 5%. OVER PACING means you'll exceed budget. UNDER PACING means you'll fall short.

This answers: Will we hit monthly budget? How much remains? What daily rate do we need to finish on target?

payload.formula_explanation: The gauge shows how much of your daily budget has been spent relative to how much time has passed today. If you've spent 50% of budget by noon (50% of the day), you're on track. Over 115% or under 85% of expected pacing triggers warning indicators.
how_to_read: The needle shows your current pacing percentage (0-150%)
The marker line indicates expected pacing based on time of day
Green zone (85-115%) means you're on track
Orange zones (70-85% or 115-130%) indicate warning
Red zones (<70% or >130%) indicate critical deviation
Bottom values show actual spend vs daily budget
decisions_answered: Are we spending too fast or too slow today?
Will we exhaust our daily budget before day end?
Should we adjust campaign bids mid-day?
Is today's spend aligned with time of day expectations?
payload.narration_text:
This gauge shows today's budget pacing in real-time, updated every 15 minutes.

The formula divides today's spend by your total daily budget, times 100. A second formula calculates expected pacing based on time of day.

To read this: the needle shows current pacing percentage. The marker line shows where you should be based on the hour. Green means on track. Orange means moderate deviation. Red means critical - take action.

This answers: Are we spending too fast or slow? Will we exhaust budget early? Should we adjust bids mid-day to stay on track?

payload.formula_explanation: First Purchase Average Order Value divides total first-order revenue by the number of newly acquired customers in the period. It captures how much a customer typically spends on their very first purchase.
how_to_read: The main value is the average value of a customer's first order
Higher first-purchase AOV improves acquisition payback
Compare against blended CAC to gauge upfront profitability
A rising trend suggests better targeting or higher-intent traffic
Channel-level differences reveal which sources bring bigger first baskets
decisions_answered: How much do new customers spend on their first order?
Is our first-purchase value high enough to offset acquisition cost?
Are recent acquisition efforts attracting higher-value buyers?
Which channels deliver the most valuable first purchases?

payload.formula_explanation: Average number of times each reached user saw an ad, computed from Meta impressions divided by Meta reach. The optimal range is roughly 1.5-3.0x; above 5.0x is flagged critical and below 1.0x or above 3.0x is a warning.
how_to_read: A value of 2.0 means the average reached user saw your ads twice in the period (Meta only).
Values in the 1.5-3.0x band are healthy; very high frequency signals audience fatigue.
Above 5.0x is critical and below 1.0x or above 3.0x carries a warning status.
Read with the saturation curves to see how rising frequency erodes CTR.
decisions_answered: Are users seeing my Meta ads too often (creative fatigue risk)?
Is my frequency within the healthy targeting range?
Do I need to expand reach or refresh creative to lower frequency?

payload.formula_explanation: This comparison shows how different traffic sources perform through the funnel. Some channels drive high volume but low conversion, while others may bring fewer visitors who convert at higher rates.
how_to_read: Blue bars show session volume by channel (quantity of traffic)
Green bars show conversions by channel (quality of traffic)
Compare the ratio of green to blue to assess channel efficiency
High sessions with low conversions suggest poor traffic quality
Small channels with high conversion rates may warrant increased investment
decisions_answered: Which channel drives the most traffic?
Which channel has the highest conversion rate?
Where should we allocate more marketing budget?
Are there underperforming channels we should optimize or reduce?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares funnel performance across your traffic channels.

Blue bars show sessions - the quantity of traffic each channel drives. Green bars show conversions - the quality of that traffic. The ratio between them reveals channel efficiency.

Look for mismatches: high sessions but low conversions suggest poor traffic quality. Small channels with high conversion rates may deserve more investment.

Use this to answer: Which channel drives volume? Which converts best? Where should budget shift? And which channels need optimization or reduction?

payload.formula_explanation: Geographic breakdown shows which states or regions have the highest user counts and revenue. This helps identify strong markets for expansion and weak markets that may need targeted campaigns or operational improvements.
how_to_read: Bar length shows user volume by state
States are sorted by user count (top markets first)
Compare user distribution to revenue distribution
High users with low revenue indicate conversion or AOV problems
Consider population size when evaluating state performance
decisions_answered: Which states have the most users?
Where is our revenue concentrated geographically?
Are there states with high traffic but low revenue per user?
Which regions should we target for growth campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows your top states by user volume, helping you understand geographic distribution.

Bar length indicates user count, sorted from highest to lowest. The top states are your strongest markets for traffic.

But traffic isn't everything - compare this to revenue data. States with many users but low revenue per user have conversion or AOV issues worth investigating. Consider population size too - small penetration in California may beat saturation in Wyoming.

Use this to answer: Where are our users? Where's revenue concentrated? Which states underperform on conversion? And where should growth campaigns focus?

payload.formula_explanation: This time-series chart tracks the volume of sessions and purchases over time, revealing patterns, seasonality, and the impact of marketing activities or site changes on funnel performance.
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily session volume (traffic)
Green line shows daily purchase volume (conversions)
The gap between lines represents unconverted traffic
Look for correlation between traffic spikes and purchase increases
Identify weekday vs weekend patterns and seasonal trends
decisions_answered: How are sessions and purchases trending over time?
Are traffic increases translating to proportional purchase increases?
When did significant performance changes occur?
What day-of-week or seasonal patterns exist in our funnel?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks sessions and purchases over time, revealing your funnel's health and trends.

The blue line shows daily sessions - your traffic. The green line shows daily purchases - your conversions. The gap between them represents lost opportunities.

Look for patterns: Do purchase spikes follow traffic spikes proportionally? What happens on weekends versus weekdays? Are there seasonal trends?

This chart answers: How is performance trending? Are traffic gains converting to sales? When did major changes occur? And what patterns can inform your marketing calendar?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows performance of GA4 audiences including predictive audiences like "Likely 7-day purchasers" and "Likely churners". These audiences use machine learning to predict user behavior.
how_to_read: Purchasers audience shows your actual buying customers for comparison
Likely 7-day purchasers are predicted to buy soon - high priority targets
Likely churning purchasers are at risk of not returning
Revenue per user shows customer value by audience
Session duration and page views indicate engagement levels
decisions_answered: How accurate are GA4 predictive audiences?
Which predictive audience should I target for remarketing?
What is the revenue potential of likely purchasers?
How large is my at-risk customer segment?
payload.narration_text:
GA4 predictive audiences use machine learning to identify users likely to take specific actions.

The Purchasers row shows actual buyers - your baseline for comparison. Likely 7-day purchasers are predicted to buy soon - these are high-value remarketing targets.

Likely churning purchasers are at risk of not returning - consider retention campaigns for this segment.

Revenue per user helps compare value across audiences. Non-purchasers typically show near-zero revenue, while purchasers and likely purchasers show much higher values.

Use these audiences to build more targeted campaigns. Export likely purchasers to Google Ads for high-value remarketing. Target likely churners with retention offers.

payload.formula_text: GA4 Attributed Revenue / Total Ad Spend
payload.formula_explanation: Platform ROAS uses revenue reported by Google/Meta (often inflated due to self-attribution). GA4 ROAS uses Google Analytics 4 attribution, which is typically more conservative and closer to actual performance.
how_to_read: Solid line (green) shows Platform-reported ROAS - what Google/Meta claim
Dashed line (blue) shows GA4-attributed ROAS - independent measurement
Gap between lines indicates attribution discrepancy (platform over-reporting)
Values above 3.0x target line indicate healthy returns
Look for convergence or divergence trends over time
decisions_answered: Is our overall ROAS improving or declining over time?
How much are platforms over-reporting compared to GA4?
When did significant performance changes occur?
Are we meeting our ROAS targets consistently?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your Return on Ad Spend over 90 days with two different measurements.

The solid green line shows Platform ROAS - what Google and Meta report. They tend to over-claim credit. The dashed blue line shows GA4 ROAS - Google Analytics attribution, which is more conservative and usually more accurate.

The gap between these lines reveals how much platforms over-report. When lines are far apart, be cautious about trusting platform numbers.

Look for values above 3x - that's healthy. This chart helps you understand: Is ROAS improving? How much do platforms exaggerate? And when did major performance shifts happen?

payload.formula_explanation: The difference between GA4-reported total revenue and actual Magento store revenue, with the percentage taken relative to Magento revenue. A positive gap means GA4 over-reports versus the store's actual sales of record.
how_to_read: The headline value is GA4 total revenue minus actual Magento revenue.
The percentage is the gap divided by Magento revenue (the source of truth).
A positive gap means GA4 is over-counting revenue relative to real store sales.
Warning is flagged above 10% and critical above 25% over-reporting.
decisions_answered: How closely does GA4 revenue match my actual store revenue?
Is GA4 over- or under-counting versus Magento?
Has GA4 tracking accuracy degraded relative to real sales?

payload.formula_explanation: The dollar difference between what Google Ads self-reports as revenue and what GA4 attributes to Google. The gap percentage is expressed relative to GA4 Google revenue. A positive gap means Google over-reports versus GA4 last-click, driven by view-through, cross-device, and wider attribution windows.
how_to_read: The headline value is the absolute dollar gap (Google platform revenue minus GA4 Google revenue).
The percentage is the gap divided by GA4 Google revenue, not by platform revenue.
A larger positive gap means Google's self-reported numbers are more inflated relative to GA4.
The card trends down/negative when the gap is positive, since over-reporting is undesirable.
decisions_answered: How much is Google Ads over-reporting revenue versus GA4?
Can I trust Google's self-reported ROAS for budget decisions?
Is the Google attribution gap widening over time?

payload.formula_explanation: Reconciles three revenue views for the period: combined platform self-reported revenue (Google + Meta), GA4 total revenue, and actual Magento revenue. Magento is the source of truth; the two gaps quantify how platform and GA4 figures over- or under-state real revenue.
how_to_read: The headline value is actual Magento store revenue.
Platform vs GA4 gap compares combined Google + Meta self-reported revenue to GA4 total.
GA4 vs Magento gap compares GA4 total to actual store revenue.
Use Magento as the trusted figure and treat large gaps as a confidence signal on the other sources.
decisions_answered: How do platform, GA4, and actual store revenue compare for this period?
Which revenue source is most trustworthy for planning?
How large are the gaps between reported and real revenue?

payload.formula_text: Campaigns WHERE ROAS > target AND spend < median
payload.formula_explanation: Identifies high-efficiency campaigns that are under-invested: strong ROAS but low spend. These are the clearest candidates for additional budget.
how_to_read: Each bar shows a campaign's current ROAS; all qualify as Hidden Gems (ROAS >= 5x, spend < $5,000).
Low spend with high ROAS means there is likely room to scale before efficiency drops.
ROAS change versus the prior period helps confirm the efficiency is stable, not a fluke.
Up to five gems are shown - prioritize testing budget increases here.
decisions_answered: Which efficient campaigns are we under-funding?
Where can incremental budget likely earn the highest return?
Are these gems consistently efficient or just lucky this period?

payload.formula_explanation: Stacked bar chart of annual ad spend by channel over a 3-year window, pulled from the annual planning summary mart. Each segment is one channel's yearly spend, and revenue is attached as metadata for context.
how_to_read: Each bar is one year; segments within it are channels stacked to show total spend.
Compare bar heights across years to see whether overall investment is rising or falling.
Compare segment sizes to see how the channel mix has shifted over time.
Hover metadata reveals the revenue each channel generated that year.
decisions_answered: How has my total marketing spend changed over the last 3 years?
Which channels have grown or shrunk as a share of spend?
Is my historical investment pattern consistent with my plan?

payload.formula_explanation: Total number of times your ads were shown across all campaigns in the selected period, summed from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. Higher impressions indicate greater visibility and reach in the market.
how_to_read: The headline value is total ad impressions for the selected date range.
The change versus the comparison period shows whether reach is growing or shrinking.
Read it alongside top-position rate — high impressions at weak position still means lost visibility.
Falling impressions can signal lost eligibility, lower budgets, or tighter targeting.
decisions_answered: How much overall ad visibility did we have this period?
Is our reach growing or shrinking versus the prior period?
Do volume changes line up with budget or targeting changes?

payload.formula_text: Klaviyo Self-Reported Revenue - GA4 Email Revenue
payload.formula_explanation: The difference between revenue Klaviyo claims for email/SMS and the email revenue GA4 attributes. The daily gap is pre-computed in the mart and summed; the percentage is relative to GA4 email revenue. A positive gap means Klaviyo over-reports email's contribution versus GA4.
how_to_read: The headline value is the summed daily Klaviyo gap (Klaviyo self-reported minus GA4 email revenue).
The percentage is the gap divided by GA4 email revenue.
A positive gap means email's self-reported revenue is inflated relative to GA4 attribution.
The card trends down/negative when the gap is positive.
decisions_answered: How much is Klaviyo over-crediting email/SMS revenue versus GA4?
Can I trust Klaviyo's revenue numbers for the email channel?
Is the email attribution gap growing?

payload.formula_explanation: Flags campaigns burning budget inefficiently: low ROAS combined with high spend. These require immediate optimization or budget reduction.
how_to_read: Each bar shows a campaign's current ROAS; only Losers and Top Losers (low ROAS, high spend) appear.
These campaigns are actively wasting budget - the larger the spend, the higher the urgency.
ROAS change versus the prior period shows whether the campaign is deteriorating further.
Up to five are shown, ordered by spend so the biggest budget leaks surface first.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are wasting the most budget?
Where should we cut or optimize spend immediately?
Are underperformers getting worse over time?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares customer quality metrics across different acquisition sources. The email marketing method field indicates how customers first subscribed (API, Magento, List Import, etc.), serving as a proxy for acquisition channel.
how_to_read: Each row represents an acquisition source/method
Customers column shows total acquired through that channel
Avg CLV reveals which sources bring highest-value customers
Total LTV shows absolute value contribution
Repeat Rate indicates loyalty potential from each source
Churn Risk highlights which sources produce at-risk customers
Table is sortable by any column - click headers to sort
decisions_answered: Which acquisition sources produce the highest-value customers?
Where should we invest more marketing budget?
Which sources have the best repeat purchase rates?
Are certain sources associated with higher churn risk?
What is the ROI potential of each acquisition channel?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your customer acquisition sources by quality and value metrics.

Each row represents a different method by which customers entered your database - API integrations, e-commerce platform imports, list uploads, and more. The metrics reveal which sources bring your best customers.

Sort by Average CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition sources. These deserve more investment. But don't ignore volume - a source with slightly lower average CLV but 10x the customers might generate more total value.

Repeat Rate is a key quality indicator. Sources with high repeat rates produce engaged customers who come back. Low repeat rates might indicate promotional-driven acquisitions who bought once for a discount and never returned.

The Churn Risk column is a warning signal. High churn risk sources might need different lifecycle marketing approaches, or they might not be worth the acquisition investment at all.

payload.formula_text: CASE WHEN VIDEO_ID IS NOT NULL THEN "Video" WHEN IMAGE_HASH IS NOT NULL THEN "Image" ELSE "Other"
payload.formula_explanation: Compares performance between image and video creative formats on Meta. This analysis helps determine whether your audience responds better to static or motion creative, informing content strategy and production priorities.
how_to_read: Cards display spend share percentage for each media type
CTR values show engagement rate per media type
Bar chart compares absolute spend between Image, Video, and Other
Higher CTR with proportional spend indicates efficient format
Large spend gaps may suggest format testing opportunities
decisions_answered: Does our audience respond better to image or video ads?
Should we invest more in video production or static images?
What is the efficiency difference between media types?
Are we allocating budget appropriately between formats?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares image versus video creative performance on Meta.

The formula categorizes creatives by checking for video ID or image hash, then aggregates spend and CTR per type. This reveals which format resonates with your audience.

Cards show spend share percentages. CTR indicates engagement per type. The bar chart displays absolute spend comparison.

Use this to answer: Does your audience prefer images or video? Should you invest more in video production? And is your budget allocation optimal between formats?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows Klaviyo email list and segment membership counts. These are your owned audiences — subscribers who opted in to receive email marketing. List size directly impacts email channel reach and revenue potential.
how_to_read: Lists are sorted by member count (largest first)
Type column distinguishes static Lists from dynamic Segments
Member count shows active subscribers in each list
Compare list sizes to understand audience distribution
Use search to find specific lists by name
decisions_answered: How large are our email marketing audiences?
Which lists have the most subscribers?
How are subscribers distributed across lists?
Which lists should we prioritize for campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your Klaviyo email lists and segments with subscriber counts.

These are your owned audiences — people who opted in to receive your email marketing. Unlike paid ad audiences, these are free to reach.

Lists are sorted by member count so your largest audiences appear first. The type column shows whether it's a static List or a dynamic Segment.

Use this to understand your email reach potential and identify which lists to target for campaigns. Larger lists offer more reach, but engagement rates matter too — cross-reference with the Creative Performance email table.

payload.formula_explanation: Marketing Efficiency Ratio measures total store revenue (Magento, the source of truth) against total ad spend. Unlike ROAS, which only counts paid-attributed revenue, MER captures the holistic impact of marketing on the whole business including organic and direct halo effects.
how_to_read: A MER of 4.0 means the business generated $4 of Magento revenue for every $1 of Google + Meta spend.
Numerator is total Magento store revenue, not just paid-attributed revenue, so MER is usually higher than ROAS.
Rising MER alongside flat spend signals marketing is lifting the whole business efficiently.
The sparkline shows daily MER computed from daily Magento revenue divided by daily spend.
decisions_answered: How efficiently is marketing driving total business revenue, not just attributed revenue?
Is overall marketing efficiency improving as I scale spend?
How does whole-business efficiency (MER) compare to channel-attributed efficiency (ROAS)?

payload.formula_explanation: Marketing Efficiency Ratio measures total business revenue against ad spend, including organic and direct traffic. Unlike ROAS which only counts paid-attributed revenue, MER shows the holistic impact of marketing on the entire business.
how_to_read: Single line shows overall business marketing efficiency
Includes ALL revenue sources, not just paid-attributed
Green zone: 4.0x and above - excellent efficiency
Yellow zone: 3.0x to 4.0x - acceptable, monitor closely
Red zone: below 3.0x - action required
decisions_answered: How efficient is marketing for the whole business?
Is our overall marketing ROI at a healthy level?
Are organic channels contributing more to offset ad costs?
What is the true holistic return on marketing investment?
payload.narration_text:
MER - Marketing Efficiency Ratio - is the most holistic view of marketing effectiveness.

The formula: total revenue from ALL sources divided by total ad spend. Unlike ROAS, this includes organic traffic, direct visitors, email - everything.

The zones matter: Above 4x is excellent - your marketing drives strong overall business results. Between 3 and 4x is acceptable but watch closely. Below 3x requires action.

MER answers the big questions: How efficient is marketing for the whole business? Is overall ROI healthy? And are organic channels pulling their weight alongside paid?

payload.formula_explanation: The dollar difference between Meta (Facebook/Instagram) self-reported revenue and GA4-attributed Meta revenue, with the gap percentage expressed relative to GA4 Meta revenue. Meta typically shows larger gaps than Google because of its 7-day-click / 1-day-view attribution windows that GA4 last-click does not capture.
how_to_read: The headline value is the absolute dollar gap (Meta platform revenue minus GA4 Meta revenue).
The percentage is the gap divided by GA4 Meta revenue, not by platform revenue.
Meta gaps are usually wider than Google gaps due to view-through and longer attribution windows.
The card trends down/negative when the gap is positive, since over-reporting is undesirable.
decisions_answered: How much is Meta over-reporting revenue versus GA4?
Should I discount Meta's self-reported ROAS when allocating budget?
Is the Meta attribution gap larger than Google's?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows historical monthly spend totals from MART_BUDGET_PACING, split by Google Ads and Meta Ads. Month-over-month variance reveals spending patterns, seasonality, and growth trends across the selected period.
how_to_read: Each bar is one calendar month's total ad spend, stacked by platform (Google Ads + Meta Ads).
Taller bars mean higher spend; compare bar heights to spot upward or downward trajectories.
The MoM Variance % shows the percentage change from the prior month — positive means spend grew, negative means it shrank.
Use it to identify seasonal peaks and to sanity-check whether current pacing is in line with history.
decisions_answered: Is our monthly spend trending up, down, or holding steady?
Which months historically see the biggest spend swings?
How does this month's spend compare to recent history?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows how spend allocation across creative types has evolved over the past year. This long-term view reveals seasonal patterns, strategic shifts, and trends in creative format investment.
how_to_read: Each line represents a different creative type over 12 months
Upward trends indicate increasing investment in that format
Crossing lines show changing format priorities over time
Seasonal patterns may appear around key shopping periods
Sudden shifts might indicate strategic pivots or market changes
decisions_answered: How has our creative mix evolved over the past year?
Are there seasonal patterns in creative type performance?
Which formats are gaining or losing share over time?
Do our spend trends align with strategic priorities?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks creative type spending trends over 12 months.

The formula aggregates spend by creative type and month, using date truncation to group by calendar month. This reveals long-term patterns.

Each line represents a creative type. Upward slopes mean increasing investment. Crossing lines show shifting priorities. Watch for seasonal patterns around holidays.

Use this to understand: How has your creative mix evolved? Are there seasonal patterns? Which formats are gaining share? And do trends match your strategy?

payload.formula_text: SUM(SPEND) per AD_TYPE per MONTH (Google Ads, last 12 months)
payload.formula_explanation: Shows how spend allocation across creative types has evolved over the past year. This long-term view reveals seasonal patterns, strategic shifts, and trends in creative format investment.
how_to_read: Each line represents a different creative type over 12 months
Upward trends indicate increasing investment in that format
Crossing lines show changing format priorities over time
Seasonal patterns may appear around key shopping periods
Sudden shifts might indicate strategic pivots or market changes
decisions_answered: How has our creative mix evolved over the past year?
Are there seasonal patterns in creative type performance?
Which formats are gaining or losing share over time?
Do our spend trends align with strategic priorities?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks creative type spending trends over 12 months.

The formula aggregates spend by creative type and month, using date truncation to group by calendar month. This reveals long-term patterns.

Each line represents a creative type. Upward slopes mean increasing investment. Crossing lines show shifting priorities. Watch for seasonal patterns around holidays.

Use this to understand: How has your creative mix evolved? Are there seasonal patterns? Which formats are gaining share? And do trends match your strategy?

payload.formula_text: Monthly Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS / 1,000,000) per month Monthly Revenue = SUM(CONVERSIONS_VALUE) per month Monthly ROAS = Monthly Revenue / Monthly Spe
payload.formula_explanation: Line chart of monthly ad spend (and revenue as metadata) per channel across the last 36 months from the marketing-mix mart. It reveals month-to-month spend cadence and longer-term trends by channel.
how_to_read: Each line is a channel; the x-axis is calendar month over 3 years.
Rising lines indicate increasing monthly spend in that channel.
Look for recurring seasonal humps that repeat year to year.
Revenue is carried in metadata to pair spend with its return.
decisions_answered: How does my monthly spend fluctuate through the year?
Which channels show seasonal spend patterns?
Are there months where spend has consistently grown or shrunk?

payload.formula_explanation: Notification channels determine how and where alerts are delivered. Each channel can be configured for specific severity levels. Multiple channels ensure alerts reach the right people through their preferred communication method.
how_to_read: Each card represents a configured notification channel
Channel type icons: envelope = email, chat = Slack, phone = SMS, code = webhook
Status badge shows enabled (green) or disabled (gray)
Severities show which alert levels trigger this channel
Toggle button enables/disables the channel
Edit button opens channel configuration
Add Channel button creates new notification destinations
decisions_answered: Where are alerts being sent?
Are the right people receiving the right alert types?
Do we need additional notification channels?
Are any channels misconfigured or disabled?
payload.narration_text:
This section shows your configured notification channels for alert delivery.

Each channel represents a destination where alerts can be sent. Email sends to specified addresses. Slack posts to a channel. SMS texts to phone numbers. Webhooks call external APIs.

Configure each channel for appropriate severities. Critical alerts might go to SMS for immediate attention. Warnings might go to email for routine review.

Multiple channels provide redundancy. If someone misses an email, they might see the Slack message.

This answers: Where do alerts go? Are the right people notified? Do we need more channels?

payload.formula_explanation: The dollar amount of spend sitting in the Optimize/Cut quadrant (high spend, low ROAS). This is budget being spent inefficiently that could be reallocated.
how_to_read: The value is total spend on campaigns classified as Optimize/Cut (above-median spend, below-median ROAS).
The description shows how many campaigns are at risk and what share of total spend they represent.
Higher values mean more budget is being deployed inefficiently.
Treat this as the reallocation opportunity: money that could move to Scale or Hidden Gem campaigns.
decisions_answered: How much budget is being spent inefficiently right now?
What share of total spend is at risk in the Optimize/Cut quadrant?
How much could be freed up for reallocation to winners?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows top Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by sends, with engagement metrics. CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures how compelling the email content is for those who actually opened it.
how_to_read: Campaigns are sorted by total sends (highest first)
Open % shows what fraction of recipients opened the email
Click % shows clicks as a percentage of total sends
CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate) measures clicks relative to opens — a better indicator of content quality
Use search to find specific campaigns by subject line
decisions_answered: Which email campaigns reached the most recipients?
Which subject lines drove the highest open rates?
Which email content generated the most clicks?
How does engagement vary across different campaign types?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your top Klaviyo email campaigns ranked by sends, with engagement metrics.

Open percentage is opens divided by sends. Click percentage is clicks divided by sends. CTOR, or click-to-open rate, measures clicks relative to opens — a better indicator of content quality since it only counts engaged recipients.

Use this to identify which subject lines drive opens, which content drives clicks, and how engagement varies across campaigns.

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows email campaign performance from Klaviyo, separated from the paid scatter plot since email campaigns have no ad spend. Metrics are sourced from Klaviyo event data (sends, opens, clicks) aggregated at the campaign level.
how_to_read: Sends = total emails delivered for the campaign
Opens = total email opens (including multiple opens per recipient)
Clicks = total link clicks within the email
Open % = percentage of sends that were opened
Click % = percentage of sends that received a click
CTOR = click-to-open rate, clicks as a percentage of opens (engagement quality)
decisions_answered: Which email campaigns drive the most engagement?
What open and click rates are our campaigns achieving?
Which campaigns have high open rates but low click-through (content issue)?
Which campaigns have the best click-to-open ratio (best content)?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your Klaviyo email campaign performance, separated from the paid campaign scatter plot since email has no ad spend.

Each row is one email campaign with its engagement metrics. Sends is total emails delivered. Opens and clicks track engagement.

Open Rate tells you subject line effectiveness. Click Rate shows overall campaign performance. CTOR - click to open rate - is the purest content quality metric, showing what percentage of people who opened actually clicked.

A good benchmark: 40 to 50 percent open rates, 2 to 3 percent click rates, and 5 percent or higher CTOR.

payload.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Optimize/Cut quadrant (High spend, low ROAS). Campaigns spending heavily with poor returns - they require immediate optimization or budget reduction. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Optimize/Cut.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Optimize/Cut quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Optimize/Cut group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

payload.formula_explanation: The 2x2 matrix plots campaigns by their spend (x-axis, log scale) and ROAS (y-axis). Quadrant boundaries are set at the median spend and median ROAS of all visible campaigns. Bubble size represents revenue - larger bubbles indicate higher-revenue campaigns.
how_to_read: Each bubble represents one campaign - size indicates revenue
Colors indicate channel: Blue = Google Ads, Purple = Meta Ads, Green = Klaviyo
Top-right (Scale) = High spend, high ROAS - increase budget here
Top-left (Scale Opportunity) = Low spend, high ROAS - hidden gems to grow
Bottom-right (Optimize/Cut) = High spend, low ROAS - needs attention or reduction
Bottom-left (Test/Kill) = Low spend, low ROAS - evaluate or pause
Dashed lines show median spend and ROAS thresholds
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are high-efficiency opportunities ready to scale?
Which campaigns are burning budget with poor returns and should be cut?
Where are our hidden gems with high ROAS but low spend?
How does campaign performance vary by channel?
What is the total addressable scaling opportunity in our portfolio?
payload.narration_text:
The Campaign Performance Matrix is a 2x2 quadrant analysis that instantly reveals which campaigns to scale, optimize, or cut.

Each bubble is one campaign. The horizontal axis shows spend on a log scale - further right means more spending. The vertical axis shows ROAS - higher means better returns. Bubble size represents revenue.

The matrix divides into four quadrants based on median spend and ROAS. Top-right is your Scale zone - high spend and high ROAS. These are your winners - increase budget here.

Top-left is Scale Opportunity - low spend but high ROAS. These are hidden gems waiting to grow. Bottom-right is Optimize or Cut - high spend but poor ROAS. These need immediate attention.

Bottom-left is Test or Kill - low spend and low ROAS. Evaluate whether to keep testing or pause entirely.

This chart answers: Which campaigns should get more budget? Which are wasting money? And where are the untapped opportunities?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares pacing between Google Ads and Meta Ads. Shows whether platforms are spending at the same rate or if one is significantly ahead or behind. Includes ROAS comparison to understand efficiency differences.
how_to_read: Progress bars show pacing percentage for each platform
Blue bar represents Google Ads pacing
Light blue bar represents Meta Ads pacing
The marker shows expected pacing for the day of month
Grid shows MTD Spend, Budget, Daily Rate, and ROAS
ROAS badges are color-coded: green 8x+, yellow 6-8x, red <6x
Status badges show ON_TRACK, OVER, or UNDER for each platform
decisions_answered: Which platform is pacing faster - Google or Meta?
Should we rebalance budget between platforms?
Which platform has better ROAS efficiency?
Is one platform significantly over or under-spending?
payload.narration_text:
This comparison shows budget pacing for Google Ads versus Meta Ads side by side.

The formula: each platform's MTD spend divided by its monthly budget gives the pacing percentage. ROAS is revenue divided by spend for each platform.

Progress bars show how each platform is tracking. The marker shows where they should be based on the day of month. Status badges indicate ON TRACK, OVER, or UNDER for each.

Compare the ROAS values carefully. If Meta has 13x ROAS but Google has 8x, Meta is more efficient per dollar spent.

This answers: Which platform paces faster? Should you rebalance? Which has better efficiency? Is one significantly off track?

payload.formula_explanation: Monitors daily spend against budget and generates alerts when significant deviations occur. Critical alerts indicate >15% overspend or >30% underspend. Warning alerts catch moderate deviations of 5-15%. Helps proactive budget management.
how_to_read: Red CRITICAL alerts require immediate attention
Yellow WARNING alerts need monitoring
Blue INFO alerts are notable but not urgent
Each alert shows the date and specific issue
Dollar amounts show actual spend vs budget for that day
Empty state (green checkmark) means budget is on track
Alerts sorted by date, most recent first
decisions_answered: Were there any days with significant overspend?
Are there patterns in budget deviations?
Which days need investigation for spend issues?
Is overall budget management healthy or problematic?
payload.narration_text:
This alert stack shows budget deviations requiring attention over the last 7 days.

The formula: daily spend divided by daily budget gives the spend ratio. Critical alerts trigger when ratio exceeds 1.15 or falls below 0.70. Warning alerts trigger at 1.05 or 0.85.

Red critical alerts need immediate attention - you're significantly over or under budget. Yellow warnings need monitoring but aren't urgent. Blue info alerts are just notable observations.

If you see a green checkmark and no alerts, congratulations - your budget management is healthy.

This answers: Were there overspend days? Are there deviation patterns? Which days need investigation?

payload.formula_text: Number of marketing touchpoints before conversion
payload.formula_explanation: Shows how many marketing interactions customers have before purchasing. Single-touchpoint conversions benefit last-click attribution. Multi-touch journeys require more sophisticated attribution models.
how_to_read: X-axis shows number of touchpoints (1, 2, 3, 4, 5+)
Bar height indicates percentage of conversions at that path length
High single-touchpoint rate favors simpler attribution
High multi-touch rate suggests need for multi-touch models
Average path length shown as annotation
Longer paths typically have higher order values
decisions_answered: How complex are our customers purchase journeys?
Do most customers convert in one touch or many?
Is last-click attribution appropriate for our business?
Should we invest in nurture sequences for longer journeys?
payload.narration_text:
Path Length Distribution shows how many marketing touchpoints customers have before converting.

If most conversions happen in a single touch, last-click attribution works fine. If customers need multiple touches, you need multi-touch attribution models.

Look at the bar heights. High bars on the left mean simpler journeys. High bars on the right mean complex, multi-touch paths.

The average path length matters too. Longer paths often correlate with higher order values - customers who research more spend more.

This helps you understand: Is your purchase journey simple or complex? And does your attribution model match reality?

payload.display_name: Per Launch Month Date Trunc Month Min Date Day Total Launched
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the monthly success rate of new product launches, defined as the percentage of products that generate any revenue within their first 90 days. This metric evaluates launch effectiveness over time.
how_to_read: Each point shows the success rate for products launched in that month
Higher values indicate better launch performance
The target line (if shown) indicates the goal success rate
Look for trends - improving rates suggest better launch processes
Declining rates may indicate market or execution issues
decisions_answered: Is our launch success rate improving or declining?
Are we meeting our target success rate benchmarks?
Which months produced the most successful launches?
How does current success rate compare to historical average?
payload.narration_text:
This trend line shows your monthly launch success rate - tracking whether new products are gaining traction.

The formula calculates the percentage of products launched each month that generate revenue within 90 days. Only months with 90-plus days of data are included for complete measurement.

Reading this chart: Each point represents a month's launch cohort success rate. Upward trends indicate improving launch effectiveness. Declining trends signal potential issues with product selection, positioning, or market conditions.

Context matters. A 25% success rate is a reasonable benchmark for new product introductions. Higher is better, but even successful catalogs see many new products fail to gain traction.

Key questions answered: Is success rate improving? Are we meeting targets? Which periods performed best? Use this to evaluate your product development and launch processes over time.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows historical percentile distribution for each metric based on 12 months of data. P10 means 10% of historical days had values at or below this level. This helps set intelligent thresholds based on actual performance history rather than arbitrary values.
how_to_read: Each metric is shown as a horizontal grouped bar
P10 (red) represents the 10th percentile - typical critical threshold
P25 (orange) represents the 25th percentile - typical warning threshold
P50 (blue) is the median - typical/expected performance
P75 (green) is above-average performance
P90 (purple) represents excellent/exceptional performance
Compare your threshold settings to these historical baselines
decisions_answered: What are historically normal ranges for each metric?
Are my thresholds aligned with historical performance?
What constitutes truly exceptional performance for each metric?
Should I adjust thresholds based on historical distribution?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows historical percentile distributions for each metric over the past year.

Percentiles help you set intelligent thresholds. P10 means only 10% of historical days performed this poorly - a reasonable critical threshold. P25 is the bottom quarter - a reasonable warning threshold.

P50 is your median, typical performance. P75 and P90 represent above-average and excellent performance respectively.

If your critical threshold is set at P5, you'll rarely get alerts even during bad performance. If set at P30, you'll get alerts too frequently. Use this chart to calibrate appropriately.

This answers: What are normal ranges? Are thresholds aligned with history? What's exceptional performance?

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies high-ROAS campaigns held back by weak ad position (top position below 70%). It estimates the extra top-of-page impressions available if the campaign reached a 90% top-position rate, then prices the rough additional cost to capture them at a 20% higher CPC. Only campaigns with ROAS above 2x are shown so recommendations stay profitable.
how_to_read: Each row is a profitable campaign that is currently winning fewer top placements than it could.
Potential Top Impressions is the additional above-organic impressions available by lifting position to ~90%.
Est. Additional Cost is the rough spend needed to capture them — weigh it against the campaign's ROAS.
Higher current ROAS plus lower top-position rate means a stronger case to invest more.
decisions_answered: Which profitable campaigns are constrained by weak ad position?
How much extra would it cost to capture the missed top-of-page impressions?
Where should I invest more to grab profitable share?

payload.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month revenue from MART_BUDGET_PACING by adding the period's average daily revenue (CONVERSION_VALUE) for each remaining day to month-to-date revenue. Current and projected ROAS show whether efficiency is holding as spend scales toward month end.
how_to_read: The headline value is the forecast total revenue for the current month at the present run rate.
Current ROAS is revenue divided by spend for the selected period — your efficiency right now.
Projected ROAS applies the same trajectory to month end; compare it to Current ROAS to see if returns are improving or eroding.
A projected ROAS below target signals the extra spend may not be paying off.
decisions_answered: How much revenue will this month likely close at?
Is our return on spend (ROAS) holding as we pace toward month end?
Are we forecast to hit our revenue goal at the current run rate?

payload.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month revenue from MART_BUDGET_PACING by adding the period's average daily revenue (CONVERSION_VALUE) for each remaining day to month-to-date revenue. Current and projected ROAS show whether efficiency is holding as spend scales toward month end.
how_to_read: The headline value is the forecast total revenue for the current month at the present run rate.
Current ROAS is revenue divided by spend for the selected period — your efficiency right now.
Projected ROAS applies the same trajectory to month end; compare it to Current ROAS to see if returns are improving or eroding.
A projected ROAS below target signals the extra spend may not be paying off.
decisions_answered: How much revenue will this month likely close at?
Is our return on spend (ROAS) holding as we pace toward month end?
Are we forecast to hit our revenue goal at the current run rate?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks month-to-date spend and projects end-of-month total based on current run rate. The projection uses your average daily spend to estimate final monthly spend. Status indicates whether you'll hit, exceed, or fall short of budget.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual MTD spend percentage
The marker shows expected percentage based on day of month
Projected Spend shows estimated month-end total at current rate
Days Remaining shows business days left in the month
Status badge shows ON_TRACK, OVER_PACING, or UNDER_PACING
Color indicates projected variance from monthly budget
decisions_answered: Will we hit our monthly budget target at current spending rate?
How much budget do we have remaining this month?
What daily spend rate do we need to hit the monthly target?
Are we trending to over-spend or under-spend for the month?
payload.narration_text:
This progress bar shows month-to-date budget pacing with end-of-month projection.

The formulas: Actual pacing is MTD spend divided by monthly budget. Expected pacing accounts for which day of the month we're on. Projection multiplies average daily spend by days remaining.

The status badge tells you the key insight: ON TRACK means you'll hit budget within 5%. OVER PACING means you'll exceed budget. UNDER PACING means you'll fall short.

This answers: Will we hit monthly budget? How much remains? What daily rate do we need to finish on target?

payload.formula_text: Median Spend AND ROAS < Median ROAS
payload.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Optimize/Cut quadrant (High spend, low ROAS). Campaigns spending heavily with poor returns - they require immediate optimization or budget reduction. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Optimize/Cut.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Optimize/Cut quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Optimize/Cut group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

payload.display_name: Quadrant Rule Spend Median Spend And Roas
payload.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Scale Opportunity quadrant (Low spend, high ROAS). Efficient but under-invested campaigns - high ROAS with low spend, representing untapped growth potential. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Scale Opportunity.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Scale Opportunity quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Scale Opportunity group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

payload.formula_text: Median), Scale Opportunity (Spend < Median AND ROAS >= Median), Optimize/Cut (Spend >= Median AND ROAS < Median), Test/Kill (Spend < Median AND ROAS < Median)
payload.formula_explanation: Four summary cards roll up the campaign matrix by quadrant, showing how many campaigns, how much spend, and the average ROAS sit in each. Quadrant boundaries use the median spend and median ROAS of campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
how_to_read: Each card represents one quadrant: Scale, Scale Opportunity, Optimize/Cut, Test/Kill.
Campaign count shows how concentrated your portfolio is in each performance zone.
Total spend exposes how much budget is committed to each quadrant - watch Optimize/Cut.
Average ROAS summarizes the efficiency of campaigns within that quadrant.
decisions_answered: How is spend distributed across performance quadrants?
How many campaigns are winners versus underperformers?
Which quadrant holds the most budget and at what efficiency?

payload.formula_explanation: The gap between the top-position rate and the absolute-top (#1) rate, computed from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. It represents impressions that appeared above organic results but not in the #1 spot — the opportunity to climb from 'above organic' to the top slot. Note: the backend metric id is position_gap; this is a top-position proxy, not a rank-lost-impression-share figure.
how_to_read: The value is the percentage of impressions in top position but not at #1.
A larger gap means more room to push from above-organic into the absolute top spot.
It is a neutral metric — neither inherently good nor bad — read it as headroom.
Compare with absolute-top rate to judge how aggressively to bid for #1.
decisions_answered: How much headroom is there to move from above-organic to the #1 position?
Are we capturing top placement but missing the #1 spot?
Is it worth bidding up to close the gap to absolute top?

payload.formula_explanation: Estimated unique users reached via Meta Ads in the period. This is approximate because daily reach values can overlap across days, so summing them may double-count some users.
how_to_read: The value is the summed Meta reach for the date range (Meta only).
Treat it as an approximate ceiling on unique users, since daily reach can overlap.
A daily-average reach figure is provided as supporting context.
Read with Average Frequency to balance breadth of reach against repetition.
decisions_answered: How many unique users am I reaching on Meta?
Is my reach growing or shrinking vs the prior period?
Do I have enough reach to support my frequency goals?

payload.formula_text: SUM(Unique Recipients per Flow) grouped by Trigger Type
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates all recipients across flows that share the same trigger type. This shows which automation triggers reach the most people in your audience.
how_to_read: Taller bars indicate trigger types reaching more recipients
Common trigger types include: Added to List, Metric, Date, Segment, Price Drop
Hover over each bar to see exact recipient counts
Compare relative bar heights to understand your automation portfolio balance
Missing or small trigger types may represent growth opportunities
decisions_answered: Which trigger types drive the most recipient volume?
Is our automation portfolio balanced across different trigger types?
Are there underutilized trigger types we should expand?
Which trigger mechanisms are most valuable for our business?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart breaks down your flow recipients by trigger type.

The formula simply sums all unique recipients across flows that share the same trigger mechanism - whether that's being added to a list, a metric being triggered, a date condition, or joining a segment.

Taller bars mean more recipients reached through that trigger type. Compare bar heights to understand your automation portfolio.

Are you over-reliant on one trigger type? Are there trigger mechanisms you're underutilizing? For example, if Price Drop triggers are small but your business has frequent price changes, that could be an opportunity.

This chart answers: Which triggers reach the most people? Is your portfolio balanced? And where should you consider building new automations?

payload.formula_text: INCREASE if Platform ROAS > Avg ROAS × 1.2
payload.formula_explanation: Analyzes 14-day platform performance and recommends budget adjustments. If a platform's ROAS is 20% above average, it recommends increasing budget by 20%. If ROAS is 20% below average, it recommends decreasing by 15%. Helps optimize budget allocation based on efficiency.
how_to_read: Each card shows a platform with its recommendation
Green INCREASE means allocate more budget to this platform
Red DECREASE means reduce budget from this platform
Gray MAINTAIN means current allocation is appropriate
14-Day ROAS shows recent efficiency for the platform
Current vs Recommended shows suggested budget change
Rationale explains the reasoning behind recommendation
decisions_answered: Should we shift budget between Google and Meta?
Which platform deserves more investment?
Is our current budget allocation optimal?
What's the expected benefit of reallocation?
payload.narration_text:
These recommendation cards suggest budget reallocation based on 14-day platform performance.

The formula: Calculate each platform's ROAS over 14 days. Compare to average. If a platform's ROAS is 20% above average, recommend increasing its budget. If 20% below, recommend decreasing.

Green INCREASE means this platform is efficient - give it more budget. Red DECREASE means it's underperforming - shift budget away. Gray MAINTAIN means current allocation is fine.

The rationale explains why: high ROAS suggests room to scale profitably. Low ROAS suggests diminishing returns.

This answers: Should we rebalance between platforms? Which deserves more investment? Is our allocation optimal?

payload.formula_explanation: Measures the gap between GA4-tracked purchases and Magento orders for the period. A non-zero delta flags tracking discrepancies between analytics and the order system.
how_to_read: The value is GA4 purchases minus Magento orders; positive means GA4 over-counts, negative means it under-counts.
The percentage gap contextualizes the delta relative to actual Magento orders.
A delta near zero indicates analytics and order data agree (healthy tracking).
The sparkline plots the daily GA4-minus-Magento difference to localize when drift occurs.
decisions_answered: Do GA4 purchases and Magento orders agree, or is tracking off?
How large is the analytics-to-orders gap this period?
Which days show the biggest tracking discrepancies?

payload.formula_text: Categories that could be attributed with proper tagging
payload.formula_explanation: Estimates what is hidden inside the "Direct" traffic bucket. True Direct traffic is bookmarks and typed URLs. The rest is likely untracked email, social, or paid traffic that lost attribution.
how_to_read: Pie chart shows estimated breakdown of Direct traffic sources
Gray slice is "True Direct" - actual bookmarks and typed URLs
Purple slice estimates email clicks without UTM parameters
Red slice estimates paid traffic with broken UTM tags
Dark gray is "Dark Social" - private messages and shares
Green insight box shows potential recoverable revenue
decisions_answered: What is really hiding in our Direct traffic?
How much revenue could we attribute with better tracking?
Which untracked source is leaking the most attribution?
Should we prioritize UTM tagging improvements?
payload.narration_text:
The Direct Traffic Black Hole breaks down what is hiding in unattributed traffic.
