Not all Direct traffic is true direct - people typing your URL or using bookmarks. Much of it is tracking leakage from other channels.

The pie chart estimates: True Direct in gray, untracked email clicks in purple, broken paid UTMs in red, and dark social - private messages and shares - in dark gray.

The insight box shows how much revenue you could recover with proper UTM tagging.

This answers: What is really in your Direct bucket? How much attribution are you losing? And is UTM improvement worth the investment?

payload.formula_explanation: Calculates the average time between alert trigger and acknowledgment over the last 7 days. Faster response times indicate efficient alert handling. Industry best practice suggests acknowledging critical alerts within 15 minutes and warnings within 1 hour.
how_to_read: The number shows average minutes to acknowledge alerts
Lower is better - faster response enables quicker action
Green (<15 min) indicates excellent response efficiency
Yellow (15-60 min) indicates acceptable but improvable
Red (>60 min) indicates response process needs improvement
Compare against your team's SLA for alert response
decisions_answered: How quickly is our team responding to alerts?
Are we meeting our response time SLAs?
Do we need to improve our alert notification channels?
Should we add SMS or additional channels for faster notification?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows average time to acknowledge alerts over the last 7 days, measured in minutes.

The formula calculates time between trigger and acknowledgment for all acknowledged alerts. Faster response enables faster action to resolve issues.

Best practice: acknowledge critical alerts within 15 minutes, warnings within 1 hour. Green means excellent response. Yellow means room for improvement. Red means your notification process needs work.

If response times are slow, consider adding SMS notifications or Slack integration for faster team awareness.

This answers: How fast does our team respond? Are we meeting SLAs? Do we need better notification channels?

payload.formula_explanation: Stacked bar chart of GA4-attributed revenue by channel grouping per year, with sessions and revenue-per-session as metadata. It reflects how GA4 attributes revenue across acquisition channels rather than ad-platform-reported conversions.
how_to_read: Each bar is a year; segments are GA4 channel groupings stacked by attributed revenue.
This is GA4's attribution view, which can differ from ad platforms' self-reported numbers.
Hover metadata shows sessions and revenue-per-session to judge channel quality.
Compare years to see which channels are gaining or losing attributed revenue share.
decisions_answered: Which channels does GA4 credit with driving revenue?
How does GA4 attribution compare to platform-reported performance?
Which channels convert sessions into revenue most effectively?

payload.formula_text: Revenue flow from first to last touch
payload.formula_explanation: Visualizes how customers journey from their first marketing touchpoint to their final converting touchpoint. Wider flows indicate more revenue following that path.
how_to_read: Left side shows first-touch channels (how customers discovered you)
Right side shows last-touch channels (where customers converted)
Flow width represents revenue volume following that path
Same-channel flows indicate single-touch or brand loyalty
Cross-channel flows show customer journey complexity
Hover for exact revenue values on each flow
decisions_answered: How do customers flow from discovery to conversion?
Which channels are primarily introducers vs closers?
What cross-channel paths generate the most revenue?
Should we invest more in channels that initiate journeys?
payload.narration_text:
This Sankey diagram shows how revenue flows from first-touch to last-touch channels.

The left side is where customers first discovered you. The right side is where they finally converted. Flow width indicates revenue volume.

When revenue flows between different channels, it shows journey complexity. When it stays in the same channel, customers converted in a single touch or returned to familiar channels.

This answers: How do customers flow through your marketing? Which channels start journeys versus close them? And where should you invest to capture more revenue at different journey stages?

payload.formula_explanation: Expected blended return on ad spend across all channels, computed as prior-year total platform conversion value divided by total spend from the marketing-mix mart. It expresses, in aggregate, how many dollars of revenue each dollar of ad spend returns.
how_to_read: A value of 7.5x means every $1 of ad spend is expected to return $7.50 in revenue.
It blends all channels together, so high-efficiency channels (Email) and lower-efficiency paid channels are averaged by spend weight.
Use it as the single headline efficiency number for the whole plan.
It is derived from prior-year actuals, so it reflects realized performance, not a target.
decisions_answered: How efficient is my marketing spend overall?
Is the plan's blended efficiency healthy relative to my benchmarks?
If I shift budget between channels, is blended ROAS likely to improve or erode?

payload.formula_explanation: Analyzes 14-day platform performance and recommends budget adjustments. If a platform's ROAS is 20% above average, it recommends increasing budget by 20%. If ROAS is 20% below average, it recommends decreasing by 15%. Helps optimize budget allocation based on efficiency.
how_to_read: Each card shows a platform with its recommendation
Green INCREASE means allocate more budget to this platform
Red DECREASE means reduce budget from this platform
Gray MAINTAIN means current allocation is appropriate
14-Day ROAS shows recent efficiency for the platform
Current vs Recommended shows suggested budget change
Rationale explains the reasoning behind recommendation
decisions_answered: Should we shift budget between Google and Meta?
Which platform deserves more investment?
Is our current budget allocation optimal?
What's the expected benefit of reallocation?
payload.narration_text:
These recommendation cards suggest budget reallocation based on 14-day platform performance.

The formula: Calculate each platform's ROAS over 14 days. Compare to average. If a platform's ROAS is 20% above average, recommend increasing its budget. If 20% below, recommend decreasing.

Green INCREASE means this platform is efficient - give it more budget. Red DECREASE means it's underperforming - shift budget away. Gray MAINTAIN means current allocation is fine.

The rationale explains why: high ROAS suggests room to scale profitably. Low ROAS suggests diminishing returns.

This answers: Should we rebalance between platforms? Which deserves more investment? Is our allocation optimal?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Scale quadrant (High spend, high ROAS). Campaigns that are both high-spending and high-performing - proven winners that are prime candidates for additional budget. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Scale.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Scale quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Scale group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Scale Opportunity quadrant (Low spend, high ROAS). Efficient but under-invested campaigns - high ROAS with low spend, representing untapped growth potential. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Scale Opportunity.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Scale Opportunity quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Scale Opportunity group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

payload.display_name: Scale Opportunity Spend Median And Roas
payload.formula_explanation: The 2x2 matrix plots campaigns by their spend (x-axis, log scale) and ROAS (y-axis). Quadrant boundaries are set at the median spend and median ROAS of all visible campaigns. Bubble size represents revenue - larger bubbles indicate higher-revenue campaigns.
how_to_read: Each bubble represents one campaign - size indicates revenue
Colors indicate channel: Blue = Google Ads, Purple = Meta Ads, Green = Klaviyo
Top-right (Scale) = High spend, high ROAS - increase budget here
Top-left (Scale Opportunity) = Low spend, high ROAS - hidden gems to grow
Bottom-right (Optimize/Cut) = High spend, low ROAS - needs attention or reduction
Bottom-left (Test/Kill) = Low spend, low ROAS - evaluate or pause
Dashed lines show median spend and ROAS thresholds
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are high-efficiency opportunities ready to scale?
Which campaigns are burning budget with poor returns and should be cut?
Where are our hidden gems with high ROAS but low spend?
How does campaign performance vary by channel?
What is the total addressable scaling opportunity in our portfolio?
payload.narration_text:
The Campaign Performance Matrix is a 2x2 quadrant analysis that instantly reveals which campaigns to scale, optimize, or cut.

Each bubble is one campaign. The horizontal axis shows spend on a log scale - further right means more spending. The vertical axis shows ROAS - higher means better returns. Bubble size represents revenue.

The matrix divides into four quadrants based on median spend and ROAS. Top-right is your Scale zone - high spend and high ROAS. These are your winners - increase budget here.

Top-left is Scale Opportunity - low spend but high ROAS. These are hidden gems waiting to grow. Bottom-right is Optimize or Cut - high spend but poor ROAS. These need immediate attention.

Bottom-left is Test or Kill - low spend and low ROAS. Evaluate whether to keep testing or pause entirely.

This chart answers: Which campaigns should get more budget? Which are wasting money? And where are the untapped opportunities?

payload.formula_explanation: The 2x2 matrix plots campaigns by their spend (x-axis, log scale) and ROAS (y-axis). Quadrant boundaries are set at the median spend and median ROAS of all visible campaigns. Bubble size represents revenue - larger bubbles indicate higher-revenue campaigns.
how_to_read: Each bubble represents one campaign - size indicates revenue
Colors indicate channel: Blue = Google Ads, Purple = Meta Ads, Green = Klaviyo
Top-right (Scale) = High spend, high ROAS - increase budget here
Top-left (Scale Opportunity) = Low spend, high ROAS - hidden gems to grow
Bottom-right (Optimize/Cut) = High spend, low ROAS - needs attention or reduction
Bottom-left (Test/Kill) = Low spend, low ROAS - evaluate or pause
Dashed lines show median spend and ROAS thresholds
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are high-efficiency opportunities ready to scale?
Which campaigns are burning budget with poor returns and should be cut?
Where are our hidden gems with high ROAS but low spend?
How does campaign performance vary by channel?
What is the total addressable scaling opportunity in our portfolio?
payload.narration_text:
The Campaign Performance Matrix is a 2x2 quadrant analysis that instantly reveals which campaigns to scale, optimize, or cut.

Each bubble is one campaign. The horizontal axis shows spend on a log scale - further right means more spending. The vertical axis shows ROAS - higher means better returns. Bubble size represents revenue.

The matrix divides into four quadrants based on median spend and ROAS. Top-right is your Scale zone - high spend and high ROAS. These are your winners - increase budget here.

Top-left is Scale Opportunity - low spend but high ROAS. These are hidden gems waiting to grow. Bottom-right is Optimize or Cut - high spend but poor ROAS. These need immediate attention.

Bottom-left is Test or Kill - low spend and low ROAS. Evaluate whether to keep testing or pause entirely.

This chart answers: Which campaigns should get more budget? Which are wasting money? And where are the untapped opportunities?

payload.formula_text: Meta Ads with REACH > 0 and SUM(SPEND) > 100
payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot showing the relationship between average frequency (how often users see ads) and CTR (engagement rate) for Meta ad sets. Bubble size indicates spend level, color indicates efficiency zone.
how_to_read: X-axis shows average frequency (times each user sees your ad)
Y-axis shows CTR - higher means better engagement at that frequency
Green bubbles (1-2x frequency) are in the optimal efficiency zone
Yellow bubbles (2-3.5x frequency) are approaching saturation
Red bubbles (3.5x+ frequency) show audience saturation - consider refresh
Larger bubbles indicate higher spend - prioritize optimizing these
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are showing audience saturation (too high frequency)?
What is the optimal frequency before CTR starts declining?
Which high-spend ad sets need creative refresh?
Should I expand reach or accept higher frequency?
payload.narration_text:
This scatter plot reveals the relationship between frequency and engagement for your Meta ad sets.

Frequency is how many times each user sees your ads. CTR measures how often they click. The goal is finding the sweet spot - enough frequency for message retention, but not so much that users tune out.

Green bubbles show ad sets in the optimal 1-2x frequency zone. Yellow indicates warning at 2 to 3.5x. Red shows saturation above 3.5x where performance typically drops.

Bubble size represents spend - bigger bubbles are your biggest investments, so prioritize those.

When you see red bubbles, consider refreshing creative, expanding the audience, or reducing bid caps to lower frequency.

payload.formula_explanation: Chart of the monthly revenue seasonality index from the seasonality coefficients mart, where 1.0 represents an average month. Values above 1.0 are peak months and below 1.0 are slow months; spend index and recommended budget allocation accompany each month as metadata.
how_to_read: Each point/bar is a month; the value is its revenue index relative to the yearly average.
Above 1.0 means that month historically over-performs; below 1.0 means it under-performs.
Use peaks to time budget concentration and promotions.
Metadata includes a recommended budget percentage per month for planning.
decisions_answered: Which months are my strongest and weakest historically?
When should I concentrate budget to match demand?
How should I distribute the annual budget across months?

payload.formula_text: Monthly Avg Revenue / Overall Avg Revenue
payload.formula_explanation: Shows seasonal performance patterns by month and channel. Index above 1.0 means above-average performance (peak season). Index below 1.0 means below-average (slow season). Use these factors to adjust thresholds seasonally and avoid false alerts during predictable low periods.
how_to_read: Each row represents a marketing channel
Each column represents a month (Jan-Dec)
Cell color indicates seasonal index: red = below average, white = average, green = above average
Numbers show the exact seasonality multiplier
Index > 1.5 indicates peak season (Feb shows 2.0+ for many channels)
Index < 0.5 indicates slow season (Jun-Sep shows low values)
Use these factors to adjust thresholds by month
decisions_answered: Which months are peak season for each channel?
How much should I adjust thresholds for seasonal variation?
When should I expect below-average performance?
Are there channels that don't follow typical seasonality?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap shows seasonal performance patterns for each marketing channel by month.

The seasonality index compares monthly average to overall average. An index of 2.0 means that month typically performs twice as well as average. An index of 0.5 means half as well.

For this business, January through March shows high indices - peak season. Summer months June through September show low indices - slow season.

Use these factors to adjust thresholds. During February with a 2.5 index, a revenue threshold of $27,000 should become $67,500. This prevents false alerts during predictably high periods and catches real issues during slow periods.

This answers: When is peak season? How much should thresholds adjust? When to expect low performance?

payload.formula_explanation: Each bar shows the percentage of users who successfully moved from one stage to the next. Higher percentages indicate smoother transitions, while lower percentages reveal friction points in the customer journey.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher conversion rates between stages
Compare bar lengths to identify weakest conversion points
Industry benchmarks help contextualize your performance
Session to View rates above 100% are common as users view multiple products
Focus optimization on stages with the lowest conversion rates
decisions_answered: Which stage transition has the lowest conversion rate?
Where should we prioritize conversion rate optimization efforts?
How do our stage conversion rates compare to industry standards?
Are there specific transitions that need immediate attention?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down conversion rates between consecutive funnel stages.

Each bar shows the percentage of users moving from one stage to the next. Session to View is views divided by sessions. View to Cart is adds divided by views. And so on through checkout to purchase.

To read it: longer bars mean better conversion. Shorter bars reveal friction points. Note that Session to View can exceed 100% since users often view multiple products.

Use this chart to identify: Which transition is weakest? Where should you optimize? And how do you compare to industry benchmarks? Focus your CRO efforts on the shortest bars.

payload.formula_text: MTD Spend + (Avg Daily Spend × Days Left)
payload.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month spend from MART_BUDGET_PACING using multiple methods. The simple projection uses the period's overall daily average; the weighted projection accounts for distinct weekday-versus-weekend spending; the confidence range is built from the spend standard deviation to bound expected variance.
how_to_read: The headline value is the forecast total spend for the current month at the present run rate.
The weighted projection is usually more accurate when weekday and weekend spend differ noticeably.
The confidence range (low to high) shows how much the final number could swing given recent volatility.
Compare the projection against the monthly budget to see whether you are tracking to over- or under-spend.
decisions_answered: How much will we spend by month end if nothing changes?
Are we projected to overspend or underspend the monthly budget?
How wide is the uncertainty around the month-end spend forecast?

payload.formula_explanation: Bar chart that groups historical days by their daily spend level and shows the average ROAS in each band. It surfaces diminishing returns: if ROAS falls as the daily-spend band rises, higher spend days were less efficient.
how_to_read: The x-axis is daily spend bands from lowest to highest.
The bar height is the average ROAS observed on days in that band.
A downward staircase means efficiency drops as you spend more per day (diminishing returns).
A flat or rising pattern means you have room to scale spend without losing efficiency.
decisions_answered: At what daily spend level does my efficiency start to drop?
Can I scale spend further without sacrificing ROAS?
What is the optimal daily spend ceiling for the plan?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares performance of different Meta Ads call-to-action buttons. CTAs signal user intent and affect both click quality and conversion likelihood. This analysis reveals which CTAs drive the most engagement and efficiency.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show total spend for each CTA button type
SHOP_NOW typically dominates for e-commerce brands
CTR percentages below indicate engagement rate per CTA type
Compare spend allocation with CTR to identify efficiency opportunities
High spend with low CTR may indicate CTA mismatch with audience intent
decisions_answered: Which call-to-action buttons drive the highest engagement?
Is our CTA selection aligned with campaign objectives?
Should we test different CTAs on high-spend, low-CTR ads?
What CTA types work best for our specific audience segments?
payload.narration_text:
This chart analyzes Meta Ads call-to-action button performance.

The formula sums spend and averages CTR by CTA type, covering buttons like Shop Now, Learn More, and Order Now. Different CTAs signal different user intents.

Horizontal bars display spend per CTA. The CTR values below show engagement rates. Compare these to spot efficiency gaps.

Use this to answer: Which CTAs drive highest engagement? Are we using the right CTAs for our goals? And should we A/B test different buttons on underperforming ads?

payload.formula_explanation: Visualizes Google Ads quality distribution by ad strength rating. Ad strength is Google's quality indicator based on headlines, descriptions, and asset variety. This reveals how many ads fall into each quality tier and their associated spend.
how_to_read: The donut chart segments represent spend allocation by ad strength category
Green segments (Excellent/Good) indicate high-quality ads following best practices
Orange (Average) and red (Poor) segments highlight improvement opportunities
The center displays total spend across all ad strength categories
CTR metrics below each category show engagement correlation with quality
decisions_answered: What percentage of our Google ads have strong quality ratings?
How much budget is going to low-quality ads that could be improved?
Is there a correlation between ad strength and click-through rate?
Which ads should be prioritized for headline and description optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart displays your Google Ads quality distribution by ad strength rating.

Ad strength is Google's quality indicator based on asset variety, headline quality, and description relevance. The formula counts distinct ads per strength category and sums their associated spend.

Green segments show Excellent and Good rated ads. Orange represents Average. Red indicates Poor ratings. The center shows your total spend.

This helps answer: What percentage of ads have strong ratings? How much budget goes to low-quality ads? And which ads need optimization attention?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares performance between image and video creative formats on Meta. This analysis helps determine whether your audience responds better to static or motion creative, informing content strategy and production priorities.
how_to_read: Cards display spend share percentage for each media type
CTR values show engagement rate per media type
Bar chart compares absolute spend between Image, Video, and Other
Higher CTR with proportional spend indicates efficient format
Large spend gaps may suggest format testing opportunities
decisions_answered: Does our audience respond better to image or video ads?
Should we invest more in video production or static images?
What is the efficiency difference between media types?
Are we allocating budget appropriately between formats?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares image versus video creative performance on Meta.

The formula categorizes creatives by checking for video ID or image hash, then aggregates spend and CTR per type. This reveals which format resonates with your audience.

Cards show spend share percentages. CTR indicates engagement per type. The bar chart displays absolute spend comparison.

Use this to answer: Does your audience prefer images or video? Should you invest more in video production? And is your budget allocation optimal between formats?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks daily revenue contribution from each major channel as a stacked area chart. Shows how channel mix evolves over time and reveals seasonal patterns, campaign impacts, and channel interdependencies.
how_to_read: Each colored area represents one marketing channel stacked on others
The total height shows combined daily revenue across all channels
Watch for channel proportions changing over time
Spikes may indicate successful campaigns or seasonal events
Consistent patterns suggest stable channel performance
Click to isolate individual channel series for closer analysis
decisions_answered: How is our channel mix evolving over time?
Are any channels growing or declining in contribution?
What impact do campaigns have on channel performance?
Are there seasonal patterns in channel effectiveness?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows how channel revenue contributions evolve over time - revealing trends, patterns, and campaign impacts.

The formula sums daily revenue for each channel, then stacks them to show both individual channel performance and total revenue trajectory.

Reading this chart: Each colored layer represents a channel. The total height shows combined revenue. Watch how layer proportions shift over time - growing layers indicate channels gaining importance.

Look for spikes that coincide with campaigns. A spike in Paid Search might show a Google Ads campaign working. A jump in Email indicates a successful send. Dips might signal issues needing investigation.

Key questions: How is channel mix evolving? Are channels growing or shrinking? What patterns exist? Click any channel in the legend to isolate its trend line.

payload.formula_explanation: The funnel visualizes customer journey progression from initial session through to purchase. Each stage shows the absolute count and the percentage that moved forward from the previous stage. Drop-off between stages represents lost potential customers.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a stage in the customer journey
Bar width shows relative volume compared to the starting point
Percentages between stages show the conversion rate from one step to the next
The largest drop-offs indicate where optimization efforts should focus
Green color for the final purchase stage highlights successful conversions
decisions_answered: What is our overall conversion rate from session to purchase?
Where are we losing the most potential customers?
Which funnel stage needs the most optimization attention?
How does our funnel compare to e-commerce benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel chart visualizes your customer journey from initial session through to purchase completion.

The formula is straightforward: each stage's conversion rate equals the stage count divided by the previous stage count, times 100. Overall conversion is purchases divided by sessions.

To read this chart: each bar represents a journey stage. Bar width shows relative volume. The percentages between stages reveal where customers drop off.

Focus on the largest drop-offs - those are your biggest optimization opportunities. This chart answers: What's our conversion rate? Where do we lose customers? And which stage needs the most attention?

payload.formula_text: GA4 Tables + Google Ads Tables + Meta Ads Tables + Klaviyo Tables
payload.formula_explanation: Shows the total number of database tables being monitored across all four data schemas. This represents the breadth of your data infrastructure. Each table contains specific marketing data that feeds into dashboard analytics.
how_to_read: The primary number shows total tables across all schemas
Breakdown by schema: GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo
Stable count indicates consistent data structure
Sudden changes may indicate new tables added or tables dropped
Higher counts generally mean more granular data available
Click to explore table inventory in Master Config
decisions_answered: How comprehensive is our data coverage?
Are all expected tables present in each schema?
Has our data infrastructure changed recently?
Which schema has the most tables to monitor?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows the total count of tables being monitored across all data schemas.

The count is a sum of tables from GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, and Klaviyo. Each schema contributes different amounts based on data granularity.

A stable count indicates consistent infrastructure. Changes might indicate schema updates from Fivetran or data model changes.

This answers: How comprehensive is our data coverage? Are all expected tables present? Has our infrastructure changed?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts campaigns in the Test/Kill quadrant (Low spend, low ROAS). Campaigns with both low spend and low ROAS - either early tests not yet optimized or underperformers to pause. The card also shows that quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
how_to_read: The value is the number of campaigns classified as Test/Kill.
Quadrant boundaries are the median spend and median ROAS across campaigns with positive spend and ROAS.
The description shows the quadrant's total spend and average ROAS.
Use this alongside the other quadrant cards to see how the portfolio is distributed.
decisions_answered: How many campaigns fall in the Test/Kill quadrant?
How much spend and what ROAS does the Test/Kill group carry?
Is the portfolio shifting toward winners or underperformers?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows how long customers take from first marketing interaction to purchase. Same-day conversions are impulse buys. Longer windows require nurturing and may lose attribution to platform cookie windows.
how_to_read: X-axis shows time buckets (Same Day, 1-3 Days, etc.)
Bar height indicates percentage of conversions in that window
Same-day conversions are typically lower-funnel or retargeting
Longer windows often involve upper-funnel discovery channels
Compare to platform attribution windows (7-day, 28-day)
Conversions beyond window may lose platform attribution
decisions_answered: How quickly do customers convert after first touch?
Are we losing attribution to expired cookie windows?
Should we adjust platform attribution window settings?
What is the typical consideration period for our products?
payload.narration_text:
Time to Conversion shows how long customers take from first marketing touch to purchase.

Same-day conversions are typically impulse buys or retargeting success. Longer windows indicate customers who need time to research or consider.

Compare your distribution to platform attribution windows. If many customers take more than 7 days, Meta's default 7-day click window may miss conversions. If more than 30 days, even Google's 30-day window might not capture the full journey.

This answers key questions: What is your typical sales cycle? Are platforms cutting off attribution too early? And should you extend attribution windows to capture delayed conversions?

payload.formula_explanation: Flags campaigns burning budget inefficiently: low ROAS combined with high spend. These require immediate optimization or budget reduction.
how_to_read: Each bar shows a campaign's current ROAS; only Losers and Top Losers (low ROAS, high spend) appear.
These campaigns are actively wasting budget - the larger the spend, the higher the urgency.
ROAS change versus the prior period shows whether the campaign is deteriorating further.
Up to five are shown, ordered by spend so the biggest budget leaks surface first.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are wasting the most budget?
Where should we cut or optimize spend immediately?
Are underperformers getting worse over time?

payload.formula_explanation: Percentage of impressions that did NOT land in top position (i.e. below organic results), computed as 100 minus the impression-weighted top-position rate from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. Lower is better — it indicates stronger ad placement. Note: the backend metric id is below_top; the absence of true impression-share data means this is a top-position proxy, not a budget-lost-impression-share figure.
how_to_read: The value is the share of impressions that fell below top position.
Lower is better — a high value means most impressions are losing prominent placement.
Track the change versus the comparison period; a rising value means placement is eroding.
Pair it with top-position rate; together they account for all impressions.
decisions_answered: What share of our impressions are stuck below top position?
Is our weak-placement share growing or shrinking?
How much visibility are we losing to poor ad placement?

payload.formula_explanation: Surfaces the highest-performing campaigns - those combining strong ROAS with meaningful spend. Campaigns are labeled Top Winner or Winner by ROAS and spend thresholds, then the top five by spend are shown.
how_to_read: Each bar is a campaign's current ROAS; only Top Winners and Winners qualify.
Qualification requires both high ROAS and substantial spend, so these are proven at scale.
ROAS change versus the prior period indicates whether a winner is still accelerating.
Up to five campaigns are shown, ordered by spend (largest committed budget first).
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are our proven, high-spend winners?
Are our top performers still improving period over period?
Where is high-efficiency spend already concentrated?

payload.formula_text: Google Platform Revenue + Meta Platform Revenue
payload.formula_explanation: Reconciles three revenue views for the period: combined platform self-reported revenue (Google + Meta), GA4 total revenue, and actual Magento revenue. Magento is the source of truth; the two gaps quantify how platform and GA4 figures over- or under-state real revenue.
how_to_read: The headline value is actual Magento store revenue.
Platform vs GA4 gap compares combined Google + Meta self-reported revenue to GA4 total.
GA4 vs Magento gap compares GA4 total to actual store revenue.
Use Magento as the trusted figure and treat large gaps as a confidence signal on the other sources.
decisions_answered: How do platform, GA4, and actual store revenue compare for this period?
Which revenue source is most trustworthy for planning?
How large are the gaps between reported and real revenue?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows Klaviyo email list and segment membership counts. These are your owned audiences — subscribers who opted in to receive email marketing. List size directly impacts email channel reach and revenue potential.
how_to_read: Lists are sorted by member count (largest first)
Type column distinguishes static Lists from dynamic Segments
Member count shows active subscribers in each list
Compare list sizes to understand audience distribution
Use search to find specific lists by name
decisions_answered: How large are our email marketing audiences?
Which lists have the most subscribers?
How are subscribers distributed across lists?
Which lists should we prioritize for campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your Klaviyo email lists and segments with subscriber counts.

These are your owned audiences — people who opted in to receive your email marketing. Unlike paid ad audiences, these are free to reach.

Lists are sorted by member count so your largest audiences appear first. The type column shows whether it's a static List or a dynamic Segment.

Use this to understand your email reach potential and identify which lists to target for campaigns. Larger lists offer more reach, but engagement rates matter too — cross-reference with the Creative Performance email table.

payload.formula_explanation: Each bar shows the percentage of users who successfully moved from one stage to the next. Higher percentages indicate smoother transitions, while lower percentages reveal friction points in the customer journey.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher conversion rates between stages
Compare bar lengths to identify weakest conversion points
Industry benchmarks help contextualize your performance
Session to View rates above 100% are common as users view multiple products
Focus optimization on stages with the lowest conversion rates
decisions_answered: Which stage transition has the lowest conversion rate?
Where should we prioritize conversion rate optimization efforts?
How do our stage conversion rates compare to industry standards?
Are there specific transitions that need immediate attention?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down conversion rates between consecutive funnel stages.

Each bar shows the percentage of users moving from one stage to the next. Session to View is views divided by sessions. View to Cart is adds divided by views. And so on through checkout to purchase.

To read it: longer bars mean better conversion. Shorter bars reveal friction points. Note that Session to View can exceed 100% since users often view multiple products.

Use this chart to identify: Which transition is weakest? Where should you optimize? And how do you compare to industry benchmarks? Focus your CRO efforts on the shortest bars.

payload.formula_explanation: Reconciles three revenue views for the period: combined platform self-reported revenue (Google + Meta), GA4 total revenue, and actual Magento revenue. Magento is the source of truth; the two gaps quantify how platform and GA4 figures over- or under-state real revenue.
how_to_read: The headline value is actual Magento store revenue.
Platform vs GA4 gap compares combined Google + Meta self-reported revenue to GA4 total.
GA4 vs Magento gap compares GA4 total to actual store revenue.
Use Magento as the trusted figure and treat large gaps as a confidence signal on the other sources.
decisions_answered: How do platform, GA4, and actual store revenue compare for this period?
Which revenue source is most trustworthy for planning?
How large are the gaps between reported and real revenue?

payload.formula_explanation: View-through conversions are when someone sees an ad but does not click, then converts later. High VTC ratios can inflate platform-reported performance since these are harder to attribute definitively.
how_to_read: Blue portion shows click-through conversions (more reliable)
Yellow portion shows view-through conversions (less reliable)
Campaigns with high yellow are claiming view-through credit
PMAX campaigns typically show highest VTC ratios
Brand campaigns usually show minimal or zero VTC
High VTC may explain platform vs GA4 attribution gap
decisions_answered: Which campaigns claim the most view-through credit?
How much of platform-reported revenue is from VTC?
Should we adjust VTC attribution windows in platform settings?
Are certain campaign types over-relying on view-through claims?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows how much each campaign relies on view-through conversions versus click conversions.

Click conversions - in blue - are more reliable. Someone clicked your ad, then converted. View-through conversions - in yellow - mean someone saw your ad, didn't click, but converted later. Platforms claim credit, but it's harder to prove causation.

Performance Max campaigns typically show the highest VTC ratios. Brand search campaigns usually have none.

High VTC explains why platforms report higher ROAS than GA4. If a campaign is mostly yellow, be skeptical of its reported performance. Consider adjusting your VTC attribution windows.

payload.formula_text: COUNT(Alerts WHERE severity = 'warning' AND triggered_at >= NOW() - 24h)
payload.formula_explanation: Counts active warning alerts triggered in the last 24 hours. Warning alerts indicate metrics approaching critical thresholds - for example, ROAS between P10 and P25 historical baselines. These provide early warning before situations become critical.
how_to_read: The number shows warning alerts active in the last 24 hours
Warnings are less severe than critical but need monitoring
Yellow/amber coloring indicates moderate concern
A few warnings is normal; many warnings suggests review is needed
Warnings often precede critical alerts if not addressed
Click to filter and view only warning alerts
decisions_answered: Are any metrics trending toward critical thresholds?
Which campaigns might become problematic soon?
Should I proactively adjust bids or budgets?
Are there patterns in which metrics generate warnings?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows warning severity alerts currently active.

Warnings are your early warning system. They trigger when metrics approach critical levels but haven't crossed them yet. For example, ROAS between the 10th and 25th percentile.

Some warnings are normal in day-to-day operations. However, multiple warnings across different metrics may indicate a broader issue worth investigating.

Act on warnings before they become critical. Proactive adjustment is easier than reactive firefighting.

This answers: Are metrics trending toward problems? Which campaigns might become critical? Should I adjust budgets proactively?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows week-to-date spend progress against the weekly budget target. The expected pacing line accounts for which day of the week we're on. Week-over-week comparison shows spending trajectory changes.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual spend percentage (solid fill)
The vertical marker shows expected pacing for current day of week
Green bar if within 10% of expected, yellow if 10-20% off, red if >20%
WTD Spend shows total spent so far this week
Days Remaining shows workdays left to hit weekly target
WoW Change shows projected spend versus last week
decisions_answered: How is this week's spend tracking against last week?
Do we need to adjust daily spending to hit weekly targets?
Are there any days where we significantly over/under-spent?
What's our spending velocity trend week-over-week?
payload.narration_text:
This progress bar tracks week-to-date spending against your weekly budget.

The formula: week-to-date spend divided by weekly budget, times 100. The expected marker shows where you should be based on the day of week.

Color coding helps: green means within 10% of expected. Yellow means 10 to 20% variance. Red means more than 20% off track.

Week-over-week change shows if you're trending higher or lower than last week. This helps answer: Are we on pace this week? Do we need to adjust daily spend? How does this compare to last week?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates impressions to weekly buckets and tracks the impression-weighted top-position rate week over week from MART_COMPETITIVE_INTELLIGENCE. The WoW change is the point difference versus the prior week, showing whether competitive placement is improving or slipping over the last several weeks.
how_to_read: Each bar is one week's average top-position rate.
WoW Change shows the point movement versus the previous week — positive means placement improved.
A run of declining bars signals competitors are gaining ground on placement.
Up to the last 8 weeks are shown, most recent first.
decisions_answered: Is our ad placement improving or slipping week over week?
Did a recent bid or budget change move our position?
Are competitors gaining ground on top-of-page placement?

payload.formula_explanation: For each campaign this stacked bar shows the share of impressions that landed below top position (below organic results), computed as 100 minus the impression-weighted top-position rate. Hover metadata also carries the top-position and absolute-top (#1) rates. Campaigns are ranked by the largest below-top share to surface the weakest placements first.
how_to_read: Taller bars mean a larger share of impressions fell below top position — worse competitive placement.
The metadata splits each campaign into top-position and absolute-top (#1) rates.
Only campaigns with more than 100 impressions are shown; the top 15 by below-top share are ranked first.
Focus on the tallest bars first — those campaigns are losing the most visibility to placement.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are losing the most impressions to weak ad position?
How does placement strength vary across my campaigns?
Where should I improve bids or quality to climb above organic results?

payload.formula_text: MTD Spend + (Weekday Avg × Weekdays Left) + (Weekend Avg × Weekends Left)
payload.formula_explanation: Projects end-of-month spend from MART_BUDGET_PACING using multiple methods. The simple projection uses the period's overall daily average; the weighted projection accounts for distinct weekday-versus-weekend spending; the confidence range is built from the spend standard deviation to bound expected variance.
how_to_read: The headline value is the forecast total spend for the current month at the present run rate.
The weighted projection is usually more accurate when weekday and weekend spend differ noticeably.
The confidence range (low to high) shows how much the final number could swing given recent volatility.
Compare the projection against the monthly budget to see whether you are tracking to over- or under-spend.
decisions_answered: How much will we spend by month end if nothing changes?
Are we projected to overspend or underspend the monthly budget?
How wide is the uncertainty around the month-end spend forecast?

payload.formula_explanation: Surfaces the highest-performing campaigns - those combining strong ROAS with meaningful spend. Campaigns are labeled Top Winner or Winner by ROAS and spend thresholds, then the top five by spend are shown.
how_to_read: Each bar is a campaign's current ROAS; only Top Winners and Winners qualify.
Qualification requires both high ROAS and substantial spend, so these are proven at scale.
ROAS change versus the prior period indicates whether a winner is still accelerating.
Up to five campaigns are shown, ordered by spend (largest committed budget first).
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are our proven, high-spend winners?
Are our top performers still improving period over period?
Where is high-efficiency spend already concentrated?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows week-to-date spend progress against the weekly budget target. The expected pacing line accounts for which day of the week we're on. Week-over-week comparison shows spending trajectory changes.
how_to_read: The progress bar shows actual spend percentage (solid fill)
The vertical marker shows expected pacing for current day of week
Green bar if within 10% of expected, yellow if 10-20% off, red if >20%
WTD Spend shows total spent so far this week
Days Remaining shows workdays left to hit weekly target
WoW Change shows projected spend versus last week
decisions_answered: How is this week's spend tracking against last week?
Do we need to adjust daily spending to hit weekly targets?
Are there any days where we significantly over/under-spent?
What's our spending velocity trend week-over-week?
payload.narration_text:
This progress bar tracks week-to-date spending against your weekly budget.

The formula: week-to-date spend divided by weekly budget, times 100. The expected marker shows where you should be based on the day of week.

Color coding helps: green means within 10% of expected. Yellow means 10 to 20% variance. Red means more than 20% off track.

Week-over-week change shows if you're trending higher or lower than last week. This helps answer: Are we on pace this week? Do we need to adjust daily spend? How does this compare to last week?

payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot mapping each campaign by competitive position (impression-weighted top-position rate on the X-axis) against efficiency (ROAS on the Y-axis). Quadrant boundaries are the averages of both axes across the displayed campaigns (impressions > 1000, spend > 0), so points sort into stars, opportunities, and laggards.
how_to_read: X-axis is top-position rate (placement strength); Y-axis is ROAS (efficiency).
Top-right campaigns are stars — strong position and strong returns.
Bottom-left campaigns lag on both and need attention.
High-ROAS but low-position points (top-left) are prime candidates to push for more placement.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are my stars (strong position and strong ROAS)?
Which efficient campaigns could win more by improving position?
Which campaigns underperform on both fronts and need rework?

payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot mapping each campaign by competitive position (impression-weighted top-position rate on the X-axis) against efficiency (ROAS on the Y-axis). Quadrant boundaries are the averages of both axes across the displayed campaigns (impressions > 1000, spend > 0), so points sort into stars, opportunities, and laggards.
how_to_read: X-axis is top-position rate (placement strength); Y-axis is ROAS (efficiency).
Top-right campaigns are stars — strong position and strong returns.
Bottom-left campaigns lag on both and need attention.
High-ROAS but low-position points (top-left) are prime candidates to push for more placement.
decisions_answered: Which campaigns are my stars (strong position and strong ROAS)?
Which efficient campaigns could win more by improving position?
Which campaigns underperform on both fronts and need rework?

payload.formula_explanation: Total planned marketing budget for the planning year. The backend sums annual ad spend from the planning summary mart; when no spend has been recorded yet it falls back to the configured $2.24M default. The change is measured against the prior year's spend.
how_to_read: The headline value is the total ad budget allocated for the planning year.
The percentage compares this budget to last year's total spend.
An up trend means you are investing more than the prior year; down means a leaner plan.
The sparkline shows the recent 7-day spend trajectory from the daily pulse mart.
decisions_answered: How much am I planning to invest in marketing this year?
Is my budget growing or shrinking versus last year?
Do I have a configured plan, or am I defaulting to the baseline budget?

payload.formula_explanation: Bar chart of the year-over-year revenue change percentage from the pre-computed YoY columns in the annual planning summary mart. Each bar is a year's revenue growth rate; spend growth and absolute spend/revenue are available as metadata.
how_to_read: Each bar is a year; the value is that year's revenue growth versus the prior year.
Positive bars indicate growth; negative bars indicate contraction.
Compare consecutive bars to see whether growth is accelerating or decelerating.
Metadata pairs revenue growth with spend growth so you can see if growth came from more spend.
decisions_answered: What growth rate has the business sustained year over year?
Is revenue growth accelerating or slowing?
Did revenue grow faster or slower than spend?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows how many customers fall into each purchase frequency bucket. One-time buyers are conversion opportunities, while high-frequency customers are your most valuable advocates.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each purchase frequency level
One-time buyers typically form the largest segment
Bar color gradient reflects average LTV at each frequency
High frequency (5+ orders) shows significantly lower churn
Click any bucket to analyze customers at that frequency
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are one-time buyers?
How many customers have become repeat purchasers?
Is our repeat purchase rate improving over time?
Where should we focus second-purchase campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows your customers distributed by purchase frequency - the key to understanding your repeat purchase opportunity.

The formula counts customers at each order frequency: 1, 2, 3 to 4, 5 to 9, and 10-plus orders. Bar colors reflect average LTV at each level.

Reading this chart: One-time buyers typically form the largest bar - this is normal but represents your biggest conversion opportunity. Getting that second purchase is the critical hurdle.

Notice the color gradient. Higher frequency customers show significantly better LTV and lower churn. Once customers reach 3-plus orders, retention becomes much easier.

Key questions: What's the one-time buyer percentage? How many repeat purchasers exist? Where should second-purchase campaigns focus? Click any bucket to target that customer group directly.

How many customers have become repeat purchasers?

Where should we focus second-purchase campaigns?

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers by days since their last tracked activity (purchase, email open, site visit). More recent engagement correlates with lower churn risk.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each engagement recency level
Active and Engaged segments are your healthy, engaged customers
At Risk and Dormant need re-engagement campaigns
Churned customers (180+ days) may be permanently lost
The churn percentage on each bar shows that segment average
decisions_answered: How engaged is our customer base currently?
How many customers are slipping toward churn?
Which engagement segments need immediate campaigns?
Is our engagement distribution improving over time?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows customers segmented by engagement recency - a leading indicator of churn before it happens.

The formula groups customers by days since last activity: Active within 7 days, Engaged 8 to 30, At Risk 31 to 90, Dormant 91 to 180, and Churned over 180 days.

Reading this chart: Longer bars in Active and Engaged indicate a healthy, engaged database. Large At Risk or Dormant segments signal re-engagement opportunity.

The average churn probability shown for each segment demonstrates the correlation. Active customers typically show lower churn, while Churned customers approach 90 percent plus churn probability.

Key questions: How engaged is your base? How many are slipping away? Which segments need campaigns? Use this to size your re-engagement and win-back initiatives.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the average churn probability across your customer base week by week. Rising trends indicate increasing churn risk across the customer base, while declining trends suggest improving retention.
how_to_read: The line shows average churn probability percentage over 90 days
Upward trends indicate increasing churn risk - customers are disengaging
Downward trends suggest improving retention - your efforts are working
Sudden spikes may indicate external events or campaign issues
Hover over any point to see the exact week and churn percentage
Click a point to analyze that specific week in detail
decisions_answered: Is our overall churn risk increasing or decreasing?
Are there seasonal patterns in churn risk?
Did recent campaigns or changes impact churn positively or negatively?
When did significant shifts in churn risk occur?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your average churn probability week by week over the last 90 days.

An upward trend is a warning sign - more of your customers are at risk of leaving. Investigate what changed. Was there a product issue? Price increase? Campaign that backfired?

A downward trend is positive - your retention efforts are working. Keep doing what's effective.

Look for sudden spikes or drops. These often correlate with specific events - a major sale, a new competitor, or changes in your product or service.

Use this chart to measure the impact of retention initiatives. If you launched a win-back campaign, did the average churn probability decrease in following weeks?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts customers who have engaged with your brand in the last 90 days through any activity: purchases, email opens, site visits, or other tracked events. The active rate shows database health.
how_to_read: The main value shows customers with recent engagement (90 days)
The active rate badge shows what percentage of your database is engaged
Healthy databases maintain 70%+ active rates
Declining active rates may indicate list hygiene issues
Click to segment customers by engagement recency
decisions_answered: What percentage of our customer base is actively engaged?
Is our database healthy or full of dormant contacts?
Do we need win-back campaigns for inactive customers?
Should we clean our list of permanently inactive contacts?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI tracks active customers - those who have engaged with your brand within 90 days. It measures the health of your customer database.

The formula counts customers whose last event date falls within the past 90 days. Events include purchases, email opens, website visits, and other tracked interactions.

Reading this card: The main number shows your active customer count. The badge displays the active rate - what percentage of your total database remains engaged.

Database health matters for deliverability and efficiency. A 70 percent or higher active rate is healthy. Below 50 percent suggests you may need aggressive win-back campaigns or list cleanup.

Key questions: How engaged is your database? Should you run win-back campaigns? Is list hygiene needed? Click to segment customers by their engagement recency.

payload.formula_explanation: Averages the total number of orders per customer across everyone who has ordered at least once in the period. It measures how frequently customers come back to purchase.
how_to_read: The value is the average number of orders each customer has placed
Values above 1 indicate meaningful repeat purchasing
Rising averages signal improving customer loyalty and frequency
Read it with Repeat Rate to separate frequency from one-time buyers
Low averages suggest most customers are not coming back
decisions_answered: How often does a typical customer purchase?
Is purchase frequency improving over time?
Should we invest more in repeat-purchase and loyalty programs?

payload.formula_explanation: Analyzes how average order value correlates with customer lifetime value. Customers are segmented by their average order value, then CLV is calculated for each tier. This reveals whether high-AOV customers also have high lifetime value.
how_to_read: X-axis shows average order value tiers from lowest to highest
Bar height represents average CLV for customers in that AOV tier
Generally, higher AOV correlates with higher CLV
However, frequency matters too - high AOV with few orders may show lower CLV than moderate AOV with many orders
Click any bar to see the detailed customer breakdown
decisions_answered: Do high-AOV customers have higher lifetime value?
Is it better to attract high-AOV customers or increase order frequency?
Which AOV tier represents the sweet spot of value and volume?
Should we focus on upselling to increase AOV?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows how average order value relates to customer lifetime value.

Intuitively, you might expect high-AOV customers to have the highest CLV. But the relationship is nuanced. Yes, $200+ AOV customers have higher individual order values, but they may place fewer orders over time.

Sometimes the $25-49 tier shows strong CLV despite lower individual orders, because these customers purchase more frequently. Multiple smaller purchases can add up to more value than few large purchases.

The strategic question this answers: Should you focus on increasing order size or order frequency? Often, the answer is both - but if you must prioritize, frequency typically has higher impact because it indicates engagement and habit formation.

Consider your product mix: Are high-AOV customers buying premium items that need less frequent replacement?

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers by days since their last tracked activity (purchase, email open, site visit). More recent engagement correlates with lower churn risk.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each engagement recency level
Active and Engaged segments are your healthy, engaged customers
At Risk and Dormant need re-engagement campaigns
Churned customers (180+ days) may be permanently lost
The churn percentage on each bar shows that segment average
decisions_answered: How engaged is our customer base currently?
How many customers are slipping toward churn?
Which engagement segments need immediate campaigns?
Is our engagement distribution improving over time?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows customers segmented by engagement recency - a leading indicator of churn before it happens.

The formula groups customers by days since last activity: Active within 7 days, Engaged 8 to 30, At Risk 31 to 90, Dormant 91 to 180, and Churned over 180 days.

Reading this chart: Longer bars in Active and Engaged indicate a healthy, engaged database. Large At Risk or Dormant segments signal re-engagement opportunity.

The average churn probability shown for each segment demonstrates the correlation. Active customers typically show lower churn, while Churned customers approach 90 percent plus churn probability.

Key questions: How engaged is your base? How many are slipping away? Which segments need campaigns? Use this to size your re-engagement and win-back initiatives.

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies high-value customers at risk of churning based on RFM scores and predictive churn probability. These are customers who have purchased frequently and spent significantly but have not been active recently. They require immediate attention.
how_to_read: Table shows customers flagged as high churn risk, sorted by lifetime value
Days since last activity shows how long since their last purchase or engagement
Churn Risk percentage is Klaviyos predictive model output
RFM Score shows their R, F, M values concatenated (e.g., 155 = R1, F5, M5)
Red badges indicate critical churn risk (>80%)
Click a customer row to view their full profile and history
decisions_answered: Which high-value customers are most likely to churn?
How much revenue is at risk from churning customers?
Which customers should receive immediate personal outreach?
What is the common pattern among at-risk customers?
payload.narration_text:
This alert table highlights your highest-value customers who are at risk of churning.

The selection criteria: Customers who used to buy frequently with high order values - indicated by F and M scores of 4 or 5 - but have not purchased recently, shown by R scores of 1 or 2. We also include anyone with churn probability above 80%.

Reading the table: Customers are sorted by lifetime value by default - your most valuable at-risk customers appear first. The churn risk percentage comes from Klaviyos predictive analytics. Days since activity shows how long since they last engaged.

This is your action list: These customers represent significant revenue at risk. A $2,000 CLV customer with 90% churn risk needs immediate attention - perhaps a personal email, exclusive offer, or phone call. Export this list for your retention campaign.

payload.formula_explanation: Sums the net lifetime value of customers flagged High or Critical churn risk in MART_CUSTOMER_HEALTH, scoped to the acquisition cohort in the selected date range. It quantifies the lifetime value that could be lost if those high-risk customers churn.
how_to_read: The main value is total NET_LTV held by High- and Critical-risk customers
Risk levels are pre-computed in MART_CUSTOMER_HEALTH, not derived from a probability cutoff here
A large figure means significant revenue is concentrated in churn-prone customers
The date range filters by first_order_date (acquisition cohort)
Use this to size the financial stakes of your retention program
decisions_answered: How much customer value is at risk of churning?
Is the potential loss large enough to justify a retention investment?
Which acquisition cohorts carry the most at-risk value?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how customer lifetime value varies by when customers were acquired. Each cohort represents customers who first appeared in the system during that quarter. Comparing average CLV across cohorts reveals acquisition quality trends and how CLV matures over time.
how_to_read: X-axis shows acquisition quarter from Q3 2021 forward
Blue line tracks average total CLV per customer for each cohort
Green dashed line shows average historic (realized) CLV only
Older cohorts typically show higher CLV as they have had more time to mature
Declining CLV in recent cohorts may indicate lower acquisition quality
Large gaps between total and historic CLV indicate strong predicted future value
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring higher or lower quality customers over time?
How does CLV mature as cohorts age?
Which acquisition period brought our most valuable customers?
Should we investigate recent cohorts showing lower CLV?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value by acquisition cohort over time.

Each point represents customers who joined in that quarter. The blue line shows total CLV including predicted future value, while the green dashed line shows only historic realized value.

Older cohorts naturally show higher CLV because customers have had more time to make purchases. Q3 2021 cohorts might show the highest average CLV after having 4+ years to mature.

Watch for declining CLV trends in recent cohorts - this could indicate lower quality acquisitions from changed marketing strategies or channels. However, some decline is expected since newer customers haven't had time to mature.

Use this to answer: Is our customer acquisition quality improving or declining? When did we acquire our best customers, and can we replicate those conditions?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how average customer lifetime value varies by acquisition month. Total CLV combines historic realized value with predicted future value. Comparing these metrics across cohorts reveals acquisition quality trends and how CLV matures over customer tenure.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort acquisition month over the past 3 years
Y-axis shows average CLV in dollars
Blue solid line shows Total CLV (historic + predicted)
Green solid line shows Historic CLV (realized purchases only)
Purple dashed line shows Predicted CLV (expected future value)
Older cohorts naturally show higher historic CLV as they have had more time to purchase
The gap between historic and predicted narrows as cohorts mature
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring higher or lower quality customers over time?
How does CLV mature as cohorts age?
Which acquisition months/seasons produce our highest-value customers?
How much future value is predicted for recent cohorts?
Is there seasonality in customer acquisition quality?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value across monthly acquisition cohorts.

The blue line shows total CLV - the sum of what customers have already spent plus what they're predicted to spend. The green line shows just the historic, realized CLV. The gap between them represents predicted future value.

Older cohorts on the left will naturally show higher CLV - they've had more time to make purchases. But watch for trends: Are recent cohorts tracking above or below where older cohorts were at the same age? This reveals whether acquisition quality is improving or declining.

The spring months often show CLV peaks for gardening businesses - customers acquired during planting season tend to be more engaged and valuable. If you see dips, investigate what marketing channels or campaigns were active during those periods.

The predicted CLV line is particularly valuable for recent cohorts - it forecasts their expected value trajectory based on similar customer behavior patterns.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how average customer lifetime value varies by acquisition month. Total CLV combines historic realized value with predicted future value. Comparing these metrics across cohorts reveals acquisition quality trends and how CLV matures over customer tenure.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort acquisition month over the past 3 years
Y-axis shows average CLV in dollars
Blue solid line shows Total CLV (historic + predicted)
Green solid line shows Historic CLV (realized purchases only)
Purple dashed line shows Predicted CLV (expected future value)
Older cohorts naturally show higher historic CLV as they have had more time to purchase
The gap between historic and predicted narrows as cohorts mature
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring higher or lower quality customers over time?

Which acquisition months/seasons produce our highest-value customers?
How much future value is predicted for recent cohorts?
Is there seasonality in customer acquisition quality?

Older cohorts on the left will naturally show higher CLV - they've had more time to make purchases. But watch for trends: Are recent cohorts tracking above or below where older cohorts were at the same age? This reveals whether acquisition quality is improving or declining.

The spring months often show CLV peaks for gardening businesses - customers acquired during planting season tend to be more engaged and valuable. If you see dips, investigate what marketing channels or campaigns were active during those periods.

The predicted CLV line is particularly valuable for recent cohorts - it forecasts their expected value trajectory based on similar customer behavior patterns.

payload.formula_text: mean of daily AVG_RATING over the 7 days ending at date_to
payload.formula_explanation: Computed from the 7-day trend ending at date_to: the backend takes each day's average star rating and then averages those daily values (an unweighted mean of daily means). The comparison value is the same calculation over the previous period's 7-day window.
how_to_read: The main value is the average star rating (1-5) over the trailing 7 days
It is an average of daily averages, so each day counts equally regardless of volume
Days with no rated reviews simply do not contribute
The change is the point difference versus the prior 7-day window
Cross-check with Rating Distribution to see whether the average hides polarized ratings
decisions_answered: What rating are customers giving our products lately?
Has average satisfaction moved versus the prior period?
Is recent product sentiment healthy?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares customer quality metrics across different acquisition sources. The email marketing method field indicates how customers first subscribed (API, Magento, List Import, etc.), serving as a proxy for acquisition channel.
how_to_read: Each row represents an acquisition source/method
Customers column shows total acquired through that channel
Avg CLV reveals which sources bring highest-value customers
Total LTV shows absolute value contribution
Repeat Rate indicates loyalty potential from each source
Churn Risk highlights which sources produce at-risk customers
Table is sortable by any column - click headers to sort
decisions_answered: Which acquisition sources produce the highest-value customers?
Where should we invest more marketing budget?
Which sources have the best repeat purchase rates?
Are certain sources associated with higher churn risk?
What is the ROI potential of each acquisition channel?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your customer acquisition sources by quality and value metrics.

Each row represents a different method by which customers entered your database - API integrations, e-commerce platform imports, list uploads, and more. The metrics reveal which sources bring your best customers.

Sort by Average CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition sources. These deserve more investment. But don't ignore volume - a source with slightly lower average CLV but 10x the customers might generate more total value.

Repeat Rate is a key quality indicator. Sources with high repeat rates produce engaged customers who come back. Low repeat rates might indicate promotional-driven acquisitions who bought once for a discount and never returned.

The Churn Risk column is a warning signal. High churn risk sources might need different lifecycle marketing approaches, or they might not be worth the acquisition investment at all.

Downward trends suggest improving retention - your efforts are working
Sudden spikes may indicate external events or campaign issues
Hover over any point to see the exact week and churn percentage
Click a point to analyze that specific week in detail

Are there seasonal patterns in churn risk?

A downward trend is positive - your retention efforts are working. Keep doing what's effective.

Look for sudden spikes or drops. These often correlate with specific events - a major sale, a new competitor, or changes in your product or service.

Use this chart to measure the impact of retention initiatives. If you launched a win-back campaign, did the average churn probability decrease in following weeks?

payload.formula_explanation: Groups customers by their historical order count and calculates average churn probability for each group. This reveals how purchase frequency correlates with churn risk - typically, customers with more orders have lower churn probability.
how_to_read: Each bar shows average churn probability for customers with that order count
Longer bars indicate higher churn risk for that customer segment
One-time buyers typically show the highest churn risk
As order count increases, churn probability usually decreases
Labels show exact churn percentage for precision
Click any bar to filter and explore customers in that order bucket
decisions_answered: How does purchase frequency correlate with churn risk?
What is the churn risk for one-time buyers vs. repeat customers?
At what order count does churn risk significantly decrease?
Should we focus retention efforts on converting one-time buyers?
payload.narration_text:
This chart reveals how order count correlates with churn risk.

One-time buyers almost always show the highest churn probability - often 80% or higher. They've tried your product once but haven't committed. This is your biggest opportunity.

Watch how churn drops with each additional order. The second purchase is the critical hurdle. Once customers buy a third, fourth, or fifth time, they become significantly more loyal.

Customers with 10+ orders are your advocates. Their churn risk is low because they've proven their loyalty through behavior.

The insight here is clear: focus your retention budget on converting one-time buyers to second-time buyers. That's where you get the biggest reduction in churn risk per dollar spent.

payload.formula_text: AVG(CHURN_RISK_SCORE) FROM MART_CUSTOMER_HEALTH JOIN MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY (existence filter: first_order_date <= date_to)
payload.formula_explanation: Averages the per-customer churn-risk score (0-100 scale) across all customers who existed by the end of the period. It is the headline gauge of how likely the customer base is to churn.
how_to_read: The value is the average churn probability across all customers
Higher percentages mean a greater portion of customers are at risk
Track the trend to see whether retention efforts are working
Read it alongside High-Risk Customers to see how the average is distributed
Spikes warrant investigating recent acquisition or engagement changes
decisions_answered: How likely is our overall customer base to churn?
Is average churn risk improving or worsening over time?
Are our retention initiatives moving the needle?

payload.formula_explanation: The mean churn-risk score across all purchasing customers, read directly from MART_CUSTOMER_RFM.CHURN_RISK_SCORE (a 0-100 score). A higher average means the base as a whole is more at risk of lapsing.
how_to_read: The main value is the average churn-risk score on a 0-100 scale, shown as a percentage
Higher values indicate a more at-risk customer base overall
The score is pre-computed in the RFM mart, not derived on the fly
Track across periods - a rising average is an early warning of base erosion
Pair with the At-Risk Customers table to act on the highest-value risks
decisions_answered: How at-risk is our customer base on average?
Is overall churn risk trending up or down?
Do we need a broader retention push or just targeted outreach?

Repeat Rate is a key quality indicator. Sources with high repeat rates produce engaged customers who come back. Low repeat rates might indicate promotional-driven acquisitions who bought once for a discount and never returned.
