payload.formula_explanation: Shows which landing pages receive the most traffic and drive the most value. Helps identify high-performing pages to replicate and underperforming pages that need optimization or elimination.
how_to_read: Each row shows a landing page with key performance metrics
Sort by Page Views to see highest traffic pages
Sort by Revenue to see pages driving the most value
High New Users indicates effective acquisition pages
Avg Engagement shows page quality and relevance
Use search to find specific pages or page categories
decisions_answered: Which landing pages receive the most traffic?
What pages drive the most revenue attribution?
Which pages have the best engagement metrics?
Where should we focus landing page optimization efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your top landing pages by traffic and performance - essential for understanding where customers enter your site and how well those entry points convert.

The formula aggregates page views, new users, revenue, and engagement time for each page path from GA4. This shows which pages serve as effective entry points.

Reading this table: Sort by Page Views for traffic volume, by Revenue for value generation. Home page often leads traffic, but product and category pages may drive more revenue per view.

Look for mismatches. A page with high traffic but low revenue may need conversion optimization. A page with great revenue per view but low traffic deserves more promotion.

Key questions: Which pages get most traffic? What drives most revenue? Where should optimization focus? Use the search to analyze specific page categories like product pages or blog posts.

payload.formula_explanation: Health score is the inverse of churn probability. A 20% health score means 80% churn probability. Lower scores indicate higher churn risk across your customer base.
how_to_read: The percentage shows average health across all scored customers
Green indicates healthy scores (50%+), red indicates concern (<30%)
Compare to industry benchmarks: 50%+ is healthy, 30-50% needs attention
Trend shows whether overall customer health is improving or declining
Low scores indicate urgent need for retention campaigns
decisions_answered: What is the overall health of our customer base?
Should we prioritize retention over acquisition right now?
Is our customer health improving or declining over time?
Do we have a systemic customer satisfaction problem?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI tracks your average customer health score - a critical indicator of retention risk across your entire customer base.

The formula inverts Klaviyo's churn probability. A health score of 20 percent means customers have an 80 percent average churn probability. Lower health scores signal higher risk.

Reading this card: The color coding provides quick assessment. Green above 50 percent is healthy. Yellow between 30 and 50 needs attention. Red below 30 percent requires urgent action.

For seed businesses, health scores may appear lower due to seasonal purchase patterns and long buying cycles. Industry context matters when interpreting this metric.

Key questions answered: How healthy is your customer base? Should retention be prioritized? Is health trending better or worse? This guides your overall retention strategy.

Each row represents a different method by which customers entered your database - API integrations, e-commerce platform imports, list uploads, and more. The metrics reveal which sources bring your best customers.

Sort by Average CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition sources. These deserve more investment. But don't ignore volume - a source with slightly lower average CLV but 10x the customers might generate more total value.

Repeat Rate is a key quality indicator. Sources with high repeat rates produce engaged customers who come back. Low repeat rates might indicate promotional-driven acquisitions who bought once for a discount and never returned.

The Churn Risk column is a warning signal. High churn risk sources might need different lifecycle marketing approaches, or they might not be worth the acquisition investment at all.

payload.formula_explanation: Lists products with the most reviews and their average ratings. High review counts with high ratings indicate successful products, while high review counts with low ratings signal products needing attention.
how_to_read: Products are sorted by total review count (most reviewed first)
The Reviews column shows total review volume
Avg Rating shows the mean star rating (out of 5)
Positive % shows the proportion of 4-5 star reviews
Green ratings are excellent (4+), yellow is average (3-4), red is poor (<3)
decisions_answered: Which products are getting the most customer feedback?
Are our best-selling products also our best-rated?
Which products need quality improvement based on ratings?
Should we feature highly-rated products more prominently?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your most-reviewed products and their ratings.

High review count combined with high ratings - that's your star performer. Feature these products prominently and study what makes them successful.

High review count with low ratings is a red flag. These products sell but disappoint. Investigate quality issues, unclear product descriptions, or mismatched expectations.

Low review count with high ratings might be hidden gems. Consider promoting these products to increase visibility.

The Positive Percentage column quickly shows sentiment - above 80% is excellent, below 60% needs attention. Focus improvement efforts on products with high volume but low positive percentages for maximum impact.

payload.formula_explanation: Churn probability comes from Klaviyo predictive analytics model. Customers are grouped into risk tiers, and the total CLV at risk is calculated for each tier. Critical risk customers have >75% probability of churning and require immediate intervention.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show CLV at risk for each churn tier
Green (Low Risk) customers are stable - focus on maintaining their engagement
Yellow (Medium Risk) shows customers worth monitoring
Orange (High Risk) indicates customers needing proactive retention
Red (Critical Risk) customers require immediate attention - highest value at stake
Dollar amounts on bars show total CLV that could be lost if customers churn
decisions_answered: How much customer value is at risk of churning?
Which risk tier requires the most urgent attention?
What is the potential revenue impact if we dont act on churn?
Should we invest more in retention or acquisition?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows customer lifetime value at risk across different churn probability tiers.

The red Critical Risk bar is the most concerning - it represents customers with greater than 75% probability of churning. In many businesses, this can represent $60M or more in potential lost value from nearly 500,000 customers.

However, not all at-risk customers are worth the same retention investment. Notice the Low Risk segment has fewer customers but higher average CLV - these stable, valuable customers are worth protecting proactively.

The key insight is prioritization: Focus retention campaigns on high-CLV customers within Critical and High Risk tiers first. A customer with $500 CLV at critical risk is more valuable to save than ten $20 CLV customers.

This answers the critical question: How much is at stake if we do nothing about churn?

payload.formula_text: Period Channel Spend / Total New Customers in Period
payload.formula_explanation: Customer Acquisition Cost is calculated by dividing total ad spend on each platform by the number of new users attributed to CPC (cost-per-click) traffic from that platform in GA4.
how_to_read: Each line tracks a platform's CAC over 90 days
Blue line is Google Ads, purple/navy is Meta Ads
Lower position means cheaper customer acquisition
Rising trend indicates declining acquisition efficiency
Compare lines to see relative platform efficiency
decisions_answered: What is our cost to acquire a customer by platform?
Is CAC trending up or down over time?
Which platform acquires customers more cost-effectively?
When did significant CAC changes occur?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks Customer Acquisition Cost by platform over 90 days.

CAC is calculated by dividing platform ad spend by the number of new users from CPC traffic. Lower is better - it means cheaper customer acquisition.

The blue line tracks Google Ads, the navy/purple line tracks Meta Ads. Compare their positions: the lower line is more efficient.

Watch for trends. Rising CAC means declining efficiency. Spikes warrant investigation. This helps answer: What does a customer cost per platform? Is CAC improving? And which platform is more cost-effective?

payload.formula_explanation: CAC Efficiency divides the average first-order value by the blended cost to acquire a customer. A ratio above 1 means a customer's first purchase already covers more than what it cost to acquire them; below 1 means the first order does not yet pay back acquisition spend.
how_to_read: The value is a ratio of first-purchase value to acquisition cost
Above 1.0 means the first order alone recoups acquisition spend
Below 1.0 means you rely on repeat purchases to become profitable
Higher is better - it reflects healthier upfront acquisition economics
Track alongside LTV:CAC for the full payback picture
decisions_answered: Does a customer's first purchase pay back what we spent to acquire them?
How quickly do we recoup acquisition cost?
Is acquisition efficiency improving or eroding?
Are we over-spending relative to the value new customers bring in?

payload.formula_explanation: Lists campaigns ranked by new user volume, with revenue and calculated Average Order Value per campaign to show both quantity and quality of acquisition.
how_to_read: Table is sorted by New Users column by default (highest first)
Green AOV values indicate strong first-purchase value (>$40)
Yellow AOV values are acceptable ($35-$40)
Red AOV values need attention (<$35)
Click any row to view detailed campaign performance
decisions_answered: Which campaigns drive the most new customer volume?
Which campaigns acquire high-value first-time buyers?
Are high-volume campaigns also generating good AOV?
Which campaigns need optimization or should be scaled?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks campaigns by new user acquisition volume over the last 30 days.

For each campaign, you see new users, revenue, and AOV - which is revenue divided by new users. AOV is color-coded: green means strong value over 40 dollars, yellow is acceptable between 35 and 40, red needs attention below 35.

Sort by any column to re-rank. Click a row for campaign details.

Use this to answer: Which campaigns drive volume? Which attract high-value customers? And where should you scale up or optimize?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows total CLV distributed across risk tiers and CLV buckets. Larger cells represent more revenue. Color indicates risk level. Helps identify high-value at-risk segments.
how_to_read: Cell size represents total CLV in that segment
Cell color indicates risk level (green = low, red = critical)
Large red cells are your highest priority - high CLV at high risk
The treemap helps visualize where revenue concentration meets risk
Click any cell to filter and view those customers
decisions_answered: Where is high-value CLV concentrated by risk level?
Which high-CLV segments need urgent retention attention?
Is our at-risk CLV concentrated or distributed?
What is the revenue exposure by risk tier?
payload.narration_text:
This treemap visualizes customer lifetime value distributed across risk tiers and value buckets - essential for prioritizing retention by revenue impact.

The formula creates cells sized by total CLV for each risk tier and CLV bucket combination. Colors range from green for low risk to red for critical risk.

Reading this treemap: Focus on large cells with warm colors. A large red cell means significant CLV at critical risk - your highest retention priority.

Small cells are less urgent regardless of color. Even critical risk customers with low CLV may not justify intensive intervention. Prioritize by cell size and color together.

Key questions: Where is high-value CLV at risk? Which segments need urgent attention? How is exposure distributed? Use this to focus retention resources where revenue impact is greatest.

payload.formula_text: current avg - previous avg (rounded to 2 dp)
payload.formula_explanation: Computed from the 7-day trend ending at date_to: the backend takes each day's average star rating and then averages those daily values (an unweighted mean of daily means). The comparison value is the same calculation over the previous period's 7-day window.
how_to_read: The main value is the average star rating (1-5) over the trailing 7 days
It is an average of daily averages, so each day counts equally regardless of volume
Days with no rated reviews simply do not contribute
The change is the point difference versus the prior 7-day window
Cross-check with Rating Distribution to see whether the average hides polarized ratings
decisions_answered: What rating are customers giving our products lately?
Has average satisfaction moved versus the prior period?
Is recent product sentiment healthy?

payload.formula_explanation: Lists the highest-value customers (up to 100) ranked by realized lifetime value (ACTUAL_LTV) from MART_CUSTOMER_LTV, joined to MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY for order history and churn score. Each row shows CLV, orders, AOV, and a churn-risk band derived from the customer's CHURN_RISK_SCORE, enabling VIP management and proactive retention.
how_to_read: Customers are ranked by total CLV, highest value first (top 100)
Orders and AOV reveal the purchase pattern behind each customer's value
Churn Risk Level is banded from CHURN_RISK_SCORE (Critical >=80 down to Minimal <20)
Emails are masked for privacy when a full name is unavailable
Customer Since shows tenure from first_order_date - the date range filters this acquisition window
decisions_answered: Who are our most valuable customers?
Are any high-value customers at risk of churning?
What purchase characteristics define our best customers?
Which VIP customers warrant personal outreach?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares customer quality metrics across different acquisition sources. The email marketing method field indicates how customers first subscribed (API, Magento, List Import, etc.), serving as a proxy for acquisition channel.
how_to_read: Each row represents an acquisition source/method
Customers column shows total acquired through that channel
Avg CLV reveals which sources bring highest-value customers
Total LTV shows absolute value contribution
Repeat Rate indicates loyalty potential from each source
Churn Risk highlights which sources produce at-risk customers
Table is sortable by any column - click headers to sort
decisions_answered: Which acquisition sources produce the highest-value customers?
Where should we invest more marketing budget?
Which sources have the best repeat purchase rates?
Are certain sources associated with higher churn risk?
What is the ROI potential of each acquisition channel?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your customer acquisition sources by quality and value metrics.

Each row represents a different method by which customers entered your database - API integrations, e-commerce platform imports, list uploads, and more. The metrics reveal which sources bring your best customers.

Sort by Average CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition sources. These deserve more investment. But don't ignore volume - a source with slightly lower average CLV but 10x the customers might generate more total value.

Repeat Rate is a key quality indicator. Sources with high repeat rates produce engaged customers who come back. Low repeat rates might indicate promotional-driven acquisitions who bought once for a discount and never returned.

The Churn Risk column is a warning signal. High churn risk sources might need different lifecycle marketing approaches, or they might not be worth the acquisition investment at all.

payload.formula_text: pre-computed CHURN_RISK_LEVEL (Minimal | Low | Medium | High | Critical) from MART_CUSTOMER_HEALTH
payload.formula_explanation: Churn probability comes from Klaviyo predictive analytics model. Customers are grouped into risk tiers, and the total CLV at risk is calculated for each tier. Critical risk customers have >75% probability of churning and require immediate intervention.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show CLV at risk for each churn tier
Green (Low Risk) customers are stable - focus on maintaining their engagement
Yellow (Medium Risk) shows customers worth monitoring
Orange (High Risk) indicates customers needing proactive retention
Red (Critical Risk) customers require immediate attention - highest value at stake
Dollar amounts on bars show total CLV that could be lost if customers churn
decisions_answered: How much customer value is at risk of churning?
Which risk tier requires the most urgent attention?
What is the potential revenue impact if we dont act on churn?
Should we invest more in retention or acquisition?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows customer lifetime value at risk across different churn probability tiers.

The red Critical Risk bar is the most concerning - it represents customers with greater than 75% probability of churning. In many businesses, this can represent $60M or more in potential lost value from nearly 500,000 customers.

However, not all at-risk customers are worth the same retention investment. Notice the Low Risk segment has fewer customers but higher average CLV - these stable, valuable customers are worth protecting proactively.

The key insight is prioritization: Focus retention campaigns on high-CLV customers within Critical and High Risk tiers first. A customer with $500 CLV at critical risk is more valuable to save than ten $20 CLV customers.

This answers the critical question: How much is at stake if we do nothing about churn?

payload.formula_text: GROUP BY CHURN_RISK_LEVEL (mart-assigned: Minimal / Low / Medium / High / Critical)
payload.formula_explanation: Customers are classified into churn risk tiers using Klaviyo predictive analytics. This stacked area chart shows how churn risk distributes across cohorts, helping identify at-risk customer groups and prioritize retention efforts.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort quarter
Y-axis shows percentage of cohort (100% stacked)
Green area (Low Risk) represents stable, engaged customers
Yellow (Medium Risk) shows customers worth monitoring
Orange (High Risk) indicates proactive retention needed
Red (Critical Risk) requires immediate intervention
Healthy cohorts should have more green area
decisions_answered: How is churn risk distributed across different cohorts?
Which cohorts have the highest proportion of at-risk customers?
Is churn risk improving or worsening over time?
What is the total value at risk in each cohort?
Where should we focus retention marketing efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows the churn risk composition of each quarterly cohort.

The colors represent risk tiers from Klaviyo predictive analytics. Green at the bottom represents low-risk customers - those with less than 25% churn probability. As you move up through yellow, orange, and red, churn probability increases.

Older cohorts naturally show different risk profiles than recent ones. Watch for patterns: Do Q1 spring cohorts consistently show lower churn risk? This might indicate that gardening season acquisitions produce more committed customers.

The critical insight is intervention timing. If you see a cohort's red area growing, these customers need immediate win-back campaigns before they're lost entirely. The cost of retention is almost always lower than the cost of acquiring replacement customers.

Target the high-value customers within critical risk tiers first - not all churning customers are equally worth saving.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers by days since their last tracked activity (purchase, email open, site visit). More recent engagement correlates with lower churn risk.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each engagement recency level
Active and Engaged segments are your healthy, engaged customers
At Risk and Dormant need re-engagement campaigns
Churned customers (180+ days) may be permanently lost
The churn percentage on each bar shows that segment average
decisions_answered: How engaged is our customer base currently?
How many customers are slipping toward churn?
Which engagement segments need immediate campaigns?
Is our engagement distribution improving over time?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows customers segmented by engagement recency - a leading indicator of churn before it happens.

The formula groups customers by days since last activity: Active within 7 days, Engaged 8 to 30, At Risk 31 to 90, Dormant 91 to 180, and Churned over 180 days.

Reading this chart: Longer bars in Active and Engaged indicate a healthy, engaged database. Large At Risk or Dormant segments signal re-engagement opportunity.

The average churn probability shown for each segment demonstrates the correlation. Active customers typically show lower churn, while Churned customers approach 90 percent plus churn probability.

Key questions: How engaged is your base? How many are slipping away? Which segments need campaigns? Use this to size your re-engagement and win-back initiatives.

payload.formula_explanation: Sums the lifetime value of every customer flagged High or Critical churn risk. It quantifies the revenue exposure tied up in customers most likely to leave.
how_to_read: The main value is the total CLV held by high- and critical-risk customers
The percentage shows what share of total CLV is at risk
Large values mean retention has a high financial payoff
Compare against total portfolio CLV to size the exposure
Use it to justify and prioritize retention spend
decisions_answered: How much customer value is exposed to churn?
What share of total CLV sits with high-risk customers?
How much could we save by retaining at-risk customers?

payload.formula_text: All customers whose first purchase was in a given month
payload.formula_explanation: Groups customers by the month of their first purchase (cohort) and measures what percentage made a repeat purchase. Higher retention rates indicate more effective customer acquisition and product-market fit.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a monthly cohort of first-time customers
The percentage shows what portion of that cohort made repeat purchases
Higher bars indicate better retention for that acquisition month
Compare recent cohorts to historical ones to spot trend changes
Labels display exact retention percentages for precision
decisions_answered: Which acquisition months produced the most loyal customers?
Is our customer quality improving or declining over time?
Did specific marketing campaigns drive better retention?
What is our baseline retention rate across cohorts?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows retention rates by customer acquisition cohort.

Each bar represents customers who made their first purchase in a specific month. The height shows what percentage came back for a second purchase.

Strong cohorts have high retention - 40% or more is excellent for most e-commerce. Weak cohorts under 25% signal problems with that month's acquisition quality or product experience.

Compare cohorts over time. If recent cohorts have lower retention, investigate. Was there a marketing change? Product issue? This helps you understand not just if customers are staying, but when they were acquired matters.

payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive table showing detailed metrics for each monthly cohort. Enables comparison of acquisition quality, customer development, and lifetime value across time periods.
how_to_read: Each row is a monthly cohort, newest at top
Cohort Size shows customers acquired that month
Avg CLV and Total LTV show value metrics
Churn Risk is color-coded (red = high risk)
Converted/Repeat/VIP show customer development counts
Q1 months (Jan-Mar) often highlighted as spring season
Sort by any column to rank cohorts
decisions_answered: Which monthly cohorts perform best across all metrics?
How do spring cohorts compare to other seasons?
Which cohorts have the highest/lowest churn risk?
What is the VIP conversion rate for each cohort?
Are recent cohorts on track compared to historical benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This comprehensive table provides detailed metrics for every monthly cohort in your database.

Each row represents customers acquired in that specific month. You can see cohort size, average and total CLV, order metrics, and customer development progression from converted to repeat to VIP.

The Churn Risk column is color-coded for quick identification. Red indicates cohorts with elevated churn probability that may need immediate retention attention. Green indicates healthy, stable cohorts.

Q1 rows (January through March) are often highlighted because spring is critical for gardening businesses. Compare Q1 cohorts across years to see if acquisition quality is improving.

Use the sorting capability to rank cohorts by any metric. Sort by Avg CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition months. Sort by VIP count to find cohorts that produced the most high-value customers.

This table is your cohort analysis command center - it aggregates everything you need to evaluate customer acquisition and development performance.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows customer concentration by US state, ranked by customer count. Geographic analysis reveals regional strengths and opportunities for targeted marketing or expansion.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count per state, ranked highest to lowest
Virginia leads due to headquarters location and regional strength
California and Texas show population-driven large customer bases
Look for states with high LTV relative to customer count
Click any state to filter and analyze regional performance
decisions_answered: Where are our customers concentrated geographically?
Which states offer expansion opportunities?
Should we invest in regional marketing campaigns?
Do certain regions show higher customer quality?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows your customer distribution across US states - revealing where your brand has the strongest presence.

The formula counts customers per state and calculates average LTV by region. States are ranked by total customer count to highlight concentration.

Reading this chart: Longer bars indicate more customers in that state. Virginia leads, likely due to headquarters proximity and regional marketing. California and Texas show high counts driven by large populations.

The opportunity: Look beyond raw counts. Some states may have fewer customers but higher average LTV. These represent quality expansion opportunities with proven customer value.

Key questions: Where are customers concentrated? Which states offer growth potential? Should you run regional campaigns? Click any state to explore that region's detailed performance.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the volume of customers acquired each month and what percentage converted to making at least one purchase. This reveals acquisition volume trends and the effectiveness of converting acquired customers into buyers.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort acquisition month
Light blue bars show total new customers acquired that month
Dark blue bars show customers who converted to purchasers
Orange line (secondary axis) shows conversion rate percentage
Tall bars indicate high acquisition volume periods
Bar gap indicates customers who never made a first purchase
Seasonal patterns often visible with Q1 peaks
decisions_answered: How many customers are we acquiring each month?
What percentage of acquired customers make their first purchase?
Are we scaling acquisition while maintaining conversion quality?
Which months show the highest acquisition and conversion?
Is there seasonality in our customer acquisition patterns?
payload.narration_text:
This combo chart shows customer acquisition volume alongside conversion effectiveness.

The light blue bars represent total customers who entered the database each month. The dark blue bars show how many of those actually made a purchase - converting from prospects to customers. The gap between them represents unconverted database entries.

The orange line tracking conversion rate is crucial. You want both high volume AND high conversion. If volume spikes but conversion drops, you may be acquiring lower-quality leads. Q1 typically shows both higher volume and higher conversion for gardening businesses due to seasonal demand.

Compare year-over-year patterns: Are you acquiring more customers than last year's same month? Is your conversion rate improving? These metrics directly tie to marketing efficiency and customer acquisition cost effectiveness.

payload.formula_explanation: Surfaces campaign-level engagement and conversion metrics from the campaign performance mart, labelled with platform. Clicks stand in for sessions and CTR for engagement rate, giving a paid-campaign view of how each campaign contributes to the customer journey and revenue.
how_to_read: Each row is a campaign (with its platform) ranked by revenue (top 20)
Sessions here are campaign clicks; Engagement Rate is the campaign CTR
Sort by Revenue to find the top revenue-driving campaigns
Conversion Rate shows how well a campaign's clicks turn into conversions
Revenue Per Session highlights campaigns delivering high value per click
Compare engagement rate against conversion rate to spot campaigns that attract clicks but fail to convert
decisions_answered: Which campaigns drive the most revenue and conversions?
Which campaigns engage users but fail to convert them?
How does campaign performance compare across platforms?
Where should campaign budget be scaled up or cut?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts customers with 80% or higher predicted churn probability. These customers are most likely to stop purchasing without intervention.
how_to_read: The main value shows customers at critical churn risk (80%+ probability)
The percentage indicates what share of scored customers are critical
Higher counts require more aggressive retention campaigns
Compare to your retention campaign capacity to prioritize

payload.narration_text:
This KPI highlights customers at critical churn risk - those with 80 percent or higher probability of churning without intervention.

The formula counts all customers whose Klaviyo predictive score exceeds the 80 percent threshold. This represents your most urgent retention priority.

Reading this card: The count shows immediate action needed. The percentage reveals what share of your database is at critical risk. High percentages indicate systemic issues.

These customers should receive priority outreach: personalized offers, check-in emails, or special attention from customer service. Every day without action increases churn likelihood.

Key questions: How many need immediate intervention? Is the volume manageable? Should you trigger emergency campaigns? Use this to size your retention response.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks new customer acquisition over 24 months with a running cumulative total. The area shows monthly additions while the cumulative line shows total database growth over time.
how_to_read: The shaded area shows new customers added each month
Peaks indicate successful acquisition periods or campaigns
Look for seasonal patterns - Q1 peaks for gardening businesses
Consistent baseline growth indicates healthy organic acquisition
Click any month to analyze that cohort in detail
decisions_answered: How is our customer base growing over time?
Are there seasonal acquisition patterns we should plan around?
Which months produced the most new customers?
Is year-over-year growth accelerating or slowing?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart shows your customer growth trend over 24 months - revealing both patterns and overall trajectory.

The formula counts new customers by month, then calculates a running cumulative total. Year-over-year comparisons show growth acceleration or deceleration.

Reading this chart: The shaded area represents monthly new customer acquisitions. Peaks indicate strong acquisition periods, often tied to campaigns or seasonality. For gardening, expect Q1 spring peaks.

The overall trend matters most. Consistent upward slope means healthy growth. Flattening suggests market saturation or competitive pressure. Declining trends require immediate attention.

Key questions: How is the customer base growing? What seasonal patterns exist? Is year-over-year growth accelerating? Click any month to analyze that acquisition cohort in detail.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers into five risk tiers based on Klaviyo predictive churn probability scores. Each tier represents a 20% probability range, with High Risk (80-100%) being customers most likely to churn.
how_to_read: The donut chart shows the proportion of customers in each risk segment
Red/orange slices indicate high churn probability - these customers need immediate attention
Yellow indicates medium risk - worth monitoring and proactive engagement
Green slices show low risk - your loyal, engaged customer base
Center number shows total customers with churn predictions
Click any segment to filter and view customers in that risk tier
decisions_answered: How is our customer base distributed across churn risk levels?
What percentage of customers are at high risk of churning?
Is our overall churn risk profile improving or worsening?
Which risk segment should we prioritize for retention campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customer base segmented by predicted churn probability from Klaviyo analytics.

Red and orange represent your danger zone - customers with 60% or higher probability of churning. These need immediate intervention with win-back campaigns or personal outreach.

Yellow is your watch list at 40-60% - they could go either way. Proactive engagement can tip them toward staying.

Green shows your loyal base under 40% churn probability. These customers are engaged and likely to continue purchasing.

The key insight is the proportions. If more than half your customers are in red or orange, you have a serious retention problem that will impact future revenue.

payload.formula_text: COUNT(*) FROM MART_CUSTOMER_HEALTH WHERE HEALTH_SCORE IS NOT NULL
payload.formula_explanation: Counts how many customers have a computed health score (i.e. a usable churn-risk prediction). This is the scored population that every other health metric on this dashboard is calculated against.
how_to_read: The main value is the number of customers who currently have a health score
A larger scored population means the dashboard's risk and CLV metrics are more representative
Compare against your total customer base to gauge prediction coverage
A sudden drop can signal a data-pipeline or scoring-model gap
Use the sparkline to see how scored coverage has trended month over month
decisions_answered: How many customers do we have a churn-risk score for?
Is our predictive coverage broad enough to trust the other health metrics?
Has scoring coverage changed recently, hinting at a data issue?

payload.formula_explanation: Computed from the 7-day trend ending at date_to: the backend takes each day's average star rating and then averages those daily values (an unweighted mean of daily means). The comparison value is the same calculation over the previous period's 7-day window.
how_to_read: The main value is the average star rating (1-5) over the trailing 7 days
It is an average of daily averages, so each day counts equally regardless of volume
Days with no rated reviews simply do not contribute
The change is the point difference versus the prior 7-day window
Cross-check with Rating Distribution to see whether the average hides polarized ratings
decisions_answered: What rating are customers giving our products lately?
Has average satisfaction moved versus the prior period?
Is recent product sentiment healthy?

payload.formula_text: SUM(SESSIONS) from MART_TRAFFIC_CHANNEL_DAILY GROUP BY period, CHANNEL_GROUPING
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks daily revenue contribution from each major channel as a stacked area chart. Shows how channel mix evolves over time and reveals seasonal patterns, campaign impacts, and channel interdependencies.
how_to_read: Each colored area represents one marketing channel stacked on others
The total height shows combined daily revenue across all channels
Watch for channel proportions changing over time
Spikes may indicate successful campaigns or seasonal events
Consistent patterns suggest stable channel performance
Click to isolate individual channel series for closer analysis
decisions_answered: How is our channel mix evolving over time?
Are any channels growing or declining in contribution?
What impact do campaigns have on channel performance?
Are there seasonal patterns in channel effectiveness?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows how channel revenue contributions evolve over time - revealing trends, patterns, and campaign impacts.

The formula sums daily revenue for each channel, then stacks them to show both individual channel performance and total revenue trajectory.

Reading this chart: Each colored layer represents a channel. The total height shows combined revenue. Watch how layer proportions shift over time - growing layers indicate channels gaining importance.

Look for spikes that coincide with campaigns. A spike in Paid Search might show a Google Ads campaign working. A jump in Email indicates a successful send. Dips might signal issues needing investigation.

Key questions: How is channel mix evolving? Are channels growing or shrinking? What patterns exist? Click any channel in the legend to isolate its trend line.

payload.formula_text: ROUND(AVG(DATEDIFF(day, FIRST_ORDER_DATE, LAST_ORDER_DATE)), 0) from MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY WHERE TOTAL_ORDERS > 0
payload.formula_explanation: This card reports average customer lifespan in days - the span between a customer's first and last order, averaged across the base. Despite the label, the backend computes lifespan (first-to-last order gap), not the gap between consecutive orders. Lower values indicate customers churn sooner; longer spans indicate sustained relationships.
how_to_read: The main value is the average number of days between a customer's first and last order
It represents active customer lifespan, not the interval between individual purchases
Longer spans indicate customers stay engaged over a longer period
A short span warns that customers lapse quickly after acquisition
Use it to time win-back and re-engagement campaigns
decisions_answered: How long do customers typically stay active with us?
Are customers lapsing soon after their first purchase?
When should re-engagement campaigns fire to extend the relationship?
Is our retention window expanding or shrinking?

payload.formula_explanation: Classifies customers based on recency of their last engagement with your brand. Active customers engaged within 30 days are healthy. Beyond 90 days, customers are considered lapsing or churned, requiring re-engagement efforts.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a lifecycle stage based on days since last activity
Green (Active) shows customers engaged in the last 30 days - your healthy core
Yellow/Orange (At Risk, Lapsing) shows customers drifting away - intervene now
Red (Lapsed, Churned) shows customers who have disengaged - need win-back campaigns
Bar length indicates customer count in each stage
Click any bar to filter and see customers in that lifecycle stage
decisions_answered: What is the lifecycle distribution of our customer base?
How many customers are actively engaged vs. at risk of churning?
Which lifecycle stage has the most CLV at risk?
When should we trigger win-back campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel chart shows your customers distributed across lifecycle stages based on recency of engagement.

Active customers - those engaged in the last 30 days - are your healthy core. Keep them engaged with regular communication.

At Risk and Lapsing stages - 31 to 90 days inactive - are your intervention window. These customers haven't churned yet, but they're drifting. A well-timed email or offer can bring them back.

Lapsed and Churned - beyond 90 days - are harder to recover. They need aggressive win-back campaigns, and expect lower response rates.

The shape of this funnel tells a story. A healthy business has most customers in Active, with progressively fewer in each subsequent stage. If you see bulging in Lapsed or Churned, you have a retention problem.

payload.formula_explanation: Net Promoter Score (NPS) segments survey respondents into three categories based on their likelihood to recommend. Promoters (9-10) are loyal enthusiasts, Passives (7-8) are satisfied but unenthusiastic, and Detractors (0-6) are unhappy customers who can damage your brand through negative word-of-mouth.
how_to_read: The green slice shows Promoters (scores 9-10) - your most loyal customers
The yellow/amber slice shows Passives (scores 7-8) - satisfied but not enthusiastic
The red slice shows Detractors (scores 0-6) - unhappy customers at risk of churning
The center number shows total survey responses in the period
Percentages in the legend show the proportion of each segment
decisions_answered: What is the overall distribution of customer sentiment?
Are we building a base of loyal brand advocates (promoters)?
How many customers are at risk of negative word-of-mouth (detractors)?
Should we focus on converting passives to promoters or reducing detractors?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your NPS survey respondents break down into three key segments.

The green slice represents your Promoters - customers who scored 9 or 10. These are your advocates who actively recommend your brand.

The amber slice shows Passives - scores of 7 or 8. They're satisfied but not enthusiastic, and could easily switch to competitors.

The red slice represents Detractors - scores from 0 to 6. These unhappy customers can damage your brand through negative reviews and word-of-mouth.

A healthy distribution has a large green slice and small red slice. If detractors are growing, investigate why. If promoters dominate, leverage them for referrals and testimonials.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution and performance across mobile, desktop, and tablet devices. Helps identify device-specific experience issues and opportunities for optimization by revealing where users engage but may not convert.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportional user distribution by device
Blue represents Mobile - typically the largest segment
Green represents Desktop - often highest conversion
Orange represents Tablet - usually smallest segment
Center shows total users across all devices
Hover to see revenue and engagement rate by device
decisions_answered: What is our device mix for website traffic?
Which devices show highest engagement and conversion?
Is our mobile experience converting as well as desktop?
Do we need device-specific UX optimizations?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how users distribute across mobile, desktop, and tablet devices - critical for understanding where to invest in UX optimization.

The formula sums users by device category from GA4, along with revenue and engagement metrics for each device type.

Reading this chart: Mobile typically dominates user volume - most browsing happens on phones. Desktop often shows higher conversion rates due to easier checkout. Tablet is usually the smallest segment.

The key insight is comparing device proportions for users vs revenue. If mobile is 70 percent of users but only 40 percent of revenue, there is a mobile conversion opportunity worth investigating.

Key questions: What's our device mix? Do conversion rates match user volume? Is mobile experience holding back revenue? Click any segment to analyze that device in detail.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the distribution of product review ratings across the 1-5 star scale. A healthy distribution skews toward 4-5 stars, with limited 1-2 star reviews indicating product quality issues.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a star rating (1-5)
Bar height shows the count of reviews with that rating
Green colors indicate positive ratings (4-5 stars)
Red colors indicate negative ratings (1-2 stars)
A J-curve shape (high 5s, low middle, spike at 1) suggests polarized opinions
decisions_answered: What is the overall sentiment from product reviews?
Are most reviews positive (4-5 stars) or negative (1-2 stars)?
Is there a significant number of 3-star reviews indicating mediocrity?
Which products or issues are driving low ratings?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how product reviews distribute across the 1 to 5 star scale.

A healthy distribution has the tallest bar at 5 stars, followed by 4 stars, with small bars at 1-2 stars. This bell curve skewed right indicates satisfied customers.

Watch out for the J-curve pattern - lots of 5-star reviews but also lots of 1-star reviews. This polarization suggests some customers love you while others have significant issues.

Heavy concentration at 3 stars is concerning - it means customers are neither impressed nor complaining. Mediocrity doesn't build loyalty.

If 1-2 star reviews are growing, dig into the comments. Common themes reveal systemic issues that need addressing.

payload.formula_text: (previous stage - current stage) / previous stage x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates GA4 e-commerce funnel events to show customer progression from initial session through final purchase. Each stage shows the absolute count and the conversion rate to the next stage, helping identify where customers drop off.
how_to_read: The funnel flows left to right showing customer journey stages
Bar width represents volume at each stage relative to sessions
Percentages show conversion rate from the previous stage
Drop-off indicators highlight where customers leave the funnel
Red drop-off arrows show percentage lost between stages
Click any stage to drill into detailed analysis for that step
decisions_answered: What is the overall conversion rate from session to purchase?
At which funnel stage do we lose the most customers?
Is cart abandonment or checkout abandonment our bigger problem?
How does our funnel conversion compare to industry benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel visualization shows your customer journey from initial session through final purchase - the core of conversion optimization.

The formula counts each funnel event type from GA4: session starts, product views, cart additions, checkout initiations, and purchases. Conversion rates are calculated between adjacent stages.

Reading this chart: Follow the horizontal funnel from left to right. Each bar represents volume at that stage. The percentages between stages show your stage-to-stage conversion rates.

Watch for the biggest drops. Is it view-to-cart where product appeal matters? Cart-to-checkout where friction enters? Or checkout-to-purchase where trust and payment issues arise?

Key questions answered: What's our overall conversion rate? Where do we lose the most customers? What's the priority for optimization? Click any stage for deeper analysis into that conversion point.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers by when Klaviyo predicts their next order should occur based on historical purchase patterns. Overdue customers have missed their expected purchase window.
how_to_read: Red (Overdue) shows customers who have missed their predicted order window
Orange and yellow show upcoming purchase opportunities
The predicted revenue shows potential value at stake
Overdue customers should receive immediate outreach
Click any segment to view and action those customers
decisions_answered: How many customers have missed their expected purchase date?
What revenue is at risk from overdue orders?
Which customers should receive proactive outreach?
Is our overdue rate improving or worsening?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows customers segmented by expected next order timing - a powerful predictor of retention opportunity.

Klaviyo calculates when each customer should next purchase based on their historical patterns. The formula groups customers by how their expected date compares to today.

Reading this chart: The red Overdue segment represents immediate action needed - these customers have passed their predicted purchase window. The predicted revenue shows what is at stake.

Green and blue segments are healthy - customers expected to purchase in the future. Yellow and orange are approaching their windows and may benefit from proactive outreach.

Key questions: How many are overdue? What revenue is at risk? Who needs outreach now? Use this to trigger timely, personalized purchase reminders.

payload.formula_explanation: Surfaces campaign-level engagement and conversion metrics from the campaign performance mart, labelled with platform. Clicks stand in for sessions and CTR for engagement rate, giving a paid-campaign view of how each campaign contributes to the customer journey and revenue.
how_to_read: Each row is a campaign (with its platform) ranked by revenue (top 20)
Sessions here are campaign clicks; Engagement Rate is the campaign CTR
Sort by Revenue to find the top revenue-driving campaigns
Conversion Rate shows how well a campaign's clicks turn into conversions
Revenue Per Session highlights campaigns delivering high value per click
Compare engagement rate against conversion rate to spot campaigns that attract clicks but fail to convert
decisions_answered: Which campaigns drive the most revenue and conversions?
Which campaigns engage users but fail to convert them?
How does campaign performance compare across platforms?
Where should campaign budget be scaled up or cut?

payload.formula_explanation: Composite health score combining lifetime value and churn probability. Excellent/Good customers are your stable core. Fair needs nurturing. Poor requires immediate retention intervention to protect at-risk LTV.
how_to_read: Four gauges show percentage of customers in each health status
Green (Excellent) and blue (Good) are your healthy customer core
Yellow (Fair) customers need nurturing to prevent decline
Red (Poor) represents customers likely to churn - act immediately
The table below shows detailed metrics and LTV at stake
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are in healthy status?
How much LTV is at risk from poor-health customers?
Which health segment needs immediate intervention?
Are customer health trends improving or declining?
payload.narration_text:
This panel provides a comprehensive view of customer health - combining LTV and churn probability into actionable segments.

The formula creates four health tiers. Excellent customers have high LTV and low churn probability. Good is solid but with moderate metrics. Fair shows warning signs. Poor indicates imminent churn risk.

Reading this panel: The four gauges show percentage in each health status. Green and blue represent your stable core. Yellow needs nurturing before declining further. Red requires immediate intervention.

The table below quantifies the stakes. Look at the LTV at Stake column for Poor status - this is the revenue you'll lose if these customers churn without intervention.

Key questions: What percentage is healthy? How much LTV is at risk? Which segment needs urgent action? Use this to prioritize your retention resources effectively.

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies high-value customers at high churn risk for prioritized retention intervention. These customers have both significant lifetime value (>$100 CLV) and elevated churn probability (70%+), making them the highest priority for personalized outreach.
how_to_read: Table shows customers with both high CLV and high churn probability
Sorted by CLV descending - highest value at-risk customers first
Churn probability is color-coded: red >90%, orange 80-90%, yellow 70-80%
Days inactive shows recency of engagement - red if 90+ days
Search by email or name to find specific customers
Click any row to view full customer profile and history
decisions_answered: Which high-value customers are most at risk of churning?
How much CLV is at risk from our top at-risk customers?
Which customers deserve personalized retention outreach?
What is the engagement history of our most valuable at-risk customers?
payload.narration_text:
This table identifies your highest priority retention targets - customers with both high lifetime value and high churn risk.

The list is sorted by CLV, putting your most valuable at-risk customers at the top. These deserve personalized attention, not just automated emails.

Pay attention to the churn probability and days inactive columns. A customer with $5,000 CLV who is 90% likely to churn and hasn't engaged in 100 days needs immediate, personal outreach.

This is where ROI on retention is highest. Saving one $5,000 CLV customer is worth more than winning back 50 low-value customers. Assign your best customer success resources here.

Export this list for your win-back campaigns, or use it to prioritize phone calls and personalized emails.

payload.formula_explanation: First Purchase Average Order Value divides total first-order revenue by the number of newly acquired customers in the period. It captures how much a customer typically spends on their very first purchase.
how_to_read: The main value is the average value of a customer's first order
Higher first-purchase AOV improves acquisition payback
Compare against blended CAC to gauge upfront profitability
A rising trend suggests better targeting or higher-intent traffic
Channel-level differences reveal which sources bring bigger first baskets
decisions_answered: How much do new customers spend on their first order?
Is our first-purchase value high enough to offset acquisition cost?
Are recent acquisition efforts attracting higher-value buyers?
Which channels deliver the most valuable first purchases?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares revenue generated from first-time purchases versus repeat purchases over the last 30 days. A healthy business typically sees 40-60% of revenue from repeat customers, indicating strong retention.
how_to_read: Blue bar shows revenue from customers making their first purchase
Green bar shows revenue from returning customers
Longer repeat bar indicates strong retention and customer loyalty
Below each bar, see order counts and average order values
Compare AOV between segments - repeat customers often spend more
decisions_answered: What percentage of revenue comes from repeat customers?
Is our revenue mix healthy (40-60% from repeat)?
Do repeat customers have higher average order values?
Should we invest more in acquisition or retention?
payload.narration_text:
This comparison chart shows where your revenue comes from - first purchases versus repeat purchases.

The blue bar is new customer revenue. The green bar is revenue from customers who've bought before. For a healthy e-commerce business, repeat revenue should be 40-60% of total.

If first purchase dominates, you might have a retention problem - customers aren't coming back. If repeat dominates heavily, you might have an acquisition problem - not enough new customers entering the funnel.

The AOV comparison below matters too. If repeat customers spend more per order, that's proof that retention investments pay off. Your best customers get better over time.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares two attribution models: First-Touch (credits the channel that first acquired the user) vs Last-Touch (credits the channel of the converting session). Reveals how channels play different roles in awareness vs conversion.
how_to_read: Blue bars show First-Touch attribution (user acquisition)
Green bars show Last-Touch attribution (conversion session)
Channels with higher First-Touch drive awareness and discovery
Channels with higher Last-Touch close sales and drive conversions
Similar bars indicate the channel works across the full funnel
Compare bars to understand each channel role in the journey
decisions_answered: Which channels drive initial awareness vs final conversion?
Are we undervaluing awareness channels with last-touch attribution?
How do channels work together in the customer journey?
Should we adjust budget based on full-funnel contribution?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares First-Touch and Last-Touch attribution models - revealing how channels play different roles across the customer journey.

First-Touch credits revenue to the channel that first brought the user to your site. Last-Touch credits the channel of the actual converting session. These often differ significantly.

Reading this chart: Blue bars show First-Touch, green shows Last-Touch. Channels with taller blue bars drive awareness - they bring new users who may convert later through other channels. Taller green bars indicate closing power.

Paid Social often shows higher First-Touch than Last-Touch - it generates awareness but users convert through Direct or Search later. Email often shows stronger Last-Touch as it closes existing customers.

Key questions: Which channels drive awareness vs conversion? Are we undervaluing upper-funnel channels? How should we allocate budget considering full-funnel impact?

payload.formula_explanation: Customers with at least 2 orders are grouped by their average days between orders. This reveals purchase frequency patterns and helps identify the natural buying cycles of your customer base.
how_to_read: X-axis shows order frequency bands (time between orders)
Y-axis shows customer count
Bars grouped by cohort year for comparison
Shorter intervals (< 30 days) indicate highly engaged customers
Annual (180-365 days) is common for seasonal products like seeds
Click any bar to see average CLV for that frequency band
Distribution shape reveals your natural purchase cycle
decisions_answered: What is the typical purchase frequency for repeat customers?
Is there a dominant buying cycle (monthly, seasonal, annual)?
How does purchase frequency differ across cohort years?
Which frequency bands have the highest customer value?
When should we trigger re-engagement campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart reveals the natural purchase cycles of your repeat customers.

Only customers with 2 or more orders are included - we need repeat behavior to measure frequency. The bars show how many customers fall into each time-between-orders band.

For gardening and seed businesses, the 180-365 day band often dominates. This makes sense - customers buy seeds for spring planting and return the following year. However, you might see more customers in shorter bands if you successfully encourage season-extension or indoor gardening.

Compare cohort years: Are more recent cohorts showing shorter purchase intervals? That would indicate improving customer engagement. If older cohorts are concentrated in longer intervals, consider whether your retention programs are successfully increasing purchase frequency.

The average CLV for each frequency band is illuminating - frequent buyers almost always have significantly higher lifetime value.

payload.display_name: From Mart Campaign Performance Where Campaign Name Is Not Null And Clicks 0 And Last Date
payload.formula_text: date_to ORDER BY REVENUE DESC LIMIT 20
payload.formula_explanation: Surfaces campaign-level engagement and conversion metrics from the campaign performance mart, labelled with platform. Which campaigns engage users but fail to convert them?
How does campaign performance compare across platforms?
Where should campaign budget be scaled up or cut?

The table below shows detailed metrics and LTV at stake

How much LTV is at risk from poor-health customers?
Which health segment needs immediate intervention?
Are customer health trends improving or declining?
payload.narration_text:

The formula creates four health tiers. Excellent customers have high LTV and low churn probability. Good is solid but with moderate metrics. Fair shows warning signs. Poor indicates imminent churn risk.

Reading this panel: The four gauges show percentage in each health status. Green and blue represent your stable core. Yellow needs nurturing before declining further. Red requires immediate intervention.

Key questions: What percentage is healthy? How much LTV is at risk? Which segment needs urgent action? Use this to prioritize your retention resources effectively.

payload.formula_explanation: Customers are segmented by their purchase frequency into behavioral tiers. This chart shows how each quarterly cohort distributes across these segments, revealing the quality and maturation of customer relationships over time.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort quarter (when customers were acquired)
Y-axis shows customer count (toggle to percentage mode available)
Each color represents a customer segment based on order frequency
Gray (No Purchase) at bottom shows unconverted customers
Green segments (Loyal, VIP) at top represent highest-value customers
Older cohorts should show larger proportions in higher segments
Recent cohorts will be bottom-heavy (mostly One-Time) until they mature
decisions_answered: How does customer quality distribute across different cohorts?
What percentage of each cohort becomes repeat, loyal, or VIP?
Are we successfully moving customers up the loyalty ladder?
Which cohorts produced the most VIP customers?
How long does it take for cohorts to mature into higher segments?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows how customers within each quarterly cohort distribute across behavioral segments.

The segments are based on purchase frequency: customers who never purchased (gray), one-time buyers (red), repeat buyers with 2-3 orders (yellow), loyal customers with 4-6 orders (light green), and VIP customers with 7+ orders (dark green).

Older cohorts on the left should show more green - they've had time to mature into higher segments. Recent cohorts will naturally be mostly gray and red since customers haven't had time to reorder yet.

The key insight is trajectory: Compare Q1 2022 to Q1 2023 at the same maturity point. Are you converting more customers to repeat buyers? Growing your VIP percentage? This reveals whether your retention and loyalty programs are improving.

Switch to percentage mode to normalize for cohort size differences and focus purely on segment mix quality.

payload.formula_explanation: This chart tracks the number of unique customers who made purchases each month over the last 12 months. It provides a direct view of customer engagement and helps identify trends in repeat purchase behavior.
how_to_read: The blue line shows the count of unique customers making purchases each month
Upward trends indicate improving customer retention and engagement
Downward trends may signal issues with customer satisfaction or marketing effectiveness
Hover over any point to see the exact customer count for that month
Click a point to filter the page to that specific month
decisions_answered: Is our customer base growing or shrinking over time?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer purchasing behavior?
When did significant changes in customer engagement occur?
Should we launch re-engagement campaigns based on declining trends?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your purchasing customer count over 12 months.

The blue line shows unique customers making purchases each month. An upward trend means you're keeping customers engaged and coming back. A downward trend signals potential problems.

Look for patterns. Q4 often spikes for retail due to holidays. Post-holiday dips in January are normal. But sustained decline needs attention.

This answers: Is our customer base healthy? When did changes happen? And should we launch win-back campaigns?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how average churn probability and critical risk percentage have changed over the past 12 months. Reveals seasonal patterns and retention campaign effectiveness.
how_to_read: The red line shows average churn probability trend over 12 months
The orange line shows critical risk percentage trend
Downward trends indicate improving customer health
Look for seasonal patterns - Q1 often shows spring buying activity
Hover over any point to see exact monthly values
decisions_answered: Is our overall customer health improving or declining?
Are retention campaigns having measurable impact?
What seasonal patterns affect customer health?
Should we expect improvement or decline going forward?
payload.narration_text:
This multi-line chart tracks customer health metrics over 12 months - essential for understanding whether retention efforts are working.

The formula calculates monthly average churn probability and critical risk percentage for customers active in each month.

Reading this chart: Follow the red churn line. Declining trends indicate improving health, rising trends signal problems. The orange critical risk line shows your most urgent segment.

Look for seasonality. Seed businesses often see better health in Q1 spring planting season when customers are actively purchasing. Off-season months may show elevated churn.

Key questions: Is health improving? Are campaigns working? What seasonal patterns exist? Use this to evaluate retention strategy effectiveness and plan seasonal campaigns.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers into four risk tiers based on Klaviyo predicted churn probability. Distribution shows the health composition of your customer base.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of customers in each risk tier
Green (Low Risk) and yellow (Medium) are your healthy customer core
Orange (High Risk) needs proactive nurturing campaigns
Red (Critical Risk) requires immediate retention intervention
Click any segment to filter and view those customers
decisions_answered: How is churn risk distributed across our customer base?
What percentage of customers are at high or critical risk?
Do we have more healthy or at-risk customers?
Where should we focus our retention resources?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how churn risk is distributed across your customer base - essential for prioritizing retention efforts.

The formula creates four risk tiers: Low risk below 30 percent churn, Medium between 30 and 60, High between 60 and 80, and Critical at 80 percent or above.

Reading this chart: A healthy distribution has more green and yellow than orange and red. If critical risk dominates, your entire customer base needs attention.

The center shows total scored customers for reference. Hover over segments to see exact counts and CLV at stake for each tier.

Key questions: How is risk distributed? What percentage is high or critical risk? Where should retention focus? Click any segment to drill into that customer list for targeted action.

payload.formula_explanation: Counts customers whose mart-assigned churn-risk level is High or Critical (churn score of 60 or above). These are the customers most likely to lapse without intervention.
how_to_read: The main value is the number of High and Critical risk customers
The percentage shows their share of customers with a prediction
Higher counts mean a larger retention workload
Compare to your outreach capacity to prioritize action
Pair with CLV at Risk to focus on the most valuable at-risk customers
decisions_answered: How many customers are at high or critical churn risk?
What share of scored customers need intervention?
Do we have enough capacity to reach all at-risk customers?

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers into value tiers based on lifetime value and churn probability. VIP customers require premium treatment, while at-risk segments need retention intervention.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of customers in each value segment
Gold (VIP) segment is small but contributes outsized revenue
Purple (High Value) represents your loyal customer core
Gray (New/Inactive) includes recent signups and dormant contacts
Click any segment to view customer list for targeted actions
decisions_answered: How are customers distributed across value tiers?
What percentage of customers are VIPs worth protecting?
How large is our at-risk or inactive segment?
Where should we focus retention and growth efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customer base segmented by lifetime value - essential for prioritizing marketing and retention efforts.

The formula creates five tiers: VIP customers have $500-plus LTV with low churn. High Value sits at $200 to $500. Medium and Low Value follow. New or Inactive captures recent signups and dormant contacts.

Reading this chart: The gold VIP slice is typically small but critical - these customers drive disproportionate revenue and deserve premium treatment. Purple High Value customers form your loyal core.

Gray New/Inactive may be your largest segment. This includes both recent signups who haven't proven value yet and dormant customers needing win-back campaigns.

Key questions: How are customers distributed? What percentage are VIPs? How large is the at-risk segment? Click any segment to take targeted action on that customer group.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows individual high-value customers who are at elevated churn risk. These customers deserve personalized retention attention due to their revenue importance.
how_to_read: Health score badge shows customer risk level (color coded)
Sort by Total CLV to prioritize highest value customers first
Days inactive indicates how long since last engagement
Overdue flag shows customers past their expected purchase date
Use search to find specific customers by name or email
decisions_answered: Which high-value customers need immediate attention?
Who should receive personalized outreach from our team?
What is the profile of our at-risk VIP customers?
Are high-value customers showing warning signs?
payload.narration_text:
This table displays your highest-priority retention targets - customers with significant lifetime value who show elevated churn risk.

The filter selects customers with CLV of $500 or more and churn probability of 60 percent or higher. These are your VIP customers at risk.

Reading this table: The health score badge provides quick risk assessment. Sort by CLV to prioritize the highest value customers. Days inactive and overdue flags indicate urgency.

These customers deserve personalized attention - not just automated emails. Consider direct outreach, special offers, or customer success calls for the highest value at-risk customers.

Key questions: Who needs immediate attention? Who deserves personalized outreach? What patterns do at-risk VIPs show? Use this as your retention priority list.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers by days since their last tracked activity (purchase, email open, site visit). More recent engagement correlates with lower churn risk.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each engagement recency level
Active and Engaged segments are your healthy, engaged customers
At Risk and Dormant need re-engagement campaigns
Churned customers (180+ days) may be permanently lost
The churn percentage on each bar shows that segment average
decisions_answered: How engaged is our customer base currently?
How many customers are slipping toward churn?
Which engagement segments need immediate campaigns?
Is our engagement distribution improving over time?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows customers segmented by engagement recency - a leading indicator of churn before it happens.

The formula groups customers by days since last activity: Active within 7 days, Engaged 8 to 30, At Risk 31 to 90, Dormant 91 to 180, and Churned over 180 days.

Reading this chart: Longer bars in Active and Engaged indicate a healthy, engaged database. Large At Risk or Dormant segments signal re-engagement opportunity.

The average churn probability shown for each segment demonstrates the correlation. Active customers typically show lower churn, while Churned customers approach 90 percent plus churn probability.

Key questions: How engaged is your base? How many are slipping away? Which segments need campaigns? Use this to size your re-engagement and win-back initiatives.
