payload.formula_explanation: Compares two attribution models: First-Touch (credits the channel that first acquired the user) vs Last-Touch (credits the channel of the converting session). Reveals how channels play different roles in awareness vs conversion.
how_to_read: Blue bars show First-Touch attribution (user acquisition)
Green bars show Last-Touch attribution (conversion session)
Channels with higher First-Touch drive awareness and discovery
Channels with higher Last-Touch close sales and drive conversions
Similar bars indicate the channel works across the full funnel
Compare bars to understand each channel role in the journey
decisions_answered: Which channels drive initial awareness vs final conversion?
Are we undervaluing awareness channels with last-touch attribution?
How do channels work together in the customer journey?
Should we adjust budget based on full-funnel contribution?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares First-Touch and Last-Touch attribution models - revealing how channels play different roles across the customer journey.

First-Touch credits revenue to the channel that first brought the user to your site. Last-Touch credits the channel of the actual converting session. These often differ significantly.

Reading this chart: Blue bars show First-Touch, green shows Last-Touch. Channels with taller blue bars drive awareness - they bring new users who may convert later through other channels. Taller green bars indicate closing power.

Paid Social often shows higher First-Touch than Last-Touch - it generates awareness but users convert through Direct or Search later. Email often shows stronger Last-Touch as it closes existing customers.

Key questions: Which channels drive awareness vs conversion? Are we undervaluing upper-funnel channels? How should we allocate budget considering full-funnel impact?

payload.formula_explanation: Classifies customers based on recency of their last engagement with your brand. Active customers engaged within 30 days are healthy. Beyond 90 days, customers are considered lapsing or churned, requiring re-engagement efforts.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a lifecycle stage based on days since last activity
Green (Active) shows customers engaged in the last 30 days - your healthy core
Yellow/Orange (At Risk, Lapsing) shows customers drifting away - intervene now
Red (Lapsed, Churned) shows customers who have disengaged - need win-back campaigns
Bar length indicates customer count in each stage
Click any bar to filter and see customers in that lifecycle stage
decisions_answered: What is the lifecycle distribution of our customer base?
How many customers are actively engaged vs. at risk of churning?
Which lifecycle stage has the most CLV at risk?
When should we trigger win-back campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel chart shows your customers distributed across lifecycle stages based on recency of engagement.

Active customers - those engaged in the last 30 days - are your healthy core. Keep them engaged with regular communication.

At Risk and Lapsing stages - 31 to 90 days inactive - are your intervention window. These customers haven't churned yet, but they're drifting. A well-timed email or offer can bring them back.

Lapsed and Churned - beyond 90 days - are harder to recover. They need aggressive win-back campaigns, and expect lower response rates.

The shape of this funnel tells a story. A healthy business has most customers in Active, with progressively fewer in each subsequent stage. If you see bulging in Lapsed or Churned, you have a retention problem.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers into four risk tiers based on Klaviyo predicted churn probability. Distribution shows the health composition of your customer base.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of customers in each risk tier
Green (Low Risk) and yellow (Medium) are your healthy customer core
Orange (High Risk) needs proactive nurturing campaigns
Red (Critical Risk) requires immediate retention intervention
Click any segment to filter and view those customers
decisions_answered: How is churn risk distributed across our customer base?
What percentage of customers are at high or critical risk?
Do we have more healthy or at-risk customers?
Where should we focus our retention resources?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how churn risk is distributed across your customer base - essential for prioritizing retention efforts.

The formula creates four risk tiers: Low risk below 30 percent churn, Medium between 30 and 60, High between 60 and 80, and Critical at 80 percent or above.

Reading this chart: A healthy distribution has more green and yellow than orange and red. If critical risk dominates, your entire customer base needs attention.

The center shows total scored customers for reference. Hover over segments to see exact counts and CLV at stake for each tier.

Key questions: How is risk distributed? What percentage is high or critical risk? Where should retention focus? Click any segment to drill into that customer list for targeted action.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers into value tiers based on lifetime value and churn probability. VIP customers require premium treatment, while at-risk segments need retention intervention.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of customers in each value segment
Gold (VIP) segment is small but contributes outsized revenue
Purple (High Value) represents your loyal customer core
Gray (New/Inactive) includes recent signups and dormant contacts
Click any segment to view customer list for targeted actions
decisions_answered: How are customers distributed across value tiers?
What percentage of customers are VIPs worth protecting?
How large is our at-risk or inactive segment?
Where should we focus retention and growth efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customer base segmented by lifetime value - essential for prioritizing marketing and retention efforts.

The formula creates five tiers: VIP customers have $500-plus LTV with low churn. High Value sits at $200 to $500. Medium and Low Value follow. New or Inactive captures recent signups and dormant contacts.

Reading this chart: The gold VIP slice is typically small but critical - these customers drive disproportionate revenue and deserve premium treatment. Purple High Value customers form your loyal core.

Gray New/Inactive may be your largest segment. This includes both recent signups who haven't proven value yet and dormant customers needing win-back campaigns.

Key questions: How are customers distributed? What percentage are VIPs? How large is the at-risk segment? Click any segment to take targeted action on that customer group.

payload.formula_explanation: Customers are segmented by their purchase frequency into behavioral tiers. This chart shows how each quarterly cohort distributes across these segments, revealing the quality and maturation of customer relationships over time.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort quarter (when customers were acquired)
Y-axis shows customer count (toggle to percentage mode available)
Each color represents a customer segment based on order frequency
Gray (No Purchase) at bottom shows unconverted customers
Green segments (Loyal, VIP) at top represent highest-value customers
Older cohorts should show larger proportions in higher segments
Recent cohorts will be bottom-heavy (mostly One-Time) until they mature
decisions_answered: How does customer quality distribute across different cohorts?
What percentage of each cohort becomes repeat, loyal, or VIP?
Are we successfully moving customers up the loyalty ladder?
Which cohorts produced the most VIP customers?
How long does it take for cohorts to mature into higher segments?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows how customers within each quarterly cohort distribute across behavioral segments.

The segments are based on purchase frequency: customers who never purchased (gray), one-time buyers (red), repeat buyers with 2-3 orders (yellow), loyal customers with 4-6 orders (light green), and VIP customers with 7+ orders (dark green).

Older cohorts on the left should show more green - they've had time to mature into higher segments. Recent cohorts will naturally be mostly gray and red since customers haven't had time to reorder yet.

The key insight is trajectory: Compare Q1 2022 to Q1 2023 at the same maturity point. Are you converting more customers to repeat buyers? Growing your VIP percentage? This reveals whether your retention and loyalty programs are improving.

Switch to percentage mode to normalize for cohort size differences and focus purely on segment mix quality.

payload.formula_text: SUM(NET_LTV) FROM MART_CUSTOMER_SUMMARY WHERE TOTAL_ORDERS > 0 AND first_order_date in range
payload.formula_explanation: Sums net lifetime value across every customer who has placed at least one order in the selected period. It represents the total realized value of the cohort.
how_to_read: The value is the combined lifetime value of all customers in the cohort
It scales with both customer count and per-customer value
Compare to the prior period to track overall portfolio growth
A rising total with flat customer count means CLV per customer is improving
Use it as the top-line value of the customers acquired in this window
decisions_answered: What is the total value of customers acquired in this period?
Is total customer value growing versus the comparison period?
Which cohorts contribute the most to overall LTV?

payload.formula_explanation: Composite health score combining lifetime value and churn probability. Excellent/Good customers are your stable core. Fair needs nurturing. Poor requires immediate retention intervention to protect at-risk LTV.
how_to_read: Four gauges show percentage of customers in each health status
Green (Excellent) and blue (Good) are your healthy customer core
Yellow (Fair) customers need nurturing to prevent decline
Red (Poor) represents customers likely to churn - act immediately
The table below shows detailed metrics and LTV at stake
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are in healthy status?
How much LTV is at risk from poor-health customers?
Which health segment needs immediate intervention?
Are customer health trends improving or declining?
payload.narration_text:
This panel provides a comprehensive view of customer health - combining LTV and churn probability into actionable segments.

The formula creates four health tiers. Excellent customers have high LTV and low churn probability. Good is solid but with moderate metrics. Fair shows warning signs. Poor indicates imminent churn risk.

Reading this panel: The four gauges show percentage in each health status. Green and blue represent your stable core. Yellow needs nurturing before declining further. Red requires immediate intervention.

The table below quantifies the stakes. Look at the LTV at Stake column for Poor status - this is the revenue you'll lose if these customers churn without intervention.

Key questions: What percentage is healthy? How much LTV is at risk? Which segment needs urgent action? Use this to prioritize your retention resources effectively.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows how customer lifetime value is distributed across your customer base. Most businesses have a long tail of low-LTV customers with a smaller segment of high-value VIPs driving disproportionate revenue.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a $50 LTV bucket showing customer count
The leftmost bars typically contain the most customers (low LTV)
Look for the distribution shape - is it heavily skewed left?
The median and average LTV are shown for reference
Click any bucket to filter and view customers in that LTV range
decisions_answered: How is customer value distributed across our base?
What percentage of customers are high-value VIPs?
Is our LTV distribution healthy or too skewed toward low value?
Should we focus on moving customers up the value ladder?
payload.narration_text:
This histogram shows how customer lifetime value is distributed across your customer base - a critical view of where your revenue really comes from.

The formula groups customers into $50 LTV buckets and counts how many fall into each. The median and average LTV lines provide reference points.

Reading this chart: Most customers cluster in the lower LTV buckets on the left. This is normal - the 80/20 rule often applies where 20 percent of customers drive 80 percent of revenue.

The key insight is the shape. A heavy left skew means most customers have low value. The goal is to move customers rightward into higher buckets through retention and upselling.

Key questions: How is value distributed? What percentage are VIPs? Should you focus on moving customers up the value ladder? Click any bucket to explore that customer segment.

payload.formula_explanation: The LTV:CAC ratio measures customer acquisition profitability. A ratio of 3:1 or higher indicates sustainable growth - you earn $3 in lifetime value for every $1 spent acquiring. Below 2:1 suggests unprofitable acquisition.
how_to_read: The line tracks monthly LTV:CAC ratio over 12 months
The green shaded zone indicates healthy ratios (3:1 or higher)
Yellow zone (2:1 to 3:1) suggests caution
Red zone (below 2:1) indicates unprofitable acquisition
Look for seasonal patterns - Q1 often shows best ratios for gardening
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring customers profitably?
Is our LTV:CAC ratio improving or declining?
Which months show the best acquisition economics?
Should we scale or reduce customer acquisition spending?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your LTV to CAC ratio over time - the single most important metric for sustainable growth.

The formula divides average customer lifetime value by customer acquisition cost. A 3 to 1 ratio means you earn $3 for every $1 spent acquiring customers.

Reading this chart: Follow the line across months. The green zone above 3 to 1 indicates healthy, profitable acquisition. Yellow between 2 and 3 to 1 suggests caution. Red below 2 to 1 means you're losing money on acquisition.

Watch for seasonality. For gardening businesses, Q1 spring planting season often shows the best ratios as high-intent customers purchase more.

Key questions: Are we profitable on acquisition? Is the ratio improving? When should we scale spending? This guides your overall marketing investment strategy.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows how customer lifetime value accumulates over time since acquisition. By plotting average realized CLV against months since acquisition, we can see the typical value trajectory and identify when customers reach maturity.
how_to_read: X-axis shows months since customer acquisition (0-36 months)
Y-axis shows average realized (historic) CLV
The curve shows how CLV grows as customers age with your brand
Steep initial slope indicates strong early purchases
Flattening curve indicates customers reaching maturity
Compare actual curve to expected payback period
decisions_answered: How does CLV mature over time?
When do customers reach CLV maturity/payback?
What is the typical value trajectory for a customer?
How quickly do we recoup customer acquisition costs?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart shows the CLV maturation curve - how customer value accumulates over time since acquisition.

The X-axis represents months since a customer joined, from 0 to 36 months. The Y-axis shows average realized CLV at each point. This creates a typical value trajectory.

Early months show steep growth as customers make initial and early repeat purchases. Over time, the curve typically flattens as purchase frequency stabilizes.

Key insights to look for: When does the curve flatten? This indicates CLV maturity. Where does the curve cross your customer acquisition cost? This is your payback period. How much value is generated in the first 12 months vs months 12-24?

This chart answers critical questions for financial planning: How long until we recover acquisition costs? What is the typical lifetime of customer value growth?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks email and SMS marketing touchpoints from Klaviyo throughout the customer journey. Shows engagement levels at each stage and how marketing communications influence customer behavior.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show event volume for each Klaviyo metric
Email metrics include Received, Opened, and Clicked
SMS metrics include Received and Clicked
Commerce events show Viewed Product through Placed Order
Compare Opened to Received for open rate insights
Click any metric to analyze that touchpoint in detail
decisions_answered: How many emails and SMS messages are we sending?
What are our email open and click rates?
How do Klaviyo touchpoints influence purchases?
Is our email/SMS engagement improving over time?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows Klaviyo touchpoint metrics - measuring how your email and SMS marketing engages customers throughout their journey.

The formula counts events by metric type from Klaviyo: emails sent, opened, clicked, SMS messages, and commerce events like product views and purchases.

Reading this chart: The bars show event volume for each metric type. Compare Received Email to Opened Email to gauge open rates. Compare Opened to Clicked for click-through performance.

Commerce events like Placed Order show how many Klaviyo-tracked customers are completing purchases. These help attribute revenue back to your email and SMS marketing efforts.

Key questions: What's our email volume? How are open and click rates performing? What commerce activity ties to Klaviyo? Click any metric to dive into that specific touchpoint.

payload.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows the percentage of customers from each cohort who made a purchase in subsequent months. Cohorts are grouped by acquisition month, and retention is calculated as the percentage of the original cohort still actively purchasing at each time interval.
how_to_read: Y-axis shows cohort months (most recent at top), with cohort size in parentheses
X-axis shows months since signup (M0 = first month, M24 = two years later)
Cell color intensity indicates retention rate - darker green = higher retention
Month 0 (M0) shows initial purchase rate, typically highest
Look for diagonal patterns to understand typical retention decay curves
The M12 column often shows a bump due to annual seasonal purchase cycles
decisions_answered: How does customer retention change over time for each cohort?
Which cohorts have the strongest/weakest retention curves?
At what month do we see the biggest retention drop-off?
Is there an annual purchase cycle (M12 bump) in our customer behavior?
Are recent cohorts retaining better or worse than historical cohorts?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap visualizes how customer cohorts retain over time after their initial acquisition.

Each row represents a monthly cohort - a group of customers who first appeared in that month. The cohort size shows how many customers were acquired. As you move right across the row, you see what percentage of those original customers made a purchase in subsequent months.

Month 0 shows the initial purchase rate. The natural decay as you move right is expected - not all customers continue purchasing. However, pay attention to patterns: Do you see a bump at Month 12? This often indicates seasonal or annual purchase behavior - customers who bought seeds last spring return again this spring.

Compare cohorts vertically to identify quality variations. If Q1 cohorts consistently show better retention than Q3 cohorts, this suggests seasonal acquisition quality differences. Spring gardening season customers may be more engaged.

Use this to identify optimal intervention timing - where does retention drop most sharply? That's when lifecycle emails and retention campaigns should trigger.

Yellow zone (2:1 to 3:1) suggests caution

payload.display_name: Monthly Cohort Metrics Mart Customer Cohort At Months Since First Order
payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive table showing detailed metrics for each monthly cohort. Enables comparison of acquisition quality, customer development, and lifetime value across time periods.
how_to_read: Each row is a monthly cohort, newest at top
Cohort Size shows customers acquired that month
Avg CLV and Total LTV show value metrics
Churn Risk is color-coded (red = high risk)
Converted/Repeat/VIP show customer development counts
Q1 months (Jan-Mar) often highlighted as spring season
Sort by any column to rank cohorts
decisions_answered: Which monthly cohorts perform best across all metrics?
How do spring cohorts compare to other seasons?
Which cohorts have the highest/lowest churn risk?
What is the VIP conversion rate for each cohort?
Are recent cohorts on track compared to historical benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This comprehensive table provides detailed metrics for every monthly cohort in your database.

Each row represents customers acquired in that specific month. You can see cohort size, average and total CLV, order metrics, and customer development progression from converted to repeat to VIP.

The Churn Risk column is color-coded for quick identification. Red indicates cohorts with elevated churn probability that may need immediate retention attention. Green indicates healthy, stable cohorts.

Q1 rows (January through March) are often highlighted because spring is critical for gardening businesses. Compare Q1 cohorts across years to see if acquisition quality is improving.

Use the sorting capability to rank cohorts by any metric. Sort by Avg CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition months. Sort by VIP count to find cohorts that produced the most high-value customers.

This table is your cohort analysis command center - it aggregates everything you need to evaluate customer acquisition and development performance.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks new customer acquisition over 24 months with a running cumulative total. The area shows monthly additions while the cumulative line shows total database growth over time.
how_to_read: The shaded area shows new customers added each month
Peaks indicate successful acquisition periods or campaigns
Look for seasonal patterns - Q1 peaks for gardening businesses
Consistent baseline growth indicates healthy organic acquisition
Click any month to analyze that cohort in detail
decisions_answered: How is our customer base growing over time?
Are there seasonal acquisition patterns we should plan around?
Which months produced the most new customers?
Is year-over-year growth accelerating or slowing?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart shows your customer growth trend over 24 months - revealing both patterns and overall trajectory.

The formula counts new customers by month, then calculates a running cumulative total. Year-over-year comparisons show growth acceleration or deceleration.

Reading this chart: The shaded area represents monthly new customer acquisitions. Peaks indicate strong acquisition periods, often tied to campaigns or seasonality. For gardening, expect Q1 spring peaks.

The overall trend matters most. Consistent upward slope means healthy growth. Flattening suggests market saturation or competitive pressure. Declining trends require immediate attention.

Key questions: How is the customer base growing? What seasonal patterns exist? Is year-over-year growth accelerating? Click any month to analyze that acquisition cohort in detail.

Older cohorts on the left should show more green - they've had time to mature into higher segments. Recent cohorts will naturally be mostly gray and red since customers haven't had time to reorder yet.

The key insight is trajectory: Compare Q1 2022 to Q1 2023 at the same maturity point. Are you converting more customers to repeat buyers? Growing your VIP percentage? This reveals whether your retention and loyalty programs are improving.

Switch to percentage mode to normalize for cohort size differences and focus purely on segment mix quality.

payload.formula_text: SUM(NEW_USERS) from GA4 User Acquisition Report
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates all new users by date from GA4 User Acquisition First User Source/Medium Report. This represents unique first-time visitors attributed to their initial acquisition source.
how_to_read: Each point on the chart represents daily new user acquisition count
The filled area shows the volume trend - larger area means more acquisitions
Rising trend indicates growing customer acquisition momentum
Dips may indicate weekends, seasonality, or campaign performance changes
Hover over any point to see exact new user count for that day
decisions_answered: How many new customers are we acquiring daily?
Is our acquisition volume trending up or down?
Are there any concerning dips that need investigation?
Which days or periods show peak acquisition?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart shows your daily new customer acquisition over the last 30 days.

The formula is straightforward: we sum all NEW_USERS from GA4's User Acquisition report for each day.

To read this chart: the filled area represents acquisition volume. A rising trend means you're acquiring more customers. Dips might indicate weekends, seasonal effects, or changes in campaign performance.

Hover over any point to see the exact count. This chart answers key questions: How many new customers are we acquiring daily? Is the trend going the right direction? And when are our peak acquisition days?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the volume of customers acquired each month and what percentage converted to making at least one purchase. This reveals acquisition volume trends and the effectiveness of converting acquired customers into buyers.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort acquisition month
Light blue bars show total new customers acquired that month
Dark blue bars show customers who converted to purchasers
Orange line (secondary axis) shows conversion rate percentage
Tall bars indicate high acquisition volume periods
Bar gap indicates customers who never made a first purchase
Seasonal patterns often visible with Q1 peaks
decisions_answered: How many customers are we acquiring each month?
What percentage of acquired customers make their first purchase?
Are we scaling acquisition while maintaining conversion quality?
Which months show the highest acquisition and conversion?
Is there seasonality in our customer acquisition patterns?
payload.narration_text:
This combo chart shows customer acquisition volume alongside conversion effectiveness.

The light blue bars represent total customers who entered the database each month. The dark blue bars show how many of those actually made a purchase - converting from prospects to customers. The gap between them represents unconverted database entries.

The orange line tracking conversion rate is crucial. You want both high volume AND high conversion. If volume spikes but conversion drops, you may be acquiring lower-quality leads. Q1 typically shows both higher volume and higher conversion for gardening businesses due to seasonal demand.

Compare year-over-year patterns: Are you acquiring more customers than last year's same month? Is your conversion rate improving? These metrics directly tie to marketing efficiency and customer acquisition cost effectiveness.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers into value tiers based on lifetime value and churn probability. VIP customers require premium treatment, while at-risk segments need retention intervention.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of customers in each value segment
Gold (VIP) segment is small but contributes outsized revenue
Purple (High Value) represents your loyal customer core
Gray (New/Inactive) includes recent signups and dormant contacts
Click any segment to view customer list for targeted actions
decisions_answered: How are customers distributed across value tiers?
What percentage of customers are VIPs worth protecting?
How large is our at-risk or inactive segment?
Where should we focus retention and growth efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customer base segmented by lifetime value - essential for prioritizing marketing and retention efforts.

The formula creates five tiers: VIP customers have $500-plus LTV with low churn. High Value sits at $200 to $500. Medium and Low Value follow. New or Inactive captures recent signups and dormant contacts.

Reading this chart: The gold VIP slice is typically small but critical - these customers drive disproportionate revenue and deserve premium treatment. Purple High Value customers form your loyal core.

Gray New/Inactive may be your largest segment. This includes both recent signups who haven't proven value yet and dormant customers needing win-back campaigns.

Key questions: How are customers distributed? What percentage are VIPs? How large is the at-risk segment? Click any segment to take targeted action on that customer group.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks average predicted customer lifetime value by the month customers were acquired. Shows whether customer quality is improving or declining over time, helping evaluate acquisition strategy effectiveness.
how_to_read: Each point represents customers acquired in that month
The Y-axis shows average predicted lifetime value
Rising line indicates improving customer quality over time
Declining line suggests acquisition strategy may be degrading
Seasonal dips may reflect promotional acquisition periods
Hover to see the cohort size and exact CLV value
decisions_answered: Is our customer quality improving or declining?
Which acquisition months brought the highest-value customers?
Are recent cohorts more or less valuable than historical ones?
Should we adjust acquisition strategy to improve CLV?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value by acquisition cohort - showing whether your customer quality is improving over time.

The formula groups customers by the month they were created in Klaviyo, then calculates the average predicted CLV for each cohort from Klaviyo predictive analytics.

Reading this chart: Each point represents a monthly cohort. The Y-axis shows their average lifetime value. An upward trend means you're acquiring higher-value customers over time - your strategy is improving.

Watch for seasonal patterns. Cohorts acquired during heavy promotional periods may show lower CLV due to discount-seeking customers. Organic growth periods often produce higher-value cohorts.

Key questions: Is customer quality improving? Which months produced the best customers? Are recent cohorts valuable? Click any point to analyze that specific acquisition cohort.

payload.formula_explanation: Splits monthly revenue between first-time purchasers and repeat customers. Healthy businesses derive 60%+ of revenue from returning customers, indicating strong retention and customer satisfaction.
how_to_read: Light green bars show revenue from first-time purchasers
Dark green bars show revenue from repeat customers
Healthy businesses have taller dark green (returning) segments
The 40/60 benchmark line shows ideal new/returning split
Click any month to analyze revenue composition in detail
decisions_answered: What percentage of revenue comes from repeat customers?
Is our returning customer revenue share improving?
Are we too dependent on new customer acquisition?
Do we need to invest more in retention to balance revenue?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows your monthly revenue split between new and returning customers - a key indicator of business health.

The formula sums order values, separating first-time purchasers from those with previous orders. The returning share percentage shows retention's contribution to revenue.

Reading this chart: Light green shows first-time purchase revenue. Dark green shows repeat customer revenue. Ideally, dark green dominates - 60 percent or more from returning customers indicates a healthy business.

Why this matters: New customer revenue is expensive to generate. Returning customer revenue has near-zero acquisition cost and higher margins. Businesses too dependent on new customers face growth challenges.

Key questions: What's the revenue split? Is returning share growing? Do you need more retention investment? Click any month for deeper revenue analysis.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows which landing pages receive the most traffic and drive the most value. Helps identify high-performing pages to replicate and underperforming pages that need optimization or elimination.
how_to_read: Each row shows a landing page with key performance metrics
Sort by Page Views to see highest traffic pages
Sort by Revenue to see pages driving the most value
High New Users indicates effective acquisition pages
Avg Engagement shows page quality and relevance
Use search to find specific pages or page categories
decisions_answered: Which landing pages receive the most traffic?
What pages drive the most revenue attribution?
Which pages have the best engagement metrics?
Where should we focus landing page optimization efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your top landing pages by traffic and performance - essential for understanding where customers enter your site and how well those entry points convert.

The formula aggregates page views, new users, revenue, and engagement time for each page path from GA4. This shows which pages serve as effective entry points.

Reading this table: Sort by Page Views for traffic volume, by Revenue for value generation. Home page often leads traffic, but product and category pages may drive more revenue per view.

Look for mismatches. A page with high traffic but low revenue may need conversion optimization. A page with great revenue per view but low traffic deserves more promotion.

Key questions: Which pages get most traffic? What drives most revenue? Where should optimization focus? Use the search to analyze specific page categories like product pages or blog posts.

payload.formula_explanation: Uses Klaviyo predictive analytics to forecast when customers are expected to make their next purchase, then segments them by urgency. "Overdue" customers have passed their expected order date and need immediate re-engagement.
how_to_read: Red (Overdue) shows customers who have passed their predicted next order date
Green (Due in 30 days) shows customers expected to order soon - good opportunity
Yellow/Blue segments show customers with longer expected purchase windows
Large Overdue segment indicates retention intervention is needed
Hover to see customer count and associated CLV for each window
Click any slice to filter and view customers in that window
decisions_answered: How many customers are overdue for their next purchase?
What is the predicted CLV at risk from overdue customers?
When should we send reminder or re-engagement emails?
How are customers distributed across expected purchase windows?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows when customers are expected to make their next purchase based on Klaviyo's predictive analytics.

The red Overdue segment is critical - these customers should have ordered by now based on their historical patterns but haven't. They're at elevated churn risk and need immediate attention.

Green shows customers due in the next 30 days. These are prime targets for reminder emails or offers to accelerate their purchase.

The longer windows - 60 days, 90 days, beyond - represent customers with naturally longer purchase cycles. They may need less frequent but well-timed engagement.

A large Overdue segment signals trouble. These are potential lost customers. Calculate the CLV at stake and prioritize win-back campaigns accordingly.

payload.formula_explanation: Customers are segmented by their purchase frequency into behavioral tiers. This chart shows how each quarterly cohort distributes across these segments, revealing the quality and maturation of customer relationships over time.
how_to_read: X-axis shows cohort quarter (when customers were acquired)
Y-axis shows customer count (toggle to percentage mode available)
Each color represents a customer segment based on order frequency
Gray (No Purchase) at bottom shows unconverted customers
Green segments (Loyal, VIP) at top represent highest-value customers
Older cohorts should show larger proportions in higher segments
Recent cohorts will be bottom-heavy (mostly One-Time) until they mature
decisions_answered: How does customer quality distribute across different cohorts?
What percentage of each cohort becomes repeat, loyal, or VIP?
Are we successfully moving customers up the loyalty ladder?
Which cohorts produced the most VIP customers?
How long does it take for cohorts to mature into higher segments?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows how customers within each quarterly cohort distribute across behavioral segments.

The segments are based on purchase frequency: customers who never purchased (gray), one-time buyers (red), repeat buyers with 2-3 orders (yellow), loyal customers with 4-6 orders (light green), and VIP customers with 7+ orders (dark green).

Older cohorts on the left should show more green - they've had time to mature into higher segments. Recent cohorts will naturally be mostly gray and red since customers haven't had time to reorder yet.

The key insight is trajectory: Compare Q1 2022 to Q1 2023 at the same maturity point. Are you converting more customers to repeat buyers? Growing your VIP percentage? This reveals whether your retention and loyalty programs are improving.

Switch to percentage mode to normalize for cohort size differences and focus purely on segment mix quality.

payload.formula_explanation: Net Promoter Score (NPS) segments survey respondents into three categories based on their likelihood to recommend. Promoters (9-10) are loyal enthusiasts, Passives (7-8) are satisfied but unenthusiastic, and Detractors (0-6) are unhappy customers who can damage your brand through negative word-of-mouth.
how_to_read: The green slice shows Promoters (scores 9-10) - your most loyal customers
The yellow/amber slice shows Passives (scores 7-8) - satisfied but not enthusiastic
The red slice shows Detractors (scores 0-6) - unhappy customers at risk of churning
The center number shows total survey responses in the period
Percentages in the legend show the proportion of each segment
decisions_answered: What is the overall distribution of customer sentiment?
Are we building a base of loyal brand advocates (promoters)?
How many customers are at risk of negative word-of-mouth (detractors)?
Should we focus on converting passives to promoters or reducing detractors?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your NPS survey respondents break down into three key segments.

The green slice represents your Promoters - customers who scored 9 or 10. These are your advocates who actively recommend your brand.

The amber slice shows Passives - scores of 7 or 8. They're satisfied but not enthusiastic, and could easily switch to competitors.

The red slice represents Detractors - scores from 0 to 6. These unhappy customers can damage your brand through negative reviews and word-of-mouth.

A healthy distribution has a large green slice and small red slice. If detractors are growing, investigate why. If promoters dominate, leverage them for referrals and testimonials.

payload.formula_explanation: Breaks down NPS by customer value segment to understand if your most valuable customers are also your most satisfied. High-value customer satisfaction is critical for revenue retention.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a customer value segment
Bar length shows the NPS score for that segment
Green bars indicate positive NPS, red bars indicate negative
Compare segments to identify satisfaction disparities
The number of responses per segment is shown alongside
decisions_answered: Are our high-value customers more or less satisfied than others?
Which customer segment needs the most attention?
Is there a correlation between customer value and satisfaction?
Should we offer different service levels by segment?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart breaks down NPS by customer value segment, helping you understand if satisfaction varies by how much customers spend.

High Value customers - those spending over $500 lifetime - are your VIPs. Their NPS matters most for revenue retention.

Medium Value customers are your growth opportunity. Good satisfaction here can help them become high value.

Low Value customers might be new or infrequent buyers. Their feedback reveals acquisition experience.

The ideal pattern is consistent high NPS across all segments. If high-value customers have lower NPS, that's a red flag - they may churn, taking significant revenue with them. Prioritize understanding and addressing their concerns.

payload.formula_explanation: Magento reviews carry no true NPS survey, so this metric repurposes the average star rating (1-5 scale) from MART_CUSTOMER_FEEDBACK_DETAIL as an NPS proxy. Despite the -100 to +100 label, the value is the mean star rating for the period, and the change is the simple difference versus the prior period.
how_to_read: The main value is the average star rating used as a satisfaction proxy, not a classic -100 to +100 NPS
Zero-rating reviews are excluded so they do not drag the average down
The change is the absolute point difference versus the comparison period
Higher is better - it reflects more positive product reviews
Read alongside Promoters % and Rating Distribution for the full sentiment picture
decisions_answered: Is overall review sentiment improving or declining?
How did satisfaction shift versus the prior period?
Are customers rating our products positively on average?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks your Net Promoter Score over time by calculating the weekly NPS based on all survey responses in each week. A positive score means more promoters than detractors, with +50 considered excellent and +70 world-class.
how_to_read: The blue line shows your NPS score over time (weekly)
Score above 0 means more promoters than detractors
Score above 50 is excellent, above 70 is world-class
Negative scores indicate serious customer satisfaction issues
Look for trends - improving, declining, or stable over time
The green reference line at 50 shows the target threshold
decisions_answered: Is our customer satisfaction improving or declining over time?
Did recent changes to products/service impact customer sentiment?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer satisfaction?
When did significant shifts in customer sentiment occur?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your NPS score over time, typically by week.

The y-axis ranges from negative 100 to positive 100. Above zero means you have more promoters than detractors. The green reference line at 50 marks excellent territory - that's your target.

Watch for patterns. A rising trend means your efforts are working. A declining trend is a warning sign that needs investigation. Sudden drops often correlate with specific events - product issues, service problems, or pricing changes.

Seasonality matters too. Some businesses see natural dips during certain periods. Compare year-over-year to separate seasonal effects from real changes in customer satisfaction.

Y-axis shows customer count (toggle to percentage mode available)
Each color represents a customer segment based on order frequency
Gray (No Purchase) at bottom shows unconverted customers
Green segments (Loyal, VIP) at top represent highest-value customers
Older cohorts should show larger proportions in higher segments
Recent cohorts will be bottom-heavy (mostly One-Time) until they mature
decisions_answered: How does customer quality distribute across different cohorts?
What percentage of each cohort becomes repeat, loyal, or VIP?
Are we successfully moving customers up the loyalty ladder?
Which cohorts produced the most VIP customers?
How long does it take for cohorts to mature into higher segments?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows how customers within each quarterly cohort distribute across behavioral segments.

The segments are based on purchase frequency: customers who never purchased (gray), one-time buyers (red), repeat buyers with 2-3 orders (yellow), loyal customers with 4-6 orders (light green), and VIP customers with 7+ orders (dark green).

Older cohorts on the left should show more green - they've had time to mature into higher segments. Recent cohorts will naturally be mostly gray and red since customers haven't had time to reorder yet.

The key insight is trajectory: Compare Q1 2022 to Q1 2023 at the same maturity point. Are you converting more customers to repeat buyers? Growing your VIP percentage? This reveals whether your retention and loyalty programs are improving.

Switch to percentage mode to normalize for cohort size differences and focus purely on segment mix quality.

payload.formula_explanation: Groups customers by their historical order count and calculates average churn probability for each group. This reveals how purchase frequency correlates with churn risk - typically, customers with more orders have lower churn probability.
how_to_read: Each bar shows average churn probability for customers with that order count
Longer bars indicate higher churn risk for that customer segment
One-time buyers typically show the highest churn risk
As order count increases, churn probability usually decreases
Labels show exact churn percentage for precision
Click any bar to filter and explore customers in that order bucket
decisions_answered: How does purchase frequency correlate with churn risk?
What is the churn risk for one-time buyers vs. repeat customers?
At what order count does churn risk significantly decrease?
Should we focus retention efforts on converting one-time buyers?
payload.narration_text:
This chart reveals how order count correlates with churn risk.

One-time buyers almost always show the highest churn probability - often 80% or higher. They've tried your product once but haven't committed. This is your biggest opportunity.

Watch how churn drops with each additional order. The second purchase is the critical hurdle. Once customers buy a third, fourth, or fifth time, they become significantly more loyal.

Customers with 10+ orders are your advocates. Their churn risk is low because they've proven their loyalty through behavior.

The insight here is clear: focus your retention budget on converting one-time buyers to second-time buyers. That's where you get the biggest reduction in churn risk per dollar spent.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows customer count and average CLV by historical order frequency. Customers with more orders typically show higher CLV and lower churn.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count at each order frequency level
One-order customers are your conversion opportunity
Higher order frequencies correlate with higher CLV shown below
The goal is to move customers rightward to higher frequency
Click any bar to filter and view that customer segment
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are one-time buyers?
How does CLV correlate with order frequency?
Where should second-purchase campaigns focus?
Are we successfully converting to repeat customers?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows customers distributed by historical order count - the key to understanding repeat purchase patterns.

The formula groups customers into order frequency buckets and calculates average CLV for each. More orders typically means higher lifetime value.

Reading this chart: One-order customers typically form the largest bar - this is your biggest conversion opportunity. The CLV metrics below each segment show the value of moving customers up.

The jump from 1 to 2 orders is the critical hurdle. Customers who make a second purchase are far more likely to continue purchasing. Focus retention campaigns here.

Key questions: What's the one-time buyer percentage? How does CLV correlate with frequency? Where should campaigns focus? Use this to design your repeat purchase journey.

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers by when Klaviyo predicts their next order should occur based on historical purchase patterns. Overdue customers have missed their expected purchase window.
how_to_read: Red (Overdue) shows customers who have missed their predicted order window
Orange and yellow show upcoming purchase opportunities
The predicted revenue shows potential value at stake
Overdue customers should receive immediate outreach
Click any segment to view and action those customers
decisions_answered: How many customers have missed their expected purchase date?
What revenue is at risk from overdue orders?
Which customers should receive proactive outreach?
Is our overdue rate improving or worsening?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows customers segmented by expected next order timing - a powerful predictor of retention opportunity.

Klaviyo calculates when each customer should next purchase based on their historical patterns. The formula groups customers by how their expected date compares to today.

Reading this chart: The red Overdue segment represents immediate action needed - these customers have passed their predicted purchase window. The predicted revenue shows what is at stake.

Green and blue segments are healthy - customers expected to purchase in the future. Yellow and orange are approaching their windows and may benefit from proactive outreach.

Key questions: How many are overdue? What revenue is at risk? Who needs outreach now? Use this to trigger timely, personalized purchase reminders.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows which landing pages receive the most traffic and drive the most value. Helps identify high-performing pages to replicate and underperforming pages that need optimization or elimination.

Sort by Revenue to see pages driving the most value
High New Users indicates effective acquisition pages
Avg Engagement shows page quality and relevance

decisions_answered: Which landing pages receive the most traffic?
What pages drive the most revenue attribution?
Which pages have the best engagement metrics?
Where should we focus landing page optimization efforts?

Look for mismatches. A page with high traffic but low revenue may need conversion optimization. A page with great revenue per view but low traffic deserves more promotion.

payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your NPS survey respondents break down into three key segments.

The green slice represents your Promoters - customers who scored 9 or 10. These are your advocates who actively recommend your brand.

The amber slice shows Passives - scores of 7 or 8. They're satisfied but not enthusiastic, and could easily switch to competitors.

The red slice represents Detractors - scores from 0 to 6. These unhappy customers can damage your brand through negative reviews and word-of-mouth.

A healthy distribution has a large green slice and small red slice. If detractors are growing, investigate why. If promoters dominate, leverage them for referrals and testimonials.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the distribution of product review ratings across the 1-5 star scale. A healthy distribution skews toward 4-5 stars, with limited 1-2 star reviews indicating product quality issues.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a star rating (1-5)
Bar height shows the count of reviews with that rating
Green colors indicate positive ratings (4-5 stars)
Red colors indicate negative ratings (1-2 stars)
A J-curve shape (high 5s, low middle, spike at 1) suggests polarized opinions
decisions_answered: What is the overall sentiment from product reviews?
Are most reviews positive (4-5 stars) or negative (1-2 stars)?
Is there a significant number of 3-star reviews indicating mediocrity?
Which products or issues are driving low ratings?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how product reviews distribute across the 1 to 5 star scale.

A healthy distribution has the tallest bar at 5 stars, followed by 4 stars, with small bars at 1-2 stars. This bell curve skewed right indicates satisfied customers.

Watch out for the J-curve pattern - lots of 5-star reviews but also lots of 1-star reviews. This polarization suggests some customers love you while others have significant issues.

Heavy concentration at 3 stars is concerning - it means customers are neither impressed nor complaining. Mediocrity doesn't build loyalty.

If 1-2 star reviews are growing, dig into the comments. Common themes reveal systemic issues that need addressing.

payload.formula_explanation: Composite health score combining lifetime value and churn probability. Excellent/Good customers are your stable core. Fair needs nurturing. Poor requires immediate retention intervention to protect at-risk LTV.
how_to_read: Four gauges show percentage of customers in each health status
Green (Excellent) and blue (Good) are your healthy customer core
Yellow (Fair) customers need nurturing to prevent decline
Red (Poor) represents customers likely to churn - act immediately
The table below shows detailed metrics and LTV at stake
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are in healthy status?
How much LTV is at risk from poor-health customers?
Which health segment needs immediate intervention?
Are customer health trends improving or declining?
payload.narration_text:
This panel provides a comprehensive view of customer health - combining LTV and churn probability into actionable segments.

The formula creates four health tiers. Excellent customers have high LTV and low churn probability. Good is solid but with moderate metrics. Fair shows warning signs. Poor indicates imminent churn risk.

Reading this panel: The four gauges show percentage in each health status. Green and blue represent your stable core. Yellow needs nurturing before declining further. Red requires immediate intervention.

The table below quantifies the stakes. Look at the LTV at Stake column for Poor status - this is the revenue you'll lose if these customers churn without intervention.

Key questions: What percentage is healthy? How much LTV is at risk? Which segment needs urgent action? Use this to prioritize your retention resources effectively.

payload.formula_explanation: Lists products with the most reviews and their average ratings. High review counts with high ratings indicate successful products, while high review counts with low ratings signal products needing attention.
how_to_read: Products are sorted by total review count (most reviewed first)
The Reviews column shows total review volume
Avg Rating shows the mean star rating (out of 5)
Positive % shows the proportion of 4-5 star reviews
Green ratings are excellent (4+), yellow is average (3-4), red is poor (<3)
decisions_answered: Which products are getting the most customer feedback?
Are our best-selling products also our best-rated?
Which products need quality improvement based on ratings?
Should we feature highly-rated products more prominently?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your most-reviewed products and their ratings.

High review count combined with high ratings - that's your star performer. Feature these products prominently and study what makes them successful.

High review count with low ratings is a red flag. These products sell but disappoint. Investigate quality issues, unclear product descriptions, or mismatched expectations.

Low review count with high ratings might be hidden gems. Consider promoting these products to increase visibility.

The Positive Percentage column quickly shows sentiment - above 80% is excellent, below 60% needs attention. Focus improvement efforts on products with high volume but low positive percentages for maximum impact.

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies high-value customers at high churn risk for prioritized retention intervention. These customers have both significant lifetime value (>$100 CLV) and elevated churn probability (70%+), making them the highest priority for personalized outreach.
how_to_read: Table shows customers with both high CLV and high churn probability
Sorted by CLV descending - highest value at-risk customers first
Churn probability is color-coded: red >90%, orange 80-90%, yellow 70-80%
Days inactive shows recency of engagement - red if 90+ days
Search by email or name to find specific customers
Click any row to view full customer profile and history
decisions_answered: Which high-value customers are most at risk of churning?
How much CLV is at risk from our top at-risk customers?
Which customers deserve personalized retention outreach?
What is the engagement history of our most valuable at-risk customers?
payload.narration_text:
This table identifies your highest priority retention targets - customers with both high lifetime value and high churn risk.

The list is sorted by CLV, putting your most valuable at-risk customers at the top. These deserve personalized attention, not just automated emails.

Pay attention to the churn probability and days inactive columns. A customer with $5,000 CLV who is 90% likely to churn and hasn't engaged in 100 days needs immediate, personal outreach.

This is where ROI on retention is highest. Saving one $5,000 CLV customer is worth more than winning back 50 low-value customers. Assign your best customer success resources here.

Export this list for your win-back campaigns, or use it to prioritize phone calls and personalized emails.

The amber slice shows Passives - scores of 7 or 8. They're satisfied but not enthusiastic, and could easily switch to competitors.

The red slice represents Detractors - scores from 0 to 6. These unhappy customers can damage your brand through negative reviews and word-of-mouth.

A healthy distribution has a large green slice and small red slice. If detractors are growing, investigate why. If promoters dominate, leverage them for referrals and testimonials.

payload.formula_explanation: This chart tracks the number of unique customers who made purchases each month over the last 12 months. It provides a direct view of customer engagement and helps identify trends in repeat purchase behavior.
how_to_read: The blue line shows the count of unique customers making purchases each month
Upward trends indicate improving customer retention and engagement
Downward trends may signal issues with customer satisfaction or marketing effectiveness
Hover over any point to see the exact customer count for that month
Click a point to filter the page to that specific month
decisions_answered: Is our customer base growing or shrinking over time?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer purchasing behavior?
When did significant changes in customer engagement occur?
Should we launch re-engagement campaigns based on declining trends?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your purchasing customer count over 12 months.

The blue line shows unique customers making purchases each month. An upward trend means you're keeping customers engaged and coming back. A downward trend signals potential problems.

Look for patterns. Q4 often spikes for retail due to holidays. Post-holiday dips in January are normal. But sustained decline needs attention.

This answers: Is our customer base healthy? When did changes happen? And should we launch win-back campaigns?

payload.formula_explanation: Visualizes customer distribution across order frequency segments from Klaviyo predictive analytics. Segments range from first-time buyers to VIP customers.
how_to_read: Bars show customer count per segment
First-time Buyer (light blue) is typically largest
VIP customers (dark blue) represent highest-value segment
Bar length indicates segment size in absolute numbers
Color progression shows increasing loyalty/value
decisions_answered: How many first-time vs repeat customers do we have?
Is our customer base weighted toward new or loyal buyers?
What is the size of our VIP customer segment?
Are we successfully converting first-time buyers to repeat?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows your customer base segmented by purchase frequency.

The segments are: First-time Buyer with 1 order, Repeat with 2-3 orders, Loyal with 4-6 orders, and VIP with 7 or more. Data comes from Klaviyo's predictive analytics.

Bar length shows absolute customer count per segment. The color progression from light to dark blue reflects increasing customer value.

This answers important questions: How many first-time versus repeat customers do you have? What's the VIP segment size? And are you converting first-timers into loyal buyers?

payload.formula_explanation: Displays a live feed of recent customer feedback including NPS responses, product reviews, and support requests. Allows quick monitoring of emerging issues or praise.
how_to_read: Feedback items are sorted by recency (newest first)
Badge color indicates sentiment: green (positive), gray (neutral), red (negative)
Rating number shows the NPS score or star rating
Feedback type shows the source (NPS Survey, Product Review, etc.)
Click items to view full feedback details and customer profile
decisions_answered: What are customers saying about us right now?
Are there emerging issues we should address immediately?
Which customers recently gave exceptional praise?
Are feedback patterns changing in real-time?
payload.narration_text:
This live feed shows your most recent customer feedback, giving you a pulse on what customers are saying right now.

Watch for patterns in real-time. Multiple negative feedbacks about the same issue within hours signals something urgent that needs investigation.

The sentiment badges give quick visual cues. A stream of green badges means happy customers. Red badges appearing frequently indicate growing problems.

Use this for daily monitoring. Check it each morning to catch any overnight issues. After launching new features or promotions, watch the feed closely for immediate customer reactions.

This is your early warning system. Acting quickly on negative feedback can turn detractors into promoters.

payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive table showing detailed metrics for each monthly cohort. Enables comparison of acquisition quality, customer development, and lifetime value across time periods.
how_to_read: Each row is a monthly cohort, newest at top
Cohort Size shows customers acquired that month
Avg CLV and Total LTV show value metrics
Churn Risk is color-coded (red = high risk)
Converted/Repeat/VIP show customer development counts
Q1 months (Jan-Mar) often highlighted as spring season
Sort by any column to rank cohorts
decisions_answered: Which monthly cohorts perform best across all metrics?
How do spring cohorts compare to other seasons?
Which cohorts have the highest/lowest churn risk?
What is the VIP conversion rate for each cohort?
Are recent cohorts on track compared to historical benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This comprehensive table provides detailed metrics for every monthly cohort in your database.

Each row represents customers acquired in that specific month. You can see cohort size, average and total CLV, order metrics, and customer development progression from converted to repeat to VIP.

The Churn Risk column is color-coded for quick identification. Red indicates cohorts with elevated churn probability that may need immediate retention attention. Green indicates healthy, stable cohorts.

Q1 rows (January through March) are often highlighted because spring is critical for gardening businesses. Compare Q1 cohorts across years to see if acquisition quality is improving.

Use the sorting capability to rank cohorts by any metric. Sort by Avg CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition months. Sort by VIP count to find cohorts that produced the most high-value customers.

This table is your cohort analysis command center - it aggregates everything you need to evaluate customer acquisition and development performance.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares revenue generated from first-time purchases versus repeat purchases over the last 30 days. A healthy business typically sees 40-60% of revenue from repeat customers, indicating strong retention.
how_to_read: Blue bar shows revenue from customers making their first purchase
Green bar shows revenue from returning customers
Longer repeat bar indicates strong retention and customer loyalty
Below each bar, see order counts and average order values
Compare AOV between segments - repeat customers often spend more
decisions_answered: What percentage of revenue comes from repeat customers?
Is our revenue mix healthy (40-60% from repeat)?
Do repeat customers have higher average order values?
Should we invest more in acquisition or retention?
payload.narration_text:
This comparison chart shows where your revenue comes from - first purchases versus repeat purchases.

The blue bar is new customer revenue. The green bar is revenue from customers who've bought before. For a healthy e-commerce business, repeat revenue should be 40-60% of total.

If first purchase dominates, you might have a retention problem - customers aren't coming back. If repeat dominates heavily, you might have an acquisition problem - not enough new customers entering the funnel.

The AOV comparison below matters too. If repeat customers spend more per order, that's proof that retention investments pay off. Your best customers get better over time.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares customer quality metrics across different acquisition sources. The email marketing method field indicates how customers first subscribed (API, Magento, List Import, etc.), serving as a proxy for acquisition channel.
how_to_read: Each row represents an acquisition source/method
Customers column shows total acquired through that channel
Avg CLV reveals which sources bring highest-value customers
Total LTV shows absolute value contribution
Repeat Rate indicates loyalty potential from each source
Churn Risk highlights which sources produce at-risk customers
Table is sortable by any column - click headers to sort
decisions_answered: Which acquisition sources produce the highest-value customers?
Where should we invest more marketing budget?
Which sources have the best repeat purchase rates?
Are certain sources associated with higher churn risk?
What is the ROI potential of each acquisition channel?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your customer acquisition sources by quality and value metrics.

Each row represents a different method by which customers entered your database - API integrations, e-commerce platform imports, list uploads, and more. The metrics reveal which sources bring your best customers.

Sort by Average CLV to find your highest-quality acquisition sources. These deserve more investment. But don't ignore volume - a source with slightly lower average CLV but 10x the customers might generate more total value.

Repeat Rate is a key quality indicator. Sources with high repeat rates produce engaged customers who come back. Low repeat rates might indicate promotional-driven acquisitions who bought once for a discount and never returned.

The Churn Risk column is a warning signal. High churn risk sources might need different lifecycle marketing approaches, or they might not be worth the acquisition investment at all.

payload.formula_explanation: The share of customers who have placed two or more orders out of all customers who have placed at least one. It measures how well first-time buyers are converted into repeat buyers.
how_to_read: The value is the percentage of buyers who came back for a second order
Higher rates mean stronger retention of first-time purchasers
The change versus the prior period shows whether loyalty is improving
A low repeat rate points to a one-and-done acquisition problem
Pair it with Avg Orders to see depth of repeat behavior
decisions_answered: What share of our customers buy more than once?
Is our repeat-purchase conversion improving or declining?
Do we need stronger post-purchase or win-back flows?

payload.formula_text: (Active Customers in Month N / Total Cohort Size) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows the percentage of customers from each cohort who made a purchase in subsequent months. Cohorts are grouped by acquisition month, and retention is calculated as the percentage of the original cohort still actively purchasing at each time interval.
how_to_read: Y-axis shows cohort months (most recent at top), with cohort size in parentheses
X-axis shows months since signup (M0 = first month, M24 = two years later)
Cell color intensity indicates retention rate - darker green = higher retention
Month 0 (M0) shows initial purchase rate, typically highest
Look for diagonal patterns to understand typical retention decay curves
The M12 column often shows a bump due to annual seasonal purchase cycles
decisions_answered: How does customer retention change over time for each cohort?
Which cohorts have the strongest/weakest retention curves?
At what month do we see the biggest retention drop-off?
Is there an annual purchase cycle (M12 bump) in our customer behavior?
Are recent cohorts retaining better or worse than historical cohorts?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap visualizes how customer cohorts retain over time after their initial acquisition.

Each row represents a monthly cohort - a group of customers who first appeared in that month. The cohort size shows how many customers were acquired. As you move right across the row, you see what percentage of those original customers made a purchase in subsequent months.

Month 0 shows the initial purchase rate. The natural decay as you move right is expected - not all customers continue purchasing. However, pay attention to patterns: Do you see a bump at Month 12? This often indicates seasonal or annual purchase behavior - customers who bought seeds last spring return again this spring.

Compare cohorts vertically to identify quality variations. If Q1 cohorts consistently show better retention than Q3 cohorts, this suggests seasonal acquisition quality differences. Spring gardening season customers may be more engaged.

Use this to identify optimal intervention timing - where does retention drop most sharply? That's when lifecycle emails and retention campaigns should trigger.

payload.formula_text: COUNT(customer_id WHERE order_count > 1 AND last_order IN period)
payload.formula_explanation: Counts customers who have made two or more purchases historically. The retention rate shows what percentage of customers with at least one order have returned to purchase again.
how_to_read: The main value shows customers with 2+ historical purchases
The retention rate badge indicates repeat purchase percentage
High retention (60%+) suggests strong product-market fit
Low retention may indicate product quality or experience issues
Click to analyze retention patterns and customer lifecycle
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers make repeat purchases?
Is our retention rate healthy compared to benchmarks?
Are we successfully converting one-time buyers to repeat customers?
Do we need to invest more in retention marketing?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI measures returning customers - those who have purchased from you more than once. Retention is often more valuable than acquisition.

The formula counts customers with two or more historical orders. The retention rate divides this by total customers who have made at least one purchase.

Reading this card: The main number shows your repeat customer count. The badge displays your retention rate percentage. For e-commerce, 60 percent or higher is considered healthy.

Why this matters: Acquiring a new customer costs 5 to 25 times more than retaining one. A high retention rate means strong customer satisfaction and product-market fit.

Key questions: What's our retention rate? Are we converting one-time buyers? Should we invest more in retention marketing? Click to explore customer lifecycle patterns.

payload.formula_explanation: Splits monthly revenue between first-time purchasers and repeat customers. Healthy businesses derive 60%+ of revenue from returning customers, indicating strong retention and customer satisfaction.
how_to_read: Light green bars show revenue from first-time purchasers
Dark green bars show revenue from repeat customers
Healthy businesses have taller dark green (returning) segments
The 40/60 benchmark line shows ideal new/returning split
Click any month to analyze revenue composition in detail
decisions_answered: What percentage of revenue comes from repeat customers?
Is our returning customer revenue share improving?
Are we too dependent on new customer acquisition?
Do we need to invest more in retention to balance revenue?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows your monthly revenue split between new and returning customers - a key indicator of business health.

The formula sums order values, separating first-time purchasers from those with previous orders. The returning share percentage shows retention's contribution to revenue.

Reading this chart: Light green shows first-time purchase revenue. Dark green shows repeat customer revenue. Ideally, dark green dominates - 60 percent or more from returning customers indicates a healthy business.

Why this matters: New customer revenue is expensive to generate. Returning customer revenue has near-zero acquisition cost and higher margins. Businesses too dependent on new customers face growth challenges.

Key questions: What's the revenue split? Is returning share growing? Do you need more retention investment? Click any month for deeper revenue analysis.

payload.formula_explanation: Splits monthly revenue between first-time purchasers and repeat customers. Healthy businesses derive 60%+ of revenue from returning customers, indicating strong retention and customer satisfaction.
how_to_read: Light green bars show revenue from first-time purchasers
Dark green bars show revenue from repeat customers
Healthy businesses have taller dark green (returning) segments
The 40/60 benchmark line shows ideal new/returning split
Click any month to analyze revenue composition in detail
decisions_answered: What percentage of revenue comes from repeat customers?
Is our returning customer revenue share improving?
Are we too dependent on new customer acquisition?
Do we need to invest more in retention to balance revenue?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked bar chart shows your monthly revenue split between new and returning customers - a key indicator of business health.

The formula sums order values, separating first-time purchasers from those with previous orders. The returning share percentage shows retention's contribution to revenue.
