payload.narration_text:
The campaign performance table gives you a complete view of all your video campaigns with key metrics in one place.

Standard formulas apply here: View rate is views divided by impressions, CPV is spend divided by views, and ROAS is conversion value divided by spend. Each row represents one campaign.

To read this table effectively, use the sort functionality. Click any column header to rank campaigns by that metric. Color coding helps quickly identify performance: green indicates strong ROAS, while red flags high CPV.

This table answers the critical allocation questions: Which campaigns deserve more budget? Which are underperforming? And how do different video formats compare? Use this as your go-to reference for campaign-level optimization decisions.

payload.formula_explanation: This horizontal bar chart breaks down video performance by device type: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV. Each bar represents total video views on that device, while the color intensity can indicate view rate efficiency.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate more video views from that device type
Compare bar lengths to understand where your audience watches videos
Mobile typically dominates YouTube viewing, but Connected TV is growing rapidly
Consider device-specific creative optimizations based on viewing patterns
Click any bar to filter all page metrics by that device type
decisions_answered: Which devices generate the most video views for our campaigns?
Should we create device-specific video creatives?
Is our Connected TV presence growing compared to mobile?
How should we adjust bidding strategies by device?
payload.narration_text:
This device performance chart reveals where your audience is actually watching your video ads, which is crucial for creative and bidding optimization.

The formula calculates view rate and share for each device category: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV. Each bar length represents total video views from that device.

When reading this chart, pay attention to which device dominates. Mobile typically leads for YouTube, but Connected TV is the fastest-growing segment. Longer bars mean more views from that device.

Understanding device distribution helps answer key questions: Where should you focus creative efforts? Should you bid differently by device? And is your Connected TV strategy keeping pace with market trends? Use these insights to optimize your video creative and targeting strategy.

payload.formula_explanation: The funnel visualizes viewer retention through four quartiles of video playback. Starting from initial views, it shows what percentage of viewers reach 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% completion. The drop-off between stages reveals where viewers lose interest.
how_to_read: Top of funnel represents all video views (100% starting point)
Each subsequent level shows the percentage of viewers who reached that quartile
Width of each stage is proportional to the viewer percentage retained
The gap between stages indicates drop-off - larger gaps mean more viewers leaving
The bottom level shows your completion rate - viewers who watched the entire video
decisions_answered: At what point in our videos do most viewers drop off?
Is our video content engaging enough to keep viewers watching?
Should we shorten our video ads based on completion patterns?
Which video formats (skippable, non-skippable, bumper) retain viewers best?
payload.narration_text:
The video completion funnel is one of the most insightful charts for understanding viewer engagement with your video content.

The formula here measures quartile retention: what percentage of viewers reach each 25% milestone in your video. Drop-off rate is calculated as the difference between early and late quartiles, divided by the starting point.

Reading this funnel is intuitive. The top represents all initial views. As you move down, each level shows how many viewers reached that point. A steep narrowing indicates high drop-off early in videos, while a gradual taper suggests strong engagement.

This answers essential questions: Where do viewers lose interest? Are videos too long? Should you front-load key messages? Understanding this funnel helps optimize video creative to maximize completed views.

payload.display_name: View Rate by Hour and Day
payload.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows video view rate across all combinations of hour of day (0-23) and day of week. Darker cells indicate higher view rates, revealing optimal scheduling patterns for video campaigns.
how_to_read: X-axis shows hour of day from midnight (00) to 11 PM (23)
Y-axis shows days of the week from Monday to Sunday
Darker/warmer colors indicate higher view rates - better performance
Lighter/cooler colors indicate lower view rates - less engagement
Look for clusters of high-performing time slots to inform ad scheduling
decisions_answered: What are the best hours and days to run video ads?
Should we use dayparting to focus budget on high-performing time slots?
Are weekends or weekdays better for video engagement?
How does evening performance compare to daytime?
payload.narration_text:
The hour-day heatmap reveals hidden patterns in when your video ads perform best, which is essential for ad scheduling optimization.

The calculation is simple: each cell shows the average view rate for that specific hour and day combination. Colors range from cool to warm based on performance intensity.

Reading this heatmap, look for clusters of dark cells - these are your golden time slots. Common patterns include evening peaks, weekend variations, and lunch hour bumps. Light areas show times when viewers are less engaged.

This answers scheduling questions: When should you concentrate your video budget? Should you use dayparting? Are weekends worth the higher CPMs? Use this data to create custom ad schedules that maximize your view rate and efficiency.

payload.formula_explanation: Total ad impressions across all YouTube/Video campaigns over the selected date range. An impression counts each time an ad is shown, whether or not it becomes a view.
how_to_read: Shows total impressions across all YouTube campaigns
Current period only; this secondary KPI has no comparison or sparkline
Pairs with view rate to gauge how many impressions become views
Higher impressions indicate broader ad delivery
Filtered to video campaigns
decisions_answered: How much ad delivery are YouTube campaigns achieving?
Is impression volume sufficient for reach goals?
How does delivery compare to view volume?

payload.formula_text: Network Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS) / 1,000,000 WHERE AD_NETWORK_TYPE = X
payload.formula_explanation: This donut chart shows how your video views are distributed across Google's video networks: YouTube Watch, YouTube Search, Video Partners, and Google TV. The center displays total views across all networks.
how_to_read: Larger slices indicate networks generating more video views
Center value shows total video views across all networks
YouTube Watch (in-stream) typically dominates video inventory
Video Partners extends reach beyond YouTube to partner sites
Hover over any slice to see exact view count and percentage share
decisions_answered: Which video networks drive the most views for our campaigns?
Should we expand to Video Partners network for more reach?
How does YouTube Search discovery perform compared to in-stream?
Is our network distribution aligned with our brand safety requirements?
payload.narration_text:
This network distribution chart shows where your video ads are actually being viewed across Google's video ecosystem.

The formula is straightforward: each network's views divided by total views gives you the percentage share. The donut visualization makes it easy to see relative proportions at a glance.

When reading this chart, note that YouTube Watch typically dominates - that's in-stream ads on YouTube videos. YouTube Search shows discovery ads. Video Partners extends your reach to Google's network of partner sites. The center value gives you total views.

Understanding network distribution helps answer strategic questions: Should you expand to Video Partners for more reach? Is YouTube Search worth the investment? And is your distribution aligned with brand safety requirements? Use this to inform network targeting decisions.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks daily cart abandonment trends including rate, count, and total value left behind. This reveals how much potential revenue is being lost when customers add items to cart but do not complete checkout.
how_to_read: The line chart shows daily abandonment rate over the selected period
Higher values indicate more carts being abandoned that day
Look for patterns on specific days of the week or around promotions
Summary metrics above the chart show totals for the period
Sudden spikes may indicate checkout issues or site problems
decisions_answered: What is our cart abandonment rate and is it improving?
How much revenue are we losing to abandoned carts?
Are there specific days or periods with higher abandonment?
Is our checkout optimization having a measurable impact?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your daily cart abandonment trends over time.

The abandonment rate formula divides abandoned carts by total carts created, times 100. The abandoned value sums up the dollar amount left behind in incomplete checkouts.

Watch the trend line. A rising rate means more customers are leaving without buying. Industry average for e-commerce is typically 60 to 80 percent, so context matters.

Look for patterns. Higher abandonment on certain days might indicate technical issues. Spikes around promotions could mean pricing friction. A steady decline shows your checkout optimizations are working.

This answers: What is our abandonment rate? How much revenue are we losing? Are there problematic days? And are our improvements making a difference?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks daily cart abandonment trends including rate, count, and total value left behind. This reveals how much potential revenue is being lost when customers add items to cart but do not complete checkout.
how_to_read: The line chart shows daily abandonment rate over the selected period
Higher values indicate more carts being abandoned that day
Look for patterns on specific days of the week or around promotions
Summary metrics above the chart show totals for the period
Sudden spikes may indicate checkout issues or site problems
decisions_answered: What is our cart abandonment rate and is it improving?
How much revenue are we losing to abandoned carts?
Are there specific days or periods with higher abandonment?
Is our checkout optimization having a measurable impact?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your daily cart abandonment trends over time.

The abandonment rate formula divides abandoned carts by total carts created, times 100. The abandoned value sums up the dollar amount left behind in incomplete checkouts.

Watch the trend line. A rising rate means more customers are leaving without buying. Industry average for e-commerce is typically 60 to 80 percent, so context matters.

Look for patterns. Higher abandonment on certain days might indicate technical issues. Spikes around promotions could mean pricing friction. A steady decline shows your checkout optimizations are working.

This answers: What is our abandonment rate? How much revenue are we losing? Are there problematic days? And are our improvements making a difference?

payload.formula_explanation: Average number of orders per customer in the period, dividing total orders by unique customers. It measures purchase frequency.
how_to_read: The main value shows average orders per customer
Compare to the prior period to track frequency
The sparkline shows the trend over time
Higher frequency lifts LTV without new acquisition
Click to explore RFM frequency segments
decisions_answered: How often does a typical customer order?
Is purchase frequency improving?
Can we drive more repeat orders from existing customers?

payload.formula_explanation: Calculates the average order value by dividing total revenue by order count for each day. AOV is a key profitability metric - higher AOV typically means better margins.
how_to_read: The line shows daily average order value fluctuations
The period average is displayed for context
Rising AOV with stable orders indicates upselling success
Falling AOV may indicate discounting or product mix shifts
Spikes may indicate high-value orders or bulk purchases
decisions_answered: Is our average order value improving over time?
Are upselling and cross-selling strategies working?
How do promotions impact average order value?
What customer segments drive higher AOV?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your average order value over time.

The formula is straightforward: total revenue divided by total orders for each day. The period average gives you a benchmark to compare daily fluctuations.

Rising AOV is generally positive - customers are spending more per transaction. This could indicate successful upselling, cross-selling, or premium product adoption.

Falling AOV needs investigation. Heavy discounting, shifts in product mix, or changes in customer segments can all reduce AOV. A lower AOV isn't always bad if order volume compensates.

Watch for spikes - they often indicate bulk purchases or high-value customers. These might be B2B buyers or VIPs worth special attention.

This answers: How much do customers spend on average? Are our strategies to increase basket size working? And what factors influence order value?

payload.formula_explanation: Average lifetime value per customer in the period, dividing total gross revenue by unique customers. It measures the revenue worth of a typical customer.
how_to_read: The main value shows average revenue per customer
Compare to the prior period to track customer value
The sparkline shows the LTV trend
Rising LTV justifies higher acquisition spend
Click to see the LTV distribution by segment
decisions_answered: How much is a typical customer worth?
Is customer value growing over time?
How much can we afford to spend on acquisition?

payload.formula_explanation: Average gap in days between a customer's orders, computed as the mean of daily AVG_DAYS_BETWEEN_ORDERS values over rows with data. Shorter gaps indicate more frequent purchasing.
how_to_read: The main value shows the average days between repeat orders
Compare to the prior period to track purchase cadence
The sparkline shows the trend over time
A shrinking gap means customers are buying more often
Click to explore frequency and recency segments
decisions_answered: How long until a customer typically buys again?
Is repurchase cadence speeding up or slowing down?
When should re-engagement campaigns fire?

payload.formula_explanation: Share of orders that were canceled over the period. A rising rate points to stock-outs, payment failures, or customer-side cancellations.
how_to_read: The main value shows the cancellation rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to monitor order quality
The sparkline shows the daily cancellation trend
Spikes warrant a look at inventory and payment flows
Click to break cancellations down by status and method
decisions_answered: What share of orders are we losing to cancellation?
Is cancellation pressure rising this period?
Are stock-outs or payment issues driving cancellations?

payload.formula_explanation: Share of carts that were started but never converted to an order, computed as abandoned carts over all carts (abandoned plus converted). It measures checkout friction.
how_to_read: The main value shows the cart abandonment rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to track checkout friction
The sparkline shows the daily abandonment trend
High abandonment points to shipping cost, account, or payment friction
Click to analyze abandoned-cart value and recovery
decisions_answered: How many shoppers abandon their carts?
Is checkout friction getting worse?
How much revenue is stuck in abandoned carts?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates revenue and order counts by product category to show which categories drive the most business. Helps identify top-performing categories and underperformers that may need attention.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product category with its total revenue and order count
Sort by revenue to find your highest-earning categories
Sort by orders to see which categories have the most purchase activity
Compare revenue per order across categories to understand average ticket size
Low revenue with high orders indicates lower-priced category items
decisions_answered: Which product categories generate the most revenue?
Are there underperforming categories that need attention?
How is revenue distributed across our product catalog?
Which categories should we prioritize for marketing and inventory?
payload.narration_text:
This chart breaks down performance by product category.

The formula aggregates revenue and order counts for each category, giving you a clear view of which parts of your catalog drive the most business.

Reading this: Look for your top categories by revenue. Are they also your most-ordered categories? If a category has high revenue but few orders, it contains premium products. If it has many orders but low revenue, those are your accessible everyday items.

Compare categories to understand balance. Heavy reliance on one category is risky. A diversified catalog with multiple strong categories is more resilient.

This answers: Which categories matter most? Where should we invest in inventory and marketing? And is our catalog balanced or too concentrated?

payload.formula_text: Category Revenue = SUM(ITEM_REVENUE) WHERE DATE BETWEEN start_date AND end_date
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates revenue and order counts by product category to show which categories drive the most business. Helps identify top-performing categories and underperformers that may need attention.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product category with its total revenue and order count
Sort by revenue to find your highest-earning categories
Sort by orders to see which categories have the most purchase activity
Compare revenue per order across categories to understand average ticket size
Low revenue with high orders indicates lower-priced category items
decisions_answered: Which product categories generate the most revenue?
Are there underperforming categories that need attention?
How is revenue distributed across our product catalog?
Which categories should we prioritize for marketing and inventory?
payload.narration_text:
This chart breaks down performance by product category.

The formula aggregates revenue and order counts for each category, giving you a clear view of which parts of your catalog drive the most business.

Reading this: Look for your top categories by revenue. Are they also your most-ordered categories? If a category has high revenue but few orders, it contains premium products. If it has many orders but low revenue, those are your accessible everyday items.

Compare categories to understand balance. Heavy reliance on one category is risky. A diversified catalog with multiple strong categories is more resilient.

This answers: Which categories matter most? Where should we invest in inventory and marketing? And is our catalog balanced or too concentrated?

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers into churn risk tiers based on recency of last purchase, historical purchase frequency, and monetary value. Higher risk tiers indicate customers who are increasingly likely to churn.
how_to_read: The funnel shows customers flowing from low risk to high risk tiers
Wider sections indicate more customers at that risk level
Active customers at the top are your healthy base
At-risk and churned segments at the bottom need intervention
Compare tier sizes over time to track churn prevention effectiveness
decisions_answered: How many customers are at risk of churning?
Is our churn risk distribution improving or worsening?
Which risk tier should we prioritize for win-back campaigns?
How effective are our retention efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel chart visualizes how your customers are distributed across churn risk tiers.

The formula analyzes each customer's recency, frequency, and monetary value to assign a risk level. Customers who haven't purchased recently and show declining patterns are flagged as higher risk.

Reading from top to bottom: active customers at the top are healthy. As you move down, risk increases. The bottom tiers represent customers who are likely churning or already lost.

Watch the proportions. If the at-risk tier is growing relative to active, your retention is weakening. A shrinking high-risk tier means win-back campaigns are working.

This answers: How many customers are we losing? Where should we focus retention efforts? And are our churn prevention strategies working?

payload.formula_explanation: Number of orders that reached the completed state over the period. It is the success endpoint of the fulfillment funnel.
how_to_read: The main value shows orders that reached completion
Compare to total orders to gauge completion rate
The sparkline shows the daily completed-order trend
A growing gap from total orders signals fulfillment friction
Click to inspect the full fulfillment funnel
decisions_answered: How many orders successfully completed?
Is our completion throughput keeping up with demand?
Where are orders dropping out of the funnel?

payload.formula_explanation: Uses market basket analysis to identify product pairs frequently purchased together. Lift measures how much more likely products are bought together versus independently. Confidence measures the probability of buying product B given product A was purchased.
how_to_read: Each row shows a product pair that customers frequently buy together
Higher lift values indicate stronger product association
Confidence shows the probability of the cross-sell succeeding
Co-purchase count shows how many times the pair was bought together
Strength rating summarizes the overall cross-sell potential
decisions_answered: Which products should we recommend together?
What are our strongest product associations?
Which cross-sell opportunities have the highest confidence?
How can we increase basket size through product bundling?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows product pairs that customers frequently purchase together, revealing cross-sell opportunities.

The formula uses market basket analysis. Lift measures how much more likely two products are bought together versus independently. A lift of 2.0 means customers are twice as likely to buy both products together. Confidence measures the probability that buying product A leads to buying product B.

Higher lift and confidence values indicate stronger opportunities. Focus on pairs with both high lift and sufficient co-purchase volume for reliable recommendations.

Use these insights to power product recommendations, create bundles, and design targeted cross-sell campaigns.

This answers: What products should we recommend together? Which pairings have the strongest affinity? And how can we increase average basket size?

payload.formula_explanation: Ranks customers by their total lifetime value - the sum of all their orders. This identifies your most valuable customers who deserve special attention.
how_to_read: Customers ranked from highest to lowest lifetime value
LTV shows total revenue from each customer
Orders column shows purchase frequency
Avg Order shows typical transaction size
RFM Segment indicates current customer health
decisions_answered: Who are our most valuable customers?
What characteristics do top customers share?
Are our VIP customers still active?
How can we replicate our best customer relationships?
payload.narration_text:
This table lists your most valuable customers ranked by total lifetime value.

The formula sums all purchases for each customer and sorts from highest to lowest.

Each row shows customer details, total LTV, order count, average order value, RFM segment, and last order date. High LTV with many orders indicates a consistent loyal customer. High LTV with few orders suggests high-ticket purchases.

Check the RFM segment column. Your top customers should be Champions or Loyal. If you see At-Risk status, act immediately - losing a top customer is costly.

Study what top customers have in common. Acquisition channel, product preferences, timing patterns. These insights help you find and develop more high-value customers.

This answers: Who are our VIPs? Are they still active and engaged? And what can we learn from our best customer relationships?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts unique customers and categorizes them by type (new vs returning, guest vs registered, etc.). This shows the composition of your customer base during the selected period.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars compare customer counts across different types
Longer bars indicate more customers of that type
New vs Returning split shows acquisition vs retention balance
Guest vs Registered shows account creation adoption
Compare proportions to understand your customer portfolio health
decisions_answered: What percentage of our customers are new vs returning?
Are we retaining customers effectively?
How many customers create accounts vs checkout as guests?
Is our customer mix healthy for sustainable growth?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down your customer base by type.

The formula counts unique customers and groups them by category - new versus returning, guest versus registered, and other relevant segments.

How to read this: Each bar represents one customer type. Longer bars mean more customers in that category. A healthy business typically shows strong returning customer numbers alongside steady new customer acquisition.

If new customers dominate, you may have retention issues. If returning customers dominate with few new ones, growth may stall. The ideal mix depends on your business stage and goals.

Guest versus registered splits help understand account adoption. More registered customers enable better marketing and personalization.

This answers: What does our customer portfolio look like? Are we balancing acquisition with retention? And how can we optimize our customer mix?

payload.formula_explanation: Count of active customers in the period. Note: the backend currently computes this identically to Total Customers - there is no separate activity filter applied.
how_to_read: The main value shows active customers for the period
Currently equals total customers (no distinct active filter in backend)
The sparkline shows the trend over time
Pair with repeat rate to gauge engagement depth
Click to explore segments and health scores
decisions_answered: How many customers are active this period?
Is our active base growing?
How engaged is the active base?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks orders through the fulfillment pipeline from placement to completion. Each stage shows how many orders have reached that milestone, revealing where orders get stuck.
how_to_read: Bars represent sequential fulfillment stages
Healthy funnels show minimal drop-off between stages
Large gaps indicate bottlenecks at that transition
The final bar shows successfully completed orders
Compare stage volumes to identify process improvements
decisions_answered: Where are orders getting stuck in fulfillment?
How quickly do orders move through each stage?
What percentage of orders reach completion?
Which fulfillment stage needs the most improvement?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel chart tracks orders through your fulfillment pipeline.

The formula counts orders at each stage: total orders placed, orders invoiced, orders shipped, and orders completed.

A healthy funnel shows minimal drop-off between stages. If 1000 orders come in, ideally 950+ complete successfully. Large gaps between stages reveal problems.

A big drop from orders to invoiced might indicate payment failures or fraud holds. From invoiced to shipped could be inventory or picking issues. Shipped to completed might indicate delivery problems or customer disputes.

Use this to prioritize process improvements. Fix the biggest gap first for maximum impact.

This answers: How efficient is our fulfillment process? Where are orders getting stuck? And what operational changes would have the biggest impact?

payload.formula_explanation: The difference between GA4-reported total revenue and actual Magento store revenue, with the percentage taken relative to Magento revenue. A positive gap means GA4 over-reports versus the store's actual sales of record.
how_to_read: The headline value is GA4 total revenue minus actual Magento revenue.
The percentage is the gap divided by Magento revenue (the source of truth).
A positive gap means GA4 is over-counting revenue relative to real store sales.
Warning is flagged above 10% and critical above 25% over-reporting.
decisions_answered: How closely does GA4 revenue match my actual store revenue?
Is GA4 over- or under-counting versus Magento?
Has GA4 tracking accuracy degraded relative to real sales?

payload.formula_text: SUM(CASE WHEN CUSTOMER_TYPE = 'guest' THEN TOTAL_ORDERS ELSE 0 END) / SUM(TOTAL_ORDERS) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Share of orders placed without a registered account, computed from MART_MAGENTO_DAILY. A high rate represents an opportunity to convert guests into known, retainable customers.
how_to_read: The main value shows the guest-checkout share of orders
Compare to the prior period to track account adoption
The sparkline shows the guest-rate trend
Lowering the rate grows the addressable retention base
Click to explore the customer mix in more detail
decisions_answered: What share of orders come from guests?
Are we converting guests into registered customers?
How much retention upside is locked in guest checkout?

payload.formula_explanation: Uses market basket analysis to identify product pairs frequently purchased together. Lift measures how much more likely products are bought together versus independently. Confidence measures the probability of buying product B given product A was purchased.
how_to_read: Each row shows a product pair that customers frequently buy together
Higher lift values indicate stronger product association
Confidence shows the probability of the cross-sell succeeding
Co-purchase count shows how many times the pair was bought together
Strength rating summarizes the overall cross-sell potential
decisions_answered: Which products should we recommend together?
What are our strongest product associations?
Which cross-sell opportunities have the highest confidence?
How can we increase basket size through product bundling?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows product pairs that customers frequently purchase together, revealing cross-sell opportunities.

The formula uses market basket analysis. Lift measures how much more likely two products are bought together versus independently. A lift of 2.0 means customers are twice as likely to buy both products together. Confidence measures the probability that buying product A leads to buying product B.

Higher lift and confidence values indicate stronger opportunities. Focus on pairs with both high lift and sufficient co-purchase volume for reliable recommendations.

Use these insights to power product recommendations, create bundles, and design targeted cross-sell campaigns.

This answers: What products should we recommend together? Which pairings have the strongest affinity? And how can we increase average basket size?

payload.formula_explanation: Calculates total lifetime value for each customer and groups them into value ranges. This shows the distribution of customer value across your base.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a lifetime value range
Taller bars indicate more customers in that range
A healthy distribution shows customers across multiple tiers
Heavy concentration in low tiers may indicate retention issues
High-value tiers represent your VIP customers
decisions_answered: How is customer value distributed across our base?
What percentage of customers are high-value?
Is there opportunity to move customers to higher tiers?
Where should we focus retention efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how customer lifetime value is distributed across your customer base.

The formula sums all purchases for each customer, then groups them into value ranges or tiers.

Each bar represents one value range. Taller bars mean more customers at that level. A healthy business shows customers distributed across tiers, with strategies to move customers upward.

If most customers cluster in the lowest tier, focus on second purchase campaigns. A strong mid-tier indicates good repeat customers. A meaningful high-value tier represents your VIP segment deserving special treatment.

The 80/20 rule often applies - a small percentage of customers drive a large share of revenue. Identify and nurture these high-value relationships.

This answers: How is value distributed across our customer base? What percentage are truly valuable? And where should we focus customer development efforts?

payload.formula_explanation: Recommends targeted marketing actions based on customer segments, their size, and revenue potential. Actions are prioritized by urgency and expected impact to help allocate marketing resources effectively.
how_to_read: Each row represents a recommended marketing action
Customer count shows the addressable audience for each action
Revenue potential estimates the value of successfully engaging the segment
Priority score indicates urgency - higher scores need immediate attention
Sort by priority to focus on the most impactful actions first
decisions_answered: What marketing campaigns should we prioritize?
Which customer segments need immediate attention?
What is the potential revenue impact of each action?
How should we allocate marketing resources across segments?
payload.narration_text:
This table provides actionable marketing recommendations based on your customer data.

The formula evaluates each customer segment's size, revenue potential, and risk level to prioritize marketing actions. Higher priority actions have the greatest urgency or potential impact.

Each action targets a specific customer behavior. Reactivation campaigns target lapsed customers. Retention actions protect at-risk relationships. Upsell and cross-sell target growth opportunities.

Focus on the highest-priority actions first. The revenue potential column helps justify budget allocation. The customer count shows the scale of each opportunity.

This answers: What should we do next? Where will marketing spend have the most impact? And which customer segments need immediate attention?

payload.formula_explanation: Sums order revenue over time periods (daily, weekly, or monthly depending on granularity setting). This shows revenue trajectory and helps identify trends.
how_to_read: The area chart shows revenue accumulation over time
Rising trends indicate revenue growth
Plateaus may indicate market saturation or seasonal slowdowns
Sharp drops warrant immediate investigation
Compare different periods to spot year-over-year changes
decisions_answered: Is our revenue growing, flat, or declining?
What periods show the strongest performance?
Are there seasonal patterns in our revenue?
How does current performance compare to historical trends?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart visualizes your revenue trajectory over time.

The formula sums order totals for each time period - daily, weekly, or monthly depending on your view setting.

The filled area helps you see the cumulative pattern. Rising trends are healthy. Flat lines might be okay or might indicate stagnation depending on your goals. Declining trends need attention.

Look for patterns. Many businesses see seasonal cycles - strong Q4 for retail, summer dips, back-to-school spikes. Understanding your seasonality helps with forecasting and planning.

Compare current trends to the same period last year. Are you outperforming? Underperforming? Context matters.

This chart answers: Where is our revenue heading? Are we meeting growth targets? And what seasonal or cyclical patterns should we plan around?

payload.formula_explanation: Count of first-time customers acquired over the period, summed from the customer segments mart. It is the core acquisition metric.
how_to_read: The main value shows newly acquired customers for the period
Compare to the prior period to track acquisition
The sparkline shows the daily new-customer trend
Pair with total customers to see acquisition's share of the base
Click to view the registration trend chart
decisions_answered: How many new customers did we acquire?
Is acquisition accelerating or slowing?
Is growth coming from new or repeat buyers?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts orders in each status category to show the current state of your order pipeline. Healthy operations show most orders in completed states with minimal stuck or canceled orders.
how_to_read: Each donut slice represents one order status
Larger slices indicate more orders in that status
Complete/Shipped should typically be the largest segments
Processing segment size indicates fulfillment backlog
Canceled/Refunded segments show lost revenue
decisions_answered: What percentage of orders complete successfully?
Is there a fulfillment backlog building up?
How many orders are getting canceled?
Are there status bottlenecks affecting customer satisfaction?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart breaks down your orders by their current status.

The formula counts orders in each status category - processing, complete, shipped, canceled, pending, and others.

A healthy distribution shows most orders completed or shipped successfully. Small processing segments mean fast fulfillment. Minimal cancellations indicate satisfied customers.

Warning signs to watch: A large or growing processing segment suggests fulfillment bottlenecks. High cancellation rates need investigation - are customers changing their minds, or are there product or shipping issues? Stuck pending orders frustrate customers and tie up inventory.

The legend shows exact counts. Track these over time to spot trends.

This answers: How healthy is our order pipeline? Are we fulfilling orders efficiently? And where are orders getting stuck or lost?

payload.formula_explanation: Total orders placed over the period, the volume baseline for all fulfillment rates on this dashboard.
how_to_read: The main value shows total orders for the period
Compare to the prior period to track demand
The sparkline shows the daily order trend
All fulfillment rates use this as the denominator
Click to see the fulfillment funnel and status mix
decisions_answered: How much order volume are we fulfilling?
Is demand growing or slowing?
What share of orders reach completion?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts total orders placed during each hour of the day (0-23), aggregated across all days in the selected period. This reveals the daily rhythm of customer purchasing behavior.
how_to_read: Each bar represents one hour of the day (midnight to 11 PM)
Taller bars indicate more orders placed during that hour
Look for peak hours that consistently drive the most orders
Early morning and late night typically show lower activity
Multiple peaks may indicate different customer segments
decisions_answered: What time of day do customers place the most orders?
When should we ensure maximum site performance?
What hours need the most customer service coverage?
When should we schedule flash sales or promotions?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows order distribution across the 24 hours of the day.

The formula counts all orders and groups them by the hour they were placed. Each bar represents one hour, from midnight through 11 PM.

Taller bars mean more orders during that hour. Most e-commerce sites see patterns - often a morning rise, lunch peak, afternoon activity, and evening browsing turning into purchases.

Understanding hourly patterns helps with operations. Schedule promotions when customers are active. Ensure your site handles peak load. Staff customer support accordingly. Plan technical maintenance during quiet hours.

If you see unusual patterns - like strong late-night activity - investigate. International customers? Night shift workers? This insight shapes your strategy.

This answers: When do customers buy? How should we allocate resources throughout the day? And when is the best time to launch time-sensitive promotions?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates order counts by the day of week and hour of day they were placed. This reveals when customers are most likely to complete purchases.
how_to_read: Rows represent days of the week (Sunday through Saturday)
Columns represent hours of the day (0-23)
Darker cells indicate higher order volume at that time
Lighter cells show lower activity periods
Look for patterns to identify peak shopping times
decisions_answered: When do customers place the most orders?
Are there specific day/hour combinations that peak?
When should we schedule marketing communications?
What hours need the most customer support coverage?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals when your customers prefer to place orders.

The formula counts orders and groups them by day of week and hour of day. Each cell represents a specific time slot - for example, Tuesday at 2 PM.

Darker blue cells indicate high order volume. Lighter cells show quieter periods. Most e-commerce sites see patterns - often higher activity during lunch hours, evening browsing, and weekend shopping sprees.

Understanding these patterns is valuable. Schedule promotions and email sends to coincide with peak shopping times. Ensure customer support coverage matches demand. Plan maintenance or updates during quiet periods.

This chart answers: When do customers shop most? What days and hours drive the most orders? And how should we allocate resources throughout the week?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts total orders placed each day, providing a view of customer purchasing activity over time. This is a key indicator of business health and demand.
how_to_read: The area chart shows daily order volume
Upward trends indicate growing demand
Dips may indicate seasonal slowdowns or issues
The total displayed shows cumulative orders for the period
Compare to revenue trends to understand average order value changes
decisions_answered: Is order volume growing or declining?
What days or periods see peak ordering activity?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer ordering behavior?
How do orders correlate with marketing activities?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart visualizes daily order volume from your customer base.

The formula counts all orders placed each day. The total displayed shows cumulative orders for your selected time period.

Watch the trend direction. Rising orders indicate growing demand and effective customer engagement. Flat trends might mean you've reached current market potential. Declining trends need investigation.

Look for patterns. Weekday versus weekend differences, monthly cycles, seasonal peaks. Understanding when customers order helps with inventory, staffing, and marketing timing.

Compare to revenue trends. If orders grow but revenue stays flat, average order value is dropping. If revenue grows faster than orders, customers are spending more per transaction.

This answers: How is our order volume trending? When do customers order most? And what factors influence ordering behavior?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts orders by the payment method used at checkout. This reveals customer payment preferences and helps optimize the checkout experience.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars compare order counts by payment method
Longer bars indicate more popular payment options
Compare to see which methods customers prefer
Low usage of certain methods may indicate friction or lack of awareness
Consider removing rarely-used options to simplify checkout
decisions_answered: Which payment methods do customers prefer?
Should we add or remove payment options?
Are there regional differences in payment preferences?
Which payment methods have the highest completion rates?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows which payment methods your customers prefer.

The formula counts orders completed with each payment method - credit cards, PayPal, buy-now-pay-later options, and others.

Longer bars mean more orders used that method. Most stores see credit cards dominate, but trends are shifting toward digital wallets and flexible payment options.

Payment insights matter for optimization. If a method has very low usage, consider whether it adds value or just clutters checkout. If a popular regional payment method is missing, you might be losing sales.

Also consider conversion rates by payment method - some options may attract more browsers who don't convert. Others might have higher average order values.

This answers: How do customers prefer to pay? Should we adjust our payment options? And are there opportunities to increase conversions through better payment choices?

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies products entering their peak selling periods by comparing current week sales to historical averages. A seasonal index above 1.0 means above-average demand. Products with high indices and peak week flags need immediate inventory attention.
how_to_read: Products are listed with their current seasonal index
Index above 1.2 indicates significantly above-average demand
Index below 0.8 indicates below-average demand
Peak week indicator shows products at their historical high-demand period
Recommended actions suggest inventory and marketing responses
decisions_answered: Which products are entering peak demand periods?
Do we have adequate inventory for seasonal surges?
Which products need immediate restocking attention?
What seasonal patterns should we prepare for?
payload.narration_text:
The seasonal alerts table flags products entering peak selling periods.

The formula calculates a seasonal index by comparing current week sales to historical weekly averages. An index of 1.0 means normal demand. Above 1.2 signals a seasonal surge. Below 0.8 indicates a slow period.

The peak week flag marks products at their historically highest-demand week of the year. These need immediate attention to ensure stock availability.

The recommended action column suggests what to do - restock, increase marketing spend, or prepare for the rush.

This answers critical operational questions: Which products are about to surge? Do we have enough inventory? And what actions should we take right now to capitalize on seasonal demand?

payload.formula_explanation: Orders that are neither completed nor canceled - i.e. still in-flight. At runtime the order-totals source lacks a canceled count, so the value effectively reduces to TOTAL_ORDERS minus COMPLETED_ORDERS (clamped at zero).
how_to_read: The main value shows orders currently in progress
A rising backlog can mean fulfillment is falling behind
The sparkline shows the in-progress trend
Compare to completed orders to judge throughput
Click to see where orders sit in the funnel
decisions_answered: How big is our in-flight order backlog?
Is fulfillment keeping pace with incoming orders?
Do we have a processing bottleneck?

payload.formula_text: SUM(line_item_revenue) grouped by product, sorted DESC
payload.formula_explanation: Ranks products by their total revenue contribution, summing all line item sales. This identifies your best-selling products by dollar value.
how_to_read: Products ranked from highest to lowest revenue
Revenue shows total sales value for each product
Units shows quantity sold
Orders shows how many transactions included this product
Click rows to see detailed product analytics
decisions_answered: Which products generate the most revenue?
How concentrated is revenue across our product catalog?
Which products should we prioritize for inventory?
What products are driving our business success?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your products by total revenue generated.

The formula sums all line item revenue for each product, then sorts from highest to lowest.

Each row shows product name, SKU, total revenue, units sold, and order count. High revenue with high units means consistent bestsellers. High revenue with few units indicates premium or high-ticket items.

Revenue concentration matters. If your top 5 products drive most of your revenue, that's efficient but also risky. Diversification provides stability.

Use this for inventory prioritization - never let top sellers go out of stock. Focus marketing on proven winners. Consider bundling top products with slower movers.

This answers: What products matter most? How diversified is our revenue? And where should we focus our operational and marketing efforts?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks refund metrics including total refund amount, refund count, and refund rate as a percentage of revenue. Also includes related metrics like abandoned cart value.
how_to_read: Refund Rate shows what percentage of revenue is refunded
Refund Amount shows total dollars returned
Refund Count shows number of refund transactions
Compare to industry benchmarks (typically 5-10% for e-commerce)
Track trends over time to spot emerging issues
decisions_answered: What is our refund rate and is it acceptable?
How much revenue are we losing to refunds?
Are refund rates trending up or down?
Which products or categories have the highest refund rates?
payload.narration_text:
This metrics panel shows key refund and return statistics.

The core formula is Refund Rate: refund amount divided by total revenue, times 100. This tells you what percentage of your sales come back as returns.

E-commerce typically sees 5-10% refund rates, though this varies by category. Apparel runs higher due to fit issues. Electronics may be lower but with higher dollar values.

Track these metrics over time. Rising refund rates might indicate quality issues, misleading product descriptions, or shipping damage. A sudden spike warrants immediate investigation.

The panel also shows related metrics like abandoned cart value - revenue left behind when customers don't complete checkout.

This answers: How healthy is our return rate? Are we losing excessive revenue to refunds? And where should we focus to reduce returns?

how_to_read: Refund Rate shows what percentage of revenue is refunded
Refund Amount shows total dollars returned
Refund Count shows number of refund transactions
Compare to industry benchmarks (typically 5-10% for e-commerce)
Track trends over time to spot emerging issues
decisions_answered: What is our refund rate and is it acceptable?
How much revenue are we losing to refunds?
Are refund rates trending up or down?
Which products or categories have the highest refund rates?
payload.narration_text:
This metrics panel shows key refund and return statistics.

The core formula is Refund Rate: refund amount divided by total revenue, times 100. This tells you what percentage of your sales come back as returns.

E-commerce typically sees 5-10% refund rates, though this varies by category. Apparel runs higher due to fit issues. Electronics may be lower but with higher dollar values.

Track these metrics over time. Rising refund rates might indicate quality issues, misleading product descriptions, or shipping damage. A sudden spike warrants immediate investigation.

The panel also shows related metrics like abandoned cart value - revenue left behind when customers don't complete checkout.

This answers: How healthy is our return rate? Are we losing excessive revenue to refunds? And where should we focus to reduce returns?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts new customer account registrations per day. This measures your ability to convert visitors into registered customers, which enables better marketing and customer relationship building.
how_to_read: The area chart shows daily new customer registrations
Rising trends indicate effective customer acquisition
Spikes may correlate with marketing campaigns or promotions
Compare to order volume to see conversion potential
Seasonal patterns may reflect industry or promotional cycles
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring new registered customers consistently?
What campaigns drive the most registrations?
Is our registration process effective?
How does registration growth align with overall business growth?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart tracks daily new customer registrations over time.

The formula counts new customer accounts created each day. Registered customers are valuable - they enable personalized marketing, loyalty programs, and better lifetime value tracking.

Rising trends indicate healthy customer acquisition. Sudden spikes often correlate with campaigns, promotions, or viral moments. Declining trends suggest acquisition challenges or increased competition.

Compare registration trends to order trends. If orders grow but registrations don't, you may have more guest checkout. If registrations outpace orders, registered customers aren't converting.

This answers: Are we building our customer base? What drives registration spikes? And how effective is our registration process?

payload.formula_explanation: Groups customers by their first purchase month (cohort) and tracks what percentage return to purchase in subsequent months. This reveals true customer retention patterns over time.
how_to_read: Each row represents a cohort of customers who first purchased in that month
Columns show retention rates for subsequent months
Darker colors indicate higher retention rates
The diagonal pattern shows how retention evolves over time
Compare cohorts to identify improvements or declines in retention
decisions_answered: What is our true customer retention rate?
Are newer cohorts retaining better than older ones?
At what point do most customers drop off?
How do retention patterns differ across acquisition periods?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap shows cohort retention - how well you keep customers over time.

The formula groups customers by their first purchase month, then tracks what percentage return each subsequent month.

Each row is a cohort. The first column shows Month 1 retention - what percentage came back for a second purchase. This is typically the biggest drop. Subsequent columns show ongoing retention.

Darker cells mean better retention. Compare rows to see if newer cohorts perform better - that means your retention strategies are improving.

The critical metric is Month 1 to Month 2 retention. If you can keep customers past their second purchase, long-term loyalty increases dramatically.

This answers: How well do we retain customers? Are we improving over time? And where is the biggest retention drop-off?

payload.formula_explanation: Sums total revenue contribution from each RFM segment. This shows which customer segments drive the most business value.
how_to_read: Bars show total revenue from each segment
Champions typically contribute disproportionate revenue
Compare segment size to revenue contribution for efficiency
High revenue from At-Risk segment indicates opportunity and risk
Low revenue from large segments indicates development potential
decisions_answered: Which customer segments drive the most revenue?
Is revenue concentrated in healthy segments?
What segments have the highest revenue potential?
How much revenue is at risk from churning customers?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how much revenue each RFM segment contributes to your business.

The formula sums all order revenue from customers in each segment.

Champions often contribute disproportionately - they might be 10% of customers but 40% of revenue. This concentration is both a strength and a risk.

Compare segment sizes to revenue contribution. A small segment with high revenue means valuable customers to protect. A large segment with low revenue has development potential.

Pay attention to revenue from At-Risk and Hibernating segments. If significant revenue comes from customers who haven't purchased recently, you're vulnerable. Prioritize reactivation campaigns for these segments.

This answers: Where does our revenue actually come from? How vulnerable is our revenue to churn? And which segments offer the best growth opportunity?

payload.formula_text: Daily Revenue = SUM(ITEM_REVENUE) GROUP BY DATE Trend Analysis = Visual pattern over 7 days
payload.formula_explanation: Sums the total revenue from all orders placed each day. Revenue is calculated from order totals including products, shipping, and taxes minus any discounts applied.
how_to_read: The area chart shows cumulative daily revenue over time
Higher peaks indicate strong revenue days
The filled area helps visualize total revenue accumulation
Compare revenue trends to order trends - divergence may indicate AOV changes
Sudden spikes often correlate with high-value orders or promotions
decisions_answered: Is revenue growing in line with our business goals?
What days generate the most revenue?
Are there revenue patterns that differ from order volume patterns?
How does revenue fluctuate throughout the selected period?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart visualizes your daily revenue over the selected time period.

The formula sums all order totals for each day. This includes product prices, shipping, and taxes, minus discounts.

Reading this chart: Higher areas mean more revenue that day. The filled area helps you see cumulative performance at a glance. Compare this to your orders trend - if revenue grows faster than orders, your average order value is increasing.

Watch for patterns. Weekends might differ from weekdays. Certain months may consistently outperform. Large spikes often indicate high-value orders or successful promotions.

This chart answers: Is revenue meeting targets? What days perform best? And how does revenue relate to order volume?

payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers based on Recency (how recently they purchased), Frequency (how often), and Monetary value (how much). Each customer receives a segment label based on their RFM profile.
how_to_read: Each donut slice represents one RFM segment
Champions are your best customers - recent, frequent, high-value
At-Risk and Hibernating segments need reactivation attention
New customers show acquisition health
Segment sizes indicate overall customer base health
decisions_answered: What is the health of our overall customer base?
How many customers are at risk of churning?
What segments should we prioritize for marketing?
Are we creating Champions from new customers?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your customers segmented by RFM analysis - Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value.

The formula assigns each customer to a segment based on three factors: how recently they bought, how often they buy, and how much they spend.

Champions are your stars - recent purchasers who buy frequently and spend well. Loyal customers are consistent buyers. New customers are recently acquired. At-Risk and Hibernating segments haven't purchased recently and need reactivation.

Segment sizes matter. A healthy business shows strong Champion and Loyal segments, steady New customer acquisition, and controlled At-Risk segments.

Use this for targeted marketing. Champions get VIP treatment. At-Risk customers get win-back campaigns. New customers get nurture sequences to build loyalty.

This answers: How healthy is our customer base? Which segments need attention? And where should we focus our marketing efforts?

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies products entering their peak selling periods by comparing current week sales to historical averages. A seasonal index above 1.0 means above-average demand. Products with high indices and peak week flags need immediate inventory attention.
how_to_read: Products are listed with their current seasonal index
Index above 1.2 indicates significantly above-average demand
Index below 0.8 indicates below-average demand
Peak week indicator shows products at their historical high-demand period
Recommended actions suggest inventory and marketing responses
decisions_answered: Which products are entering peak demand periods?
Do we have adequate inventory for seasonal surges?
Which products need immediate restocking attention?
What seasonal patterns should we prepare for?
payload.narration_text:
The seasonal alerts table flags products entering peak selling periods.

The formula calculates a seasonal index by comparing current week sales to historical weekly averages. An index of 1.0 means normal demand. Above 1.2 signals a seasonal surge. Below 0.8 indicates a slow period.

The peak week flag marks products at their historically highest-demand week of the year. These need immediate attention to ensure stock availability.

The recommended action column suggests what to do - restock, increase marketing spend, or prepare for the rush.

This answers critical operational questions: Which products are about to surge? Do we have enough inventory? And what actions should we take right now to capitalize on seasonal demand?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts orders by the shipping method selected. This shows customer shipping preferences and helps optimize shipping options and pricing.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars compare order counts by shipping method
Longer bars indicate more popular shipping options
Free shipping often dominates when offered
Express/expedited usage indicates urgency tolerance
Multiple methods with similar usage may indicate customer confusion
decisions_answered: Which shipping methods do customers choose most?
Is free shipping effective at driving conversions?
What percentage of customers pay for expedited shipping?
Should we adjust our shipping options or pricing?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down shipping method preferences.

The formula counts orders by which shipping option customers selected at checkout.

When free shipping is available, it typically dominates. But the ratio of customers choosing paid expedited options tells you about urgency tolerance. If many customers pay extra for speed, you might increase express pricing or offer it more prominently.

Look at the distribution. If several methods have similar low usage, customers might be confused by too many options. Simplification often improves conversion.

Consider seasonal patterns too. Holiday shoppers may choose express more often. Understanding this helps with carrier negotiations and inventory positioning.

This answers: How do customers prefer their orders shipped? Is our free shipping threshold effective? And should we adjust our shipping options to better match customer needs?

payload.formula_explanation: Counts orders in each status category (processing, complete, canceled, etc.). This provides a snapshot of your current order pipeline health.
how_to_read: Each slice represents one order status category
Larger slices indicate more orders in that status
Green typically indicates completed/healthy statuses
Red or orange may indicate issues requiring attention
The legend shows exact counts for each status
decisions_answered: How many orders are currently in each stage?
Is our fulfillment pipeline healthy?
Are there bottlenecks causing orders to pile up?
What percentage of orders get canceled or refunded?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows the distribution of orders across different status categories.

The formula simply counts orders in each status - processing, complete, shipped, canceled, and so on.

Each colored slice represents one status. Larger slices mean more orders in that category. A healthy distribution typically shows most orders in completed or shipped status, with minimal cancellations or stuck orders.

Watch for warning signs. A large processing segment might indicate fulfillment bottlenecks. Growing cancellation rates need investigation. Pending orders that stay pending too long affect customer satisfaction.

The legend provides exact counts so you can track specific numbers, not just proportions.

This answers: Where are our orders in the pipeline? Are there operational issues causing delays? And what percentage of orders complete successfully versus getting canceled?

payload.formula_explanation: Total product units sold over the period, summed from the Magento daily mart. It measures physical demand independent of price.
how_to_read: The main value shows total units sold for the period
Compare to the prior period to track demand volume
The sparkline shows the daily units trend
Compare against revenue to spot pricing or discount effects
Click to see top products and category mix
decisions_answered: How many units did the store move?
Is unit demand growing or shrinking?
Are revenue changes from price or from volume?

payload.formula_explanation: Reconciles three revenue views: platform self-reported revenue, GA4-attributed paid revenue, and actual Magento store revenue. Magento is treated as the source of truth, and the gap percentages quantify how much platform and GA4 figures over- or under-state real revenue.
how_to_read: The headline value is actual Magento store revenue for the period.
Platform vs Magento gap shows how much Google/Meta self-reported revenue exceeds real revenue (usually inflated).
GA4 vs Magento gap shows how GA4 paid revenue compares to real revenue.
Large positive gaps mean platform/GA4 numbers should be trusted less for budgeting decisions.
decisions_answered: How much are the ad platforms over-reporting revenue versus my actual store sales?
Which revenue source should I trust for budgeting and forecasting?
How big is the attribution gap between reported and actual revenue?

payload.formula_explanation: Meta uses a default attribution window of 7-day click and 1-day view. This chart shows how conversions are attributed across these windows for each action type in the conversion funnel.
how_to_read: Blue segments show 7-day click attribution - conversions after ad click
Purple segments show 1-day view attribution - conversions after viewing without clicking
Longer bars indicate more total conversions for that action type
Purchase and Add to Cart are the most valuable funnel actions
Heavy view-through attribution suggests strong brand awareness impact
decisions_answered: How do conversions distribute between click and view attribution?
Is view-through attribution a significant conversion driver?
Which funnel stages show the most activity?
Should we adjust our attribution model expectations?
payload.narration_text:
This chart breaks down how Meta attributes conversions using their default 7-day click and 1-day view window.

The formula: 7-day click captures conversions within 7 days after someone clicks your ad. 1-day view captures conversions within 1 day of viewing your ad without clicking.

Reading this chart: Blue segments are click-through conversions - someone clicked and later converted. Purple segments are view-through - they saw the ad, didn't click, but converted anyway.

This matters because view-through can represent significant value, especially for brand awareness campaigns. Heavy view attribution suggests your ads influence purchase decisions even without direct clicks.

Key insights: How do click versus view conversions compare? Is view-through driving meaningful value? Which funnel stages show the most activity? This helps calibrate your attribution expectations.

payload.formula_explanation: Meta uses a default attribution window of 7-day click and 1-day view. This chart shows how conversions are attributed across these windows for each action type in the conversion funnel.
how_to_read: Blue segments show 7-day click attribution - conversions after ad click
Purple segments show 1-day view attribution - conversions after viewing without clicking
Longer bars indicate more total conversions for that action type
Purchase and Add to Cart are the most valuable funnel actions
Heavy view-through attribution suggests strong brand awareness impact
decisions_answered: How do conversions distribute between click and view attribution?
Is view-through attribution a significant conversion driver?
Which funnel stages show the most activity?
Should we adjust our attribution model expectations?
payload.narration_text:

Reading this chart: Blue segments are click-through conversions - someone clicked and later converted. Purple segments are view-through - they saw the ad, didn't click, but converted anyway.

This matters because view-through can represent significant value, especially for brand awareness campaigns. Heavy view attribution suggests your ads influence purchase decisions even without direct clicks.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the total number of unique creative assets (images and videos) in your Meta advertising library, along with how many are currently active in running ads.
how_to_read: Images count shows total unique image creatives uploaded
Videos count shows total unique video creatives uploaded
Compare active vs total to understand utilization rate
Regular creative refresh prevents ad fatigue
Click to navigate to the full Creative Library for asset management
decisions_answered: How large is our creative asset library?
What is the ratio of images to videos in our strategy?
Are we regularly refreshing creative or reusing old assets?
Do we have enough creative variety to prevent fatigue?
payload.narration_text:
This summary shows your Meta creative asset library at a glance.

The formula counts distinct image and video IDs in your account. We track both total assets and those actively running in ads.

Reading these cards: The images count shows your static creative inventory. Videos count shows motion creative assets. A healthy library has thousands of assets with regular additions.

Creative variety matters for Meta's algorithm. Ad fatigue sets in when the same users see the same creatives repeatedly. Fresh assets maintain engagement and performance.

Key questions answered: How large is the creative library? Are you image-heavy or video-focused? When were new assets last added? Click through to the full Creative Library for detailed asset management.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows spend distribution and engagement metrics across age groups. Different age groups respond differently to advertising, with some showing higher engagement rates despite lower spend allocation.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show spend amount per age group
Longer bars indicate higher budget allocation to that demographic
35-54 age range typically shows highest seed/garden purchase intent
Compare CTR across groups - higher CTR may justify increased allocation
Click any bar to filter the page by that age group
decisions_answered: Which age groups receive the most ad spend?
Are we reaching our core customer demographic effectively?
Should we adjust targeting to focus on high-performing age groups?
Are younger demographics worth additional investment?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows how your Meta ad spend distributes across age demographics.

The formula: Each age group's spend divided by total spend shows allocation percentage. CTR by age group reveals which demographics engage most.

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean more budget going to that age group. For gardening and seed products, the 35 to 65-plus range typically shows strongest purchase intent and engagement.

Key insight: High spend doesn't always equal high performance. Some age groups may have lower spend but higher CTR and conversion rates. These represent expansion opportunities.

Questions answered: Which ages get the most spend? Are you reaching your core demographic? Should you shift budget to high-engaging age groups? Click any bar to filter and investigate that demographic further.

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows ad set level performance, including learning status which indicates whether Meta has optimized delivery for the ad set. Ad sets in learning may show variable performance.
how_to_read: Ad sets are sorted by spend - highest investment at top
Learning Status shows optimization progress: SUCCESS (green), LEARNING (yellow), LIMITED (orange)
SUCCESS means Meta has optimized delivery for your target audience
LIMITED indicates insufficient data or budget for proper optimization
Click any ad set to drill into individual ad performance
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are successfully optimized vs still learning?
Are there ad sets stuck in learning limited status?
Which ad set optimization goals are performing best?
Should we consolidate ad sets to accelerate learning?
payload.narration_text:
This table displays ad set performance with a critical addition: learning status.

The metrics formulas are standard: CPM, CTR, and CPC. But the learning status column is key to understanding Meta's algorithm optimization.

Reading learning status: Green SUCCESS means Meta has gathered enough data to optimize delivery effectively. Yellow LEARNING means optimization is still in progress - expect variable performance. Orange LIMITED means the ad set lacks sufficient data or budget to exit learning phase.

Ad sets stuck in LIMITED for more than 5-7 days need intervention. Consider consolidating similar ad sets or increasing budget to accelerate learning.

Key questions: Which ad sets are optimized? Which are stuck? Should you consolidate for faster learning? This insight helps maximize algorithm efficiency.

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows ad set level performance, including learning status which indicates whether Meta has optimized delivery for the ad set. Ad sets in learning may show variable performance.
how_to_read: Ad sets are sorted by spend - highest investment at top
Learning Status shows optimization progress: SUCCESS (green), LEARNING (yellow), LIMITED (orange)
SUCCESS means Meta has optimized delivery for your target audience
LIMITED indicates insufficient data or budget for proper optimization
Click any ad set to drill into individual ad performance
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are successfully optimized vs still learning?
Are there ad sets stuck in learning limited status?
Which ad set optimization goals are performing best?
Should we consolidate ad sets to accelerate learning?
