how_to_read: Horizontal bars show spend amount per age group
Longer bars indicate higher budget allocation to that demographic
35-54 age range typically shows highest seed/garden purchase intent
Compare CTR across groups - higher CTR may justify increased allocation
Click any bar to filter the page by that age group
decisions_answered: Which age groups receive the most ad spend?
Are we reaching our core customer demographic effectively?
Should we adjust targeting to focus on high-performing age groups?
Are younger demographics worth additional investment?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows how your Meta ad spend distributes across age demographics.

The formula: Each age group's spend divided by total spend shows allocation percentage. CTR by age group reveals which demographics engage most.

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean more budget going to that age group. For gardening and seed products, the 35 to 65-plus range typically shows strongest purchase intent and engagement.

Key insight: High spend doesn't always equal high performance. Some age groups may have lower spend but higher CTR and conversion rates. These represent expansion opportunities.

Questions answered: Which ages get the most spend? Are you reaching your core demographic? Should you shift budget to high-engaging age groups? Click any bar to filter and investigate that demographic further.

payload.formula_explanation: This chart tracks daily spend, CPM, and CTR over a 30-day period. Spend is the raw dollar amount, CPM measures cost efficiency per thousand impressions, and CTR measures engagement quality.
how_to_read: The solid blue line shows daily advertising spend in dollars
Watch for spending spikes or dips that may indicate campaign changes or budget adjustments
Consistent upward trends in spend should correlate with proportional revenue increases
Look for day-of-week patterns - weekends often show different performance characteristics
Hover over any point to see exact values for that date
decisions_answered: Is our daily spend consistent or highly variable?
Are there specific days where performance significantly differs?
When did notable changes in spending patterns occur?
Should we adjust budget pacing based on day-of-week performance?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your Meta advertising performance over the last 30 days.

The formula is straightforward: we sum total spend per day, calculate CPM as spend divided by impressions times 1000, and CTR as clicks divided by impressions times 100.

To read this chart, follow the solid blue line showing daily spend. Look for patterns - consistent spending is healthy, while large spikes or dips may indicate campaign issues or intentional adjustments.

Pay attention to day-of-week patterns. Weekends often perform differently than weekdays for e-commerce. Hover over any point to see exact values.

This chart answers key questions: Is spend consistent? Which days perform best? When did notable changes occur? Use these insights to optimize your budget pacing strategy.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows advertising performance across device types - mobile app, mobile web, and desktop. Mobile typically dominates Meta advertising due to platform usage patterns.
how_to_read: Vertical bars show spend by device type
Mobile App typically shows highest volume on Meta platforms
Desktop often shows higher conversion rates despite lower volume
Mobile Web represents browser-based mobile users
Compare device performance to optimize cross-device strategy
decisions_answered: How does our spend distribute across devices?
Which device type shows the best engagement?
Should we adjust bids by device based on performance?
Is desktop worth higher investment despite lower volume?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how performance varies across device types.

The formula: Device spend divided by total spend shows allocation. Device CTR measures engagement quality per device.

Reading this chart: Mobile App dominates Meta platforms - that's where most users are. Mobile Web represents browser users. Desktop typically has lower volume but often higher conversion rates and order values.

Here's the insight: Mobile may drive volume, but desktop users often convert better. Consider your attribution carefully - users may discover on mobile and purchase on desktop.

Key questions: How does spend distribute? Which device shows best engagement? Should you bid differently by device? This helps optimize your cross-device strategy.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows advertising performance across device types - mobile app, mobile web, and desktop. Mobile typically dominates Meta advertising due to platform usage patterns.
how_to_read: Vertical bars show spend by device type
Mobile App typically shows highest volume on Meta platforms
Desktop often shows higher conversion rates despite lower volume
Mobile Web represents browser-based mobile users
Compare device performance to optimize cross-device strategy
decisions_answered: How does our spend distribute across devices?
Which device type shows the best engagement?
Should we adjust bids by device based on performance?
Is desktop worth higher investment despite lower volume?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how performance varies across device types.

The formula: Device spend divided by total spend shows allocation. Device CTR measures engagement quality per device.

Reading this chart: Mobile App dominates Meta platforms - that's where most users are. Mobile Web represents browser users. Desktop typically has lower volume but often higher conversion rates and order values.

Here's the insight: Mobile may drive volume, but desktop users often convert better. Consider your attribution carefully - users may discover on mobile and purchase on desktop.

Key questions: How does spend distribute? Which device shows best engagement? Should you bid differently by device? This helps optimize your cross-device strategy.

payload.formula_explanation: Frequency is the average number of times each unique person saw your ads. It is total impressions divided by total reach, indicating how repeatedly your audience is being exposed.
how_to_read: The value is the average exposures per person
A frequency of 2-3 is typically healthy; very high values risk fatigue
The sparkline shows how exposure intensity moved over the period
Rising frequency with falling CTR is a classic sign of ad fatigue
Low frequency may mean you are not reinforcing your message enough
decisions_answered: How many times is each person seeing our ads on average?
Are we over-exposing our audience and risking fatigue?
Is frequency climbing as reach plateaus?
Should we expand reach or refresh creative to control frequency?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows how advertising spend is distributed between male and female audiences. The center percentage highlights the dominant gender in your audience mix.
how_to_read: Pie slices show spend proportion by gender
Pink represents female audience, blue represents male
Center percentage shows the dominant gender share
Gardening products often skew female (60-70%) in purchasing
Compare spend share to conversion share for efficiency insights
decisions_answered: What is our audience gender split?
Does our spend allocation match our customer base?
Should we adjust targeting toward a specific gender?
Is one gender significantly more efficient than the other?
payload.narration_text:
This pie chart shows gender distribution in your Meta advertising.

The formula is straightforward: gender spend divided by total spend gives you the percentage split.

Reading this chart: Pink represents female audience, blue represents male. The center percentage highlights your dominant audience gender.

For gardening and seed products, female purchasers often represent 60 to 70 percent of customers. If your ad spend doesn't roughly match this ratio, you may be over or under-investing in certain segments.

Key questions: What's the gender split? Does it match your customer base? Is one gender showing better efficiency? Use this to align spend allocation with actual purchase behavior.

payload.display_name: Metadata Also Exposes Total Cohort Clv
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how customer lifetime value varies by when customers were acquired. Each cohort represents customers who first appeared in the system during that quarter. Comparing average CLV across cohorts reveals acquisition quality trends and how CLV matures over time.
how_to_read: X-axis shows acquisition quarter from Q3 2021 forward
Blue line tracks average total CLV per customer for each cohort
Green dashed line shows average historic (realized) CLV only
Older cohorts typically show higher CLV as they have had more time to mature
Declining CLV in recent cohorts may indicate lower acquisition quality
Large gaps between total and historic CLV indicate strong predicted future value
decisions_answered: Are we acquiring higher or lower quality customers over time?
How does CLV mature as cohorts age?
Which acquisition period brought our most valuable customers?
Should we investigate recent cohorts showing lower CLV?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value by acquisition cohort over time.

Each point represents customers who joined in that quarter. The blue line shows total CLV including predicted future value, while the green dashed line shows only historic realized value.

Older cohorts naturally show higher CLV because customers have had more time to make purchases. Q3 2021 cohorts might show the highest average CLV after having 4+ years to mature.

Watch for declining CLV trends in recent cohorts - this could indicate lower quality acquisitions from changed marketing strategies or channels. However, some decline is expected since newer customers haven't had time to mature.

Use this to answer: Is our customer acquisition quality improving or declining? When did we acquire our best customers, and can we replicate those conditions?

payload.formula_text: Metrics BY platform (Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network)
payload.formula_explanation: Each slice represents the percentage of Meta ad spend allocated to that platform - Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network, Messenger, or Threads. The center value shows combined spend across all Meta platforms.
how_to_read: Larger slices indicate higher budget allocation to that Meta platform
Facebook (blue) typically dominates spend for most advertisers
Instagram (pink) often shows higher engagement rates despite smaller share
Audience Network and Messenger represent extended reach opportunities
Click any slice to filter the entire page by that platform
decisions_answered: How is our Meta budget distributed across platforms?
Is Facebook receiving the majority of spend as expected?
Should we increase Instagram allocation based on performance?
Are we leveraging Audience Network and Messenger effectively?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your Meta advertising budget is distributed across platforms.

The formula is simple: each platform's spend divided by total spend, times 100, gives you the percentage share.

Facebook typically dominates - that's normal for most advertisers. Instagram often shows higher engagement rates, making it valuable despite potentially smaller spend share.

Audience Network extends your reach beyond Facebook and Instagram apps. Messenger and Threads are newer placements worth monitoring.

Key questions this answers: How is budget distributed? Is Facebook dominant as expected? Should you shift more to Instagram? Are you maximizing extended reach through Audience Network?

Click any slice to filter the entire page and dive deeper into that platform's performance.

payload.formula_explanation: Reach is the number of unique people who saw your ads at least once during the period. Unlike impressions, reach counts each person only once regardless of how many times they saw an ad.
how_to_read: The value counts distinct people exposed to your ads
Reach paired with frequency reveals how broadly versus how repeatedly you advertised
The sparkline shows daily reach trends across the period
Growing reach indicates audience expansion into new people
Flat reach with rising impressions means you are re-showing ads to the same people
decisions_answered: How many unique people did our ads reach?
Are we expanding into new audiences or repeating to the same ones?
Is reach growing alongside our spend?
Do we have headroom to reach more people before saturating?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows advertising performance by US state, enabling geographic analysis of spend distribution and efficiency. High-spend regions should correlate with strong customer concentrations.
how_to_read: Table shows top 20 states ranked by advertising spend
California, Texas, and Florida typically lead in spend volume
Compare CPM across regions - some states cost more to reach
CTR variations may indicate regional message resonance
Click any region to filter and analyze geographic performance
decisions_answered: Which states receive the most advertising investment?
Are there regional CPM or CTR variations to exploit?
Should we expand or reduce investment in specific states?
Do regional patterns align with customer distribution?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows advertising performance by US state, ranked by spend.

The formulas: Region spend sums all advertising in that state. Region CTR and CPC measure efficiency metrics per geography.

Reading this table: Top states by population like California, Texas, and Florida typically lead. But look beyond volume - compare CPM across regions. Some states cost more to reach than others.

CTR variations reveal regional message resonance. If certain states show higher engagement, your creative or offer may particularly resonate there. These are expansion opportunities.

Key questions: Which states get the most investment? Are there CPM or CTR variations to exploit? Should you adjust regional targeting? Does your spend match your customer geographic distribution?

payload.formula_explanation: Return on Ad Spend is the revenue generated for every dollar spent, using Meta's default 7-day click plus 1-day view attribution window. A ROAS of 4.0 means $4 of attributed revenue per $1 spent.
how_to_read: The value is a multiple - higher means more revenue per dollar of spend
ROAS is computed as total conversion value divided by total spend, not an average of daily ROAS
The sparkline shows how efficiency moved across the period
Compare against your breakeven ROAS to judge profitability
A declining trend with rising spend can signal audience saturation
decisions_answered: Is our Meta advertising profitable this period?
How much revenue does each dollar of spend return?
Is efficiency improving or declining over time?
Should we scale spend or pull back based on returns?

payload.formula_explanation: This chart aggregates spend and performance by creative type across both Google Ads (RSA, Shopping, Display, Video) and Meta Ads (Share, Video, Photo). It reveals which creative formats receive the most investment and generate the best engagement.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars represent total spend for each creative type
Google types (blue) and Meta types (purple) are grouped separately
Longer bars indicate higher investment in that creative format
Compare bar lengths to identify budget allocation patterns across formats
Hover over bars to see exact spend values and related metrics
decisions_answered: Which creative types receive the most budget allocation?
How does spend distribution differ between Google and Meta platforms?
Should we shift budget from underperforming formats to winning ones?
Are we diversifying our creative mix adequately across formats?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows your spend distribution across creative types for both Google and Meta platforms.

The formula aggregates spend by creative type: Google spend from ad stats divided by one million to convert from micros, and Meta spend summed directly.

Horizontal bars display total investment per type. Blue represents Google formats like RSA and Shopping. Purple shows Meta formats like Share and Video.

Use this to answer: Which types get most budget? How does allocation differ between platforms? And should you shift investment to better-performing formats?

payload.formula_text: COUNT(DISTINCT CREATIVE_ID) WHERE SPEND > 0 in last 7 days
payload.formula_explanation: The number of unique creatives that received ad spend in the last 7 days. This shows how much of your creative library is currently active versus dormant.
how_to_read: The value counts creatives that actually spent in the last 7 days
Compare against total creatives to gauge library utilization
The sparkline shows the daily active-creative count over recent days
A small active set against a large library means under-utilization
A shrinking active count can signal creatives being paused or exhausted
decisions_answered: How many creatives are actively running right now?
Are we utilizing our creative library or letting assets sit idle?
Is the number of active creatives enough to avoid fatigue?
Should we activate more assets from the library?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares how different call-to-action button types (Shop Now, Learn More, Sign Up, etc.) perform in terms of driving clicks and conversions. This reveals which CTAs resonate most with your audience.
how_to_read: Grouped horizontal bars show clicks (blue) and conversions (green) per CTA type
Compare the ratio between clicks and conversions for each CTA
CTAs with high clicks but low conversions may attract wrong audience
CTAs with proportionally high conversions indicate strong intent signals
The top 8 CTA types are shown ranked by performance
decisions_answered: Which call-to-action buttons drive the most conversions?
Are certain CTAs attracting clicks without converting?
What CTA types should we prioritize in new creatives?
Is there a mismatch between click volume and conversion quality?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares call-to-action button performance across your Meta ads.

Blue bars show clicks, green bars show conversions for each CTA type like Shop Now, Learn More, or Sign Up.

Watch the ratio between clicks and conversions. High clicks with low conversions suggests the CTA attracts the wrong audience. Proportionally high conversions indicate strong purchase intent.

This answers key questions: Which CTAs drive most conversions? Are some CTAs wasting clicks? And what CTAs should new creatives use?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares how different call-to-action button types (Shop Now, Learn More, Sign Up, etc.) perform in terms of driving clicks and conversions. This reveals which CTAs resonate most with your audience.

Is there a mismatch between click volume and conversion quality?

payload.formula_explanation: Categorizes ad copy by character length and measures average CTR for each category. This reveals whether your audience responds better to concise messaging or detailed descriptions.
how_to_read: Vertical bars represent average CTR for each copy length category
Short copy is typically under 50 characters, Long over 150 characters
Higher bars indicate better engagement for that copy length
Compare adjacent categories to find the optimal copy length range
Consider product complexity when interpreting - some products need more explanation
decisions_answered: What ad copy length performs best for our audience?
Should we write shorter or longer ad descriptions?
Is there an optimal copy length sweet spot?
Do different products need different copy length strategies?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how ad copy length affects click-through rates.

Copy is categorized by character count: short under 50 characters, long over 150. The formula calculates average CTR for each length category.

Taller bars mean better engagement at that length. Compare adjacent categories to find your optimal range. Remember: complex products may legitimately need longer copy.

Use this to answer: What copy length works best? Should you write shorter or longer? And is there a sweet spot for your specific products?

payload.formula_explanation: The average cost per click across all creatives - total spend divided by total link clicks. Lower CPC means you are buying engagement more cheaply.
how_to_read: The value is the dollar cost of each link click
Lower CPC means more efficient click acquisition
The sparkline shows the daily CPC trend over recent days
Rising CPC with falling CTR points to creative fatigue
Compare CPC across formats and CTAs to find the cheapest engagement
decisions_answered: How much are we paying per click on our creatives?
Is click efficiency improving or worsening?
Are fatiguing creatives driving up CPC?
Which formats or CTAs deliver the cheapest clicks?

payload.formula_explanation: The total number of unique creative assets used in ads during the selected period. This measures the breadth of your creative library actually in market.
how_to_read: The value counts distinct creatives that ran in the period
A larger library supports rotation and reduces fatigue risk
The sparkline traces the count of active creatives over recent days
Compare to active creatives to see how much of the library is spending
Too few creatives in market increases the risk of audience fatigue
decisions_answered: How many distinct creatives are we running?
Is our creative library broad enough to rotate?
Are we testing enough creative variety?
Do we have sufficient assets to prevent fatigue?

payload.formula_explanation: The average click-through rate across all creatives - link clicks as a percentage of impressions. It is the headline measure of how engaging your creative is.
how_to_read: The value is the percentage of impressions that produced link clicks
Higher CTR means more compelling creative
The sparkline shows the daily CTR trend over recent days
Falling CTR over time is a classic fatigue signal
Compare CTR across formats and copy lengths to find winners
decisions_answered: How engaging are our creatives overall?
Is creative engagement trending up or down?
Are our creatives fatiguing as CTR declines?
Which creative approaches drive the best click rates?

payload.formula_explanation: This chart tracks daily advertising spend and click-through rate for all creative assets over the past 30 days. The spend line shows investment patterns while CTR indicates engagement quality.
how_to_read: The area chart shows daily spend amounts with gradient fill for easy trend visualization
Upward trends indicate increasing investment or engagement with creatives
Look for correlations between spend spikes and performance patterns
Hover over any point to see exact spend and CTR values for that day
Sharp drops may indicate paused campaigns or budget exhaustion
decisions_answered: How has our creative spend varied over the past month?
Are there specific days or patterns when performance peaks?
When did significant spend or CTR changes occur?
Is our daily creative investment consistent or volatile?
payload.narration_text:
This chart displays your daily creative performance over the past 30 days.

The area chart shows your daily advertising spend with a gradient fill, making trends easy to spot at a glance.

To read this chart: look for upward trends indicating increased investment. Correlate spend spikes with performance outcomes. Hover over any point for exact values.

Sharp drops might signal paused campaigns or budget limits. This helps you understand: How has spend varied? When do performance peaks occur? And is your investment pattern consistent or volatile?

payload.formula_explanation: Detects creative fatigue by comparing initial CTR (first 7 days) to current CTR. Creatives with significant CTR decline are flagged for refresh or replacement. Early detection prevents wasted spend.
how_to_read: Status column shows Healthy (green), Warning (yellow), or Fatigued (red)
Decline percentage shows how much CTR has dropped from initial performance
Days Active indicates how long the creative has been running
Recent Spend shows investment at risk from fatigued creatives
Sort by Decline to prioritize creatives needing immediate attention
decisions_answered: Which creatives are experiencing performance fatigue?
When should we refresh or replace a creative?
How much budget is at risk from fatigued creatives?
What is the typical lifespan before fatigue sets in?
payload.narration_text:
This table detects creative fatigue before it drains your budget.

The formula compares initial CTR from the first week to current CTR. A decline over 30% triggers a Fatigued status, 20 to 30% shows Warning, under 20% is Healthy.

Status is color-coded for quick scanning. The Decline column shows the exact CTR drop. Days Active reveals creative lifespan. Recent Spend shows money at risk.

Sort by decline to prioritize action. This answers: Which creatives are fatiguing? When to refresh? How much budget is at risk? And what's typical creative lifespan?

payload.formula_text: (Total Clicks / Total Impressions) x 100 per format
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates performance metrics by creative format type (video, image, carousel, link ads) to reveal which format delivers the best engagement and efficiency for your audience.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars represent total spend by creative format type
Longer bars indicate higher investment in that format
Compare bar lengths to see relative budget allocation across formats
Color coding helps distinguish between format types quickly
The format with highest spend may not always have best CTR - check both metrics
decisions_answered: Which creative format receives the most investment?
How does spend distribution align with format performance?
Should we shift budget from underperforming formats to winning ones?
Are we testing enough variety across creative formats?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down performance by creative format - video, image, carousel, and link ads.

The formula aggregates spend and CTR for each format type, revealing which format works best for your audience.

Longer bars mean more investment in that format. Compare bar lengths to see budget allocation. Remember: highest spend doesn't always mean best CTR.

Use this to answer: Which format gets most investment? Does spend match performance? Should you shift budget? And are you testing enough format variety?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates performance metrics by creative format type (video, image, carousel, link ads) to reveal which format delivers the best engagement and efficiency for your audience.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars represent total spend by creative format type
Longer bars indicate higher investment in that format
Compare bar lengths to see relative budget allocation across formats
Color coding helps distinguish between format types quickly
The format with highest spend may not always have best CTR - check both metrics
decisions_answered: Which creative format receives the most investment?
How does spend distribution align with format performance?
Should we shift budget from underperforming formats to winning ones?
Are we testing enough variety across creative formats?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down performance by creative format - video, image, carousel, and link ads.

The formula aggregates spend and CTR for each format type, revealing which format works best for your audience.

Longer bars mean more investment in that format. Compare bar lengths to see budget allocation. Remember: highest spend doesn't always mean best CTR.

Use this to answer: Which format gets most investment? Does spend match performance? Should you shift budget? And are you testing enough format variety?

payload.formula_explanation: Ranks your creative assets by spend, displaying key performance metrics including ROAS, CTR, CPC, and conversion counts. Color-coded ROAS helps quickly identify winners and underperformers.
how_to_read: Table rows are sorted by total spend over the last 30 days
ROAS is color-coded: green (3x+), yellow (2-3x), red (under 2x)
Thumbnail and format columns help identify creative quickly
Click any row to drill into detailed creative-level analytics
Use search and sort to find specific creatives or rank by different metrics
decisions_answered: Which creatives are getting the most budget allocation?
Are our highest-spend creatives also highest ROAS?
Which creatives should we scale up or pause?
What creative patterns appear in top performers?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your creatives by spend, showing which assets receive the most investment and how they perform.

Key formulas: ROAS is revenue divided by spend. CTR is clicks over impressions times 100. CPC is spend divided by clicks.

ROAS is color-coded for quick scanning: green means 3x or better, yellow is 2 to 3x, red is under 2x.

Click any row for detailed analytics. Use search and sort to explore. This answers: Which creatives get most budget? Are high-spend creatives also high ROAS? And what should you scale or pause?

payload.formula_explanation: Total advertising spend allocated to creatives during the selected period. This is the dollar investment behind the creative assets being analyzed.
how_to_read: The large number is total dollars spent across creatives
The sparkline traces daily creative spend over recent days
The change percentage compares against the prior period
Pair spend with CTR and CPC to judge creative efficiency
Concentrated spend on few creatives raises fatigue exposure
decisions_answered: How much are we investing across our creatives?
Is creative spend growing or shrinking versus last period?
Is spend concentrated or spread across the library?
Is the investment producing efficient engagement?

payload.formula_explanation: Lists all lookalike audiences available for prospecting campaigns. Lookalike audiences find people similar to your existing customers. Smaller percentages (1%) are closer matches but smaller pools; larger percentages (5%+) are bigger pools but less precise.
how_to_read: Green 1% lookalikes are highest quality - closest match to your seed audience
Yellow 3% lookalikes balance quality and scale
Blue 5%+ lookalikes offer maximum reach but lower precision
Size columns show approximate audience pool size
Click any row to see campaigns using that audience
decisions_answered: What lookalike audiences are available for prospecting?
Should we test smaller, higher-quality lookalikes?
Are we over-relying on broad lookalikes?
Which lookalike tier provides the best efficiency?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your lookalike audiences.

Lookalikes are Meta's way of finding people similar to your customers. The percentage tells you how selective the matching is. A 1% lookalike finds the closest matches - highest quality but smaller pool. A 5% lookalike casts a wider net.

For prospecting, start with 1% lookalikes. They're your best bet for finding likely converters. As those saturate, expand to 3%, then 5%.

The size columns tell you how many people are in each audience. Bigger isn't always better. A 1% lookalike with 2 million people might outperform a 5% with 10 million.

Use this to plan your audience expansion strategy.

payload.formula_explanation: The average cost per 1,000 impressions across prospecting campaigns, computed as the mean of daily CPM values. Industry benchmark is around $7.50. It reflects auction competition for impressions.
how_to_read: The value is the average dollar cost per 1,000 impressions
Lower CPM means cheaper impression buying in the auction
The sparkline shows the most recent 7-day CPM trend
Around $7.50 is a typical industry benchmark
CPM differs from cost per 1k reach - CPM counts impressions, not unique people
decisions_answered: How expensive are our prospecting impressions?
Is auction competition rising for our placements?
Are we above or below the industry CPM benchmark?
Is CPM pressure driving our overall costs up?

payload.formula_explanation: The cost to reach 1,000 unique people with prospecting ads. This is the core efficiency metric for top-of-funnel campaigns - lower is better. Target is under $6.00.
how_to_read: The value is the dollar cost to reach 1,000 distinct people
Lower means you are acquiring new-audience reach more cheaply
The sparkline traces the most recent 7-day efficiency trend
Under roughly $6 is a healthy prospecting benchmark
Rising cost per reach signals saturation or auction pressure
decisions_answered: How efficiently are we reaching new people?
Is our prospecting reach getting more or less expensive?
Are we hitting the under-$6 efficiency target?
Should we shift budget to cheaper-to-reach audiences?

payload.formula_explanation: Provides granular visibility into ad set level performance within your prospecting campaigns. Ad sets control targeting, so this view helps identify which audience segments are most efficient to reach.
how_to_read: Sorted by spend to show your highest-investment ad sets first
Compare cost per 1k reach across different targeting approaches
Green values indicate efficient ad sets worth scaling
Red values suggest targeting that is too narrow or saturated
Click any row to see detailed ad set configuration
decisions_answered: Which ad sets within campaigns are performing best?
Are certain targeting approaches more efficient than others?
Should we consolidate underperforming ad sets?
Which ad sets have room for budget scaling?
payload.narration_text:
This table dives into ad set level performance.

While campaigns set objectives, ad sets control targeting. This is where you define who sees your prospecting ads. Each row is an ad set with its own audience definition.

Compare the cost per reach across ad sets. Big differences reveal which targeting works. Lookalike audiences might outperform interest targeting. Broad audiences might beat narrow ones.

The top 25 by spend are shown. Focus on the ones spending real money. If a high-spend ad set has poor efficiency, that's budget going to waste.

Use this to find your best audiences and double down on them.

payload.formula_explanation: Provides granular visibility into ad set level performance within your prospecting campaigns. Ad sets control targeting, so this view helps identify which audience segments are most efficient to reach.
how_to_read: Sorted by spend to show your highest-investment ad sets first
Compare cost per 1k reach across different targeting approaches
Green values indicate efficient ad sets worth scaling
Red values suggest targeting that is too narrow or saturated
Click any row to see detailed ad set configuration
decisions_answered: Which ad sets within campaigns are performing best?
Are certain targeting approaches more efficient than others?
Should we consolidate underperforming ad sets?
Which ad sets have room for budget scaling?
payload.narration_text:

Compare the cost per reach across ad sets. Big differences reveal which targeting works. Lookalike audiences might outperform interest targeting. Broad audiences might beat narrow ones.

The top 25 by spend are shown. Focus on the ones spending real money. If a high-spend ad set has poor efficiency, that's budget going to waste.

Use this to find your best audiences and double down on them.

payload.formula_explanation: Ranks all prospecting campaigns by their reach efficiency. The primary sort is by cost per 1,000 reach - the most efficient campaigns appear first. Color coding highlights performance tiers to quickly spot winners and underperformers.
how_to_read: Green cost values indicate efficient campaigns (under $5 per 1k reach)
Yellow indicates moderate efficiency ($5-$8 per 1k reach)
Red flags expensive reach (over $8 per 1k reach)
Sort by any column to change the ranking perspective
Click any row to drill into that specific campaign
decisions_answered: Which campaigns deliver the most efficient new audience reach?
Are there campaigns we should pause due to poor efficiency?
Which campaigns should receive additional budget?
How does frequency relate to campaign efficiency?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your prospecting campaigns by reach efficiency.

The key metric is cost per 1,000 reach. Green means efficient - under $5 to reach a thousand new people. Yellow is moderate. Red means expensive reach that needs attention.

CTR and frequency columns add context. High frequency means the same people see your ads repeatedly - that's fine for retargeting but problematic for prospecting. You want fresh reach.

Look at the top performers. What makes them work? Creative, audience, or objective? Look at the bottom performers. Should they be paused or restructured?

This table helps you allocate budget to what's working and cut what's not.

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates prospecting spend by day of week to reveal patterns in budget delivery. This helps identify if Meta is spending more on certain days and whether those days correlate with better or worse performance.
how_to_read: Each bar shows total spend for that day of week across the period
Taller bars mean more budget was spent on that day
Weekend patterns may differ from weekdays
Look for consistent patterns vs random variation
Consider correlating with conversion data for optimization
decisions_answered: Which days of the week receive the most prospecting spend?
Is weekend performance different from weekday performance?
Should we implement day-parting strategies?
Are there days we should increase or decrease budget?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart reveals your prospecting spend patterns across the week.

Meta's delivery algorithm decides when to show your ads. But understanding the pattern helps you optimize. Maybe Mondays see heavy spend but poor conversion. Maybe weekends are efficient.

Each bar is a day's aggregated spend. Look for the tall bars - that's where most budget flows. Look for short bars - those might be opportunities or dead zones.

Day-parting strategies can help. If you know Tuesdays convert well, you might want to ensure budget is available. If Saturdays underperform, consider manual budget adjustments.

This answers: When are we spending, and should we change the pattern?

payload.formula_text: Cost per 1k Reach by Age/Gender segment
payload.formula_explanation: Summarizes prospecting performance by demographic segments including age and gender. Identifies which demographic combinations receive the most spend and which deliver the most efficient reach.
how_to_read: Percentage circles show male vs female spend distribution
Top Spending Segment reveals where most budget flows
Most Efficient Segment shows the best cost-per-reach demographic
Compare top spending vs most efficient to find optimization opportunities
Consider if current allocation matches your target customer profile
decisions_answered: Is our prospecting reaching the right demographic mix?
Which age/gender segment is most efficient to reach?
Are we over-investing in any demographic segment?
Should we adjust targeting to focus on efficient segments?
payload.narration_text:
This summary card gives you a quick demographic overview.

The percentage circles show your gender split. Most businesses skew one way or another based on their products and Meta's audience availability.

Two key callouts matter here. Top Spending Segment tells you where most of your budget actually goes - maybe 35-44 females. Most Efficient Segment tells you where you get the best cost per reach - maybe 25-34 males.

If these don't match, you have an optimization opportunity. You might be spending heavily on a segment that's expensive to reach while underinvesting in a cheap, efficient one.

This helps align your demographic targeting with efficiency.

payload.formula_explanation: The average number of times each person saw the prospecting ads, taken as the mean of daily frequency. For prospecting, keep this under 2 - the goal is fresh reach, not repeated exposure.
how_to_read: The value is the average exposures per person
For prospecting, under 2 is ideal - you want new people, not repeats
The sparkline shows the most recent 7-day frequency trend
Rising frequency means you are re-showing ads instead of reaching new people
High prospecting frequency wastes budget and signals saturation
decisions_answered: Are our prospecting ads reaching new people or repeating?
Is frequency staying under the prospecting target of 2?
Are we saturating audiences and wasting top-of-funnel budget?
Should we expand audiences to lower frequency?

Consider if current allocation matches your target customer profile
decisions_answered: Is our prospecting reaching the right demographic mix?

If these don't match, you have an optimization opportunity. You might be spending heavily on a segment that's expensive to reach while underinvesting in a cheap, efficient one.

This helps align your demographic targeting with efficiency.

payload.formula_text: Size of lookalike / Total addressable audience
payload.formula_explanation: Lists all lookalike audiences available for prospecting campaigns. Lookalike audiences find people similar to your existing customers. Smaller percentages (1%) are closer matches but smaller pools; larger percentages (5%+) are bigger pools but less precise.
how_to_read: Green 1% lookalikes are highest quality - closest match to your seed audience
Yellow 3% lookalikes balance quality and scale
Blue 5%+ lookalikes offer maximum reach but lower precision
Size columns show approximate audience pool size
Click any row to see campaigns using that audience
decisions_answered: What lookalike audiences are available for prospecting?
Should we test smaller, higher-quality lookalikes?
Are we over-relying on broad lookalikes?
Which lookalike tier provides the best efficiency?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your lookalike audiences.

Lookalikes are Meta's way of finding people similar to your customers. The percentage tells you how selective the matching is. A 1% lookalike finds the closest matches - highest quality but smaller pool. A 5% lookalike casts a wider net.

For prospecting, start with 1% lookalikes. They're your best bet for finding likely converters. As those saturate, expand to 3%, then 5%.

The size columns tell you how many people are in each audience. Bigger isn't always better. A 1% lookalike with 2 million people might outperform a 5% with 10 million.

Use this to plan your audience expansion strategy.

payload.formula_text: Monthly Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS / 1,000,000) per month Monthly Revenue = SUM(CONVERSIONS_VALUE) per month Monthly ROAS = Monthly Revenue / Monthly Spe
payload.formula_explanation: This chart displays two key metrics over 12 months: total spend and cost efficiency (cost per 1,000 unique people reached). The dual-axis view lets you see if increased spending is driving proportional reach or if efficiency is declining.
how_to_read: Blue line shows monthly spend in dollars across prospecting campaigns
Green line shows cost per 1,000 reach - lower values indicate better efficiency
Diverging lines suggest changing efficiency - if spend rises but cost/reach rises faster, audience saturation may be occurring
Click any point to filter the page to that specific month
Seasonal patterns help plan budget allocation throughout the year
decisions_answered: Is our prospecting spend efficiency improving or declining over time?
When did significant performance shifts occur?
Are there seasonal patterns we should account for in budget planning?
Should we scale up or pull back based on cost trends?
payload.narration_text:
This dual-axis chart tracks your Meta prospecting performance over 12 months.

The blue line shows how much you spent each month. The green line shows cost per 1,000 reach - this is your efficiency metric. Lower is better.

Here's the key insight: if spend goes up but cost per reach goes up faster, you're hitting audience saturation. That means you're paying more to reach the same number of new people.

Look for patterns. Q4 often shows higher costs due to auction competition. Spring might be your sweet spot for efficient prospecting.

This chart answers: Is efficiency improving? When did shifts happen? And should you scale up or pull back?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares spending across different Meta campaign objectives like Traffic, Sales, Awareness, and Leads. The horizontal bars show total spend, while the color gradient indicates efficiency - helping identify which objectives deliver the best reach for your investment.
how_to_read: Longer bars indicate higher spend allocation to that objective
Compare bar lengths to see budget distribution across objectives
Click any bar to filter the entire page to that objective
Labels show exact spend amount for precision
Use this to identify over or under-invested objectives
decisions_answered: Which campaign objectives receive the most budget?
Should we shift budget between Traffic, Sales, and Awareness objectives?
Are we over-investing in any particular objective?
Which objectives deliver the best prospecting results?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down your prospecting spend by campaign objective.

Each bar represents an objective type - Sales, Traffic, Awareness, Leads. The length shows how much budget that objective receives.

Campaign objectives matter because they tell Meta's algorithm what to optimize for. Traffic objectives reach people likely to click. Sales objectives target likely buyers. Awareness maximizes reach.

The question is: Is your budget allocation aligned with your goals? If you want new customers, Sales objectives should be well-funded. If you're building brand awareness in new markets, Awareness might deserve more budget.

Use this to rebalance your prospecting strategy.

payload.formula_text: Metrics BY platform (Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network)
payload.formula_explanation: Shows how your prospecting budget is distributed across Meta platforms including Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network, Messenger, and Threads. The donut visualization makes it easy to see which platforms dominate your media mix.
how_to_read: Each slice represents spend on a specific Meta platform
Center value shows total prospecting spend across all platforms
Larger slices indicate higher budget allocation
Hover for exact spend amounts and percentages
Click a slice to filter the page to that platform
decisions_answered: How is our prospecting budget split between Facebook and Instagram?
Are we utilizing Audience Network and Messenger effectively?
Should we test more spend on emerging platforms like Threads?
Is our platform mix aligned with where our target audience spends time?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows your prospecting spend distribution across Meta platforms.

Facebook typically dominates - it has the largest user base. Instagram often comes second, especially for visual product discovery. Audience Network extends your reach beyond Meta's owned properties. Messenger and Threads are usually smaller slices.

The center shows your total spend. Each slice is a platform's share.

Here's what to consider: Your platform mix should match where your prospective customers spend time. Younger demographics lean Instagram. Broader demos are on Facebook. Test allocation shifts and watch your cost per reach change.

This helps answer: Are we on the right platforms for our audience?

payload.formula_explanation: The unique number of people who saw the prospecting ads at least once. For prospecting, reach is the headline metric since the goal is to introduce the brand to as many new people as possible.
how_to_read: The value counts distinct people exposed to prospecting ads
Higher reach means a wider top-of-funnel audience
The sparkline shows the most recent 7-day reach trend
Compare reach growth against spend to gauge efficiency
Stalling reach despite spend suggests audience saturation
decisions_answered: How many new people did our prospecting reach?
Is our top-of-funnel audience expanding?
Are we saturating our prospecting pools?
Should we broaden targeting to grow reach?

payload.formula_explanation: Total advertising spend across all Meta prospecting campaigns for the selected period. Prospecting spend is the investment aimed at reaching brand-new, non-customer audiences.
how_to_read: The large number is total dollars invested in prospecting
The sparkline traces the most recent 7 days of daily spend
The change percentage compares against the prior period
Prospecting spend should buy fresh reach, not repeat exposure
Pair with cost per 1k reach to judge whether spend is efficient
decisions_answered: How much are we investing in reaching new audiences?
Is prospecting spend growing or shrinking versus last period?
Is our top-of-funnel budget pacing as planned?
Are we committing enough budget to feed new customer acquisition?

payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive table of all retargeting ad sets with key metrics. Status badges automatically flag ad sets showing signs of fatigue or those performing well.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank ad sets on that metric
Green status = healthy (low frequency, high CTR)
Yellow status = warning (approaching fatigue thresholds)
Red status = fatigue (high frequency or low CTR, needs action)
Click any row to drill into detailed ad set performance
decisions_answered: Which specific ad sets are performing best?
Which ad sets need immediate attention due to fatigue?
How do metrics compare across all retargeting ad sets?
Which campaigns should receive more or less budget?
payload.narration_text:
This data table provides a comprehensive view of all your retargeting ad sets with key performance metrics.

The status column uses a simple formula: If frequency is below 3 and CTR above 2%, it's marked healthy with a green badge. If frequency reaches 3 to 5 or CTR drops to 1 to 2%, you see a yellow warning. Red fatigue status appears when frequency exceeds 5 or CTR falls below 1%.

You can sort by any column - try sorting by spend to see where budget goes, by ROAS to find top performers, or by frequency to identify fatigued audiences.

The table answers practical questions: Which ad sets deserve more budget? Which need creative refresh or pausing? How does each campaign's retargeting component perform?

Click any row to dive deeper into that specific ad set's daily performance, creative breakdown, and audience details.

payload.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows how many ad sets use the same audience pairs. High overlap means you may be competing with yourself and over-spending on the same users across multiple ad sets.
how_to_read: Each cell shows how many ad sets target both audiences in that row/column pair
Darker blue cells indicate higher overlap (more ad sets sharing audiences)
Diagonal represents self-overlap (how many ad sets use each individual audience)
High overlap pairs may cause internal competition and inflated CPMs
Consider consolidating or excluding audiences with high overlap
decisions_answered: Which custom audiences have the most overlap?
Are we targeting the same users from multiple ad sets?
Should certain audiences be consolidated?
Where is internal competition potentially driving up costs?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals overlap between your custom audiences used in retargeting.

Each cell shows how many ad sets use both the row and column audience simultaneously. Darker colors mean higher overlap - more ad sets competing for the same users.

Why does this matter? When multiple ad sets target overlapping audiences, you're essentially bidding against yourself. This internal competition drives up your CPMs and wastes budget reaching the same users multiple times through different ad sets.

Look for the darkest cells off the diagonal. These pairs have significant overlap and may need attention. Consider consolidating overlapping audiences into a single ad set, or use audience exclusions to eliminate overlap.

The diagonal shows how widely each individual audience is used across your ad sets. A very high number suggests that audience might be over-utilized and could benefit from segmentation.

payload.formula_explanation: Frequency measures how many times each unique user sees the ad on average. CTR (Click-Through Rate) indicates the percentage of impressions that resulted in clicks. This chart reveals the relationship between ad exposure frequency and engagement.
how_to_read: Each bar represents an ad set, sorted by total spend (highest spend at top)
Blue bars show CTR percentage - higher is better (target: 2%+)
Orange bars show average frequency - lower is typically better (target: 1-3)
Look for inverse relationship: high frequency often correlates with low CTR (fatigue)
Ad sets with both high frequency (5+) and low CTR (<1%) indicate severe audience fatigue
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are showing signs of audience fatigue?
At what frequency level does CTR start declining significantly?
Which ad sets should have their frequency caps adjusted?
Are we over-exposing certain audiences while under-utilizing others?
payload.narration_text:
This chart analyzes the relationship between ad frequency and click-through rate for your retargeting campaigns.

The formula combines two key metrics: Frequency equals impressions divided by reach - how many times each user sees your ad. CTR equals clicks divided by impressions times 100.

To read this chart, look at the horizontal bars. Blue bars show CTR - you want these higher, targeting 2% or above. Orange bars show frequency - you generally want these lower, ideally between 1 and 3.

The key insight here is the inverse relationship: as frequency increases, CTR typically decreases. This is audience fatigue - people stop engaging when they see the same ad too many times. Ad sets showing frequency above 5 with CTR below 1% are red flags requiring immediate attention.

Use this chart to identify fatigued audiences, set optimal frequency caps, and decide which ad sets need creative refresh.

payload.formula_explanation: This multi-line chart tracks daily retargeting performance over 30 days, showing both spend levels and efficiency metrics to identify trends, anomalies, and seasonal patterns.
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily spend amounts (left axis) - track investment levels
Green line shows daily ROAS (right axis) - track efficiency over time
Look for correlation: does higher spend maintain strong ROAS?
Identify anomaly dates where ROAS spikes or crashes unexpectedly
Watch for weekly patterns - performance may vary by day of week
decisions_answered: Is our retargeting efficiency improving or declining over time?
When did significant performance changes occur?
Can we scale spend while maintaining acceptable ROAS?
Are there weekly patterns we should optimize around?
payload.narration_text:
This dual-axis line chart tracks your retargeting performance day by day over the past 30 days.

The blue line shows daily spend, measured on the left axis. The green line shows daily ROAS, measured on the right axis. This combination reveals both how much you're investing and how efficiently that investment performs.

Key things to watch: Does ROAS hold steady as you scale spend, or does it drop? That indicates whether you have room to grow. Sudden ROAS spikes or crashes on specific dates warrant investigation - what changed? Also look for weekly patterns - many brands see different performance on weekdays versus weekends.

This trend analysis helps answer fundamental questions: Is efficiency improving over time? When did things change? And critically - can we spend more while maintaining good returns, or are we hitting diminishing returns?

payload.formula_text: Excluded In / Total Ad Sets Using
payload.formula_explanation: Inventory of all custom audiences available for retargeting, showing size estimates, usage across ad sets, and identification of underutilized audiences.
how_to_read: Type badges show audience source: Custom (email), Website, Engagement, Lookalike
Size estimates show approximate audience reach
Ad Sets Using column reveals which audiences are most actively targeted
Excluded In shows how often audiences are used as exclusions
Low usage audiences may represent untapped opportunities
decisions_answered: Which custom audiences are available but underutilized?
What are the sizes of our key retargeting audiences?
Which audiences are most commonly used in targeting?
Are we properly excluding purchasers from prospecting campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This inventory table shows all custom audiences available for your Meta retargeting campaigns.

The type badges help categorize audiences: Custom typically means email uploads from Klaviyo, Website means pixel-based audiences, Engagement tracks platform interactions, and Lookalike are Meta-generated similar audiences.

Size estimates show approximate audience counts - useful for forecasting reach. The Ad Sets Using column reveals your most utilized audiences, while Excluded In shows exclusion usage - important for preventing ads to recent purchasers.

Key insights come from finding patterns: Are your largest audiences being fully utilized? Are there audiences with zero ad sets using them - potential untapped opportunities? Is your purchaser audience properly excluded from prospecting?

This inventory helps you understand your full retargeting toolkit and identify both over-relied-upon audiences and hidden opportunities.

payload.formula_explanation: Measures CTR decline of the current period versus the comparison (historical) period. A higher percentage means engagement has dropped, indicating audience fatigue. The reference target is 15%.
how_to_read: The value is the percentage CTR decline versus the historical baseline
Higher percentages mean more fatigue - audiences are tuning out
Around 15% is used as the comparison reference threshold
The sparkline shows how the fatigue score moved across the recent period
A climbing fatigue score is the cue to refresh creative or cap frequency
decisions_answered: Are our retargeting audiences showing fatigue?
How much has engagement declined versus the baseline?
Which campaigns need creative refresh most urgently?
Should we cap frequency to slow the fatigue?

payload.formula_explanation: Frequency measures how many times each unique user sees the ad on average. CTR (Click-Through Rate) indicates the percentage of impressions that resulted in clicks. This chart reveals the relationship between ad exposure frequency and engagement.
how_to_read: Each bar represents an ad set, sorted by total spend (highest spend at top)
Blue bars show CTR percentage - higher is better (target: 2%+)
Orange bars show average frequency - lower is typically better (target: 1-3)
Look for inverse relationship: high frequency often correlates with low CTR (fatigue)
Ad sets with both high frequency (5+) and low CTR (<1%) indicate severe audience fatigue
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are showing signs of audience fatigue?
At what frequency level does CTR start declining significantly?
Which ad sets should have their frequency caps adjusted?
Are we over-exposing certain audiences while under-utilizing others?

The formula combines two key metrics: Frequency equals impressions divided by reach - how many times each user sees your ad. CTR equals clicks divided by impressions times 100.

To read this chart, look at the horizontal bars. Blue bars show CTR - you want these higher, targeting 2% or above. Orange bars show frequency - you generally want these lower, ideally between 1 and 3.

The key insight here is the inverse relationship: as frequency increases, CTR typically decreases. This is audience fatigue - people stop engaging when they see the same ad too many times. Ad sets showing frequency above 5 with CTR below 1% are red flags requiring immediate attention.

Use this chart to identify fatigued audiences, set optimal frequency caps, and decide which ad sets need creative refresh.

payload.formula_explanation: The total unique users who saw retargeting ads during the period. For retargeting this represents the size of the warm audience you are actively re-engaging.
how_to_read: The value counts distinct people reached by retargeting ads
The sparkline shows daily reach trends across the recent period
Reach here is bounded by your re-engageable audience pool
Flat reach with rising impressions means rising frequency
Shrinking reach can indicate the warm pool is being exhausted
decisions_answered: How many warm users are we re-engaging?
Is our retargeting audience pool growing or shrinking?
Are we exhausting the people available to retarget?
Do we need to feed more visitors into the retargeting funnel?

payload.formula_explanation: Return on Ad Spend measures the revenue generated for every dollar spent on advertising. This chart segments ROAS by audience type to identify which retargeting segments deliver the best returns.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show ROAS for each audience type (higher is better)
Green bars indicate strong ROAS (5x+), yellow is acceptable (2-5x), red needs attention (<2x)
Bar length represents relative performance - longer bars mean better returns
The vertical reference line at 4x marks the typical breakeven threshold
Hover to see exact ROAS values, spend amounts, and revenue generated
decisions_answered: Which audience segments deliver the highest return on ad spend?
Should we increase budget for high-performing audience types?
Which audience types are underperforming and need optimization?
How do email-based audiences (Klaviyo synced) compare to website visitors?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart breaks down Return on Ad Spend by audience type for your retargeting campaigns.

The formula is straightforward: ROAS equals total revenue divided by total spend. Revenue comes from purchase events tracked by Meta.

Reading the chart is simple - longer bars mean higher ROAS, which is what you want. The color coding helps quickly identify performance: green bars at 5x or above are excellent, yellow between 2 and 5x is acceptable, and red below 2x needs attention. The dashed line at 4x marks a common breakeven threshold.

This chart answers critical questions: Which segments return the most revenue per dollar? Where should you shift budget? Are your email list audiences from Klaviyo outperforming website visitor retargeting?

Use these insights to prioritize budget toward your highest-performing audience types while investigating or pausing underperformers.

payload.formula_explanation: Total spend across all Meta retargeting campaigns for the period. Retargeting spend is the investment in re-engaging people who already interacted with the brand or visited the site.
how_to_read: The large number is total dollars invested in retargeting
The sparkline traces daily retargeting spend over the recent period
The change percentage compares against the prior period
Retargeting typically carries higher ROAS than prospecting
Watch that spend does not outpace your re-engageable audience size
decisions_answered: How much are we investing in re-engaging past visitors?
Is retargeting spend growing or shrinking versus last period?
Is our bottom-of-funnel budget pacing as planned?
Are we over-spending against a limited retargeting pool?

payload.formula_text: Ad Sets Using / Total Active Ad Sets

how_to_read: Type badges show audience source: Custom (email), Website, Engagement, Lookalike
Size estimates show approximate audience reach
Ad Sets Using column reveals which audiences are most actively targeted
Excluded In shows how often audiences are used as exclusions
Low usage audiences may represent untapped opportunities

payload.formula_explanation: Calculates the average time elapsed since the last successful sync across all monitored schemas. Lower values indicate fresher data. This metric helps identify if data is becoming stale across the board.
how_to_read: Displays average minutes behind across all schemas
Formatted as minutes (m), hours (h), or days (d)
Green: < 60 minutes (data is fresh)
Yellow: 60-360 minutes (acceptable but monitor)
Red: > 360 minutes (data is stale, investigate)
Sparkline shows lag trend over time
decisions_answered: How fresh is my data on average?
Is there a general slowdown in data syncing?
Do I need to wait for fresher data before reporting?
Is the overall sync performance degrading over time?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows the average time since last successful sync across all schemas.

The formula: sum of minutes since sync for each schema, divided by schema count. Each schema's lag is calculated from the most recent Fivetran sync timestamp.

Under 60 minutes is fresh and green. 60 to 360 minutes is acceptable but worth monitoring. Over 360 minutes indicates stale data requiring investigation.

This answers: How fresh is my data? Is syncing slowing down? Should I wait for fresher data before reporting?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the percentage of NULL values in critical columns across key tables. High NULL percentages indicate data quality issues that could affect analysis accuracy. The 99% completeness threshold represents best practice for marketing data.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a table and its NULL percentage
Shorter bars (lower %) indicate better data quality
Orange color highlights fields with NULL values
The 99% threshold line shows completeness target
Bars extending beyond 1% warrant investigation
Hover for exact NULL counts and percentages
Click a bar to see field-level breakdown
decisions_answered: Are there data quality issues with any tables?
Which fields have the most missing values?
Is my data complete enough for accurate reporting?
Do I need to address data quality with upstream systems?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows NULL value percentages for critical columns in key tables.

The formula: count of NULL values divided by total rows, times 100. Lower percentages mean better data quality. The target is 99% completeness or under 1% NULLs.

Orange bars show the NULL percentage. Bars extending beyond 1% need investigation. Common causes include tracking issues, schema changes, or upstream data problems.

This answers: Are there data quality issues? Which fields have missing values? Is data complete enough for reporting?

payload.formula_text: Days with data / Total days in range
payload.formula_explanation: A heatmap visualization showing which dates have data for each schema. Green cells indicate data is present; red cells indicate missing data for that date/schema combination. This helps identify gaps in historical data that could affect reporting accuracy.
how_to_read: Each row represents a data schema (GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo)
Each column represents a date (last 14 days shown)
Green cells mean data exists for that date
Red cells mean data is missing for that date
Gray cells indicate future dates or today
Click a cell to see details for that date/schema
Patterns of red may indicate sync failures or data issues
decisions_answered: Are there any missing dates in my data?
Which schemas have date coverage gaps?
Was data collection working properly last week?
Do I have complete data for reporting periods?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap shows data availability by date and schema over the last 14 days.

Each row is a schema. Each column is a date. Green means data exists. Red means data is missing. The formula checks if any records exist for each date-schema combination.

Look for patterns: a column of red indicates a day when all syncs failed. A row with red spots indicates schema-specific issues.

This answers: Are there missing dates? Which schemas have gaps? Was data collection working properly?

payload.formula_text: COUNT(Alerts WHERE severity = 'critical' AND triggered_at >= NOW() - 24h)

payload.formula_explanation: Uses statistical z-scores to detect unusual patterns in key metrics like sessions and revenue. A z-score measures how many standard deviations a value is from the mean. Values beyond 2 standard deviations occur less than 5% of the time and warrant investigation.
how_to_read: The number shows count of days with critical anomalies in the past 7 days
Zero (green) means no unusual patterns detected
One or more (yellow/red) indicates days with significant deviations
Accompanying warning count shows less severe anomalies
Click to see anomaly details and investigate causes
Red pulsing indicates active critical anomalies
decisions_answered: Are there any data quality issues I should investigate?
Did any unusual events affect my metrics recently?
Is my data behaving normally compared to historical patterns?
Should I be concerned about any sudden changes?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI counts days with critical anomalies detected in the past 7 days using statistical z-scores.

The formula: calculate z-score as value minus mean, divided by standard deviation. Critical anomalies have z-scores beyond 2, meaning they're outside 95% of normal values.

Zero is ideal - no unusual patterns. Higher counts mean investigate further. Anomalies could indicate real events like promotions, or data quality issues.

This answers: Are there data quality issues? Did unusual events affect metrics? Should I investigate any sudden changes?

payload.formula_explanation: Summarizes the historical date range available for each data schema. Shows how far back data goes and how current it is. Longer coverage enables better historical analysis; being behind today indicates potential sync issues.
how_to_read: Each row represents one data schema
Earliest Date shows how far back data is available
Latest Date shows the most recent data
Days Coverage shows total span of historical data
Days Behind shows how far behind current date
Green (0-1 days) means data is current
Yellow (2-3 days) means slight delay
Red (>3 days) means significant delay requiring action
Click a schema to see detailed table breakdown
decisions_answered: How far back does my historical data go?
Is my data current or lagging behind?
Which schemas have the longest historical coverage?
Do I have enough history for trend analysis?
payload.narration_text:
This table summarizes date range coverage for each data schema.

The formulas calculate days between earliest and latest dates, and days between latest date and today. Longer coverage enables better historical analysis.

Days Behind is key: zero or one means data is current. Two or three is a slight delay. More than three days indicates sync issues requiring investigation.

This answers: How far back does data go? Is data current or lagging? Do I have enough history for trends?

payload.formula_text: SPIKE if Z > 0, DROP if Z < 0
payload.formula_explanation: Lists all detected statistical anomalies from the past 30 days sorted by date. Each entry shows the date, metric values, z-scores, anomaly type, and direction. This log helps prioritize which anomalies to investigate first based on severity.
how_to_read: Date column shows when the anomaly occurred
Sessions and Revenue show actual values that day
Z-Score columns show how many standard deviations from mean
Higher absolute z-scores indicate more severe anomalies
Type indicates which metric triggered the anomaly
Direction shows SPIKE (positive) or DROP (negative)
Red background indicates critical severity
Click a row to investigate that date further
decisions_answered: What anomalies have occurred recently?
Which anomalies are most severe and need attention first?
Are anomalies mostly spikes or drops?
Is there a pattern to when anomalies occur?
payload.narration_text:
This table logs all detected statistical anomalies from the past 30 days.

Each row shows: date, sessions value, revenue value, z-scores for each, anomaly type, and direction. Z-scores over 2 are critical. Direction tells you if it was a spike or drop.

Color coding helps prioritize: red rows are most severe. Orange are moderate. Focus on red rows first.

This answers: What anomalies occurred recently? Which are most severe? Are they mostly spikes or drops?

This table summarizes date range coverage for each data schema.

The formulas calculate days between earliest and latest dates, and days between latest date and today. Longer coverage enables better historical analysis.

Days Behind is key: zero or one means data is current. Two or three is a slight delay. More than three days indicates sync issues requiring investigation.

This answers: How far back does data go? Is data current or lagging? Do I have enough history for trends?

payload.formula_explanation: Displays Klaviyo email and SMS event volumes by type over the last 7 days. Each event type (Received Email, Opened Email, Clicked Email, etc.) has its own count and sync status. This helps validate that Klaviyo events are flowing correctly.
how_to_read: Each bar represents an event type
Bar length indicates event volume (count)
Labels show formatted counts (e.g., 1.2M)
Event types sorted by volume (highest first)
Top 10 event types displayed
Freshness badge next to each shows sync status
Click an event type to drill into Klaviyo details
decisions_answered: Are Klaviyo events syncing properly?
What's the volume distribution across event types?
Are email opens tracking correctly relative to sends?
Has event volume changed unexpectedly?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows Klaviyo event volumes by type over the last 7 days.

The formula counts events by type and tracks the last sync timestamp for each. Types include Received Email, Opened Email, Clicked Email, Placed Order, and more.

Volume distribution helps validate tracking: opens should be a percentage of receives. Clicks should be a percentage of opens. Unexpected ratios indicate tracking issues.

This answers: Are Klaviyo events syncing? What's the volume distribution? Are email metrics tracking correctly?

payload.formula_explanation: A heatmap visualization showing which dates have data for each schema. Green cells indicate data is present; red cells indicate missing data for that date/schema combination. This helps identify gaps in historical data that could affect reporting accuracy.
how_to_read: Each row represents a data schema (GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads, Klaviyo)
Each column represents a date (last 14 days shown)
Green cells mean data exists for that date
Red cells mean data is missing for that date
Gray cells indicate future dates or today
Click a cell to see details for that date/schema
Patterns of red may indicate sync failures or data issues
decisions_answered: Are there any missing dates in my data?
Which schemas have date coverage gaps?
Was data collection working properly last week?
Do I have complete data for reporting periods?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap shows data availability by date and schema over the last 14 days.

Each row is a schema. Each column is a date. Green means data exists. Red means data is missing. The formula checks if any records exist for each date-schema combination.

Look for patterns: a column of red indicates a day when all syncs failed. A row with red spots indicates schema-specific issues.

This answers: Are there missing dates? Which schemas have gaps? Was data collection working properly?

Bar length indicates event volume (count)

Event types sorted by volume (highest first)
