Volume distribution helps validate tracking: opens should be a percentage of receives. Clicks should be a percentage of opens. Unexpected ratios indicate tracking issues.

This answers: Are Klaviyo events syncing? What's the volume distribution? Are email metrics tracking correctly?

payload.formula_explanation: Calculates the average time elapsed since the last successful sync across all monitored schemas. Lower values indicate fresher data. This metric helps identify if data is becoming stale across the board.
how_to_read: Displays average minutes behind across all schemas
Formatted as minutes (m), hours (h), or days (d)
Green: < 60 minutes (data is fresh)
Yellow: 60-360 minutes (acceptable but monitor)
Red: > 360 minutes (data is stale, investigate)
Sparkline shows lag trend over time
decisions_answered: How fresh is my data on average?
Is there a general slowdown in data syncing?
Do I need to wait for fresher data before reporting?
Is the overall sync performance degrading over time?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows the average time since last successful sync across all schemas.

The formula: sum of minutes since sync for each schema, divided by schema count. Each schema's lag is calculated from the most recent Fivetran sync timestamp.

Under 60 minutes is fresh and green. 60 to 360 minutes is acceptable but worth monitoring. Over 360 minutes indicates stale data requiring investigation.

This answers: How fresh is my data? Is syncing slowing down? Should I wait for fresher data before reporting?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the percentage of NULL values in critical columns across key tables. High NULL percentages indicate data quality issues that could affect analysis accuracy. The 99% completeness threshold represents best practice for marketing data.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a table and its NULL percentage
Shorter bars (lower %) indicate better data quality
Orange color highlights fields with NULL values
The 99% threshold line shows completeness target
Bars extending beyond 1% warrant investigation
Hover for exact NULL counts and percentages
Click a bar to see field-level breakdown
decisions_answered: Are there data quality issues with any tables?
Which fields have the most missing values?
Is my data complete enough for accurate reporting?
Do I need to address data quality with upstream systems?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows NULL value percentages for critical columns in key tables.

The formula: count of NULL values divided by total rows, times 100. Lower percentages mean better data quality. The target is 99% completeness or under 1% NULLs.

Orange bars show the NULL percentage. Bars extending beyond 1% need investigation. Common causes include tracking issues, schema changes, or upstream data problems.

This answers: Are there data quality issues? Which fields have missing values? Is data complete enough for reporting?

payload.formula_explanation: Displays daily sessions and revenue trends over 60 days with anomaly detection. The chart overlays anomaly markers when values deviate significantly from historical averages. This helps identify both data quality issues and genuine business events.
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily sessions (left Y-axis)
Green line shows daily revenue (right Y-axis)
Red circles mark anomaly points (z-score > 2)
Yellow triangles mark warning points (z-score 1.5-2)
Hover over any point to see exact values and z-scores
Sudden spikes or drops warrant investigation
Consistent patterns indicate stable data quality
Click a date to drill into detailed breakdown
decisions_answered: Are there unusual spikes or drops in my key metrics?
When did the last anomaly occur?
Is the variance in my data normal or concerning?
Should I investigate any specific dates?
payload.narration_text:
This dual-line chart shows daily sessions and revenue over 60 days with anomaly detection.

Sessions are in blue on the left axis. Revenue is in green on the right axis. Red markers indicate critical anomalies where z-score exceeds 2. Yellow markers show warnings between 1.5 and 2.

Look for: sudden drops could indicate tracking issues. Spikes could indicate real events or data errors. Consistent patterns indicate stable data quality.

This answers: Are there unusual spikes or drops? When did anomalies occur? Should I investigate specific dates?

payload.formula_explanation: Lists all detected statistical anomalies from the past 30 days sorted by date. Each entry shows the date, metric values, z-scores, anomaly type, and direction. This log helps prioritize which anomalies to investigate first based on severity.
how_to_read: Date column shows when the anomaly occurred
Sessions and Revenue show actual values that day
Z-Score columns show how many standard deviations from mean
Higher absolute z-scores indicate more severe anomalies
Type indicates which metric triggered the anomaly
Direction shows SPIKE (positive) or DROP (negative)
Red background indicates critical severity
Click a row to investigate that date further
decisions_answered: What anomalies have occurred recently?
Which anomalies are most severe and need attention first?
Are anomalies mostly spikes or drops?
Is there a pattern to when anomalies occur?
payload.narration_text:
This table logs all detected statistical anomalies from the past 30 days.

Each row shows: date, sessions value, revenue value, z-scores for each, anomaly type, and direction. Z-scores over 2 are critical. Direction tells you if it was a spike or drop.

Color coding helps prioritize: red rows are most severe. Orange are moderate. Focus on red rows first.

This answers: What anomalies occurred recently? Which are most severe? Are they mostly spikes or drops?

payload.formula_text: Schema Health % = (Fresh Schemas + Acceptable Schemas) / Total Schemas * 100
payload.formula_explanation: Measures the overall health of your data pipeline by tracking how recently each schema was synced. Fivetran syncs data at regular intervals, and this metric shows what percentage of your data sources are current. A 100% score means all schemas synced within 6 hours.
how_to_read: The percentage shows the proportion of schemas that are fresh or acceptable
Green (100%) means all 4 data sources are syncing properly
Yellow (75%) means 1 schema is stale or critical
Red (<50%) means multiple schemas have sync issues requiring immediate attention
Sparkline shows schema health trend over recent days
Click to navigate to Master Config for detailed schema management
decisions_answered: Are all my data sources syncing properly?
Is the data pipeline healthy enough for reliable reporting?
Do I need to investigate any sync failures?
Which schemas are experiencing delays?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows overall schema health as a percentage of data sources that are fresh or acceptable.

The formula: add fresh and acceptable schema counts, divide by total schemas, times 100. Fresh means synced within 60 minutes. Acceptable means within 6 hours.

A 100% score means all data sources are current. Below 75% indicates sync issues. Below 50% is critical and requires immediate investigation.

This answers: Are all data sources syncing? Is the pipeline healthy for reporting? Which schemas need attention?

payload.formula_explanation: Actual weekly revenue compared to a baseline that averages revenue across weeks with the same week-of-year number within the selected window. Variance shows how far each week deviates from its same-week-of-year baseline.
how_to_read: The actual line shows weekly revenue for the selected period
The baseline line is the average for that week-of-year across the range
Weeks above the baseline have positive variance; below is negative
Large variances flag unusual weeks worth investigating
Sustained deviations from baseline signal a shift in trend
decisions_answered: Are recent weeks outperforming or underperforming their historical norm?
Which weeks deviated most from the expected baseline?
Is revenue trending above or below seasonal expectations?
Where should I dig in to explain an anomalous week?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates event counts from GA4 Events Report, grouped by date. Each line shows the daily volume at each funnel stage: item views, cart additions, and purchases. The gap between lines represents drop-off at each stage.
how_to_read: Three stacked areas show View, Add to Cart, and Purchase volumes
The purple area (Add to Cart) relative to blue (Views) shows cart intent
The green area (Purchases) relative to purple shows fulfillment success
A widening gap between cart and purchase suggests inventory issues
Look for sudden drops in purchase volume while cart stays high - stockout signal
Hover over any date to see exact values for all three metrics
decisions_answered: Are customers adding items to cart but not completing purchases?
Which days show unusual gaps between cart activity and purchases?
Is the overall funnel health improving or degrading over time?
Are there specific days where potential stockouts are evident?
payload.narration_text:
This trend chart shows your daily shopping funnel - from item views through cart additions to purchases.

The formula tracks three event counts from GA4. Views in blue show customer interest. Add to cart in purple shows purchase intent. Purchases in green show fulfillment.

Reading this chart: The key insight is the gap between purple and green. When cart adds stay high but purchases drop, customers want to buy but cannot. This is your stockout signal.

Look for days where the green line suddenly drops while purple stays flat or rises. These are likely stockout events costing you revenue.

Key questions answered: Is inventory limiting sales? Which days show the biggest fulfillment gaps? Is the problem getting better or worse? Use this to prioritize restocking and monitor recovery.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares each item's cart-to-purchase conversion rate against the healthy benchmark. Items with significant cart activity but below-benchmark conversion indicate potential availability issues - customers add to cart but cannot complete purchase at normal rates.
how_to_read: Actual % vs Benchmark % shows how far below normal the item converts
Large gaps suggest inventory constraints limiting purchases
Estimated Lost Revenue quantifies the opportunity cost
Items are sorted by lost revenue - biggest opportunities first
Red highlighting on Est. Lost Revenue emphasizes the impact
Click any row to investigate the specific item further
decisions_answered: Which items have abnormally low cart-to-purchase conversion?
How much revenue are we losing from conversion underperformance?
Which items might have inventory issues causing low conversion?
Where should we investigate potential stockouts or limited inventory?
payload.narration_text:
This table identifies items converting below benchmark - potential low stock situations.

The formula compares each item's cart to purchase rate against the average of healthy items. When customers add to cart at normal rates but purchase at lower rates, something is blocking completion.

Reading this table: The gap between Actual and Benchmark tells the story. A 5% actual versus 27% benchmark means customers want to buy but 80% are blocked. That is often limited inventory.

Est. Lost Revenue shows the stakes. This is calculated conservatively - the real impact may be higher if frustrated customers leave entirely.

Key questions answered: Which items have suspicious conversion gaps? How much revenue is at risk? Where should we check inventory levels? Use this to find items with limited stock before they fully stock out.

payload.formula_explanation: Categorizes products into performance tiers based on their first 90-day revenue compared to historical percentile benchmarks. This distribution shows how new products perform relative to established success thresholds.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of products in each performance tier
Green (Top Performer) and blue (Strong) are your success stories
Gray (Average) products meet baseline expectations
Amber (Below Average) and red (Underperformer) need attention
Light gray (No Revenue) indicates products not yet generating sales
Center number shows total products being evaluated
decisions_answered: What percentage of new products meet performance benchmarks?
How many products are top performers vs underperformers?
Is our new product success rate healthy?
How many products have not generated any revenue?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your new products distribute across performance tiers - a health check for your launch success.

The formula compares each product's first 90-day revenue against historical benchmarks. Top performers exceed the 90th percentile at $4,061. Strong is 75th to 90th. Average is 50th to 75th, and so on.

Reading this chart: The donut segments show what percentage of products fall into each tier. Green and blue segments represent success. Gray is acceptable. Amber and red need attention. Light gray shows products with zero revenue.

A healthy distribution has meaningful green and blue segments. If most products cluster in underperformer or no revenue segments, your product development or launch process needs review.

Key questions answered: What's our success rate? How many top performers? How many failures? Use this to evaluate and improve your new product launch effectiveness.

payload.formula_explanation: Average per-day revenue for each day of the week, computed as total revenue on that weekday divided by the number of distinct dates of that weekday in the period. Percent-of-week shows each day's share of the summed daily averages.
how_to_read: Each bar is the average revenue for one day of the week
Higher bars indicate consistently stronger sales days
Order Count (ITEMS_PURCHASED) provides volume context behind revenue
Percent of Week shows each day's share of the weekly average total
Compare weekday vs weekend bars to see the shopping rhythm
decisions_answered: Which days of the week drive the most revenue?
When should I schedule promotions or email sends for maximum impact?
Are weekends or weekdays stronger for this catalog?
Which days are underperforming and may need a boost?

payload.formula_explanation: Calculates the average selling price per item by dividing total revenue by total units sold. This metric helps track pricing trends, mix shifts, and promotional impact on average order composition.
how_to_read: The main value shows average price per item sold
Rising average price may indicate mix shift to premium items
Declining price could signal promotional activity or category mix changes
The sparkline shows 14-day price trend stability
Click to view price distribution by category
decisions_answered: What is the typical price point customers are paying?
Is our product mix shifting toward higher or lower prices?
Are promotions impacting average selling price?
How does our average price compare to category benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI shows your average item price - a crucial metric for understanding pricing and product mix.

The formula divides total revenue by total items sold. This weighted average reflects the actual prices customers pay across your catalog.

Reading this card: The main value shows your average selling price. Changes here don't always mean you changed prices - they often reflect shifts in product mix. More premium item sales raise the average; more value items lower it.

Price trends are diagnostic. A rising average price with flat volume may indicate successful premium positioning. A declining price with rising volume could signal effective promotional activity or mix shift to essentials.

Key questions: What's the typical item price? Is the mix shifting? How are promotions impacting pricing? Click to explore price distribution across categories.

payload.formula_explanation: Average number of items per order, computed by dividing total items ordered by total orders over the period from MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY_DAILY. Higher values indicate more successful cross-selling and basket building.
how_to_read: The main value shows the average number of items per order
Higher values indicate effective cross-sell and bundling
Compare to the comparison period to gauge basket-size momentum
The sparkline shows the daily average-items trend
Click to explore the item-count distribution
decisions_answered: How many items does a typical order contain?
Is our cross-sell strategy increasing basket size?
Are customers discovering complementary products?
Is items-per-order trending up or down?

payload.formula_explanation: Groups orders by the number of unique products purchased, then counts orders and calculates average order value for each bucket. Shows the distribution of single-item versus multi-item orders.
how_to_read: Each bar represents orders with a specific number of items (1-item, 2-item, etc.)
Bar height shows the count of orders in each bucket
Single-item orders typically dominate, representing cross-sell opportunities
Multi-item orders (3+) indicate successful product discovery and bundling
The 6+ bucket aggregates high-quantity orders to prevent chart distortion
Click any bar to drill down into orders with that item count
decisions_answered: What percentage of orders are single-item vs multi-item?
Are customers discovering and purchasing complementary products?
Is our cross-sell strategy effectively increasing items per order?
Which item count bucket contributes most to total orders?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how orders are distributed by the number of items purchased - a key indicator of cross-sell success.

The formula counts unique products per order, then groups orders into buckets: 1-item, 2-item, 3-item, and so on. The 6-plus bucket aggregates larger orders.

Reading this chart: Each bar represents orders with that specific item count. Single-item orders typically form the tallest bar - this is normal but represents your biggest cross-sell opportunity.

Multi-item orders at 3-plus indicate customers who discovered complementary products. Higher bars in these buckets mean your product recommendations and bundling strategy are working.

Key questions answered: What percentage are single vs multi-item orders? Are customers finding complementary products? Is cross-selling effective? Click any bar to explore orders in that bucket.

payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive sortable table showing all key metrics for each category. Includes inline sparklines for trend visualization and enables multi-metric comparison and export for deeper analysis.
how_to_read: Click column headers to sort by any metric
Sparklines show 14-day trend direction
Growth badges are color-coded (green positive, red negative)
Use search to filter specific categories
Export button downloads data as CSV
decisions_answered: How do all metrics compare across categories?
Which categories excel in specific metrics but lag in others?
What are the detailed numbers behind chart visualizations?
Which categories need attention based on multiple metrics?

payload.formula_explanation: Total revenue from newly launched products divided by the count of distinct new products, giving the average revenue each new product has generated since its launch.
how_to_read: The value is the mean revenue per newly launched product
A higher figure means new launches are monetizing more effectively on average
It is diluted by products that launched but generated little or no revenue
Read alongside Success Rate to separate breadth from depth of performance
Compare to prior period to see if launch quality is improving
decisions_answered: How much revenue does a typical new product generate?
Is the quality of new launches improving over time?
Are we launching too many low-revenue products?
How does average new-product revenue compare to the prior period?

Gray (Average) products meet baseline expectations
Amber (Below Average) and red (Underperformer) need attention
Light gray (No Revenue) indicates products not yet generating sales

decisions_answered: What percentage of new products meet performance benchmarks?
How many products are top performers vs underperformers?
Is our new product success rate healthy?
How many products have not generated any revenue?

The formula compares each product's first 90-day revenue against historical benchmarks. Top performers exceed the 90th percentile at $4,061. Strong is 75th to 90th. Average is 50th to 75th, and so on.

Reading this chart: The donut segments show what percentage of products fall into each tier. Green and blue segments represent success. Gray is acceptable. Amber and red need attention. Light gray shows products with zero revenue.

A healthy distribution has meaningful green and blue segments. If most products cluster in underperformer or no revenue segments, your product development or launch process needs review.

Key questions answered: What's our success rate? How many top performers? How many failures? Use this to evaluate and improve your new product launch effectiveness.

payload.formula_text: AVG(purchased/adds for items with adds > 50 AND purchased > 0) * 100

how_to_read: Actual % vs Benchmark % shows how far below normal the item converts
Large gaps suggest inventory constraints limiting purchases
Estimated Lost Revenue quantifies the opportunity cost
Items are sorted by lost revenue - biggest opportunities first
Red highlighting on Est. Lost Revenue emphasizes the impact
Click any row to investigate the specific item further
decisions_answered: Which items have abnormally low cart-to-purchase conversion?
How much revenue are we losing from conversion underperformance?
Which items might have inventory issues causing low conversion?
Where should we investigate potential stockouts or limited inventory?
payload.narration_text:
This table identifies items converting below benchmark - potential low stock situations.

The formula compares each item's cart to purchase rate against the average of healthy items. When customers add to cart at normal rates but purchase at lower rates, something is blocking completion.

Reading this table: The gap between Actual and Benchmark tells the story. A 5% actual versus 27% benchmark means customers want to buy but 80% are blocked. That is often limited inventory.

Est. Lost Revenue shows the stakes. This is calculated conservatively - the real impact may be higher if frustrated customers leave entirely.

Key questions answered: Which items have suspicious conversion gaps? How much revenue is at risk? Where should we check inventory levels? Use this to find items with limited stock before they fully stock out.

payload.formula_text: AVG(cart_to_purchase_rate) for items with >100 cart adds and purchases > 0
payload.formula_explanation: Calculates lost revenue by comparing each category's cart-to-purchase conversion against the benchmark. The gap multiplied by cart volume gives lost units, then multiplied by average price gives lost revenue opportunity.
how_to_read: Each horizontal bar represents a product category
Bar length shows estimated lost revenue from below-benchmark conversion
Categories are sorted by lost revenue - biggest opportunities at top
Longer bars indicate categories with more recovery potential
Click on a category bar to drill down to specific items
The color gradient intensifies with higher lost revenue amounts
decisions_answered: Which categories have the most revenue recovery potential?
Where should inventory restocking efforts be prioritized?
Which categories are underperforming against the benchmark?
How much revenue could be recovered by fixing availability issues?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows estimated lost revenue by product category - your restock priority list.

The formula calculates a benchmark conversion from healthy items, then measures how much each category falls short. The gap times cart volume times average price equals lost revenue.

Reading this chart: Start from the top. These categories have the biggest recovery potential. A 50 thousand dollar bar means if you fixed availability, you could capture that revenue.

The calculation is conservative - actual lost revenue may be higher if stockouts are driving customers away entirely.

Key questions answered: Which categories need restocking first? How much is each category costing you? Where will inventory investment have the biggest return? Use this to prioritize purchasing decisions.

payload.formula_explanation: Plots products on a 2x2 matrix with revenue on the x-axis and growth rate on the y-axis. Products are classified into quadrants: Stars (high revenue, high growth), Rising Stars (low revenue, high growth), Cash Cows (high revenue, low growth), and Dogs (low revenue, low growth).
how_to_read: Each bubble represents a product - size indicates units sold
Top-right (green): Stars - high revenue AND growing fast - your winners
Top-left (blue): Rising Stars - low revenue but growing - watch and nurture
Bottom-right (yellow): Cash Cows - high revenue but slowing - milk but watch
Bottom-left (red): Dogs - low revenue and declining - consider action
Hover over bubbles to see product details
decisions_answered: Which products are our current stars driving growth?
Which emerging products should we invest in promoting?
Are any high-revenue products showing declining trends?
Which products might need to be discontinued or repositioned?

payload.formula_explanation: Percentage of orders that contain bundle products, computed as bundle orders divided by total orders over the period from MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY_DAILY. Indicates how much of the order base is driven by bundle/kit/pack products.
how_to_read: The main value shows the percentage of orders containing bundle products
Rising bundle penetration indicates successful bundle merchandising
Compare to the comparison period to track bundle adoption
The sparkline shows the daily bundle-percentage trend
Click to explore which bundles are driving the share
decisions_answered: What share of orders include bundle products?
Is bundle adoption growing over time?
Are our bundle merchandising efforts working?
Should we create or promote more bundles?

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies products explicitly marketed as bundles (containing keywords like bundle, kit, pack, set, or collection) and tracks their sales performance including orders, units, revenue, and average pricing.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product marketed as a bundle
Sort by Revenue to see top-performing bundles
Compare Units to Orders to see quantity per order patterns
Avg Price helps evaluate bundle pricing strategy
Empty table means no products match bundle naming conventions
Click any bundle to navigate to its product detail page
decisions_answered: Which bundles generate the most revenue?
Are our bundles priced competitively?
How many bundle units sell per order?
Which bundle types (kits, packs, collections) perform best?
payload.narration_text:
This table tracks performance of products explicitly marketed as bundles - products named with bundle, kit, pack, set, or collection keywords.

The formula filters products by naming convention, then aggregates order count, units sold, total revenue, and average price for each bundle product.

Reading this table: Sort by Revenue to identify your best-performing bundles. Compare the Units column to Orders - higher ratios mean customers buy multiple of the same bundle. Average Price helps evaluate your bundle pricing strategy.

Note that this table may be empty if no products use bundle-related naming. Consider standardizing bundle naming conventions to enable this tracking.

Key questions: What bundles drive the most revenue? Is bundle pricing effective? Which bundle types perform best? Click any row to see that product in detail.

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates event counts from GA4 Events Report, grouped by date. Each line shows the daily volume at each funnel stage: item views, cart additions, and purchases. The gap between lines represents drop-off at each stage.
how_to_read: Three stacked areas show View, Add to Cart, and Purchase volumes
The purple area (Add to Cart) relative to blue (Views) shows cart intent
The green area (Purchases) relative to purple shows fulfillment success
A widening gap between cart and purchase suggests inventory issues
Look for sudden drops in purchase volume while cart stays high - stockout signal
Hover over any date to see exact values for all three metrics
decisions_answered: Are customers adding items to cart but not completing purchases?
Which days show unusual gaps between cart activity and purchases?
Is the overall funnel health improving or degrading over time?
Are there specific days where potential stockouts are evident?
payload.narration_text:
This trend chart shows your daily shopping funnel - from item views through cart additions to purchases.

The formula tracks three event counts from GA4. Views in blue show customer interest. Add to cart in purple shows purchase intent. Purchases in green show fulfillment.

Reading this chart: The key insight is the gap between purple and green. When cart adds stay high but purchases drop, customers want to buy but cannot. This is your stockout signal.

Look for days where the green line suddenly drops while purple stays flat or rises. These are likely stockout events costing you revenue.

Key questions answered: Is inventory limiting sales? Which days show the biggest fulfillment gaps? Is the problem getting better or worse? Use this to prioritize restocking and monitor recovery.

payload.formula_text: ROUND(AVG(ITEMS_PURCHASED / ITEMS_ADDED_TO_CART) * 100, 2) over the last 30 days, averaged across rows WHERE ITEMS_ADDED_TO_CART BETWEEN 50 and 10000 AND ITEMS_PURCHASED > 0. Source: MARTS.MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY (backend/app/repositories/inventory.py:314-316).
payload.formula_explanation: The benchmark conversion rate from cart add to completed purchase across products with meaningful demand. It is computed as the average of each qualifying product's own purchased-over-added ratio, expressed as a percent, limited to items with 50 to 10000 cart adds and at least one purchase so outliers and noise do not skew the benchmark.
how_to_read: Shown as a percentage of cart adds that convert into purchases
It is a benchmark across qualifying products, not a single sitewide ratio
Only items with 50 to 10000 cart adds and at least one purchase are included
Higher is better; a falling rate signals widening availability or checkout friction
Use it as the reference line for spotting under-converting items
decisions_answered: What is our typical cart-to-purchase conversion benchmark?
Are products converting at or below the expected rate?
Is overall conversion health improving or declining?
What benchmark should under-performing items be measured against?
payload.narration_text:
This metric is your benchmark for how well cart adds turn into purchases.

We take every product with between fifty and ten thousand cart adds and at least one sale, compute its own conversion rate, and average them. The result is a clean, outlier-resistant benchmark expressed as a percentage.

Reading it: this is the bar your catalog converts at on average. When an individual item sits well below this line, it is a candidate for the items-at-risk and lost-revenue analyses.

Watch the trend. A declining benchmark usually means availability or checkout friction is spreading across the catalog.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares cart-to-view and purchase-to-view conversion rates across top products. Filters to products with sufficient view volume (500+) to ensure statistically meaningful comparisons.
how_to_read: Blue bars show Cart Rate - percentage of views that add to cart
Green bars show Purchase Rate - percentage of views that purchase
Higher bars indicate better converting products
Gap between blue and green shows cart abandonment
Products are ordered by purchase conversion rate
decisions_answered: Which products convert views to purchases most effectively?
Where is cart abandonment highest (large gap between cart and purchase)?
Are there products with high cart adds but low purchases (pricing issue)?
Which product pages should we use as templates for optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares conversion rates across products - identifying your most effective sellers.

The formula calculates two rates: cart-to-view shows how many views add to cart, purchase-to-view shows complete conversions. We filter to products with 500 plus views for statistical validity.

Reading this chart: Blue bars show cart interest. Green bars show purchases. A large gap between them indicates cart abandonment. Products with high green bars are your conversion champions - study their pages for best practices.

Low green bars with high blue might indicate pricing friction or checkout issues specific to those products.

Key questions answered: Which products convert best? Where is cart abandonment an issue? What can we learn from top converters? Use this to prioritize conversion optimization efforts.

payload.formula_explanation: Displays product category performance as a hierarchical treemap where rectangle size represents revenue contribution. Revenue is summed per L1/L2/L3 category and nested into the category hierarchy (e.g., Plants and Seeds > Vegetable Seeds > Tomato Seeds).
how_to_read: Rectangle size indicates revenue contribution - larger = more revenue
Colors differentiate category groups for visual distinction
Click on a category to drill down into sub-categories
Use the breadcrumb trail to navigate back up the hierarchy
Labels show category name and revenue for larger segments
decisions_answered: Which product categories drive the most revenue?
How is revenue distributed across the category hierarchy?
Are there underperforming categories with growth potential?
Which sub-categories within a parent are the strongest?

payload.formula_explanation: Monthly revenue per top-level category (CATEGORY_L1), arranged as a heatmap with categories as rows and calendar months as columns. Color intensity reflects revenue magnitude, revealing which categories peak in which months.
how_to_read: Each row is a top-level product category; each column is a calendar month
Brighter / more intense cells indicate higher revenue for that category-month
Scan a row left-to-right to see a category's seasonal shape across the year
Compare rows to find categories that peak in the same months
Empty or dim cells show months where a category generates little revenue
decisions_answered: Which categories peak in which months?
When should I plan inventory and promotions for each category?
Are there categories with complementary seasonal patterns?
Which category-month combinations drive the most revenue?

payload.formula_explanation: Creates a matrix showing how often products from different categories appear in the same order. Higher values indicate categories frequently purchased together, revealing cross-category shopping patterns.
how_to_read: Each cell shows co-purchase frequency between two categories
Darker colors indicate more frequent co-purchases
Diagonal cells show same-category purchases
Look for bright off-diagonal cells - these reveal cross-category affinities
Categories are limited to top 10 by order volume
Click any cell to explore products in that category combination
decisions_answered: Which product categories are purchased together?
What cross-category merchandising opportunities exist?
Should we create cross-category bundles?
How do customers navigate between product categories?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap visualizes category-level purchasing patterns - showing which product categories are bought together.

The formula counts orders containing products from both categories in each cell. The matrix is symmetric - the cell for Seeds plus Tools equals Tools plus Seeds.

Reading this heatmap: Color intensity shows co-purchase frequency. Darker cells indicate categories frequently bought together. The diagonal shows same-category purchases.

The key insight comes from off-diagonal cells. Bright cells reveal cross-category shopping patterns. These inform store layout, email campaigns, and cross-category recommendations.

Key questions: What categories pair together? Where are cross-category opportunities? Should we create cross-category bundles? Click any cell to explore specific products in that category combination.

payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot positioning categories by both their absolute revenue (X-axis) and growth rate (Y-axis). Bubble size represents volume. This reveals the strategic position of each category in your portfolio.
how_to_read: X-axis shows current year revenue (right = more revenue)
Y-axis shows YoY growth (up = faster growth)
Bubble size indicates items sold volume
Green bubbles are growing, red are declining
Upper-right quadrant = Stars (high revenue, high growth)
decisions_answered: Which categories are stars (high revenue, high growth)?
Which are cash cows (high revenue, low/negative growth)?
Which are rising opportunities (low revenue, high growth)?
Which categories need turnaround or discontinuation?

payload.formula_explanation: Monthly revenue per top-level category (CATEGORY_L1), arranged as a heatmap with categories as rows and calendar months as columns. Color intensity reflects revenue magnitude, revealing which categories peak in which months.
how_to_read: Each row is a top-level product category; each column is a calendar month
Brighter / more intense cells indicate higher revenue for that category-month
Scan a row left-to-right to see a category's seasonal shape across the year
Compare rows to find categories that peak in the same months
Empty or dim cells show months where a category generates little revenue
decisions_answered: Which categories peak in which months?
When should I plan inventory and promotions for each category?
Are there categories with complementary seasonal patterns?
Which category-month combinations drive the most revenue?

payload.formula_explanation: Uses association-rule-mining metrics from the pre-computed MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY mart. Support shows co-occurrence frequency, confidence shows conditional purchase probability, and lift shows affinity relative to random chance (values above 1 are positive associations). Pairs are filtered to lift > 1 and ranked by lift.
how_to_read: Sort by Lift to find strongest true affinities (not just popular products)
Lift > 3.0 indicates very strong affinity worth acting on
Lift > 1.5 shows moderate positive association
Lift = 1.0 means products are purchased independently
Use Support % to ensure statistical significance (higher is better)
decisions_answered: Which product pairs have the strongest true affinity?
Are high co-occurrence pairs actually related or just popular?
Which affinities are statistically significant enough to act on?
What is the probability of buying B given purchase of A?

payload.formula_explanation: Uses association-rule-mining metrics from the pre-computed MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY mart. Support shows co-occurrence frequency, confidence shows conditional purchase probability, and lift shows affinity relative to random chance (values above 1 are positive associations). payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates inventory health metrics by product category. Combines conversion rates, out-of-stock item counts, and estimated lost revenue to provide a comprehensive category-level view of availability status.
how_to_read: Status badges show category health at a glance - red needs immediate attention
OOS Items column shows count of stockout items within each category
Conversion % relative to benchmark indicates overall category health
Est. Lost Revenue quantifies the business impact
Click on a category row to see specific items within it
Sort by any metric to prioritize your category review
decisions_answered: Which product categories have the worst inventory health?
How many items are out of stock in each category?
Which categories are underperforming on conversion?
What is the total revenue impact by category?
payload.narration_text:
This table provides a category-level health check - your strategic inventory dashboard.

The formula aggregates multiple signals per category: conversion rate, out of stock count, and lost revenue. The Status badge summarizes the situation - Critical means under 10% conversion.

Reading this table: Start with red Critical badges. These categories have systemic availability issues. The OOS Items count shows how many products need attention. Lost Revenue shows the business impact.

Categories in Monitor or Warning are at risk of becoming Critical. Keep these on your radar for proactive management.

Key questions answered: Which categories need immediate inventory attention? How is availability affecting each product line? Where should purchasing prioritize? Use this for strategic inventory planning across your catalog.

payload.formula_explanation: Calculates lost revenue by comparing each category's cart-to-purchase conversion against the benchmark. The gap multiplied by cart volume gives lost units, then multiplied by average price gives lost revenue opportunity.
how_to_read: Each horizontal bar represents a product category
Bar length shows estimated lost revenue from below-benchmark conversion
Categories are sorted by lost revenue - biggest opportunities at top
Longer bars indicate categories with more recovery potential
Click on a category bar to drill down to specific items
The color gradient intensifies with higher lost revenue amounts
decisions_answered: Which categories have the most revenue recovery potential?
Where should inventory restocking efforts be prioritized?
Which categories are underperforming against the benchmark?
How much revenue could be recovered by fixing availability issues?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows estimated lost revenue by product category - your restock priority list.

The formula calculates a benchmark conversion from healthy items, then measures how much each category falls short. The gap times cart volume times average price equals lost revenue.

Reading this chart: Start from the top. These categories have the biggest recovery potential. A 50 thousand dollar bar means if you fixed availability, you could capture that revenue.

The calculation is conservative - actual lost revenue may be higher if stockouts are driving customers away entirely.

Key questions answered: Which categories need restocking first? How much is each category costing you? Where will inventory investment have the biggest return? Use this to prioritize purchasing decisions.

payload.formula_explanation: Products are bucketed into lifecycle stages by days since first appearance (launch date = MIN(DATE_DAY)), then aggregated over the last 90 days of activity to show count, revenue, views, purchases, conversion and average revenue per product per stage.
how_to_read: Each row is a lifecycle stage from Launch through Established
Product Count shows how many products sit in each stage
Revenue and Avg Revenue/Product reveal which stages monetize best
Conversion Rate (purchases / views) compares efficiency across stages
Aggregates reflect only the last 90 days of activity
decisions_answered: How do new products perform as they age through their lifecycle?
At which stage do products start meaningfully converting?
Are recently launched products underperforming established ones?
Where in the lifecycle should I focus optimization effort?

payload.formula_text: ROUND(confidence_a_to_b, 2) (% of buyers of A who also bought B)
payload.formula_explanation: Analyzes sequential purchase patterns to identify products frequently bought together by the same customer across multiple orders. Higher cross-sell rates indicate strong follow-on purchase potential.
how_to_read: Initial Product is the first purchase; Recommended is the follow-on
Customer Count shows how many customers bought both products
Cross-Sell Rate shows what percentage of initial buyers also bought the recommendation
High rates (>10%) indicate strong follow-on potential
Moderate rates (5-10%) still represent viable recommendations
Click any row to explore the customer segment
decisions_answered: What product should we recommend after a specific purchase?
Which products drive repeat purchases from the same customers?
What cross-sell recommendations have the highest success rate?
How should we sequence post-purchase email recommendations?
payload.narration_text:
This table reveals sequential purchase patterns - what customers buy after their initial purchase. This powers your cross-sell strategy.

The formula tracks customers who bought an initial product and later returned to buy a different product. The cross-sell rate shows what percentage of initial buyers became followup buyers.

Reading this table: The Initial Product column shows the first purchase. Recommended Cross-Sell shows what they bought next. Customer Count gives sample size. The Cross-Sell Rate is the key metric.

Rates above 10 percent indicate strong follow-on potential - these should definitely appear in post-purchase emails and recommendation widgets. Rates between 5 and 10 percent are still actionable.

Key questions: What should we recommend after each purchase? What drives repeat buying? How should we sequence email recommendations? Click any row to explore that customer segment.

payload.formula_explanation: Ranks new products by total revenue since launch, with supporting metrics including units sold, daily average revenue, and view-to-purchase conversion rate. Performance status compares against historical percentile benchmarks.
how_to_read: Products are ranked by total revenue since launch - top performers first
Launch Date and Days Live show how long each product has been available
Daily Avg Revenue normalizes for different launch dates
CVR (Conversion Rate) shows product page effectiveness
Status badges indicate performance tier vs historical benchmarks
decisions_answered: Which new products are generating the most revenue?
How do new products compare in conversion effectiveness?
Which recent launches show the strongest early traction?
Are there high-revenue products with low conversion (price premium)?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your new products by total revenue - your new product performance leaderboard.

The formula calculates multiple metrics per product: total revenue since launch, units sold, daily average revenue, and conversion rate. Status badges compare against historical performance benchmarks.

Reading this table: Top performers appear first. Compare daily average revenue across products with different launch dates for fair comparison. Conversion rate reveals product page effectiveness.

Watch for patterns. High revenue with low conversion suggests price premium products. High conversion with lower revenue indicates promotion opportunity. Use status badges to quickly identify winners and underperformers.

Key questions answered: Who are the new product winners? Which need more promotion? Which have conversion issues? Use this to prioritize marketing and optimization efforts for new products.

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates product revenue from GA4 ecommerce data at the selected granularity (daily, weekly, or monthly). Each data point represents total revenue from all products sold during that time period.
how_to_read: The area chart displays total product revenue over the selected date range
Toggle between Daily, Weekly, and Monthly views using the dropdown
Peaks indicate high-revenue periods - correlate with campaigns or seasonality
Look for consistent upward trends indicating healthy business growth
Hover over any point to see exact revenue for that period
decisions_answered: Is product revenue trending up or down over time?
Which days or weeks generated the most product revenue?
Are there seasonal patterns in product sales?
How does current performance compare to previous periods?
payload.narration_text:
This trend chart shows your product revenue over time - the fundamental measure of your e-commerce health.

The formula sums all item revenue from GA4 data, grouped by your selected time period. You can view daily, weekly, or monthly trends depending on your analysis needs.

Reading this chart: The shaded area represents total product revenue. Rising slopes indicate growth periods. Look for peaks that may correlate with marketing campaigns, seasonal events like spring planting season, or catalog launches.

For Rare Seeds, expect significant peaks in Q1 when gardeners prepare for spring planting. December often shows catalog pre-order activity.

Key questions answered: Is revenue growing? What periods perform best? Are there patterns to plan around? Use this to align inventory and marketing with demand cycles.

payload.formula_explanation: Monthly revenue (plus units and unique products) grouped by year and month, spanning the current year and the two prior years so the same calendar months can be compared across years.
how_to_read: Each series is a year; the x-axis is the month
Compare the same month across years to gauge year-over-year change
Diverging lines show months where performance shifted between years
Three years of history reveal whether a pattern is consistent
Units and unique-product counts add context behind revenue swings
decisions_answered: Is this month's revenue up or down versus the same month last year?
Which months show the strongest year-over-year growth or decline?
Are seasonal peaks consistent across years?
Is the catalog's overall trajectory improving year over year?

payload.formula_explanation: Average per-day revenue for each day of the week, computed as total revenue on that weekday divided by the number of distinct dates of that weekday in the period. Percent-of-week shows each day's share of the summed daily averages.
how_to_read: Each bar is the average revenue for one day of the week
Higher bars indicate consistently stronger sales days
Order Count (ITEMS_PURCHASED) provides volume context behind revenue
Percent of Week shows each day's share of the weekly average total
Compare weekday vs weekend bars to see the shopping rhythm
decisions_answered: Which days of the week drive the most revenue?
When should I schedule promotions or email sends for maximum impact?
Are weekends or weekdays stronger for this catalog?
Which days are underperforming and may need a boost?

how_to_read: Products are ranked by total revenue since launch - top performers first
Launch Date and Days Live show how long each product has been available
Daily Avg Revenue normalizes for different launch dates
CVR (Conversion Rate) shows product page effectiveness
Status badges indicate performance tier vs historical benchmarks
decisions_answered: Which new products are generating the most revenue?
How do new products compare in conversion effectiveness?
Which recent launches show the strongest early traction?
Are there high-revenue products with low conversion (price premium)?

This table ranks your new products by total revenue - your new product performance leaderboard.

The formula calculates multiple metrics per product: total revenue since launch, units sold, daily average revenue, and conversion rate. Status badges compare against historical performance benchmarks.

Reading this table: Top performers appear first. Compare daily average revenue across products with different launch dates for fair comparison. Conversion rate reveals product page effectiveness.

Watch for patterns. High revenue with low conversion suggests price premium products. High conversion with lower revenue indicates promotion opportunity. Use status badges to quickly identify winners and underperformers.

Key questions answered: Who are the new product winners? Which need more promotion? Which have conversion issues? Use this to prioritize marketing and optimization efforts for new products.

payload.formula_text: COUNT(DISTINCT DATE) where item had cart or view activity
payload.formula_explanation: Identifies items with significant cart activity but zero purchases over the past 30 days. These are high-confidence stockouts - customers clearly want these items but cannot complete purchases. The demand score weights cart adds higher than views since cart adds show stronger purchase intent.
how_to_read: Items are sorted by cart adds - highest demand items at top
Zero purchases with high cart adds is a strong stockout indicator
Days Active shows how long the availability issue has persisted
Demand Score combines views and cart adds to prioritize restocking
Use the search to find specific items by name
Click any row to see the item's detailed history
decisions_answered: Which items are definitely out of stock based on customer behavior?
How many customers have tried to buy items we cannot fulfill?
Which out-of-stock items have the highest customer demand?
How long have these availability issues been ongoing?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your confirmed stockouts - items customers want but cannot buy.

The formula is simple: significant cart activity plus zero purchases equals out of stock. The threshold of 50 cart adds ensures we are seeing real demand, not one-off adds.

Reading this table: Every row is lost revenue. Cart adds of 6 thousand means 6 thousand times customers tried to buy and could not. Multiply by average order value to estimate impact.

Days Active shows persistence. Thirty days of activity means you have been losing sales for a month. The Demand Score helps prioritize - higher scores mean more customer demand.

Key questions answered: What items are definitely out of stock? How much demand are we missing? What should we restock first? This is your urgent action list.

payload.formula_text: COUNT(DISTINCT ITEM_NAME) over a fixed last-60-day window (uses CURRENT_DATE, not the dashboard date filter) WHERE this_week purchases < four_weeks_ago purchases * 0.5 AND four_weeks_ago purchases > 20. this_week = SUM(ITEMS_PURCHASED) in the current ISO week; four_weeks_ago = SUM(ITEMS_PURCHASED) in the week four weeks prior. Source: MARTS.MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY (backend/app/repositories/inventory.py:333-354).
payload.formula_explanation: Counts products whose weekly sales have collapsed to less than half of what they were four weeks ago. Only items that had a meaningful base (more than 20 purchases four weeks ago) qualify, so the metric flags real momentum loss rather than normal small-number noise. It intentionally uses a fixed 60-day window anchored to today so the week-over-week comparison always has data, regardless of the dashboard date filter.
how_to_read: The number counts products whose current-week sales dropped below half their level four weeks ago
Only items with more than 20 purchases four weeks ago are eligible, filtering out noise
It always uses a fixed 60-day lookback, independent of the dashboard date range
A rising count means more of your assortment is losing momentum
Investigate flagged items for stockouts, pricing, seasonality, or competitive pressure
decisions_answered: Which products are rapidly losing sales momentum?
Is demand softening across a growing share of the catalog?
Where should we intervene before a slow item becomes a dead item?
Are declines driven by availability, pricing, or seasonality?
payload.narration_text:
This metric flags products that are losing steam fast.

An item is counted when its sales this week are less than half of what they were four weeks ago, and only if it had a real base of more than twenty purchases back then. That base requirement keeps small-number noise out.

Note the window: this metric always looks back a fixed sixty days from today, so the week-over-week comparison always has data even if your dashboard date filter is narrower.

Reading it: a rising count means momentum is draining from more of your catalog. Use it as an early-warning list to act before a fading item becomes a dead one.

payload.formula_text: (Benchmark Conv - Actual Conv) * Cart Adds * Avg Price
