payload.formula_explanation: Identifies new products with excellent conversion rates but low visibility. These hidden gems sell well when found but need more traffic through promotion, featured placement, or advertising to maximize revenue potential.
how_to_read: Products listed here convert visitors to buyers effectively
Low view counts indicate these products are not being discovered
High conversion proves the product and page work - they need visibility
Prioritize by conversion rate - highest converters have most potential
Consider featured placement, email features, or paid promotion
decisions_answered: Which new products deserve more promotional support?
What hidden gems are converting well but not getting traffic?
Where should we allocate promotional budget for new products?
Which products should be featured in email campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This table identifies your promotion opportunities - new products that convert well but need more visibility.

The formula filters for products with conversion rates of 15% or higher but fewer than 5,000 views. High conversion proves the product page works. Low views means customers simply are not finding them.

Reading this table: Each product listed is a hidden gem. When customers find these products, they buy. The opportunity is getting more eyes on them through featured placement, email campaigns, search optimization, or paid advertising.

Revenue potential is simple math. A 20% converting product with 500 views will double revenue if you double the views.

Key questions answered: Which new products need promotion? Where should we focus visibility efforts? What are the hidden gems? Use this to allocate promotional budget to maximize new product revenue.

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies new products by finding the first date each ITEM_ID appears in the ecommerce data. Products are grouped by their launch month to show the cadence of new product introductions over time.
how_to_read: Each bar represents the count of new products launched in that month
Taller bars indicate periods of high product introduction activity
Look for seasonal patterns - seed catalogs often launch new varieties in certain periods
Compare month-over-month to understand launch cadence trends
Hover over bars to see exact counts
decisions_answered: How many new products are we launching each month?
Are there seasonal patterns in our new product introductions?
Is our product introduction cadence accelerating or slowing?
Which months show the highest new product activity?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows your new product launch cadence by month - essential for understanding your catalog expansion rate.

The formula identifies new products by finding the first date each product ID appears in your ecommerce data. Products are then grouped by their launch month.

Reading this chart: Each bar shows the count of new products launched that month. Taller bars indicate periods of high introduction activity. For seed catalogs, expect peaks when new variety catalogs are released.

Look for patterns. Consistent introduction rates suggest a healthy product development pipeline. Sudden spikes may indicate catalog launches or acquisitions. Declining trends might signal inventory or sourcing challenges.

Key questions answered: How many new products per month? What's the seasonal launch pattern? Is introduction rate healthy? Use this to plan inventory and marketing for new product launches.

payload.formula_explanation: Displays monthly revenue as a calendar heatmap with years as rows and months as columns. Color intensity indicates revenue magnitude, enabling quick identification of seasonal patterns and year-over-year comparisons.
how_to_read: Rows represent years, columns represent months (Jan-Dec)
Darker colors indicate higher revenue months
Compare same months across years to see YoY trends
Look for consistent patterns - spring planting season typically shows Q1 peaks
Hover over cells for exact revenue values and YoY change
decisions_answered: What are the seasonal revenue patterns for our products?
Which months consistently perform best year over year?
Is this year tracking ahead or behind prior years?
When should we ramp up inventory and marketing?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares weekly purchase volumes across a 60-day window. Items that were previously selling well (>20 units 4 weeks ago) but have now stopped or dramatically declined are flagged as potential inventory issues.
how_to_read: Compare the three week columns left to right for trend direction
Zero in "This Week" with high "4 Weeks Ago" indicates stopped selling
Red status badges highlight the most severe declines
Total 60-day revenue shows the item's importance to your business
Sort by any column to find patterns - try sorting by revenue
Items here were successful - the decline is likely availability, not demand
decisions_answered: Which previously successful products have suddenly stopped selling?
Are we losing revenue from items that were performing well?
Which products may have run out of inventory recently?
What is the revenue impact of products that stopped selling?
payload.narration_text:
This table catches gradual stockouts - items that were selling well but have stopped.

The formula compares weekly sales over 60 days. Items selling 20 plus units four weeks ago but zero now are flagged Stopped Selling. Those below 25 percent of prior volume are Major Decline.

Reading this table: The 60-day revenue column shows what you are losing. A 500 dollar item that stopped selling is costing you roughly 250 per month.

The pattern matters. Zero this week after steady sales is inventory. A gradual decline might be seasonality or trend. But for proven sellers, sudden stops are almost always stock issues.

Key questions answered: What popular items have we run out of? How much historical revenue is at risk? What items need urgent inventory review? This is your early warning system.

payload.display_name: From Mart Product Affinity Where Co Purchase Count
payload.formula_explanation: Analyzes sequential purchase patterns to identify products frequently bought together by the same customer across multiple orders. Higher cross-sell rates indicate strong follow-on purchase potential.
how_to_read: Initial Product is the first purchase; Recommended is the follow-on
Customer Count shows how many customers bought both products
Cross-Sell Rate shows what percentage of initial buyers also bought the recommendation
High rates (>10%) indicate strong follow-on potential
Moderate rates (5-10%) still represent viable recommendations
Click any row to explore the customer segment
decisions_answered: What product should we recommend after a specific purchase?
Which products drive repeat purchases from the same customers?
What cross-sell recommendations have the highest success rate?
How should we sequence post-purchase email recommendations?
payload.narration_text:
This table reveals sequential purchase patterns - what customers buy after their initial purchase. This powers your cross-sell strategy.

The formula tracks customers who bought an initial product and later returned to buy a different product. The cross-sell rate shows what percentage of initial buyers became followup buyers.

Reading this table: The Initial Product column shows the first purchase. Recommended Cross-Sell shows what they bought next. Customer Count gives sample size. The Cross-Sell Rate is the key metric.

Rates above 10 percent indicate strong follow-on potential - these should definitely appear in post-purchase emails and recommendation widgets. Rates between 5 and 10 percent are still actionable.

Key questions: What should we recommend after each purchase? What drives repeat buying? How should we sequence email recommendations? Click any row to explore that customer segment.

payload.formula_text: ROUND((1 - SUM(EVENT_COUNT WHERE EVENT_NAME = 'purchase') / NULLIF(SUM(EVENT_COUNT WHERE EVENT_NAME = 'add_to_cart'), 0)) * 100, 2) over the last 30 days. Source: MARTS.MART_GA4_EVENTS_DAILY (backend/app/repositories/inventory.py:370-371).
payload.formula_explanation: The percentage of add-to-cart events that never reach a purchase event over the past 30 days. It is one minus the ratio of purchase events to add-to-cart events, expressed as a percent. Unlike the cart-to-purchase rate (which is a product-level benchmark from product daily data), this is a sitewide event-funnel measure drawn from the GA4 events mart.
how_to_read: Shown as a percentage of add-to-cart events that drop off before purchase
It is the inverse of the sitewide add-to-cart to purchase conversion
Computed from GA4 event counts, not product-level rows
Lower is better; a high drop rate signals checkout or availability friction
Spikes warrant investigating the checkout flow and stock levels
decisions_answered: What share of cart additions fail to convert into purchases?
How leaky is our overall add-to-cart to purchase funnel?
Is checkout friction getting better or worse?
Should we prioritize fixing the funnel over driving more traffic?
payload.narration_text:
This metric measures how leaky your add-to-cart to purchase funnel is.

The formula is one minus the ratio of purchase events to add-to-cart events, shown as a percentage. So if eighty percent of cart adds never become purchases, the drop rate is eighty percent.

Unlike the cart-to-purchase benchmark, which looks at individual products, this is a sitewide view built from GA4 event counts over the last thirty days.

Reading it: lower is better. A high or rising drop rate points to checkout friction, stockouts, or pricing shock at the cart stage. When this climbs, fixing the funnel usually beats buying more traffic.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares each item's cart-to-purchase conversion rate against the healthy benchmark. Items with significant cart activity but below-benchmark conversion indicate potential availability issues - customers add to cart but cannot complete purchase at normal rates.
how_to_read: Actual % vs Benchmark % shows how far below normal the item converts
Large gaps suggest inventory constraints limiting purchases
Estimated Lost Revenue quantifies the opportunity cost
Items are sorted by lost revenue - biggest opportunities first
Red highlighting on Est. Lost Revenue emphasizes the impact
Click any row to investigate the specific item further
decisions_answered: Which items have abnormally low cart-to-purchase conversion?
How much revenue are we losing from conversion underperformance?
Which items might have inventory issues causing low conversion?
Where should we investigate potential stockouts or limited inventory?
payload.narration_text:
This table identifies items converting below benchmark - potential low stock situations.

The formula compares each item's cart to purchase rate against the average of healthy items. When customers add to cart at normal rates but purchase at lower rates, something is blocking completion.

Reading this table: The gap between Actual and Benchmark tells the story. A 5% actual versus 27% benchmark means customers want to buy but 80% are blocked. That is often limited inventory.

Est. Lost Revenue shows the stakes. This is calculated conservatively - the real impact may be higher if frustrated customers leave entirely.

Key questions answered: Which items have suspicious conversion gaps? How much revenue is at risk? Where should we check inventory levels? Use this to find items with limited stock before they fully stock out.

payload.formula_text: SUM(CASE WHEN revenue >= 959 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), Above P75 = SUM(CASE WHEN revenue >= 2001 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END),
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the monthly success rate of new product launches, defined as the percentage of products that generate any revenue within their first 90 days. This metric evaluates launch effectiveness over time.
how_to_read: Each point shows the success rate for products launched in that month
Higher values indicate better launch performance
The target line (if shown) indicates the goal success rate
Look for trends - improving rates suggest better launch processes
Declining rates may indicate market or execution issues
decisions_answered: Is our launch success rate improving or declining?
Are we meeting our target success rate benchmarks?
Which months produced the most successful launches?
How does current success rate compare to historical average?
payload.narration_text:
This trend line shows your monthly launch success rate - tracking whether new products are gaining traction.

The formula calculates the percentage of products launched each month that generate revenue within 90 days. Only months with 90-plus days of data are included for complete measurement.

Reading this chart: Each point represents a month's launch cohort success rate. Upward trends indicate improving launch effectiveness. Declining trends signal potential issues with product selection, positioning, or market conditions.

Context matters. A 25% success rate is a reasonable benchmark for new product introductions. Higher is better, but even successful catalogs see many new products fail to gain traction.

Key questions answered: Is success rate improving? Are we meeting targets? Which periods performed best? Use this to evaluate your product development and launch processes over time.

payload.display_name: Group By Item Name Having Sum Items Viewed
payload.formula_text: 500, ORDER BY PURCHASE_TO_VIEW_PCT DESC LIMIT 20 (source: MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY)
payload.formula_explanation: Compares cart-to-view and purchase-to-view conversion rates across top products. Filters to products with sufficient view volume (500+) to ensure statistically meaningful comparisons.
how_to_read: Blue bars show Cart Rate - percentage of views that add to cart
Green bars show Purchase Rate - percentage of views that purchase
Higher bars indicate better converting products
Gap between blue and green shows cart abandonment
Products are ordered by purchase conversion rate
decisions_answered: Which products convert views to purchases most effectively?
Where is cart abandonment highest (large gap between cart and purchase)?
Are there products with high cart adds but low purchases (pricing issue)?
Which product pages should we use as templates for optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares conversion rates across products - identifying your most effective sellers.

The formula calculates two rates: cart-to-view shows how many views add to cart, purchase-to-view shows complete conversions. We filter to products with 500 plus views for statistical validity.

Reading this chart: Blue bars show cart interest. Green bars show purchases. A large gap between them indicates cart abandonment. Products with high green bars are your conversion champions - study their pages for best practices.

Low green bars with high blue might indicate pricing friction or checkout issues specific to those products.

Key questions answered: Which products convert best? Where is cart abandonment an issue? What can we learn from top converters? Use this to prioritize conversion optimization efforts.

payload.formula_explanation: Products are bucketed into lifecycle stages by days since first appearance (launch date = MIN(DATE_DAY)), then aggregated over the last 90 days of activity to show count, revenue, views, purchases, conversion and average revenue per product per stage.
how_to_read: Each row is a lifecycle stage from Launch through Established
Product Count shows how many products sit in each stage
Revenue and Avg Revenue/Product reveal which stages monetize best
Conversion Rate (purchases / views) compares efficiency across stages
Aggregates reflect only the last 90 days of activity
decisions_answered: How do new products perform as they age through their lifecycle?
At which stage do products start meaningfully converting?
Are recently launched products underperforming established ones?
Where in the lifecycle should I focus optimization effort?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates inventory health metrics by product category. Combines conversion rates, out-of-stock item counts, and estimated lost revenue to provide a comprehensive category-level view of availability status.
how_to_read: Status badges show category health at a glance - red needs immediate attention
OOS Items column shows count of stockout items within each category
Conversion % relative to benchmark indicates overall category health
Est. Lost Revenue quantifies the business impact
Click on a category row to see specific items within it
Sort by any metric to prioritize your category review
decisions_answered: Which product categories have the worst inventory health?
How many items are out of stock in each category?
Which categories are underperforming on conversion?
What is the total revenue impact by category?
payload.narration_text:
This table provides a category-level health check - your strategic inventory dashboard.

The formula aggregates multiple signals per category: conversion rate, out of stock count, and lost revenue. The Status badge summarizes the situation - Critical means under 10% conversion.

Reading this table: Start with red Critical badges. These categories have systemic availability issues. The OOS Items count shows how many products need attention. Lost Revenue shows the business impact.

Categories in Monitor or Warning are at risk of becoming Critical. Keep these on your radar for proactive management.

Key questions answered: Which categories need immediate inventory attention? How is availability affecting each product line? Where should purchasing prioritize? Use this for strategic inventory planning across your catalog.

payload.formula_text: AVG(weekly_revenue) over weeks sharing the same WEEK(week)-of-year within the selected range
payload.formula_explanation: Actual weekly revenue compared to a baseline that averages revenue across weeks with the same week-of-year number within the selected window. Variance shows how far each week deviates from its same-week-of-year baseline.
how_to_read: The actual line shows weekly revenue for the selected period
The baseline line is the average for that week-of-year across the range
Weeks above the baseline have positive variance; below is negative
Large variances flag unusual weeks worth investigating
Sustained deviations from baseline signal a shift in trend
decisions_answered: Are recent weeks outperforming or underperforming their historical norm?
Which weeks deviated most from the expected baseline?
Is revenue trending above or below seasonal expectations?
Where should I dig in to explain an anomalous week?

payload.formula_explanation: Groups orders by the number of unique products purchased, then counts orders and calculates average order value for each bucket. Shows the distribution of single-item versus multi-item orders.
how_to_read: Each bar represents orders with a specific number of items (1-item, 2-item, etc.)
Bar height shows the count of orders in each bucket
Single-item orders typically dominate, representing cross-sell opportunities
Multi-item orders (3+) indicate successful product discovery and bundling
The 6+ bucket aggregates high-quantity orders to prevent chart distortion
Click any bar to drill down into orders with that item count
decisions_answered: What percentage of orders are single-item vs multi-item?
Are customers discovering and purchasing complementary products?
Is our cross-sell strategy effectively increasing items per order?
Which item count bucket contributes most to total orders?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how orders are distributed by the number of items purchased - a key indicator of cross-sell success.

The formula counts unique products per order, then groups orders into buckets: 1-item, 2-item, 3-item, and so on. The 6-plus bucket aggregates larger orders.

Reading this chart: Each bar represents orders with that specific item count. Single-item orders typically form the tallest bar - this is normal but represents your biggest cross-sell opportunity.

Multi-item orders at 3-plus indicate customers who discovered complementary products. Higher bars in these buckets mean your product recommendations and bundling strategy are working.

Key questions answered: What percentage are single vs multi-item orders? Are customers finding complementary products? Is cross-selling effective? Click any bar to explore orders in that bucket.

payload.formula_text: COUNT of distinct ITEM_NAME over the last 30 days WHERE SUM(ITEMS_PURCHASED) = 0 AND SUM(ITEMS_ADDED_TO_CART) > 50 (cart adds capped at < 10000 to exclude data anomalies). Source: MARTS.MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY (backend/app/repositories/inventory.py:230-238).
payload.formula_explanation: Counts products that customers added to their cart but nobody actually purchased in the past 30 days. The threshold of more than 50 cart adds filters out incidental activity so only items with real, repeated purchase intent are flagged. Zero purchases despite genuine cart demand is a strong signal of a stockout, broken checkout, or pricing problem on that item.
how_to_read: The number is a count of distinct products meeting the at-risk criteria over the last 30 days
Higher counts mean more products are attracting demand but failing to convert to sales
Items must clear the >50 cart-add bar, so each one represents meaningful lost demand
Pair this with Est. Lost Revenue to size the financial impact
Investigate flagged items for stockouts, pricing, or checkout issues
decisions_answered: How many products are generating cart demand but zero sales?
Where should we focus availability and merchandising fixes first?
Is the at-risk pool growing or shrinking over time?
Are conversion failures concentrated in a few SKUs or widespread?
payload.narration_text:
This metric counts products that customers tried to buy but could not, over the last thirty days.

The formula is direct: an item is at risk when it has more than fifty cart adds yet zero purchases. The fifty-add threshold ensures we are seeing real, repeated demand rather than a one-off click.

Reading this number: every counted item is unmet demand sitting in your catalog. A rising count means more of your assortment is failing to convert, often because of stockouts, pricing, or a broken checkout path.

Use it as a triage list. Cross-reference at-risk items with estimated lost revenue to prioritize the fixes that recover the most sales.

payload.formula_explanation: Counts total units purchased across all categories. This volume metric helps understand demand patterns independent of price changes and is critical for inventory planning and fulfillment capacity.
how_to_read: The main value shows total units sold across all categories
Compare to revenue growth to identify pricing changes
Faster item growth than revenue may indicate discounting
The sparkline shows 14-day volume trends
Click to view item breakdown by category
decisions_answered: How many items are customers purchasing?
Is unit volume growing faster or slower than revenue?
Are we seeing healthy demand across the catalog?
Do we need to adjust inventory levels?
payload.narration_text:
This KPI tracks total items sold across all product categories - a key volume metric for understanding customer demand.

The formula sums all items purchased within the selected date range. This is a pure volume measure independent of pricing.

Reading this card: The main number shows total units sold. The percentage change compares to the prior period. Compare this growth rate to revenue growth - if items grow faster than revenue, you may be discounting.

Volume trends matter for inventory planning. Strong item growth requires adequate stock levels. Declining volume may indicate demand issues even if revenue holds steady through price increases.

Key questions: How many items are selling? Is volume trending up? Are items and revenue growing at similar rates? Click to explore volume by category.

payload.formula_explanation: Count of distinct products whose first-ever appearance in the data falls within the selected launch window. Each product is counted as launched on the first date it shows any activity.
how_to_read: The value is the number of products that launched in the selected period
Products are identified by their first appearance (MIN(DATE_DAY))
Placeholder IDs '(not set)' and '(other)' are excluded
A rising count indicates an accelerating launch cadence
Pair with revenue metrics to judge whether volume is translating to results
decisions_answered: How many new products did we launch this period?
Is our launch cadence accelerating or slowing?
Are we hitting our new-product introduction targets?
Is launch volume aligned with the revenue these products produce?

payload.formula_explanation: Uses association-rule-mining metrics from the pre-computed MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY mart. Support shows co-occurrence frequency, confidence shows conditional purchase probability, and lift shows affinity relative to random chance (values above 1 are positive associations). Pairs are filtered to lift > 1 and ranked by lift.
how_to_read: Sort by Lift to find strongest true affinities (not just popular products)
Lift > 3.0 indicates very strong affinity worth acting on
Lift > 1.5 shows moderate positive association
Lift = 1.0 means products are purchased independently
Use Support % to ensure statistical significance (higher is better)
decisions_answered: Which product pairs have the strongest true affinity?
Are high co-occurrence pairs actually related or just popular?
Which affinities are statistically significant enough to act on?
What is the probability of buying B given purchase of A?

payload.formula_text: Lost Units * (Category Revenue / Category Purchases OR Avg Item Price)
payload.formula_explanation: Calculates lost revenue by comparing each category's cart-to-purchase conversion against the benchmark. The gap multiplied by cart volume gives lost units, then multiplied by average price gives lost revenue opportunity.
how_to_read: Each horizontal bar represents a product category
Bar length shows estimated lost revenue from below-benchmark conversion
Categories are sorted by lost revenue - biggest opportunities at top
Longer bars indicate categories with more recovery potential
Click on a category bar to drill down to specific items
The color gradient intensifies with higher lost revenue amounts
decisions_answered: Which categories have the most revenue recovery potential?
Where should inventory restocking efforts be prioritized?
Which categories are underperforming against the benchmark?
How much revenue could be recovered by fixing availability issues?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows estimated lost revenue by product category - your restock priority list.

The formula calculates a benchmark conversion from healthy items, then measures how much each category falls short. The gap times cart volume times average price equals lost revenue.

Reading this chart: Start from the top. These categories have the biggest recovery potential. A 50 thousand dollar bar means if you fixed availability, you could capture that revenue.

The calculation is conservative - actual lost revenue may be higher if stockouts are driving customers away entirely.

Key questions answered: Which categories need restocking first? How much is each category costing you? Where will inventory investment have the biggest return? Use this to prioritize purchasing decisions.

Reading this chart: The shaded area represents total product revenue. Rising slopes indicate growth periods. Look for peaks that may correlate with marketing campaigns, seasonal events like spring planting season, or catalog launches.

For Rare Seeds, expect significant peaks in Q1 when gardeners prepare for spring planting. December often shows catalog pre-order activity.

Key questions answered: Is revenue growing? What periods perform best? Are there patterns to plan around? Use this to align inventory and marketing with demand cycles.

payload.formula_explanation: Categorizes products into performance tiers based on their first 90-day revenue compared to historical percentile benchmarks. This distribution shows how new products perform relative to established success thresholds.

Amber (Below Average) and red (Underperformer) need attention

payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your new products distribute across performance tiers - a health check for your launch success.

The formula compares each product's first 90-day revenue against historical benchmarks. Top performers exceed the 90th percentile at $4,061. Strong is 75th to 90th. Average is 50th to 75th, and so on.

Reading this chart: The donut segments show what percentage of products fall into each tier. Green and blue segments represent success. Gray is acceptable. Amber and red need attention. Light gray shows products with zero revenue.

A healthy distribution has meaningful green and blue segments. If most products cluster in underperformer or no revenue segments, your product development or launch process needs review.

Key questions answered: What's our success rate? How many top performers? How many failures? Use this to evaluate and improve your new product launch effectiveness.

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates inventory health metrics by product category. Combines conversion rates, out-of-stock item counts, and estimated lost revenue to provide a comprehensive category-level view of availability status.
how_to_read: Status badges show category health at a glance - red needs immediate attention
OOS Items column shows count of stockout items within each category
Conversion % relative to benchmark indicates overall category health
Est. Lost Revenue quantifies the business impact
Click on a category row to see specific items within it
Sort by any metric to prioritize your category review
decisions_answered: Which product categories have the worst inventory health?
How many items are out of stock in each category?
Which categories are underperforming on conversion?
What is the total revenue impact by category?
payload.narration_text:
This table provides a category-level health check - your strategic inventory dashboard.

The formula aggregates multiple signals per category: conversion rate, out of stock count, and lost revenue. The Status badge summarizes the situation - Critical means under 10% conversion.

Reading this table: Start with red Critical badges. These categories have systemic availability issues. The OOS Items count shows how many products need attention. Lost Revenue shows the business impact.

Categories in Monitor or Warning are at risk of becoming Critical. Keep these on your radar for proactive management.

Key questions answered: Which categories need immediate inventory attention? How is availability affecting each product line? Where should purchasing prioritize? Use this for strategic inventory planning across your catalog.

payload.formula_explanation: Single chart with two tabs. The Optimize tab lists high-traffic, low-conversion products (>=1000 views, <5% conversion) that have proven demand but fail to convert. The Promote tab lists low-traffic, high-conversion products (100-1000 views, >15% conversion) that sell well but need visibility. The backend returns the two sets pre-split as optimize[] and promote[].
how_to_read: Use the Optimize / Promote tabs to switch between the two opportunity types
Optimize products get many views but convert poorly - fix product page, price, or positioning
Promote products convert well but get few views - feature, email, or advertise them
Conversion Rate and Views columns prioritize where to act first
The Action column gives the recommended next step
decisions_answered: Which high-traffic products are underconverting and need page/pricing fixes?
Which proven converters deserve more promotion and visibility?
Where is the largest revenue opportunity - optimization or promotion?
What products should be featured in campaigns?

payload.formula_explanation: Groups orders by the number of unique products purchased, then counts orders and calculates average order value for each bucket. Shows the distribution of single-item versus multi-item orders.
how_to_read: Each bar represents orders with a specific number of items (1-item, 2-item, etc.)
Bar height shows the count of orders in each bucket
Single-item orders typically dominate, representing cross-sell opportunities
Multi-item orders (3+) indicate successful product discovery and bundling
The 6+ bucket aggregates high-quantity orders to prevent chart distortion
Click any bar to drill down into orders with that item count
decisions_answered: What percentage of orders are single-item vs multi-item?
Are customers discovering and purchasing complementary products?
Is our cross-sell strategy effectively increasing items per order?
Which item count bucket contributes most to total orders?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows how orders are distributed by the number of items purchased - a key indicator of cross-sell success.

The formula counts unique products per order, then groups orders into buckets: 1-item, 2-item, 3-item, and so on. The 6-plus bucket aggregates larger orders.

Reading this chart: Each bar represents orders with that specific item count. Single-item orders typically form the tallest bar - this is normal but represents your biggest cross-sell opportunity.

Multi-item orders at 3-plus indicate customers who discovered complementary products. Higher bars in these buckets mean your product recommendations and bundling strategy are working.

Key questions answered: What percentage are single vs multi-item orders? Are customers finding complementary products? Is cross-selling effective? Click any bar to explore orders in that bucket.

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies products explicitly marketed as bundles (containing keywords like bundle, kit, pack, set, or collection) and tracks their sales performance including orders, units, revenue, and average pricing.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product marketed as a bundle
Sort by Revenue to see top-performing bundles
Compare Units to Orders to see quantity per order patterns
Avg Price helps evaluate bundle pricing strategy
Empty table means no products match bundle naming conventions
Click any bundle to navigate to its product detail page
decisions_answered: Which bundles generate the most revenue?
Are our bundles priced competitively?
How many bundle units sell per order?
Which bundle types (kits, packs, collections) perform best?
payload.narration_text:
This table tracks performance of products explicitly marketed as bundles - products named with bundle, kit, pack, set, or collection keywords.

The formula filters products by naming convention, then aggregates order count, units sold, total revenue, and average price for each bundle product.

Reading this table: Sort by Revenue to identify your best-performing bundles. Compare the Units column to Orders - higher ratios mean customers buy multiple of the same bundle. Average Price helps evaluate your bundle pricing strategy.

Note that this table may be empty if no products use bundle-related naming. Consider standardizing bundle naming conventions to enable this tracking.

Key questions: What bundles drive the most revenue? Is bundle pricing effective? Which bundle types perform best? Click any row to see that product in detail.

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies items with significant cart activity but zero purchases over the past 30 days. These are high-confidence stockouts - customers clearly want these items but cannot complete purchases. The demand score weights cart adds higher than views since cart adds show stronger purchase intent.
how_to_read: Items are sorted by cart adds - highest demand items at top
Zero purchases with high cart adds is a strong stockout indicator
Days Active shows how long the availability issue has persisted
Demand Score combines views and cart adds to prioritize restocking
Use the search to find specific items by name
Click any row to see the item's detailed history
decisions_answered: Which items are definitely out of stock based on customer behavior?
How many customers have tried to buy items we cannot fulfill?
Which out-of-stock items have the highest customer demand?
How long have these availability issues been ongoing?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your confirmed stockouts - items customers want but cannot buy.

The formula is simple: significant cart activity plus zero purchases equals out of stock. The threshold of 50 cart adds ensures we are seeing real demand, not one-off adds.

Reading this table: Every row is lost revenue. Cart adds of 6 thousand means 6 thousand times customers tried to buy and could not. Multiply by average order value to estimate impact.

Days Active shows persistence. Thirty days of activity means you have been losing sales for a month. The Demand Score helps prioritize - higher scores mean more customer demand.

Key questions answered: What items are definitely out of stock? How much demand are we missing? What should we restock first? This is your urgent action list.

payload.formula_explanation: Identifies product pairs that appear together in the same order most frequently. Uses self-join on order items to find co-occurrences, then ranks by frequency.
how_to_read: Each bar shows a product pair that frequently appears in the same order
Longer bars indicate pairs purchased together more often
Product names are truncated - hover for full names
Top pairs are natural candidates for bundle creation
Consider these pairs for product page recommendations
Click any bar to see detailed pair analysis
decisions_answered: Which products are most commonly purchased together?
What product pairs should we promote as bundles?
Which products should appear in "Frequently Bought Together" sections?
Are there unexpected product affinities we should leverage?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows your most frequently co-purchased product pairs - the foundation of your bundling strategy.

The formula identifies every pair of products appearing in the same order, counts distinct orders containing each pair, and ranks by frequency.

Reading this chart: Longer bars indicate pairs purchased together more often. These are natural bundle candidates. Product names may be truncated - hover to see full names.

The top pairs here should directly inform your merchandising decisions. Add these to Frequently Bought Together recommendations on product pages. Consider creating pre-made bundles with discounts.

Key questions answered: What products are bought together most? What should we bundle? What goes in product recommendations? Click any bar for deeper pair analysis.

payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive sortable table showing all key metrics for each category. Includes inline sparklines for trend visualization and enables multi-metric comparison and export for deeper analysis.
how_to_read: Click column headers to sort by any metric
Sparklines show 14-day trend direction
Growth badges are color-coded (green positive, red negative)
Use search to filter specific categories
Export button downloads data as CSV
decisions_answered: How do all metrics compare across categories?
Which categories excel in specific metrics but lag in others?
What are the detailed numbers behind chart visualizations?
Which categories need attention based on multiple metrics?

payload.display_name: Per New Product Having Sum Items Viewed
payload.formula_explanation: Identifies new products with significant view volume but poor conversion rates. These products have proven demand (people are looking) but something in the buying process is broken - requiring product page, pricing, or content optimization.
how_to_read: Products listed here receive significant traffic but few purchases
High views prove customer interest exists
Low conversion indicates friction in the purchase process
Common issues: pricing, product images, descriptions, or availability
Prioritize by view count - fixing high-traffic products has most impact
decisions_answered: Which new products have traffic but poor conversion?
Where should we focus product page optimization efforts?
Are there pricing issues causing conversion problems?
What is the revenue opportunity from fixing these products?
payload.narration_text:
This table identifies your optimization opportunities - new products with traffic but poor conversion.

The formula filters for products with 1,000 or more views but less than 5% conversion. High views prove customer interest exists. Low conversion means something in the selling process is broken.

Reading this table: Each row represents lost revenue. These products attract customers but fail to close the sale. Issues might include poor product images, unclear descriptions, pricing concerns, or availability messaging.

Prioritize by view count. Fixing a 10,000 view product from 2% to 5% conversion has 10 times the impact of fixing a 1,000 view product.

Key questions answered: Where are we losing conversions? What needs page improvement? Are pricing issues causing friction? This is your optimization priority list for new products.

payload.formula_explanation: Categorizes products into performance tiers based on their first 90-day revenue compared to historical percentile benchmarks. This distribution shows how new products perform relative to established success thresholds.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of products in each performance tier
Green (Top Performer) and blue (Strong) are your success stories
Gray (Average) products meet baseline expectations
Amber (Below Average) and red (Underperformer) need attention
Light gray (No Revenue) indicates products not yet generating sales
Center number shows total products being evaluated
decisions_answered: What percentage of new products meet performance benchmarks?
How many products are top performers vs underperformers?
Is our new product success rate healthy?
How many products have not generated any revenue?

At which stage do products start meaningfully converting?
Are recently launched products underperforming established ones?

payload.formula_text: ROUND(AVG(per-product weekly views), 0), Avg Purchased = ROUND(AVG(per-product weekly purchased), 0), Product Count = COUNT(DISTINCT ITEM_ID)
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the average revenue trajectory of new products from launch through their first 12 weeks. This creates a benchmark curve showing typical performance patterns that new products follow as they mature.
how_to_read: The line shows average revenue per product at each week since launch
Week 0 represents launch week performance - often shows initial spike
Upward slope indicates products gaining momentum over time
Flat or declining curves may signal product or market issues
The benchmark line (if shown) indicates median historical performance
decisions_answered: What is the typical revenue trajectory for new products?
How long does it take new products to reach steady-state revenue?
Is there an initial launch spike followed by stabilization?
At what week should we evaluate new product success?
payload.narration_text:
This velocity curve shows the average revenue trajectory of new products over their first 12 weeks - your benchmark for launch performance.

The formula calculates weeks since launch for each product, then averages revenue across all products at each week milestone. This creates a typical performance curve.

Reading this chart: Week 0 is launch week. The line shows how average revenue evolves as products mature. An initial spike followed by dip then growth is common - launch excitement, then organic discovery.

The shape matters. Consistent upward trajectory indicates products gain momentum. Flat lines suggest products stabilize quickly. Declining curves after launch may indicate marketing or product issues.

Key questions answered: What's the typical launch trajectory? How long to steady state? When should we evaluate success? Use this to set realistic expectations for new product launches.

payload.formula_explanation: Average per-day revenue for each day of the week, computed as total revenue on that weekday divided by the number of distinct dates of that weekday in the period. Percent-of-week shows each day's share of the summed daily averages.
how_to_read: Each bar is the average revenue for one day of the week
Higher bars indicate consistently stronger sales days
Order Count (ITEMS_PURCHASED) provides volume context behind revenue
Percent of Week shows each day's share of the weekly average total
Compare weekday vs weekend bars to see the shopping rhythm
decisions_answered: Which days of the week drive the most revenue?
When should I schedule promotions or email sends for maximum impact?
Are weekends or weekdays stronger for this catalog?
Which days are underperforming and may need a boost?

payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot positioning categories by both their absolute revenue (X-axis) and growth rate (Y-axis). Bubble size represents volume. This reveals the strategic position of each category in your portfolio.
how_to_read: X-axis shows current year revenue (right = more revenue)
Y-axis shows YoY growth (up = faster growth)
Bubble size indicates items sold volume
Green bubbles are growing, red are declining
Upper-right quadrant = Stars (high revenue, high growth)
decisions_answered: Which categories are stars (high revenue, high growth)?
Which are cash cows (high revenue, low/negative growth)?
Which are rising opportunities (low revenue, high growth)?
Which categories need turnaround or discontinuation?

payload.formula_text: products WHERE SUM(ITEMS_VIEWED) BETWEEN 100 AND 1000 AND (SUM(ITEMS_PURCHASED)/NULLIF(SUM(ITEMS_VIEWED),0)) > 0.15
payload.formula_explanation: Single chart with two tabs. The Optimize tab lists high-traffic, low-conversion products (>=1000 views, <5% conversion) that have proven demand but fail to convert. The Promote tab lists low-traffic, high-conversion products (100-1000 views, >15% conversion) that sell well but need visibility. The backend returns the two sets pre-split as optimize[] and promote[].

Promote products convert well but get few views - feature, email, or advertise them
Conversion Rate and Views columns prioritize where to act first
The Action column gives the recommended next step

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates event counts from GA4 Events Report, grouped by date. Each line shows the daily volume at each funnel stage: item views, cart additions, and purchases. The gap between lines represents drop-off at each stage.
how_to_read: Three stacked areas show View, Add to Cart, and Purchase volumes
The purple area (Add to Cart) relative to blue (Views) shows cart intent
The green area (Purchases) relative to purple shows fulfillment success
A widening gap between cart and purchase suggests inventory issues
Look for sudden drops in purchase volume while cart stays high - stockout signal
Hover over any date to see exact values for all three metrics
decisions_answered: Are customers adding items to cart but not completing purchases?
Which days show unusual gaps between cart activity and purchases?
Is the overall funnel health improving or degrading over time?
Are there specific days where potential stockouts are evident?
payload.narration_text:
This trend chart shows your daily shopping funnel - from item views through cart additions to purchases.

The formula tracks three event counts from GA4. Views in blue show customer interest. Add to cart in purple shows purchase intent. Purchases in green show fulfillment.

Reading this chart: The key insight is the gap between purple and green. When cart adds stay high but purchases drop, customers want to buy but cannot. This is your stockout signal.

Look for days where the green line suddenly drops while purple stays flat or rises. These are likely stockout events costing you revenue.

Key questions answered: Is inventory limiting sales? Which days show the biggest fulfillment gaps? Is the problem getting better or worse? Use this to prioritize restocking and monitor recovery.

payload.formula_explanation: Compares cart-to-view and purchase-to-view conversion rates across top products. Filters to products with sufficient view volume (500+) to ensure statistically meaningful comparisons.
how_to_read: Blue bars show Cart Rate - percentage of views that add to cart
Green bars show Purchase Rate - percentage of views that purchase
Higher bars indicate better converting products
Gap between blue and green shows cart abandonment
Products are ordered by purchase conversion rate
decisions_answered: Which products convert views to purchases most effectively?
Where is cart abandonment highest (large gap between cart and purchase)?
Are there products with high cart adds but low purchases (pricing issue)?
Which product pages should we use as templates for optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares conversion rates across products - identifying your most effective sellers.

The formula calculates two rates: cart-to-view shows how many views add to cart, purchase-to-view shows complete conversions. We filter to products with 500 plus views for statistical validity.

Reading this chart: Blue bars show cart interest. Green bars show purchases. A large gap between them indicates cart abandonment. Products with high green bars are your conversion champions - study their pages for best practices.

Low green bars with high blue might indicate pricing friction or checkout issues specific to those products.

Key questions answered: Which products convert best? Where is cart abandonment an issue? What can we learn from top converters? Use this to prioritize conversion optimization efforts.

payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive product-level table showing revenue, quarterly distribution, seasonality classification, and year-over-year growth. Enables detailed analysis and identification of individual products with unusual or changing seasonal patterns.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product meeting the minimum revenue threshold
Q1-Q4 columns show percentage of annual revenue in each quarter with visual bars
Type column shows seasonality classification with color-coded badges
Index column shows seasonality intensity (higher = more concentrated demand)
YoY column shows year-over-year revenue growth with color coding
Click column headers to sort by any metric
Use search to filter to specific products
Export button downloads data for external analysis
decisions_answered: What are the detailed seasonal patterns for individual products?
Which products have the most concentrated demand periods?
Are specific products showing unusual seasonal behavior?
Which products are growing or declining year-over-year?
How do top products compare in their seasonality profiles?
payload.narration_text:
This data table provides product-level seasonality details - the comprehensive view for individual product analysis and inventory planning.

The table shows revenue, quarterly distribution percentages, seasonality type, index, and year-over-year growth for each product meeting minimum revenue thresholds.

Reading this table: Sort by revenue to see top performers. The Q1-Q4 columns show visual bars proportional to quarterly concentration. High seasonality index values indicate products with very concentrated demand.

Look for anomalies. Products switching seasonality type year-over-year may indicate market shifts. High-revenue products with negative YoY growth need attention.

Key questions: Which products are most seasonal? Who are the top performers? Are patterns changing? Use search to find specific products, sort to rank by any metric, export for deeper analysis.

payload.formula_explanation: Revenue split into the four calendar quarters for each top-level category (CATEGORY_L1), limited to the top 10 categories by total revenue. Reveals which quarter each category leans on most.
how_to_read: Each category shows its revenue broken out across Q1-Q4
Compare the four quarter segments within a category to find its peak quarter
Categories are ranked by total annual revenue (top 10 shown)
A category dominated by one quarter is highly seasonal
Even quarter splits indicate a year-round seller
decisions_answered: Which quarter drives the most revenue for each category?
Which categories are highly seasonal vs year-round?
How should quarterly inventory and budget be allocated by category?
Which categories contribute the most total revenue?

how_to_read: Bars show revenue for top 10 categories, ranked highest to lowest
Longer bars indicate higher revenue contribution
Growth badges at bar ends show YoY change (green positive, red negative)
Vegetable Seeds typically leads for this seed company
Click any bar to drill into that category's subcategories
decisions_answered: Which categories generate the most revenue?
How are top categories trending year-over-year?
Is there healthy diversity or concentration in top categories?
Which categories are gaining or losing market share?

payload.formula_text: Daily Revenue = SUM(ITEM_REVENUE) GROUP BY DATE Trend Analysis = Visual pattern over 7 days
payload.formula_explanation: Time series visualization of daily revenue for selected top categories. Reveals seasonal patterns, promotional impacts, and relative performance trends across the category portfolio.
how_to_read: Each line represents a different product category
Color coding matches category legend below the chart
Peaks indicate high-revenue days (promotions, seasonality)
Hover over any point for exact values
Click legend items to show/hide specific categories
decisions_answered: How does category revenue fluctuate over time?
Are there seasonal patterns in category performance?
Which categories show the most volatility?
How do promotional events impact different categories?

payload.display_name: Scope New Products Item Id With Min Date Day
payload.formula_text: launch_since_date), activity from each product's launch_date through date_to
payload.formula_explanation: Total revenue from newly launched products divided by the count of distinct new products, giving the average revenue each new product has generated since its launch.
how_to_read: The value is the mean revenue per newly launched product
A higher figure means new launches are monetizing more effectively on average
It is diluted by products that launched but generated little or no revenue
Read alongside Success Rate to separate breadth from depth of performance
Compare to prior period to see if launch quality is improving
decisions_answered: How much revenue does a typical new product generate?
Is the quality of new launches improving over time?
Are we launching too many low-revenue products?
How does average new-product revenue compare to the prior period?

payload.formula_explanation: Month-by-category heatmap showing seasonal revenue patterns. Color intensity is normalized within each category to reveal relative seasonality regardless of absolute revenue differences between categories.
how_to_read: X-axis shows months, Y-axis shows categories
Darker cells indicate higher revenue for that month
Color is normalized per row - compare horizontally within a category
Look for vertical patterns indicating market-wide seasonality
Click any cell to filter the page to that month and category
decisions_answered: When do different categories peak throughout the year?
Are there off-season opportunities to address?
How should marketing and inventory align to seasonality?
Which categories are most and least seasonal?

payload.formula_explanation: Classifies each product into a seasonality type based on quarterly revenue distribution. Products are filtered by minimum revenue threshold to ensure meaningful patterns. This enables portfolio-level understanding of demand timing.
how_to_read: The donut chart shows distribution of products by seasonality type
Center number displays total products analyzed
Each segment is color-coded by type (green=Spring, orange=Fall, red=Holiday, blue=Year-Round, purple=Mixed)
Segment size indicates count of products in each category
Hover over segments for exact product counts and revenue totals
Click a segment to filter the table below to that seasonality type
decisions_answered: What portion of the catalog is spring-peak versus year-round?
How many products are holiday-dependent?
Is the product mix well-diversified across seasons?
Which seasonality type drives the most revenue?
Are there concentration risks in seasonal timing?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart reveals your product seasonality distribution - how your catalog breaks down by demand timing patterns.

The formula classifies each product based on quarterly revenue concentration. Spring Peak products have over 60 percent of revenue in Q1. Year-Round products have no quarter exceeding 40 percent.

Reading this chart: The center number shows total products analyzed. Segment sizes indicate how many products fall into each type. For a seed company, expect a large Spring Peak segment.

Diversification matters. Heavy concentration in one seasonality type creates cash flow challenges and inventory risks. A healthy portfolio balances seasonal peaks with year-round sellers.

Key questions: How seasonal is the catalog? Is there diversity across seasons? Which type dominates? Click segments to explore products in each category.

payload.formula_explanation: Comprehensive product-level table showing revenue, quarterly distribution, seasonality classification, and year-over-year growth. Enables detailed analysis and identification of individual products with unusual or changing seasonal patterns.
how_to_read: Each row represents a product meeting the minimum revenue threshold
Q1-Q4 columns show percentage of annual revenue in each quarter with visual bars
Type column shows seasonality classification with color-coded badges
Index column shows seasonality intensity (higher = more concentrated demand)
YoY column shows year-over-year revenue growth with color coding
Click column headers to sort by any metric
Use search to filter to specific products
Export button downloads data for external analysis
decisions_answered: What are the detailed seasonal patterns for individual products?
Which products have the most concentrated demand periods?
Are specific products showing unusual seasonal behavior?
Which products are growing or declining year-over-year?
How do top products compare in their seasonality profiles?
payload.narration_text:
This data table provides product-level seasonality details - the comprehensive view for individual product analysis and inventory planning.

The table shows revenue, quarterly distribution percentages, seasonality type, index, and year-over-year growth for each product meeting minimum revenue thresholds.

Reading this table: Sort by revenue to see top performers. The Q1-Q4 columns show visual bars proportional to quarterly concentration. High seasonality index values indicate products with very concentrated demand.

Look for anomalies. Products switching seasonality type year-over-year may indicate market shifts. High-revenue products with negative YoY growth need attention.

Key questions: Which products are most seasonal? Who are the top performers? Are patterns changing? Use search to find specific products, sort to rank by any metric, export for deeper analysis.

payload.display_name: Stopped Selling Four Weeks Ago 20 And This Week
payload.formula_explanation: Compares weekly purchase volumes across a 60-day window. Items that were previously selling well (>20 units 4 weeks ago) but have now stopped or dramatically declined are flagged as potential inventory issues.
how_to_read: Compare the three week columns left to right for trend direction
Zero in "This Week" with high "4 Weeks Ago" indicates stopped selling
Red status badges highlight the most severe declines
Total 60-day revenue shows the item's importance to your business
Sort by any column to find patterns - try sorting by revenue
Items here were successful - the decline is likely availability, not demand
decisions_answered: Which previously successful products have suddenly stopped selling?
Are we losing revenue from items that were performing well?
Which products may have run out of inventory recently?
What is the revenue impact of products that stopped selling?
payload.narration_text:
This table catches gradual stockouts - items that were selling well but have stopped.

The formula compares weekly sales over 60 days. Items selling 20 plus units four weeks ago but zero now are flagged Stopped Selling. Those below 25 percent of prior volume are Major Decline.

Reading this table: The 60-day revenue column shows what you are losing. A 500 dollar item that stopped selling is costing you roughly 250 per month.

The pattern matters. Zero this week after steady sales is inventory. A gradual decline might be seasonality or trend. But for proven sellers, sudden stops are almost always stock issues.

Key questions answered: What popular items have we run out of? How much historical revenue is at risk? What items need urgent inventory review? This is your early warning system.

payload.formula_explanation: Categorizes products into performance tiers based on their first 90-day revenue compared to historical percentile benchmarks. This distribution shows how new products perform relative to established success thresholds.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportion of products in each performance tier
Green (Top Performer) and blue (Strong) are your success stories
Gray (Average) products meet baseline expectations
Amber (Below Average) and red (Underperformer) need attention
Light gray (No Revenue) indicates products not yet generating sales
Center number shows total products being evaluated
decisions_answered: What percentage of new products meet performance benchmarks?
How many products are top performers vs underperformers?
Is our new product success rate healthy?
How many products have not generated any revenue?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your new products distribute across performance tiers - a health check for your launch success.

The formula compares each product's first 90-day revenue against historical benchmarks. Top performers exceed the 90th percentile at $4,061. Strong is 75th to 90th. Average is 50th to 75th, and so on.

Reading this chart: The donut segments show what percentage of products fall into each tier. Green and blue segments represent success. Gray is acceptable. Amber and red need attention. Light gray shows products with zero revenue.

A healthy distribution has meaningful green and blue segments. If most products cluster in underperformer or no revenue segments, your product development or launch process needs review.

Key questions answered: What's our success rate? How many top performers? How many failures? Use this to evaluate and improve your new product launch effectiveness.

payload.formula_text: ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN revenue > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) * 100, 1)
payload.formula_explanation: Percentage of newly launched products that generated any revenue (revenue > 0) since launch, out of all new products in the window. Measures the share of launches that achieved at least minimal commercial traction.
how_to_read: The value is the percent of new products that earned any revenue
Only revenue > 0 counts as a 'success' here (a low bar by design)
A low rate means many launches never sold at all
Read with Avg Revenue Per Product to gauge both breadth and depth
Compare to prior period to track launch-quality trend
decisions_answered: What share of our new products actually sold anything?
Are too many launches failing to gain traction?
Is launch success improving versus the prior period?
Should we tighten the criteria for what we launch?

payload.formula_explanation: Uses association-rule-mining metrics from the pre-computed MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY mart. Support shows co-occurrence frequency, confidence shows conditional purchase probability, and lift shows affinity relative to random chance (values above 1 are positive associations). Pairs are filtered to lift > 1 and ranked by lift.
how_to_read: Sort by Lift to find strongest true affinities (not just popular products)
Lift > 3.0 indicates very strong affinity worth acting on
Lift > 1.5 shows moderate positive association
Lift = 1.0 means products are purchased independently
Use Support % to ensure statistical significance (higher is better)
decisions_answered: Which product pairs have the strongest true affinity?
Are high co-occurrence pairs actually related or just popular?
Which affinities are statistically significant enough to act on?
What is the probability of buying B given purchase of A?

Red status badges highlight the most severe declines

payload.formula_explanation: Highest affinity lift score among product pairs from the pre-computed MART_PRODUCT_AFFINITY mart, restricted to pairs with at least 5 co-purchases. Lift above 1 indicates a positive association; the maximum surfaces the single strongest product relationship.
how_to_read: The main value shows the strongest lift score among all product pairs
Lift > 3.0 indicates a very strong, actionable affinity
Lift near 1.0 means products are bought independently
Only pairs with 5+ co-purchases are considered for stability
Click to view the full affinity table behind this score
decisions_answered: What is the strongest product affinity in the catalog?
Is there a pair worth bundling or cross-promoting?
How strong is our best co-purchase relationship?
Which pairing should anchor recommendation widgets?

payload.formula_explanation: Hierarchical visualization showing revenue distribution with nested rectangles. Rectangle size represents revenue proportion while color indicates year-over-year growth. Enables quick identification of both scale and momentum.
how_to_read: Rectangle size shows revenue proportion - larger areas mean more revenue
Nested rectangles show parent-child category relationships
Color indicates YoY growth: green for growth, red for decline
Labels show category name and abbreviated revenue
Click any rectangle to drill into its subcategories
decisions_answered: How is revenue distributed across the category hierarchy?
Which categories are large but declining (red, large boxes)?

payload.formula_text: COUNT of distinct ITEM_NAME over the last 30 days WHERE SUM(ITEMS_PURCHASED) = 0 AND SUM(ITEMS_ADDED_TO_CART) > 100 (cart adds capped at < 10000). Source: MARTS.MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY (backend/app/repositories/inventory.py:290-298).
payload.formula_explanation: A high-confidence count of products that are effectively out of stock based on customer behavior. It uses a stricter bar than Items At Risk (more than 100 cart adds versus more than 50) so that only items with very strong, sustained demand and zero sales are counted. These are your most certain stockouts where many customers tried to buy and none succeeded.
how_to_read: The number counts distinct products with heavy cart demand and zero purchases over 30 days
The >100 cart-add threshold makes this stricter than the Items At Risk metric
Each counted item is a near-certain stockout or fulfillment failure
Lower is better; a rising count signals worsening availability
Use the Out of Stock Alerts table to see the specific items
decisions_answered: Which products are almost certainly out of stock right now?
How severe is our availability problem?
How many strong-demand items are we completely failing to fulfill?
Is our stockout situation improving or deteriorating?
payload.narration_text:
This metric is your high-confidence stockout counter.

An item is counted when it has more than one hundred cart adds in the last thirty days and zero purchases. That hundred-add bar is deliberately strict, so these are not maybes, they are near-certain stockouts.

Reading the number: every count is a product many customers actively tried to buy and could not. A rising count means availability is slipping and revenue is walking out the door.

Treat this as your urgent restock list. Open the Out of Stock Alerts table to see exactly which items to act on.

payload.formula_explanation: Monthly revenue (plus units and unique products) grouped by year and month, spanning the current year and the two prior years so the same calendar months can be compared across years.
how_to_read: Each series is a year; the x-axis is the month
Compare the same month across years to gauge year-over-year change
Diverging lines show months where performance shifted between years
Three years of history reveal whether a pattern is consistent
Units and unique-product counts add context behind revenue swings
decisions_answered: Is this month's revenue up or down versus the same month last year?
Which months show the strongest year-over-year growth or decline?
Are seasonal peaks consistent across years?
Is the catalog's overall trajectory improving year over year?

payload.formula_explanation: Total number of product units purchased across all products in the period, from MART_GA4_PRODUCT_DAILY. A pure volume measure independent of price, useful for demand and inventory planning.
how_to_read: The main value shows total units sold across all products
Compare units growth to revenue growth to detect pricing/mix shifts
Units growing faster than revenue can indicate discounting
The sparkline shows daily unit volume across the period
Click to view units by product or category
decisions_answered: How many product units did customers buy?
Is unit volume growing faster or slower than revenue?
Is demand healthy across the catalog?
Do we need to adjust inventory levels?

payload.formula_explanation: Actual weekly revenue compared to a baseline that averages revenue across weeks with the same week-of-year number within the selected window. Variance shows how far each week deviates from its same-week-of-year baseline.
how_to_read: The actual line shows weekly revenue for the selected period
The baseline line is the average for that week-of-year across the range
Weeks above the baseline have positive variance; below is negative
Large variances flag unusual weeks worth investigating
Sustained deviations from baseline signal a shift in trend
decisions_answered: Are recent weeks outperforming or underperforming their historical norm?
Which weeks deviated most from the expected baseline?
Is revenue trending above or below seasonal expectations?
Where should I dig in to explain an anomalous week?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates event counts from GA4 Events Report, grouped by date. Each line shows the daily volume at each funnel stage: item views, cart additions, and purchases. The gap between lines represents drop-off at each stage.
how_to_read: Three stacked areas show View, Add to Cart, and Purchase volumes
The purple area (Add to Cart) relative to blue (Views) shows cart intent
The green area (Purchases) relative to purple shows fulfillment success
A widening gap between cart and purchase suggests inventory issues
Look for sudden drops in purchase volume while cart stays high - stockout signal
Hover over any date to see exact values for all three metrics
decisions_answered: Are customers adding items to cart but not completing purchases?
Which days show unusual gaps between cart activity and purchases?
Is the overall funnel health improving or degrading over time?
Are there specific days where potential stockouts are evident?
payload.narration_text:
This trend chart shows your daily shopping funnel - from item views through cart additions to purchases.

The formula tracks three event counts from GA4. Views in blue show customer interest. Add to cart in purple shows purchase intent. Purchases in green show fulfillment.

Reading this chart: The key insight is the gap between purple and green. When cart adds stay high but purchases drop, customers want to buy but cannot. This is your stockout signal.

Look for days where the green line suddenly drops while purple stays flat or rises. These are likely stockout events costing you revenue.

Key questions answered: Is inventory limiting sales? Which days show the biggest fulfillment gaps? Is the problem getting better or worse? Use this to prioritize restocking and monitor recovery.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows the conversion funnel stages for a selected category. Tracks customer journey from product view through cart addition to purchase, identifying where drop-offs occur and optimization opportunities exist.
how_to_read: Three horizontal stages show Views, Cart Adds, and Purchases
Stage width narrows proportionally to drop-off rate
Conversion rates between stages shown as percentages
Use dropdown to switch between categories
Wide drop-offs indicate optimization opportunities
decisions_answered: Where do customers drop off in the purchase journey?
Is the view-to-cart rate healthy for this category?
Is cart abandonment a problem for certain categories?
Which categories have the best end-to-end conversion?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the product conversion journey from initial view through cart addition to final purchase. Each stage shows the volume of products that progressed, with conversion rates calculated between stages.
how_to_read: The funnel visualizes the customer journey: Views → Cart Adds → Purchases
Width of each stage represents relative volume - wider means more products
Drop-off between stages shows where customers abandon the path
Conversion rates below the funnel show percentage progression between stages
A healthy funnel maintains reasonable ratios at each stage
decisions_answered: At which stage are we losing the most potential customers?
What is our overall product conversion rate from view to purchase?
Is the cart-to-purchase rate healthy or is cart abandonment high?
Should we focus on improving product pages or checkout experience?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates product revenue from GA4 ecommerce data at the selected granularity (daily, weekly, or monthly). Each data point represents total revenue from all products sold during that time period.
how_to_read: The area chart displays total product revenue over the selected date range
Toggle between Daily, Weekly, and Monthly views using the dropdown
Peaks indicate high-revenue periods - correlate with campaigns or seasonality
Look for consistent upward trends indicating healthy business growth
Hover over any point to see exact revenue for that period
decisions_answered: Is product revenue trending up or down over time?
Which days or weeks generated the most product revenue?
Are there seasonal patterns in product sales?
How does current performance compare to previous periods?
payload.narration_text:
This trend chart shows your product revenue over time - the fundamental measure of your e-commerce health.

The formula sums all item revenue from GA4 data, grouped by your selected time period. You can view daily, weekly, or monthly trends depending on your analysis needs.

Reading this chart: The shaded area represents total product revenue. Rising slopes indicate growth periods. Look for peaks that may correlate with marketing campaigns, seasonal events like spring planting season, or catalog launches.

For Rare Seeds, expect significant peaks in Q1 when gardeners prepare for spring planting. December often shows catalog pre-order activity.

Key questions answered: Is revenue growing? What periods perform best? Are there patterns to plan around? Use this to align inventory and marketing with demand cycles.

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the average revenue trajectory of new products from launch through their first 12 weeks. This creates a benchmark curve showing typical performance patterns that new products follow as they mature.
how_to_read: The line shows average revenue per product at each week since launch
Week 0 represents launch week performance - often shows initial spike
Upward slope indicates products gaining momentum over time
Flat or declining curves may signal product or market issues
The benchmark line (if shown) indicates median historical performance
decisions_answered: What is the typical revenue trajectory for new products?
How long does it take new products to reach steady-state revenue?
Is there an initial launch spike followed by stabilization?
At what week should we evaluate new product success?
payload.narration_text:
This velocity curve shows the average revenue trajectory of new products over their first 12 weeks - your benchmark for launch performance.

The formula calculates weeks since launch for each product, then averages revenue across all products at each week milestone. This creates a typical performance curve.

Reading this chart: Week 0 is launch week. The line shows how average revenue evolves as products mature. An initial spike followed by dip then growth is common - launch excitement, then organic discovery.

The shape matters. Consistent upward trajectory indicates products gain momentum. Flat lines suggest products stabilize quickly. Declining curves after launch may indicate marketing or product issues.

Key questions answered: What's the typical launch trajectory? How long to steady state? When should we evaluate success? Use this to set realistic expectations for new product launches.

payload.formula_text: SUM(CASE WHEN date in current period THEN item_revenue END)
payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot positioning categories by both their absolute revenue (X-axis) and growth rate (Y-axis). Bubble size represents volume. This reveals the strategic position of each category in your portfolio.
how_to_read: X-axis shows current year revenue (right = more revenue)
Y-axis shows YoY growth (up = faster growth)
Bubble size indicates items sold volume
Green bubbles are growing, red are declining
Upper-right quadrant = Stars (high revenue, high growth)
decisions_answered: Which categories are stars (high revenue, high growth)?
Which are cash cows (high revenue, low/negative growth)?
Which are rising opportunities (low revenue, high growth)?
Which categories need turnaround or discontinuation?

payload.formula_explanation: Plots products on a 2x2 matrix with revenue on the x-axis and growth rate on the y-axis. Products are classified into quadrants: Stars (high revenue, high growth), Rising Stars (low revenue, high growth), Cash Cows (high revenue, low growth), and Dogs (low revenue, low growth).
how_to_read: Each bubble represents a product - size indicates units sold
Top-right (green): Stars - high revenue AND growing fast - your winners
Top-left (blue): Rising Stars - low revenue but growing - watch and nurture
Bottom-right (yellow): Cash Cows - high revenue but slowing - milk but watch
Bottom-left (red): Dogs - low revenue and declining - consider action
Hover over bubbles to see product details
decisions_answered: Which products are our current stars driving growth?
Which emerging products should we invest in promoting?
Are any high-revenue products showing declining trends?
Which products might need to be discontinued or repositioned?

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows performance metrics for all SMS campaigns sent in the selected period. Each row represents one campaign with its sending stats, engagement metrics, and attributed revenue.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank campaigns - try sorting by Revenue or Click Rate to find top performers
Click Rate above 5% indicates strong engagement; below 2% needs content or targeting review
