payload.narration_text:
This table breaks down performance for every SMS campaign you have sent.

The key metrics are click rate, conversions, and revenue. Click rate tells you if your message resonated - aim for 5% or higher. Conversions and revenue show actual business impact.

Sort the table to find insights. Sort by revenue to see your money-makers. Sort by click rate to find your most engaging content. Sort by unsubscribes to catch campaigns that hurt list health.

Watch the unsubscribe column carefully. A campaign that generates revenue but causes mass opt-outs is not sustainable.

Click any campaign row to see detailed analytics - open times, device breakdown, and more.

Use this to answer: What campaigns work? What should we repeat? And what is hurting our list?

payload.formula_explanation: Compares SMS engagement metrics across different channels or message sources (campaigns vs flows). This helps identify which channel drives stronger engagement and where to focus optimization efforts.
how_to_read: Each bar group compares performance across channels side by side
Higher bars indicate better engagement for that channel
Flows typically outperform campaigns due to triggered, timely delivery
Large gaps between channels suggest optimization opportunities
Use this to decide resource allocation between campaigns and flows
decisions_answered: Which SMS channel drives the best engagement?

Which channel type needs the most improvement?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares SMS engagement across different channels - typically campaigns versus automated flows.

Each bar group shows metrics side by side so you can directly compare performance. Flows usually outperform campaigns because they trigger at the right moment in the customer journey.

Look for large gaps between channels. If flows dramatically outperform campaigns, consider shifting more messaging to automated sequences.

This answers: Which channel works best? Where should you focus? And how big is the performance gap between your SMS channels?

payload.formula_explanation: This bar chart compares SMS performance between manually-sent campaigns and automated flows. Campaigns are one-time sends to segments, while flows are triggered automatically by customer behavior or events.
how_to_read: Blue bars show total SMS messages sent by source type (Campaign vs Flow)
Green bars show total clicks received by source type
Compare bar heights to see which source drives more volume and engagement
Flows typically have higher engagement rates due to behavioral triggers
A balanced mix of campaigns and flows indicates a mature SMS program
decisions_answered: Are we relying too heavily on campaigns versus automated flows?
Which source type drives more SMS volume?
Should we invest more in building SMS flows?
Are our campaign and flow SMS performing comparably?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart compares your SMS performance between campaigns and automated flows.

Blue bars show total SMS sent from each source. Green bars show the clicks generated. Compare the heights to understand where your volume and engagement come from.

Campaigns are one-time sends you manually trigger - promotions, announcements, flash sales. Flows are automated sequences triggered by customer behavior - abandoned carts, welcome series, post-purchase.

A mature SMS program has a healthy balance of both. Flows tend to have higher engagement because they are timely and relevant. If your campaigns vastly outweigh flows, consider building more automation.

Use this to answer: Where does our SMS volume come from? Are we leveraging automation? Which source performs better?

payload.formula_explanation: This donut chart shows the current composition of your SMS subscriber list. Active subscribers are those who have opted in and can receive marketing SMS. The breakdown helps identify list health issues and growth opportunities.
how_to_read: Green segment shows active subscribed contacts - these are your reachable SMS audience
Red segment shows unsubscribed contacts - a growing red segment indicates list health issues
Gray segment shows contacts with phone numbers but no SMS consent
Center value displays total active subscribers - this is your addressable SMS audience
The consent rate percentage shows what portion of your database has SMS consent

payload.narration_text:
This donut chart gives you a snapshot of your SMS list composition and health.

The green segment shows your active subscribers - people who have opted in and can receive your SMS marketing. This is your addressable audience.

The red segment shows unsubscribed contacts. Some churn is normal, but if this segment grows faster than your subscriber base, you have a retention problem.

The gray segment represents contacts with phone numbers but no SMS consent. This is your growth opportunity - these people could be converted through opt-in campaigns.

The center number is your total active subscribers. The consent rate below tells you what percentage of your phone-number database has consented to SMS.

Use this to answer: How large is our SMS audience? Are we retaining subscribers? And where is our growth potential?

payload.formula_text: Running Sum of Net Change over 90 days
payload.formula_explanation: This area chart tracks the cumulative net growth of your SMS subscriber list over 90 days. It shows the running total of new subscriptions minus unsubscriptions, helping you understand if your list is growing or shrinking.
how_to_read: Rising line indicates net positive list growth - more subscribers joining than leaving
Flat line means new subscriptions equal unsubscriptions - no net growth
Declining line indicates list shrinkage - more people leaving than joining
Steep positive slopes often follow successful acquisition campaigns
Sudden drops may indicate high-unsubscribe campaigns or delivery issues
decisions_answered: Is our SMS list growing or shrinking over time?
What is our net subscriber growth rate?
When did major list growth or decline events occur?
Are acquisition efforts outpacing list churn?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart tracks your SMS list growth over the past 90 days as a cumulative net number.

Each point shows the running total of new subscribers minus unsubscribers. A rising line means your list is growing - more people opt in than opt out. A flat line means you are treading water. A declining line means your list is shrinking.

Watch for patterns. Steep rises often follow acquisition campaigns - sign-up promotions, contests, or checkout opt-ins. Sudden drops may indicate a problematic campaign that drove mass unsubscribes.

Healthy SMS programs show steady upward momentum. If you see prolonged flatness or decline, investigate your acquisition channels and your messaging quality.

This answers: Is our list healthy? Are we growing? And when did significant changes occur that need investigation?

payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the growth of your SMS subscriber list over time. Shows new subscriptions, unsubscribes, and net growth to help you understand whether your list is expanding or shrinking.
how_to_read: Upward trend indicates healthy list growth
Compare new subscribers vs unsubscribes to understand churn
Flat or declining trends may signal the need for new opt-in strategies
Spikes often correlate with promotions or sign-up incentives
Watch for sudden drops that could indicate compliance or technical issues
decisions_answered: Is our SMS list growing or shrinking?
Are we acquiring subscribers faster than we lose them?
What periods show the best growth and why?
Do we need new strategies to drive SMS opt-ins?
payload.narration_text:

A healthy SMS program shows steady upward growth - new subscribers consistently outnumbering unsubscribes.

Watch for the balance between acquisition and churn. If unsubscribes are climbing toward your new subscriber rate, you need to address retention before growth stalls.

Spikes in growth often correlate with promotions, pop-up campaigns, or checkout opt-in incentives. Study what drove your best growth periods and replicate those strategies.

This answers: Is our list growing? Are we retaining subscribers? And what drives our best acquisition periods?

Use this to build templates for future success. The patterns in your top messages are your playbook for what works with your audience.

payload.formula_explanation: This heatmap shows click rate performance by hour of day, helping you identify optimal send times. Darker colors indicate higher click rates. Analysis is based on the last 30 days of SMS activity.
how_to_read: Darker blue cells indicate hours with higher click rates - these are your optimal send windows
Lighter cells show hours with lower engagement - avoid scheduling campaigns here
Hover over any cell to see the exact click rate percentage for that hour
Look for patterns - morning, lunch, evening - to align with customer behavior
Note that early morning and late night typically show lower engagement
decisions_answered: What time of day gets the best SMS response?
When should we avoid sending SMS messages?
Are there multiple high-engagement windows we can use?
Does our audience engage more in morning or evening?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals your optimal SMS send times based on actual click performance.

Each cell represents an hour of the day. Darker blue means higher click rates at that hour. Lighter colors mean lower engagement.

Your goal is to find patterns. Most brands see peaks during morning commute, lunch breaks, and evening leisure time. But your audience may be different.

Avoid the light-colored hours - sending then wastes messages on low-engagement periods. Focus your campaigns and flows during the dark blue windows.

Hover over cells to see exact click rates. Look for 2-3 hour windows where engagement is consistently strong.

This answers critical questions: When should we send? When should we avoid? And does our audience prefer morning or evening engagement?

payload.formula_explanation: Total number of SMS messages sent over the period, summed from the SMS performance mart. This is the volume denominator for the SMS rate metrics.
how_to_read: The main value shows total SMS sent in the period

The sparkline shows the daily send trend
Spikes typically reflect campaign blasts on top of always-on flows
Click to split volume by campaign vs. flow source
decisions_answered: How much SMS volume did we send this period?
Is our SMS cadence growing or shrinking?
Did a campaign drive an unusual spike?

payload.formula_explanation: Average SMS opt-out rate over the period, computed as the mean of the precomputed daily opt-out-rate values. A rising rate signals message fatigue or poor targeting.
how_to_read: The main value shows the average SMS opt-out rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to watch for list fatigue
The sparkline shows the daily opt-out trend
Sustained increases mean cadence or relevance needs attention
Click to investigate list health and send-time patterns
decisions_answered: Are we sending too much or too irrelevant SMS?

payload.formula_explanation: This chart aggregates SMS sending volume and click engagement on a weekly basis over 12 weeks. Each data point represents the total count of unique SMS events for that week, allowing you to identify trends and patterns in your SMS program.
how_to_read: Blue area shows total SMS messages sent per week - higher peaks indicate more active sending periods
Green area shows total clicks received - should correlate with sending volume
Look for the gap between sent and clicks to gauge overall engagement health
Sudden drops in either metric may indicate delivery issues or list health problems
Compare week-over-week to identify seasonal patterns or campaign impact
decisions_answered: Is our SMS sending volume increasing or decreasing over time?
Are click volumes keeping pace with our sending growth?
What weeks had the best SMS engagement?
Are there seasonal patterns we should plan around?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows your SMS performance over the past 12 weeks, tracking both sending volume and engagement.

The blue area represents total SMS messages sent each week. Higher peaks mean more active sending periods, which could be due to campaigns, promotions, or automated flows.

The green area shows clicks - the number of times recipients tapped links in your messages. Healthy SMS programs show clicks moving in proportion to sends.

Watch for the gap between these two lines. If sends are rising but clicks stay flat, engagement is dropping. If both grow together, your SMS strategy is scaling well.

Use this to answer: Is our program growing? Are we maintaining engagement as we scale? And when do we see our best performance weeks?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution by age bracket and gender from GA4 demographic data. Age distribution is displayed as a bar chart and gender as a donut chart. These inform messaging, channel selection, creative direction, and product positioning decisions.
how_to_read: Left side shows age distribution - bars for each bracket from 18-24 to 65+
Right side shows gender split as a donut chart
The dominant age bracket indicates your core audience
Gender composition affects creative direction and targeting
Compare engagement rates across age groups for content insights
Note: GA4 demographic data requires user consent and may be sampled
decisions_answered: What is the age and gender distribution of our visitors?
Which age groups are most engaged with our content?
Should our marketing messaging target specific demographics?
Does our demographic mix match our target audience?
How do demographic patterns align with our product positioning?
payload.narration_text:
This combined chart shows user demographics - age distribution as a bar chart and gender split as a donut chart.

The formula counts users by GA4 age brackets and gender, excluding unknown values. Together they paint a picture of who your visitors are.

Reading this chart: On the left, bars show user count per age group, ordered 18-24 through 65+. Your dominant bracket reveals the core audience. On the right, the donut shows gender split.

Consider the implications. If 35-44 dominates, messaging should resonate with established adults. If 25-34 leads, social media may be more effective. A female-dominant audience suggests imagery and copy resonating with women.

Key questions: What's the age and gender mix? Which demographics engage most? Should messaging target specific groups? Use this for persona development and creative direction.

payload.formula_explanation: Average revenue per purchase over the period, dividing revenue by purchases from the funnel mart.
how_to_read: The main value shows average revenue per purchase
Compare to the prior period to track basket size
The sparkline shows the daily AOV trend
Rising AOV grows revenue even at a flat conversion rate
Click to see what drives basket size
decisions_answered: How large is the typical purchase?
Is basket size growing?
Should we push upsells to lift AOV?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows performance of GA4 audience segments like All Users, Purchasers, Cart Abandoners, and custom segments. Helps identify high-value audience characteristics.
how_to_read: Audience Name identifies the segment definition
Sessions show engagement level of each segment
Active Users indicates segment size
New Users shows growth of each segment
Revenue Per User identifies highest-value segments
Purchasers segment shows confirmed buyer behavior
Cart Abandoners segment reveals retargeting opportunity size
decisions_answered: Which audience segments are most valuable?
How large is our cart abandoner segment?
What is the revenue per user for purchasers vs general users?
Which segments should we prioritize for remarketing?
How do custom audience segments perform?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows performance by GA4 audience segment - revealing which user groups drive the most value.

The formula aggregates metrics by audience name, including both standard segments like Purchasers and custom-defined audiences.

Reading this table: Audience Name shows the segment. Active Users shows size. Revenue Per User identifies value. Compare segments to understand what makes users valuable.

Key segments to watch: Purchasers are confirmed buyers. Cart Abandoners represent recovery opportunity. High-value users warrant VIP treatment. New Users show acquisition effectiveness.

Key questions: Which segments are valuable? How large is cart abandonment? What defines high-value users? Where should remarketing focus? Use for audience-based marketing.

payload.formula_explanation: This geographic breakdown shows conversion rates and order values by region. It helps identify strong markets for expansion, weak markets needing investigation, and regional patterns in customer behavior.
how_to_read: Regions are ranked by user volume
Compare conversion rates across regions for consistency
AOV differences may reflect demographic or product preferences
Low conversion in high-population regions represents opportunity
Consider shipping costs and delivery times as conversion factors
decisions_answered: Which regions have the highest conversion rates?
Are there underperforming regions we should investigate?
Which regions generate the highest order values?
Should we adjust regional marketing or shipping strategies?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows conversion performance broken down by geographic region.

Regional conversion rate is conversions divided by users in that region. Average order value is revenue divided by conversions.

Compare regions for conversion consistency. Large variations may indicate shipping cost concerns, delivery time issues, or regional marketing gaps. High-population regions with low conversion represent major opportunity.

This answers: Which regions convert best? Are any regions underperforming? Where are order values highest? And should we adjust regional strategies?

payload.formula_explanation: Ranks pages by user visits, showing which content drives the most traffic. Includes engagement duration to indicate content quality and revenue attribution for commerce pages.
how_to_read: Pages are ranked by unique user visits, not just page views
Homepage (/) typically leads but product pages show intent
Page Views vs Users ratio indicates repeat viewing behavior
Avg Time on Page shows content engagement - longer is usually better
Revenue column shows direct attribution for product/category pages
Search result pages indicate high search usage - consider improving navigation
Use search to find specific pages or URL patterns
decisions_answered: Which pages attract the most visitors?
What product pages are most popular?
Are users spending enough time on key pages?
Which pages generate the most revenue?
Are there high-traffic pages with low engagement needing optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your top landing pages by traffic - revealing which content drives visitors and engagement.

The formula counts page views and users, calculates average engagement time, and attributes revenue to each page path.

Reading this table: Pages ranked by user count, not views. Homepage typically leads. Product pages show purchase intent. Check the views-to-users ratio - high ratios mean repeat viewing. Average time indicates engagement quality.

Look for opportunities. High-traffic pages with low engagement need optimization. Product pages with high time-on-page may have friction. Search results pages suggest navigation improvements needed.

Key questions: Which pages attract visitors? What products are popular? Are users engaging? Which pages need optimization? Search for specific URL patterns.

payload.formula_explanation: Share of sessions that were not engaged, computed as one minus the engaged-session ratio. It is a weighted average across the period, not a simple mean of daily rates.
how_to_read: The main value shows the bounce rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to track landing quality
The sparkline shows the daily bounce-rate trend
High bounce points to landing-page or traffic-quality issues
Click to analyze bounce by channel and landing page
decisions_answered: Are visitors engaging or leaving immediately?
Is landing-page quality holding up?
Which channels send low-quality traffic?

payload.formula_explanation: This table compares conversion rates across different web browsers. Significant variations may indicate technical compatibility issues or bugs specific to certain browsers that harm conversions.
how_to_read: Browsers are listed with their user counts for context
Compare conversion rates across browsers for parity
Large variations suggest browser-specific technical issues
Focus on high-volume browsers with below-average conversion
Safari on iOS may show different behavior than Safari on Mac
decisions_answered: Do all browsers convert equally well?
Are there browser-specific technical issues hurting conversion?
Which browsers should QA prioritize for testing?
Is Chrome conversion representative of overall performance?
payload.narration_text:
This table compares conversion rates across web browsers like Chrome, Safari, Firefox, and Edge.

Browser conversion rate equals conversions divided by users for that browser. Ideally, all browsers should convert similarly.

Large variations indicate technical problems. If Safari converts much worse than Chrome, there might be checkout bugs on Safari. High-volume browsers with low conversion deserve immediate QA attention.

This answers: Do browsers convert equally? Are there browser-specific bugs? Which browsers need QA focus? And is Chrome performance representative?

payload.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution by web browser (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, etc.) and operating system (iOS, Android, Windows, macOS). Important for QA prioritization and identifying compatibility issues.
how_to_read: Top bars show browser distribution - Chrome typically leads
Bottom bars show operating system distribution
iOS and Safari dominance indicates strong Apple user base
Chrome + Android combination shows Google ecosystem users
Lower bars may reveal niche browsers needing attention
Compare engagement rates to spot browser-specific issues
decisions_answered: Which browsers do our users prefer?
What operating systems should we prioritize for testing?
Are there browser-specific performance or conversion issues?
Should we support legacy browsers like Internet Explorer?
Is our mobile app consideration warranted based on OS distribution?
payload.narration_text:
This dual bar chart shows user distribution by browser and operating system - essential for QA prioritization and compatibility planning.

The formula counts users by browser and operating system separately, showing which technologies your visitors use most.

Reading this chart: Top bars show browser distribution. Chrome typically leads for general audiences, while Safari indicates Apple users. Bottom bars show operating systems - iOS and Android for mobile, Windows and macOS for desktop.

Use this for testing prioritization. Focus QA on your top browsers. If a browser has high users but low engagement, investigate compatibility issues. Consider dropping support for browsers below 1 percent share.

Key questions: Which browsers need testing? What OS mix do we have? Are there browser-specific issues? Should we support legacy browsers? This guides technical decisions.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows performance of named marketing campaigns from Google Ads, Meta, email, and other sources. Excludes generic traffic categories like (direct) and (organic).
how_to_read: Each row represents a named campaign driving traffic
Sessions show how much traffic each campaign generates
Engagement Rate indicates traffic quality from each campaign
Revenue shows direct attribution to campaigns
Revenue per Session measures campaign efficiency
Sort by any metric to identify top or underperforming campaigns
Campaign names often indicate platform, audience, and strategy
decisions_answered: Which campaigns drive the most traffic?
What is the revenue return from each campaign?
Which campaigns have the best engagement rates?
Are there campaigns with high traffic but low conversion?
How does campaign performance compare to spend?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows performance of named marketing campaigns - essential for evaluating campaign effectiveness.

The formula aggregates sessions, users, engagement, and revenue by campaign name, excluding generic categories.

Reading this table: Each row is a named campaign. Sessions show traffic volume. Engagement rate indicates quality. Revenue shows direct attribution. Revenue per session measures efficiency.

Compare campaigns to find winners and losers. High sessions with low engagement may need audience refinement. High engagement with low revenue may need landing page optimization. Campaign names often reveal strategy - PMAX, Brand, Retargeting.

Key questions: Which campaigns perform best? What's the revenue return? Are there efficiency issues? Should budget shift? Cross-reference with ad platform data for full picture.

payload.formula_explanation: Share of add-to-cart sessions that did not result in a purchase, from the funnel mart. It quantifies how much demand is lost between cart and checkout.
how_to_read: The main value shows the cart abandonment rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to track checkout friction
The sparkline shows the daily abandonment trend
High abandonment points to shipping, payment, or account friction
Click to view the funnel and checkout drop-off
decisions_answered: How many shoppers abandon their carts?
Is checkout friction worsening?
Where in checkout are we losing buyers?

payload.formula_explanation: This daily trend shows the percentage of users who added items to their cart but did not complete a purchase. Lower abandonment rates indicate better performance. The 90-day view helps identify patterns and the impact of optimization efforts.
how_to_read: The X-axis shows daily data over 90 days
The Y-axis shows the cart abandonment rate as a percentage
Lower values are better - they indicate fewer lost carts
Weekend vs. weekday patterns may reveal behavioral differences
Spikes may correlate with site issues, pricing changes, or competitive events
decisions_answered: Is cart abandonment improving or worsening over time?
Are there day-of-week patterns in abandonment behavior?
Did recent optimizations reduce cart abandonment?
What external events correlate with abandonment spikes?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart shows daily cart abandonment rates over 90 days. Cart abandonment measures users who add items but don't purchase.

The formula: users who added to cart minus those who purchased, divided by cart adders, times 100. Lower is better.

Look for trends - a declining line means your cart experience is improving. Spikes may indicate technical issues or external factors like competitor promotions. Weekend patterns might reveal different user intentions.

This answers: Is abandonment improving? What day patterns exist? Did recent changes help? And what events trigger spikes?

payload.formula_explanation: This chart compares conversion rates across traffic acquisition channels. Some channels drive high traffic volume with lower conversion, while others bring fewer but more purchase-ready visitors. This informs budget allocation and channel optimization priorities.
how_to_read: Channels are sorted to highlight relative performance
Longer bars indicate higher conversion rates for that channel
Compare paid channels (Paid Search, Paid Social) to organic ones
High-volume channels with low conversion may need targeting refinement
High-converting channels with low volume may warrant increased investment
decisions_answered: Which channel has the highest conversion rate?
Are paid channels converting better than organic?
Which channels should receive increased budget?
Which channels need conversion optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart compares conversion rates across your traffic channels - from Direct to Paid Search to Social.

Each bar shows that channel's conversion rate: conversions divided by users, times 100. Longer bars mean better conversion.

Compare paid versus organic channels. High conversion with low volume suggests opportunity for increased spend. Low conversion despite high volume means targeting or landing page issues need attention.

This answers: Which channel converts best? How do paid and organic compare? Where should budget increase? And which channels need optimization?

payload.formula_explanation: Share of begun checkouts that completed in a purchase, from the funnel mart. It isolates friction inside the checkout flow itself.
how_to_read: The main value shows the checkout completion rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to track checkout health
The sparkline shows the daily completion trend
Low completion points to payment or form friction
Click to view the checkout drop-off breakdown
decisions_answered: Once started, how often is checkout completed?
Is checkout friction increasing?
Which checkout step is leaking buyers?

payload.formula_explanation: This focused funnel tracks user progression through the four checkout stages: Begin Checkout, Add Shipping Info, Add Payment Info, and Purchase. It reveals which specific checkout step causes the most friction and abandonment.
how_to_read: Begin Checkout is the starting point - users who initiated the checkout process
Add Shipping and Add Payment show users who completed those form steps
Purchase shows users who successfully completed the transaction
The largest drop-off between stages indicates the most problematic checkout step
Compare this to the main funnel to isolate checkout-specific issues
decisions_answered: Where exactly in checkout are users abandoning?
Is shipping information or payment information causing more friction?
What is our checkout completion rate?
Should we invest in shipping info simplification or payment optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This focused funnel zooms into the checkout process, breaking it into four stages: Begin Checkout, Add Shipping, Add Payment, and Purchase.

Drop-off at each stage equals users lost divided by the starting users for that step. Checkout completion is purchases divided by those who began checkout.

Look for the biggest gap between bars - that's where users struggle most. Shipping drop-off suggests address form issues. Payment drop-off might indicate trust concerns or payment method problems.

This answers: Where exactly do checkout abandonments happen? Is shipping or payment the bigger issue? And where should checkout optimization focus?

payload.formula_explanation: Share of sessions that ended in a purchase, computed as total purchases over total sessions from the funnel mart. It is the headline measure of site conversion effectiveness.
how_to_read: The main value shows the conversion rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to track effectiveness
The sparkline shows the daily conversion trend
Sudden drops can signal site or checkout issues
Click to analyze conversion by device, channel, or region
decisions_answered: What share of visitors are buying?
Is conversion healthy versus recent performance?
Are there site issues hurting conversion?

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows Google Ads landing page metrics including click-through rate, cost efficiency, and page speed scores. It helps optimize paid traffic landing experiences for better conversion and lower acquisition costs.
how_to_read: Landing Page shows the destination URL for ad clicks
Clicks shows how many times users clicked ads to that page
Impressions shows how many times ads for that page were shown
CTR measures ad effectiveness in driving clicks
Cost shows total spend driving traffic to each landing page
decisions_answered: Which landing pages receive the most ad clicks?
Which landing pages have the best click-through rates?
Are we overspending on underperforming landing pages?
Which landing pages need speed or UX optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows Google Ads landing page metrics - the pages where paid clicks land.

CTR equals clicks divided by impressions, times 100. Cost per click equals total cost divided by clicks.

High CTR means your ads resonate with the audience. High cost with low conversions suggests the landing page needs work. Speed scores below 50 indicate page load issues that hurt conversions.

This answers: Which landing pages get most clicks? Which have best CTR? Are we overspending on poor performers? And which pages need speed optimization?

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows Google Ads landing page metrics including click-through rate, cost efficiency, and page speed scores. It helps optimize paid traffic landing experiences for better conversion and lower acquisition costs.

Which landing pages have the best click-through rates?

Which landing pages need speed or UX optimization?

payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates session counts, unique user counts, and revenue by day from GA4 traffic acquisition data. Sessions represent individual visits, users represent unique visitors, and revenue shows e-commerce transactions attributed to each day.
how_to_read: The blue line shows daily session counts - total visits to your website
The green line shows daily unique users who initiated sessions
The purple dashed line shows daily revenue (right axis scale)
Peaks indicate high-traffic days, often correlating with marketing campaigns or seasonal events
Look for patterns: weekday vs weekend traffic, monthly trends, holiday impacts
Hover over any point to see exact values for all metrics on that date
decisions_answered: How is our website traffic trending over time?
Which days had the highest traffic and revenue?
Are there seasonal patterns in our traffic?
Is revenue correlated with traffic volume?
Did recent campaigns impact traffic levels?
payload.narration_text:
This multi-line chart shows your website traffic trend over time - the foundation for understanding visitor patterns and marketing impact.

The formula aggregates three key metrics by day: sessions (total visits), users (unique visitors), and revenue. Each metric comes from GA4 traffic acquisition reports.

Reading this chart: The blue area shows session volume. The green line tracks unique users. Purple shows revenue on a secondary axis. Peaks indicate high-traffic periods.

Look for patterns. Does traffic spike on certain weekdays? Are there seasonal trends? Do revenue peaks align with traffic peaks? Gaps between sessions and users indicate returning visitors.

Key questions answered: How is traffic trending? Which days perform best? Are campaigns driving traffic? Is revenue correlated with visits? Click any point for daily details.

payload.formula_explanation: This comparison shows how mobile, desktop, and tablet users convert and spend differently. Desktop typically shows higher conversion rates while mobile drives more traffic volume. The dual metrics help balance conversion rate with revenue potential.
how_to_read: Blue bars show conversion rate by device type
Green bars show revenue per user by device type
Compare mobile vs. desktop gaps to assess mobile UX quality
A large mobile conversion gap suggests mobile optimization opportunity
Higher desktop revenue per user is typical but the gap size matters
decisions_answered: How much worse is mobile conversion than desktop?
Is our mobile experience adequately optimized?
Which device type generates the most revenue per user?
Should we prioritize mobile UX improvements?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart breaks down performance by device: desktop, mobile, and tablet. Two metrics are shown: conversion rate and revenue per user.

Conversion rate is conversions divided by users. Revenue per user is total revenue divided by users.

Desktop typically converts higher, but mobile often drives more traffic. If the mobile conversion gap is large, there's significant optimization opportunity. Revenue per user shows which device attracts higher-value customers.

This answers: How big is the mobile gap? Is mobile UX good enough? Which device drives most value? And should mobile optimization be prioritized?

payload.formula_explanation: Segments users by their device type. Mobile, desktop, and tablet users often have different behaviors, conversion rates, and session characteristics that inform UX and marketing decisions.
how_to_read: The donut chart shows user distribution across device types
Center displays total users across all devices
Mobile typically dominates for most e-commerce sites (60%+)
Desktop users often have higher conversion rates and order values
Tablet represents a smaller but distinct user segment
The trend chart shows how device mix changes over time
Click any segment to filter the page to that device type
decisions_answered: What percentage of users access our site via mobile?
Is our mobile vs desktop split healthy for our industry?
Are device proportions changing over time?
Should we prioritize mobile-first design improvements?
Does tablet traffic warrant dedicated optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart and trend visualization shows how users access your website across device types - critical for UX prioritization.

The formula counts unique users by device category, filtered to mobile, desktop, and tablet. The share percentage shows each device's proportion of total users.

Reading this chart: The donut shows current device distribution. Mobile typically dominates at 60 percent or more. The center displays total users. The trend chart shows how the mix evolves over time.

Device insights matter for design decisions. Mobile users may have lower conversion rates but higher volume. Desktop users often spend more per order. Watch for mobile growth trends.

Key questions: What's the mobile percentage? Is the mix changing? Should you prioritize mobile UX? How does each device convert? Click a segment for device-specific analysis.

payload.formula_text: SUM(TOTAL_USERS) WHERE DEVICE_CATEGORY IN (mobile, desktop, tablet)

payload.formula_explanation: Share of sessions that were engaged, computed as engaged sessions over total sessions. It is a weighted average across the period and is the inverse of bounce rate.
how_to_read: The main value shows the engagement rate as a percentage
Compare to the prior period to track content stickiness
The sparkline shows the daily engagement trend
Rising engagement usually precedes better conversion
Click to compare engagement by channel and device
decisions_answered: Are visitors engaging with the site?
Is engagement improving over time?
Which channels deliver the most engaged traffic?

payload.formula_explanation: Ranks user events by frequency, showing which actions visitors take most often. Events include page views, product interactions, cart actions, and conversions.
how_to_read: Bars show event frequency, ranked by total count
view_item_list indicates product browsing behavior
add_to_cart shows shopping intent
begin_checkout and purchase show conversion funnel progress
page_view is a baseline engagement metric
Unusual event spikes may indicate bugs or bot activity
Events per user shows engagement depth
decisions_answered: What actions do users take most frequently?
How many users progress through the purchase funnel?
Is add_to_cart volume healthy relative to purchases?
Are there unexpected events indicating issues?
How engaged are users based on events per session?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows top user events by frequency - revealing how visitors interact with your website.

The formula counts event occurrences and calculates events per user for engagement depth measurement.

Reading this chart: Bars show event count, ranked highest to lowest. View_item_list shows browsing. Add_to_cart shows intent. Purchase is your conversion event. Page_view is baseline engagement.

Ratios matter. Compare add_to_cart to purchase - a large gap indicates cart abandonment. Compare view_item_list to add_to_cart for product page effectiveness. Unusual event spikes warrant investigation.

Key questions: What actions are common? How's the funnel ratio? Is cart abandonment high? Are there unusual patterns? Events reveal user journey details.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution by age bracket and gender from GA4 demographic data. Age distribution is displayed as a bar chart and gender as a donut chart. These inform messaging, channel selection, creative direction, and product positioning decisions.
how_to_read: Left side shows age distribution - bars for each bracket from 18-24 to 65+
Right side shows gender split as a donut chart
The dominant age bracket indicates your core audience
Gender composition affects creative direction and targeting
Compare engagement rates across age groups for content insights
Note: GA4 demographic data requires user consent and may be sampled
decisions_answered: What is the age and gender distribution of our visitors?
Which age groups are most engaged with our content?
Should our marketing messaging target specific demographics?
Does our demographic mix match our target audience?
How do demographic patterns align with our product positioning?
payload.narration_text:
This combined chart shows user demographics - age distribution as a bar chart and gender split as a donut chart.

The formula counts users by GA4 age brackets and gender, excluding unknown values. Together they paint a picture of who your visitors are.

Reading this chart: On the left, bars show user count per age group, ordered 18-24 through 65+. Your dominant bracket reveals the core audience. On the right, the donut shows gender split.

Consider the implications. If 35-44 dominates, messaging should resonate with established adults. If 25-34 leads, social media may be more effective. A female-dominant audience suggests imagery and copy resonating with women.

Key questions: What's the age and gender mix? Which demographics engage most? Should messaging target specific groups? Use this for persona development and creative direction.

payload.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution by web browser (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, etc.) and operating system (iOS, Android, Windows, macOS). Important for QA prioritization and identifying compatibility issues.
how_to_read: Top bars show browser distribution - Chrome typically leads
Bottom bars show operating system distribution
iOS and Safari dominance indicates strong Apple user base
Chrome + Android combination shows Google ecosystem users
Lower bars may reveal niche browsers needing attention
Compare engagement rates to spot browser-specific issues
decisions_answered: Which browsers do our users prefer?
What operating systems should we prioritize for testing?
Are there browser-specific performance or conversion issues?
Should we support legacy browsers like Internet Explorer?
Is our mobile app consideration warranted based on OS distribution?

The formula counts users by browser and operating system separately, showing which technologies your visitors use most.

Reading this chart: Top bars show browser distribution. Chrome typically leads for general audiences, while Safari indicates Apple users. Bottom bars show operating systems - iOS and Android for mobile, Windows and macOS for desktop.

Use this for testing prioritization. Focus QA on your top browsers. If a browser has high users but low engagement, investigate compatibility issues. Consider dropping support for browsers below 1 percent share.

payload.formula_explanation: This table analyzes individual page performance, showing which pages receive traffic and generate revenue. Pages with high traffic but low revenue may have UX issues or be missing conversion opportunities.
how_to_read: Page paths show the URL structure of each page
Page Views indicates traffic volume to each page
Users shows unique visitors to the page
Revenue shows direct attribution to that page
High traffic pages with zero revenue may be informational or problematic
decisions_answered: Which pages receive the most traffic?
Which pages generate the most revenue?
Are there high-traffic pages failing to convert?
Which pages should be prioritized for optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This table analyzes your site pages by traffic and revenue contribution.

Revenue per view equals page revenue divided by page views. This measures how well each page contributes to conversions.

High-traffic pages with low revenue need attention - they might be missing CTAs or have poor user experience. Product pages should show revenue; if they don't, there may be add-to-cart friction.

This answers: Which pages get most traffic? Which generate revenue? Are popular pages failing to convert? And where should page optimization focus?

payload.formula_explanation: Ranks pages by user visits, showing which content drives the most traffic. Includes engagement duration to indicate content quality and revenue attribution for commerce pages.
how_to_read: Pages are ranked by unique user visits, not just page views
Homepage (/) typically leads but product pages show intent
Page Views vs Users ratio indicates repeat viewing behavior
Avg Time on Page shows content engagement - longer is usually better
Revenue column shows direct attribution for product/category pages
Search result pages indicate high search usage - consider improving navigation
Use search to find specific pages or URL patterns
decisions_answered: Which pages attract the most visitors?
What product pages are most popular?
Are users spending enough time on key pages?
Which pages generate the most revenue?
Are there high-traffic pages with low engagement needing optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your top landing pages by traffic - revealing which content drives visitors and engagement.

The formula counts page views and users, calculates average engagement time, and attributes revenue to each page path.

Reading this table: Pages ranked by user count, not views. Homepage typically leads. Product pages show purchase intent. Check the views-to-users ratio - high ratios mean repeat viewing. Average time indicates engagement quality.

Look for opportunities. High-traffic pages with low engagement need optimization. Product pages with high time-on-page may have friction. Search results pages suggest navigation improvements needed.

Key questions: Which pages attract visitors? What products are popular? Are users engaging? Which pages need optimization? Search for specific URL patterns.

payload.formula_explanation: This table shows products ranked by revenue, with their view-to-purchase conversion data. It helps identify products that convert well and drive revenue, versus products that get views but fail to convert.
how_to_read: Products are ranked by revenue contribution
Views shows how many times the product was viewed
Purchases shows actual units sold
Purchase Rate reveals conversion efficiency
High views with low purchase rate indicates pricing or description issues
decisions_answered: Which products are our top revenue drivers?
Which products have the best view-to-purchase conversion?
Are there popular products that fail to convert?
Which products should receive more marketing attention?
payload.narration_text:
This table ranks your products by revenue while showing their conversion metrics.

Purchase rate equals items purchased divided by items viewed, times 100. This tells you how efficiently each product converts viewers to buyers.

High revenue products validate your strategy. High views but low purchase rate suggests pricing or description problems. High conversion rate with low views means marketing opportunity.

This answers: What are our top revenue products? Which convert best? Are popular products failing to convert? And which deserve more marketing?

payload.formula_explanation: This geographic breakdown shows conversion rates and order values by region. It helps identify strong markets for expansion, weak markets needing investigation, and regional patterns in customer behavior.
how_to_read: Regions are ranked by user volume
Compare conversion rates across regions for consistency
AOV differences may reflect demographic or product preferences
Low conversion in high-population regions represents opportunity
Consider shipping costs and delivery times as conversion factors
decisions_answered: Which regions have the highest conversion rates?
Are there underperforming regions we should investigate?
Which regions generate the highest order values?
Should we adjust regional marketing or shipping strategies?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows conversion performance broken down by geographic region.

Regional conversion rate is conversions divided by users in that region. Average order value is revenue divided by conversions.

Compare regions for conversion consistency. Large variations may indicate shipping cost concerns, delivery time issues, or regional marketing gaps. High-population regions with low conversion represent major opportunity.

This answers: Which regions convert best? Are any regions underperforming? Where are order values highest? And should we adjust regional strategies?

payload.formula_explanation: Total revenue over the period from the funnel mart. It ties conversion performance to financial outcome.
how_to_read: The main value shows total revenue for the period
Compare to the prior period to track sales
The sparkline shows the daily revenue trend
Pair with conversion rate and AOV to interpret changes
Click to connect funnel stages to revenue
decisions_answered: How much revenue did conversions generate?
Is revenue growing with conversion?
What is driving revenue - rate or order value?

payload.formula_explanation: This table provides granular conversion data at the source/medium level, going deeper than channel groupings. It reveals which specific traffic sources (google, facebook, direct) combined with their medium (organic, cpc, referral) perform best for conversions and revenue.
how_to_read: Source/Medium combines the traffic origin with how users arrived
Sessions show traffic volume from each source/medium pair
Conversions and Revenue show the business outcome
Conv Rate helps compare efficiency regardless of volume
Sort by different columns to find top performers or problem areas
decisions_answered: Which source/medium combinations convert best?
Are specific paid campaigns underperforming?
Which referral sources drive valuable traffic?
Where should we focus acquisition budget?
payload.narration_text:
This table breaks down conversion performance by source and medium - for example, google organic versus google paid, or facebook referral versus facebook paid.

Conversion rate equals conversions divided by sessions. Revenue per session shows the dollar value each visit generates.

Use this to compare similar sources with different mediums. A source with high traffic but low conversion needs landing page work. High conversion with low volume might deserve more budget.

This answers: Which source/medium pairs convert best? Are campaigns underperforming? Which referrals are valuable? And where should acquisition spend focus?

payload.formula_explanation: This comparison shows how mobile, desktop, and tablet users convert and spend differently. Desktop typically shows higher conversion rates while mobile drives more traffic volume. The dual metrics help balance conversion rate with revenue potential.
how_to_read: Blue bars show conversion rate by device type
Green bars show revenue per user by device type
Compare mobile vs. desktop gaps to assess mobile UX quality
A large mobile conversion gap suggests mobile optimization opportunity
Higher desktop revenue per user is typical but the gap size matters
decisions_answered: How much worse is mobile conversion than desktop?
Is our mobile experience adequately optimized?
Which device type generates the most revenue per user?
Should we prioritize mobile UX improvements?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart breaks down performance by device: desktop, mobile, and tablet. Two metrics are shown: conversion rate and revenue per user.

Conversion rate is conversions divided by users. Revenue per user is total revenue divided by users.

Desktop typically converts higher, but mobile often drives more traffic. If the mobile conversion gap is large, there's significant optimization opportunity. Revenue per user shows which device attracts higher-value customers.

This answers: How big is the mobile gap? Is mobile UX good enough? Which device drives most value? And should mobile optimization be prioritized?

payload.formula_explanation: Average revenue generated per session, dividing revenue by sessions. Despite the 'per user' shorthand, the backend computes this per session - it blends conversion rate and AOV into one efficiency number.
how_to_read: The main value shows revenue earned per session
Compare to the prior period to track monetization
The sparkline shows the daily revenue-per-session trend
It combines conversion rate and AOV into one figure
Click to compare monetization by channel and device
decisions_answered: How well does each visit monetize?
Is our traffic getting more or less valuable?
Which channels monetize best per session?

payload.formula_explanation: Groups all website sessions by their default channel grouping from GA4 - including Direct, Organic Search, Paid Search, Email, Social, and Referral. This shows where your traffic originates.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars represent each traffic channel, sorted by session volume
Longer bars indicate channels driving more traffic
Direct traffic comes from users typing your URL or using bookmarks
Organic Search represents unpaid Google/Bing search traffic
Paid Search and Paid Social show advertising-driven traffic
Email shows traffic from email marketing campaigns
Click any channel bar to filter the entire page to that channel
decisions_answered: Which channels drive the most traffic to our website?
What is our paid vs organic traffic mix?
Is our email marketing driving significant traffic?
Should we invest more in specific channels?
How dependent are we on any single traffic source?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows traffic distribution across marketing channels - essential for understanding your traffic mix and acquisition strategy.

The formula groups sessions by GA4's default channel grouping and calculates each channel's share of total traffic. Channels include Direct, Organic Search, Paid Search, Email, Social, and Referral.

Reading this chart: Bars are sorted by session volume. Longer bars mean more traffic from that channel. Direct typically leads for established brands. Organic Search shows SEO effectiveness. Paid channels show advertising ROI.

A healthy mix avoids over-dependence on any single channel. If one channel dominates excessively, diversification may reduce risk. Compare engagement rates across channels for quality insights.

Key questions: Which channels drive traffic? What's the paid vs organic mix? Is email effective? Should you shift budget? Click any bar to analyze that channel in depth.

payload.formula_explanation: Breaks down traffic by specific source (like google, facebook, newsletter) and medium (like organic, cpc, email). This provides granular insight into exactly where traffic originates.
how_to_read: Source identifies the specific origin (google, facebook, direct, etc.)
Medium identifies the marketing mechanism (organic, cpc, email, referral)
Sessions column shows traffic volume from each source/medium pair
Engagement Rate indicates traffic quality - higher is better
Revenue shows the direct value generated by each traffic source
Sort by any column to identify top performers or problem areas
Use the search box to find specific sources or mediums
decisions_answered: Which specific sources drive the most traffic?
What mediums are most effective for each source?
Which source/medium combinations have the best engagement?
Where is our revenue coming from at a granular level?
Are there underperforming sources that need optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows traffic broken down by source and medium - the most granular view of where your visitors originate.

The formula groups sessions by both source (the specific origin like google or facebook) and medium (the mechanism like organic or cpc). Engagement rate measures traffic quality.

Reading this table: Each row represents a unique source/medium combination. Google organic is typically the largest. Compare engagement rates to identify quality sources. Revenue shows direct attribution.

Sort by different columns to find insights. High sessions with low engagement may indicate targeting issues. High engagement with low sessions suggests promotion opportunities.

Key questions: Which sources drive traffic? What mediums work best? Where does revenue come from? Which sources need optimization? Use search to find specific sources.

payload.formula_explanation: The funnel visualizes the customer journey from initial session through to completed purchase. Each stage shows the absolute user count and the percentage who progressed from the previous stage. The overall conversion rate represents the end-to-end efficiency of turning visitors into buyers.
how_to_read: Each bar represents a stage in the e-commerce funnel: Sessions, View Item, Add to Cart, Begin Checkout, Purchase
Bar width indicates the relative volume compared to the initial session count
Percentages between stages show the step-to-step conversion rate (drop-off)
Identify the largest drop-offs to prioritize optimization efforts
Green highlighting on the final Purchase stage indicates successful conversions
decisions_answered: What is our overall site conversion rate?
Where in the funnel are we losing the most potential customers?
Which funnel stage requires the most urgent optimization?
How does our funnel performance compare to e-commerce benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel chart shows the complete customer journey from initial site visit to purchase completion.

The formula calculates each stage's conversion rate by dividing users at that stage by the previous stage, times 100. Overall conversion is purchases divided by sessions.

To read this: each bar is a funnel stage. Larger bars mean more users at that point. The percentages between bars reveal where customers drop off. The biggest drops indicate your highest-priority optimization targets.

This answers: What's our conversion rate? Where do we lose customers? Which stage needs attention? And how do we compare to industry standards?

payload.formula_text: ((Stage N Users - Stage N+1 Users) / Stage N Users) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: This focused funnel tracks user progression through the four checkout stages: Begin Checkout, Add Shipping Info, Add Payment Info, and Purchase. It reveals which specific checkout step causes the most friction and abandonment.
how_to_read: Begin Checkout is the starting point - users who initiated the checkout process
Add Shipping and Add Payment show users who completed those form steps
Purchase shows users who successfully completed the transaction
The largest drop-off between stages indicates the most problematic checkout step
Compare this to the main funnel to isolate checkout-specific issues
decisions_answered: Where exactly in checkout are users abandoning?
Is shipping information or payment information causing more friction?
What is our checkout completion rate?
Should we invest in shipping info simplification or payment optimization?
payload.narration_text:
This focused funnel zooms into the checkout process, breaking it into four stages: Begin Checkout, Add Shipping, Add Payment, and Purchase.

Drop-off at each stage equals users lost divided by the starting users for that step. Checkout completion is purchases divided by those who began checkout.

Look for the biggest gap between bars - that's where users struggle most. Shipping drop-off suggests address form issues. Payment drop-off might indicate trust concerns or payment method problems.

This answers: Where exactly do checkout abandonments happen? Is shipping or payment the bigger issue? And where should checkout optimization focus?

payload.formula_explanation: Maps user distribution across US states/regions, revealing geographic patterns in traffic, engagement, and customer value. Useful for regional marketing and logistics decisions.
how_to_read: Bars show user count by state, ranked highest to lowest
Large population states (CA, TX, FL) typically lead in volume
Look for states with high users relative to population (brand strength)
Engagement rate varies by region - some areas convert better
Revenue per user identifies high-value geographic segments
Climate patterns may affect gardening product interest
decisions_answered: Where are our customers concentrated geographically?
Which states have the highest customer density?
Are there regional engagement or conversion differences?
Should we run geo-targeted marketing campaigns?
Which regions represent expansion opportunities?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows user distribution across US states - revealing geographic patterns in your customer base.

The formula counts users by region, calculates engagement rates, and computes revenue per user for each state.

Reading this chart: Bars ranked by user count. Large states like California and Texas naturally lead. More interesting is density - states with high users relative to population indicate brand strength.

Look beyond volume. Some states may have fewer users but higher engagement or revenue per user. These represent quality expansion opportunities. Consider climate zones for seasonal products.

Key questions: Where are customers concentrated? Which states over-index? Are there regional conversion differences? Should you geo-target? This informs regional strategy.

payload.formula_explanation: Total unique users who visited over the period, summed from the website performance mart (GA4 sourced). It measures audience reach.
how_to_read: The main value shows total users for the period
Compare to the prior period to track reach
The sparkline shows the daily user trend
Compare to sessions to estimate repeat visits
Click to explore audiences and demographics
decisions_answered: How many distinct visitors reached the site?
Is our audience growing?
Are visitors returning or mostly new?

payload.formula_explanation: This trend line shows how the overall conversion rate changes week over week across a 26-week period. Upward trends indicate improving site performance, while downward trends or volatility may signal issues requiring investigation.
how_to_read: The X-axis shows weeks, spanning 26 weeks (roughly 6 months)
The Y-axis shows the conversion rate as a percentage
An upward trend indicates improving conversion performance
Sudden drops may correlate with site issues, seasonality, or campaign changes
Consistent performance within a tight range suggests stable operations
decisions_answered: Is our conversion rate trending up, down, or stable?
When did significant conversion rate changes occur?
Are there seasonal patterns in our conversion rate?
How quickly do we recover from conversion rate drops?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks your weekly conversion rate over 26 weeks, revealing long-term performance trends.

The formula is simple: weekly conversions divided by weekly users, times 100. Each data point represents one week's aggregate performance.

An upward slope means your site is converting better over time. Sudden dips warrant investigation - check for site issues, failed experiments, or external factors. Seasonal patterns can inform campaign timing.

This answers: Is conversion trending positively? When did major changes occur? What seasonal patterns exist? And how quickly do we bounce back from problems?
